prompts.title: Staff-Level Code Review
prompts.when: Reviewing a PR about to merge into main. You need real signal, not 'looks good'.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a staff engineer. Mindset: this code will run in production at 3am.</role>

<pr_diff>{{paste diff or file paths}}</pr_diff>

<output>
  ## Blocker (must fix before merge)
  Only items that can cause: data loss, security hole, silent corruption, or irreversible state.

  ## Major (should fix this iteration)
  Bugs, missing edge cases, performance cliffs under realistic load, observability gaps.

  ## Minor (next pass or follow-up issue)
  Naming, structure, missed abstractions. Format: `file:line: issue: suggestion`.

  ## Praise (be specific, 1-3 lines)
  What's well done? Without this, the review reads as a wall of criticism.

  ## Tests I'd want to see
  Concrete cases that would have caught the issues above.

  ## Verdict
  APPROVE | REQUEST CHANGES | COMMENT.
</output>
notes: Blocker/major/minor with severity is the only review structure that survives at scale. Everything else turns into style debate.
Praise matters: a review that only criticizes gets skipped on the next PR.
antiPatterns: Generic reviews ('looks good, just a few nits'). Useless.
Style-only reviews on a logic-heavy PR. Prioritize by blast radius.
Mixing praise and blame in the same line. Separate the sections so the author can read them differently.

prompts.title: Refactor Without Behavior Change
prompts.when: Cleaning up code that works but is hard to change. Must preserve behavior.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a senior engineer refactoring with surgical discipline.</role>

<target>{{file or module}}</target>

<constraints>
  - Behavior preservation: public API and observable behavior must not change.
  - Capture current behavior in tests FIRST if they don't exist.
  - No drive-by changes. If you want to fix a bug or improve naming, file it as a follow-up, do not bundle.
  - Small commits: 3-7 commits, each independently runnable.
  - Migration safety: feature flag or shadow mode if anything is risky.
</constraints>

<deliverables>
  ## Plan (commit-by-commit)
  ## Risk per commit (rollback strategy)
  ## Tests to add before starting
  ## Diff preview (only first commit)
</deliverables>
notes: The 'no drive-by changes' rule is what separates a clean PR history from a swamp. A bug fix doesn't need surrounding code cleaned up.
antiPatterns: Bundling 'while we're here' improvements into a refactor PR. The diff becomes un-reviewable.

prompts.title: Test Generation From Behavior
prompts.when: You have a function/module and want real test coverage, not 100%-coverage theater.
prompts.prompt:
Generate tests for {{function_or_module}}.

Constraints:
1. Test behavior, not implementation. If you change internals, tests should still pass.
2. Cover the 5 highest-risk scenarios first:
   - Boundary inputs (empty, max, off-by-one)
   - Failure paths (network down, malformed input, auth missing)
   - Concurrency if relevant
   - Idempotency if relevant
   - The 1 case the original author probably didn't think about
3. Each test name reads as a sentence.
4. No 'it works' tests. Every test asserts a specific behavior.
5. Use the testing framework already in the project: {{framework}}

Output: test file + 1-paragraph rationale per test.
notes: Pattern from frontier AI tool docs (Cursor, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex) and engineering practice.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the structural fields (role, task, method, output). The shape is the value.

prompts.title: Bug Hunt: Hypothesis Tree
prompts.when: Production bug, intermittent, no clear repro. Time to think before flailing.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a senior debugger. Goal: find the root cause, not the first plausible explanation.</role>

<symptom>{{1 sentence: what's broken}}</symptom>
<first_seen>{{timestamp}}</first_seen>
<last_known_good>{{timestamp or 'never'}}</last_known_good>
<repro>{{if any, paste; if none, say 'unknown'}}</repro>
<logs>{{last 200 lines or paste relevant snippets}}</logs>

<process>
  Step 1. List 5 candidate root causes, ranked by likelihood.
  Step 2. For each, design a verification: a log query, a metric, or a 1-line change that would confirm or eliminate it.
  Step 3. Pick the most likely. State confidence % and what evidence would move you off it.
  Step 4. Propose the minimal repro and fix.
  Step 5. Propose the tripwire that catches this class of bug earlier next time.
</process>

<anti_instructions>
  - Do not propose architectural rewrites.
  - Do not list every plausible cause equally. Force a ranking.
  - If 3 hypotheses remain plausible after evidence, name the missing evidence and how to collect it.
</anti_instructions>
notes: Force ranking is what kills 'list of 10 things that could be wrong'. Without it, the model hedges and the user wastes 3 hours.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the structural fields (role, task, method, output). The shape is the value.

prompts.title: Frontend Aesthetic: Beat The AI Slop
prompts.when: Generating a UI from a prompt. Need it to feel designed, not generated.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a senior product designer. You reject the 'AI slop' aesthetic.</role>

<directives>
  Typography:
  - Single font family with character (Inter for utility, Fraunces for editorial, Geist for tech).
  - Tight scale: 2-3 body sizes, 1-2 display. No 6+ sizes.
  - Body line-height 1.5-1.7. Display 1.0-1.2.
  - Optical kerning on.

  Color:
  - 1 neutral base (zinc, slate, stone).
  - Up to 2 accent colors. One reserved for primary actions.
  - Background not pure white/black: #FAFAF7 or #1A1816.
  - Contrast: 7:1 body text, 4.5:1 large text.

  Spacing:
  - Base unit 4px. All spacing a multiple: 4, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 48, 64.
  - Asymmetric padding reads more designed than symmetric.
  - Whitespace is content. Never fill it.

  Motion:
  - 120-280ms micro, 320-480ms transitions.
  - cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1) for entry.
  - Respect prefers-reduced-motion. Always.
</directives>

<avoid>
  - Centered hero + 2 buttons + stock illustration (every AI landing page look).
  - Three-column feature cards with icons in colored circles.
  - Gradient text on colored background.
  - Tailwind defaults without thought: gray-100, blue-500, rounded-2xl on everything.
</avoid>

Build {{component or page}}. Take design risks where appropriate. Surprise me.
notes: The 'AI slop' aesthetic comes from defaults the model overuses (rounded-2xl, gray-100, gradient text, three-column cards). Pushing back on each default produces distinctive output.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the structural fields (role, task, method, output). The shape is the value.

prompts.title: API Contract First
prompts.when: Designing a new REST or RPC API. Catch breaking-change risks before clients integrate.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an API designer who ships contracts that survive 5+ years of evolution.</role>

Design the v1 contract for {{endpoint_or_service}}.

<constraints>
  - Idempotency required on every mutating endpoint.
  - All errors return a stable {code, message, retryable, trace_id} shape.
  - Pagination supports both cursor and offset. Client picks at call time.
  - Versioning: path-based, never header-only. Deprecations announced 6 months ahead with sunset header.
  - Resource names plural nouns. Actions live on /actions, not verbs on the resource.
</constraints>

<deliverables>
  1. OpenAPI 3.1 skeleton (paths, schemas, error envelope, examples).
  2. Migration story: how a client written today survives 3 breaking-change scenarios you invent.
  3. 5 negative test cases the contract must reject.
</deliverables>
notes: Pattern from frontier AI tool docs (Cursor, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex) and engineering practice.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the structural fields (role, task, method, output). The shape is the value.

prompts.title: Database Schema Migration
prompts.when: Migrating schema in a live production database. Zero downtime, safe rollback.
prompts.prompt:
Migrate {{current_schema}} to {{target_schema}} in a live database with {{N}} rows and {{QPS}} req/s.

Constraints:
- Zero downtime. No maintenance window.
- Rollback must be safe at every step, not just at the end.
- Backward-compatible reads during the migration.
- Backward-compatible writes during the migration.

Output:
1. The migration as a sequence of steps, each individually reversible.
2. For each step: the SQL, expected lock duration, expected write throughput impact.
3. The cutover step (when old code paths are removed).
4. The rollback procedure (tested, not theoretical).
5. Monitoring: 3 metrics that tell you the migration is going wrong before users feel it.

If the migration CANNOT be done zero-downtime with safe rollback, say so explicitly. Don't pretend.
notes: Pattern from frontier AI tool docs (Cursor, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex) and engineering practice.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the structural fields (role, task, method, output). The shape is the value.

prompts.title: Security Audit (Threat-First)
prompts.when: Auditing code or a system for security issues. Threat-first, not checklist-first.
prompts.prompt:
<system>{{describe the system: web app, API, agent, etc.}}</system>
<data>{{what data does it touch: PII, secrets, payments, etc.}}</data>
<entry_points>{{who can call it and from where}}</entry_points>

<process>
  Step 1. List the top 10 threats in STRIDE format (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information disclosure, Denial of service, Elevation of privilege). For each: what the threat is, where it lives in this system, how an attacker would exploit it.
  Step 2. For each threat, name the existing mitigation or 'no mitigation'.
  Step 3. Rank by likelihood × impact.
  Step 4. For the top 3 risks, propose a concrete fix (code change, config change, or new control).
  Step 5. For each fix, estimate effort and risk of the fix itself.
</process>

Output: a 1-page report a CISO can act on. Not a 50-page checklist.
notes: Pattern from frontier AI tool docs (Cursor, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex) and engineering practice.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the structural fields (role, task, method, output). The shape is the value.

prompts.title: Performance Profiling
prompts.when: Something is slow. Don't guess. Profile.
prompts.prompt:
<symptom>{{p99 latency is X, expected Y; or throughput dropped Z%}}</symptom>
<profiling_data>{{paste flame graph, traces, or metric excerpts}}</profiling_data>

<process>
  Step 1. Identify the hot path. Where does the time/memory actually go? Not where you think it goes.
  Step 2. For the hot path, name the dominant cost (CPU, IO, lock contention, allocation, GC).
  Step 3. Propose 3 optimizations, ranked by ROI. Each must have a measurable pass/fail criterion.
  Step 4. For the top optimization, write the diff. Be specific: file:line, before/after.
  Step 5. Identify the risk: what could the change break? How do we test before merging?
</process>

Anti-instructions:
- Do not propose optimizations without evidence. 'Premature optimization' is the default.
- Do not propose micro-optimizations unless they show up in the profile.
- Do not propose caching unless you name what's cached and the invalidation rule.
notes: Pattern from frontier AI tool docs (Cursor, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex) and engineering practice.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the structural fields (role, task, method, output). The shape is the value.

prompts.title: Type Safety Upgrade (JS to TS)
prompts.when: Migrating JS to TS, or tightening types in an existing TS codebase.
prompts.prompt:
Migrate {{file or module}} from JS to TS (or from loose TS to strict TS).

Constraints:
- Incremental: every commit type-checks. No 'add // @ts-nocheck and fix later'.
- No `any` except at external boundaries. Justify each one inline.
- Strict mode on from the start: strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny.
- Public types are part of the API. Internal types are an implementation detail.

Output:
1. The migration order (file by file, or layer by layer).
2. The shared types to introduce first.
3. The 'any' escape hatches and why each is necessary.
4. The CI gate: a typecheck step that fails on regression.
notes: Pattern from frontier AI tool docs (Cursor, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex) and engineering practice.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the structural fields (role, task, method, output). The shape is the value.

prompts.title: Error Handling Strategy
prompts.when: Designing or refactoring how errors flow through a system.
prompts.prompt:
Design the error handling strategy for {{system_or_module}}.

Cover:
1. Error taxonomy: what classes of error exist (validation, transient, permanent, security, internal).
2. Where errors are caught: at boundaries (user input, external APIs) vs. internal calls.
3. What gets logged: structured fields, correlation IDs, redaction rules.
4. What gets surfaced to the caller: stable error code + message + retryability flag. No stack traces in user-facing responses.
5. Retry strategy: which errors retry, with what backoff (jittered), and what max attempts.
6. Circuit breaker: when to open, when to half-open, when to close.
7. User-facing messages: friendly by default, technical when relevant (CLI, dev tools).

Constraint: errors must be actionable. 'Something went wrong' is not acceptable.
notes: Pattern from frontier AI tool docs (Cursor, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex) and engineering practice.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the structural fields (role, task, method, output). The shape is the value.

prompts.title: Logging Strategy
prompts.when: Designing logs that help debug incidents, not floods.
prompts.prompt:
Define the logging strategy for {{system}}.

Cover:
1. Format: structured (JSON), required fields (timestamp, level, service, trace_id, message).
2. Levels: DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR. What goes at each level with examples.
3. What NEVER gets logged: PII, secrets, full request bodies, full stack traces of expected errors.
4. Correlation: how logs link to traces, how traces link to user requests.
5. Sampling: at high traffic, which logs are sampled and which are not (errors never sampled).
6. Volume discipline: target bytes/sec per service. If you're above it, fix the logging before adding more.

Rule: a log line must answer one of: who did what, what failed, what is unusual, what changed.
notes: Pattern from frontier AI tool docs (Cursor, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex) and engineering practice.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the structural fields (role, task, method, output). The shape is the value.

prompts.title: Observability: RED + USE
prompts.when: Instrumenting a service so you can debug it in production.
prompts.prompt:
Instrument {{service}} for production observability.

Apply the RED method for requests:
- Rate (req/s)
- Errors (req/s failing, broken down by class)
- Duration (p50, p95, p99)

Apply the USE method for resources:
- Utilization (% time busy)
- Saturation (queue depth, backlog)
- Errors (resource-specific)

Plus tracing:
- Trace context propagation across services.
- Span naming convention: service.operation.
- Critical path tags for filtering.

Output:
1. The metric definitions (name, type, labels, cardinality budget).
2. The trace points to add (one per request boundary, one per IO, one per external call).
3. The 5 dashboards that cover 90% of incident triage.
4. The 3 alerts that wake someone up (with runbook link).
notes: Pattern from frontier AI tool docs (Cursor, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex) and engineering practice.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the structural fields (role, task, method, output). The shape is the value.

prompts.title: Dependency Audit
prompts.when: Quarterly review of third-party deps. Or before adopting a new one.
prompts.prompt:
Audit {{package}} (or current dependency tree) for production use.

Per dependency, score:
- Maintenance: last commit, last release, response time to issues.
- Security: known CVEs, time-to-patch history, surface area.
- License: compatible with our distribution? (e.g., GPL in commercial product = no)
- Footprint: bundle size, install size, native deps.
- Maintainability: 1 maintainer vs. team, bus factor.
- Alternatives: 2-3 credible alternatives and why this one was chosen.

Output:
## Per-dependency scorecard (table)
## Replace / remove candidates (with reasoning)
## Adopt if needed (for new deps)
## Decisions for the next quarter
notes: Pattern from frontier AI tool docs (Cursor, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex) and engineering practice.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the structural fields (role, task, method, output). The shape is the value.

prompts.title: Code Archaeology
prompts.when: Inheriting an unfamiliar codebase. Need to understand it without breaking it.
prompts.prompt:
Build my mental model of {{codebase_or_module}}.

Inputs:
- The repo at {{path}}.
- The README and any ADRs (if they exist).
- {{N hours}} of my time.

Produce:
1. The 30-second elevator pitch: what is this, who uses it, why does it exist.
2. The 5-minute architecture: components, their responsibilities, their boundaries.
3. The 30-minute tour: where the important state lives, where the important IO happens, where the bugs are likely.
4. The 3 things the original author would want me to know (from git blame, comments, surviving tribal knowledge).
5. The 3 landmines to avoid (places where a small change has historically caused big breakage).
6. The vocabulary: project-specific terms I need to know before talking to the team.

Output as a short doc I can read once and refer back to.
notes: Pattern from frontier AI tool docs (Cursor, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex) and engineering practice.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the structural fields (role, task, method, output). The shape is the value.

prompts.title: Framework Migration Plan
prompts.when: Migrating from one framework to another (React class to hooks, Vue 2 to 3, etc.).
prompts.prompt:
Plan migration from {{from_framework}} to {{to_framework}} for {{codebase_size}} codebase.

Constraints:
- Production stays up throughout. No big-bang.
- Both frameworks coexist during migration.
- Each commit is independently shippable.
- Tests cover the migration (snapshot tests for parity, behavioral tests for new).

Output:
1. The migration phases (3-7 phases, each with a goal and a stop condition).
2. The bridging layer (how old code calls new, and vice versa).
3. The order of components to migrate (least-coupled first, highest-risk last or with shadow).
4. The rollback plan per phase.
5. The metrics that prove the migration is working (perf, error rate, feature parity coverage).
notes: Pattern from frontier AI tool docs (Cursor, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex) and engineering practice.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the structural fields (role, task, method, output). The shape is the value.

prompts.title: Feature Flag Strategy
prompts.when: Adding a feature that needs gradual rollout, A/B testing, or instant kill switch.
prompts.prompt:
Design the feature-flag rollout strategy for {{feature}}.

Cover:
1. Flag type: boolean, percentage, user-cohort, multivariate.
2. Lifecycle: when created, when promoted to permanent, when removed. The cleanup step is the part most teams skip.
3. Targeting: who sees the flag first (internal, beta, percentage ramp).
4. Metrics per stage: how you know it's working (success) and how you know it's broken (abort criteria).
5. Kill switch: how to disable instantly without a deploy.
6. Cleanup: who removes the flag and when. Stale flags are tech debt.

Output: a rollout plan with named stages, durations, and exit criteria.
notes: Pattern from frontier AI tool docs (Cursor, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex) and engineering practice.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the structural fields (role, task, method, output). The shape is the value.

prompts.title: Load Test Design
prompts.when: Validating that a system holds up at expected (or peak) load.
prompts.prompt:
Design a load test for {{system}}.

Cover:
1. Realistic workload: not just request count, but the mix of endpoints, payload sizes, and user behavior patterns.
2. Ramp profile: warmup, sustained, spike, soak (hours or days, not minutes).
3. Scenarios: nominal (steady), peak (Black Friday), failure (downstream slow/dead).
4. Metrics to capture: latency percentiles, error rates, saturation (CPU, memory, connections, queue depth).
5. Stop conditions: when to abort the test (error budget exhausted, latency SLO violated).
6. Post-mortem template: what to record if it goes wrong.

Anti-pattern: load tests that confirm the system works at 100 req/s when production sees 10,000. Match the reality.
notes: Pattern from frontier AI tool docs (Cursor, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex) and engineering practice.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the structural fields (role, task, method, output). The shape is the value.

prompts.title: PR Description From Diff
prompts.when: Opening a PR. Make it easy for the reviewer to know what changed and why.
prompts.prompt:
Generate a PR description from this diff.

<diff>{{paste}}</diff>

Output:
## What
(2-3 sentences: what changed. Plain language, not jargon.)

## Why
(The problem this solves or the gap it fills. Link the issue if there is one.)

## How
(1-3 bullets: the implementation approach. Only if non-obvious.)

## Risk
(What could break. What was tested. What was NOT tested and why that's OK or not.)

## Screenshots / recordings
(If user-facing, paste here. Otherwise N/A.)

## Checklist
- [ ] Tests added/updated
- [ ] Docs updated if user-facing
- [ ] Migration plan if applicable
- [ ] Rollback plan if high-risk
notes: Risk section is the most-leveraged part. Most PRs skip it, and reviewers have to guess.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the structural fields (role, task, method, output). The shape is the value.

prompts.title: Technical Debt Triage
prompts.when: Backlog of tech debt items. Need to pick what to pay down this quarter.
prompts.prompt:
Triage the technical debt in {{codebase_or_area}}.

Inputs:
- The debt items: {{list, one per line, with brief description}}
- Team velocity: {{story points per sprint}}
- Capacity for non-feature work this quarter: {{percentage or story points}}

For each item, score:
- Cost of carrying: how much it slows down new work, how many incidents it causes, how many devs hit it.
- Cost of fixing: effort in story points.
- Risk of fixing: regression risk, blast radius.
- Strategic value: does the fix unlock other work?

Output:
## Scored table (cost × effort × risk × strategic)
## Top 3 to fix this quarter (with the first PR for each)
## Top 3 to defer (with the trigger that would re-promote them)
## Top 3 to never fix (with why)
notes: Pattern from frontier AI tool docs (Cursor, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex) and engineering practice.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the structural fields (role, task, method, output). The shape is the value.

prompts.title: Reverse Engineer A Protocol
prompts.when: You have a binary, a network protocol, or a closed file format. Need to understand it.
prompts.prompt:
Reverse engineer {{artifact: binary | pcap | file_format | protocol}}.

Inputs:
- Sample(s): {{paste or describe}}
- Known context: {{vendor, version, observed behavior}}

Process:
1. Document every observable field/byte/header.
2. Propose a hypothesis for each unknown field, ranked by likelihood.
3. For each hypothesis, the test that confirms or eliminates it.
4. Build the spec as you go. Note which fields are confirmed vs. hypothesized.
5. Write a parser that handles the confirmed subset. Add tests for each field.
6. For the hypothesized fields, add comments and tests that fail until confirmed.

Output:
- A spec doc (formal where possible, prose where not).
- A reference parser implementation.
- A list of open questions and the experiments that would answer them.
notes: Pattern from frontier AI tool docs (Cursor, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex) and engineering practice.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the structural fields (role, task, method, output). The shape is the value.

prompts.title: Strip AI tells from any draft
prompts.when: After you have a draft (yours or AI-generated) and want it to read like a real human wrote it. The single most useful prompt in this category.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a senior editor at a respected trade publication. You have edited copy from founders, engineers, and operators for 15 years. You have a low tolerance for filler.</role>

<task>Rewrite the draft below so it reads like a sharp human wrote it on a deadline. Do not summarize, restructure, or improve the argument. Keep the meaning. Replace the language.</task>

<draft>
{{DRAFT}}
</draft>

<what_to_kill>
A non-exhaustive list of phrases and patterns to remove or rewrite. If any appear, cut them. Do not preserve them in scare quotes.

- "leverage", "leveraging", "leveraged"
- "robust", "seamless", "scalable" (when used as filler, not as a technical claim)
- "in today's fast-paced world", "in the modern era"
- "dive in", "dive into", "delve into", "delve deeper"
- "it's worth noting", "it's important to note"
- "let's explore", "let's take a look"
- "at the end of the day", "when all is said and done"
- "unlock", "unlock the power of", "elevate", "transform your"
- "navigate the complexities of", "in the realm of"
- "harness the power of", "harness"
- "game-changer", "game-changing", "revolutionize"
- "cutting-edge", "state-of-the-art", "next-generation"
- "moreover", "furthermore", "additionally" (as paragraph starters)
- "in conclusion", "to wrap up"
- "I hope this helps", "feel free to", "don't hesitate to"
- any sentence that begins with "So," or "Well,"
- any sentence that ends with an exclamation mark unless the source draft has one
</what_to_kill>

<voice_rules>
1. Short sentences. Average under 20 words. Mix in some under 10.
2. Prefer the active voice. "We shipped X" not "X was shipped".
3. Cut adjectives that do not change meaning. "real-time dashboard" is fine. "powerful real-time dashboard" is not.
4. Replace abstract nouns with the concrete thing they refer to. "The solution" -> name the solution.
5. Vary sentence openings. Do not start three sentences in a row with the same word.
6. Use "you" or a specific named person, not "users" or "one".
7. Contractions are allowed and often preferred.
8. Numbers, names, dates, dollar amounts stay as written. Do not round.
</voice_rules>

<output_format>
Return two sections, separated by a horizontal rule.

## Rewritten

The cleaned draft, plain text only. No headings inside unless the source had them.

## Edit notes

A short bullet list. For each notable change, one line: what you cut and why. If a sentence was untouched, do not list it. Aim for 5-10 notes total. Do not write more than 10.
</output_format>

<constraints>
- Do not add a preamble, intro, or sign-off.
- Do not explain what you changed in prose form. Use the edit notes.
- If the draft is already clean, say so in one sentence and return it unchanged.
- Do not change technical claims, numbers, or quoted material.
</constraints>

variants.chatgpt: Same prompt. GPT-5 responds well to the explicit kill list and benefits from a one-line reinforcement at the end: 'Treat the kill list as a contract. If a banned phrase appears in your rewrite, start that paragraph over.'

notes: Most 'AI tells' are not vocabulary words but rhythm tells: three-sentence paragraphs that each start with the same pattern, or a long sentence followed by two short ones followed by a long one. The voice rules force variation.
Separating the output into 'rewritten' and 'edit notes' makes the change auditable. The writer can disagree with any note without losing the rewrite.
The kill list is a contract, not a suggestion. Naming the exact phrases turns a vibe into a check.
antiPatterns: Do not turn this into a 'make it better' prompt. The task is purely linguistic. Argument stays put.
Do not add examples of good writing. The model will imitate them and lose the draft's actual voice.
Do not ask for a tone (confident, friendly, etc). Tone is what the source draft already has.

prompts.title: Landing page hero, sub-headline, CTA
prompts.when: You have a product and need the above-the-fold section of a landing page. Not the full page. Just the hero block.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write landing page copy for B2B SaaS. You have shipped hero blocks that convert above 5%. Your copy passes the 'skeptical engineer' test: a senior engineer reads it and does not roll their eyes.</role>

<task>Write the hero block for the product below. Three parts: eyebrow tag, headline, sub-headline, primary CTA, secondary CTA. No nav, no features, no testimonials.</task>

<product>
- Name: {{PRODUCT_NAME}}
- What it does (one sentence): {{ONE_LINE}}
- Primary audience: {{AUDIENCE}}
- Main alternative they use today: {{ALTERNATIVE}}
- Single sharpest benefit (the one a paying user would put in their review): {{SHARPEST_BENEFIT}}
- Pricing entry point if relevant: {{PRICE}}
</product>

<copy_rules>
1. Headline is 6-10 words. It states the outcome, not the product.
   Bad: "The all-in-one platform for modern teams"
   Good: "Ship pull requests in 4 hours, not 4 days"
2. Sub-headline is 15-25 words. It adds the qualifier the headline needs: who, for what, with what tradeoff.
3. Use the customer's words. Pull from the SHARPEST_BENEFIT field. If the benefit sounds like marketing, rewrite it in plain language.
4. The CTA button is a verb phrase, 2-3 words. Not "Get Started", not "Learn More".
   Examples: "Run my first query", "Generate a draft", "Connect my repo".
5. The secondary CTA is for the undecided. "See an example", "Watch the 90s demo", "Read the docs".
6. Eyebrow tag is 2-4 words. It frames the category. "For backend engineers", "Open source", "Self-hosted".
7. No AI tells. See the kill list in the system reference.
</copy_rules>

<output_format>
Return in this exact shape, plain text:

Eyebrow: ...
Headline: ...
Sub-headline: ...
Primary CTA: ...
Secondary CTA: ...

Then a short 'Rationale' block (3-5 bullets) explaining the headline angle and the choice of CTA verb.
</output_format>

<constraints>
- Do not write more than what fits above the fold of a 1440x900 screen.
- Do not include logos, social proof, or feature bullets.
- Do not invent numbers. If SHARPEST_BENEFIT has no number, do not add one.
- Write 5 headline candidates. Pick the strongest as Headline. Put the other four under 'Alternatives' for the user to swap in.
</constraints>

variants.chatgpt: Same prompt. GPT-5 tends to default to feature-led headlines. Add a final line: 'If your headline mentions a feature the customer did not ask for, replace it with an outcome they would type into a search bar.'
variants.gemini: Same prompt. For Gemini, prepend a temperature hint in the wrapper: temperature 0.7, topP 0.9. Gemini benefits from being told to generate all 5 candidates in one pass, not stream-of-thought.

notes: Headlines that name an outcome with a number beat headlines that name a category. 'Ship PRs in 4 hours' beats 'Faster code review'.
Two CTAs split intent: the primary is for the convinced, the secondary is for the curious. Same button text for both kills conversion.
The 5-headline-then-pick format stops the model from anchoring on its first idea.
antiPatterns: Do not ask for the full landing page. The hero is a different genre than the rest.
Do not request 'punchy' or 'bold'. Define the constraint (word count, structure) instead.
Do not let the model invent case-study claims. The product brief is the only source of truth.

prompts.title: Cold sales email with personalization
prompts.when: You are doing outbound sales and need a short, specific cold email that does not sound like a template.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write outbound sales email for a B2B product. Your reply rate on cold email averages above 8%. You write like a peer, not a vendor.</role>

<task>Write a cold email to one specific prospect. Under 90 words in the body. The prospect must feel that the email could only have been written to them, not to a list.</task>

<prospect>
- Name: {{FIRST_NAME}}
- Title: {{TITLE}}
- Company: {{COMPANY}}
- Public signal you noticed (a post, a hire, a launch, a job listing, a podcast appearance): {{SIGNAL}}
- Known pain you can address, in their language: {{PAIN}}
- The thing they almost certainly do today that your product replaces or speeds up: {{WORKAROUND}}
</prospect>

<your_product>
- What it does in one line: {{ONE_LINE}}
- The single most relevant capability for this prospect: {{CAPABILITY}}
- A proof point relevant to their industry or company size: {{PROOF}}
</your_product>

<email_structure>
Four sentences max in the body, in this order:

1. The signal: name what you noticed, in a way that shows you actually read it. One sentence. No flattery.
2. The cost: what that signal implies is hard, expensive, or slow for them today. One sentence. Concrete.
3. The bridge: a peer who faced the same thing used {{CAPABILITY}} and got a specific result. One sentence. Use PROOF.
4. The ask: a low-friction next step. "Worth a 15-minute look?", "Can I send the one-pager?", "Open to a 10-minute call next week?". One sentence. Give them an easy out.
</email_structure>

<subject_line>
Three subject line options, 5-8 words each. Do not use clickbait tactics (numbers in brackets, fake urgency, all caps). The subject must match the body, not tease something the body does not deliver.

Options to write:
- The signal + their company: "Saw your post on {{topic}}"
- A peer reference: "How {{peer_company}} handled {{pain}}"
- A direct ask: "Quick question about {{specific_thing}}"
</subject_line>

<signoff>
First name only. No "Best regards", no "Cheers", no quote-unquote signature block. The email ends after one sentence in signoff like "Happy to send the one-pager either way." or "No pressure if the timing is off."
</signoff>

<constraints>
- Total body under 90 words.
- No links in the body.
- No attachment mention unless you can actually attach.
- Do not include "I noticed" more than once.
- Do not use "I hope this email finds you well" or any of its cousins.
</constraints>

variants.glm-5.2: Same prompt. GLM 5.2 follows structured templates tightly but can over-index on the template. Add: 'If a sentence in the body could be sent to a different prospect by swapping the company name, rewrite it.'

notes: Cold email reply rate correlates with specificity. The signal + cost + bridge + ask structure forces specificity into each slot.
The 'low-friction ask' is the single highest-leverage line in cold email. 'Worth a 15-minute look?' beats 'Let me know if you'd like to chat' because it offers a default of yes.
Three subject lines gives the sender a real choice without forcing a regen.
antiPatterns: Do not add a PS line with an offer or urgency. It signals template.
Do not include multiple CTAs. One ask per email.
Do not write 'I came across your profile' as the opener. It is true but it is also what every other cold email says.

prompts.title: Follow-up email that does not beg
prompts.when: Someone did not reply to your last message and you need a follow-up that adds value or makes it easy to say no.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write follow-up emails for B2B sales and partnership work. You treat the recipient's inbox as a finite resource they have given you one unit of attention. You never beg.</role>

<context>
- Your previous email was sent on {{PREVIOUS_DATE}}.
- The subject line and the first sentence of that email are below. Do not repeat them verbatim.
- You have not received a reply.
- This is follow-up number {{N}} of 3. (N is 1, 2, or 3.)
</context>

<previous_email>
Subject: {{PREVIOUS_SUBJECT}}
First line: {{PREVIOUS_FIRST_LINE}}
</previous_email>

<recipient>
- Name: {{FIRST_NAME}}
- Title: {{TITLE}}
- Company: {{COMPANY}}
- Their likely current focus (infer from title and signal): {{FOCUS}}
</recipient>

<followup_rules>
Pick exactly one of these three modes, based on N:

### Mode A (N=1, the bump)
You are reminding them you exist. Add one new piece of value: a relevant article, a fresh customer win, a relevant change in their world that you noticed. Length: 3 sentences. No "just floating this back to the top of your inbox". Do not apologize for following up.

### Mode A1 (N=1, the breakup alternative)
Send a shorter message that explicitly grants permission to ignore. "No reply needed if the timing is off." This gets more replies than another value-add bump, and it gives you a clean exit.

### Mode B (N=2, the new angle)
Open with a different entry point: a peer they respect who uses your product, a case study with their industry, a different feature. Do not reference that you have emailed before except in one phrase: "Following up on my note from last week."

### Mode C (N=3, the close)
This is the last email. You will not email again. Make it easy to say no by giving them an explicit off-ramp, or pivot the ask down: "Even a one-line 'not now' is useful." Length: 2 sentences. Sign off clean.
</followup_rules>

<output_format>
Subject: ...
Body: ...

Then one sentence: 'Why this mode.' Pick from A, A1, B, or C, and justify in 8-12 words.
</output_format>

<constraints>
- Body under 70 words for A and B. Under 40 words for A1 and C.
- Do not include "circling back", "touching base", "wanted to follow up", "just checking in".
- Do not ask "Did you get my last email?". It assumes they ignored you.
- Do not include a calendar link in any version.
</constraints>
notes: Mode A1 (the breakup alternative) is the counterintuitive move that often gets the reply. Granting permission to ignore lowers the cost of responding.
The mode depends on N, not on vibes. Forcing the choice makes the model pick by structure, not by what sounds good.
The third follow-up should be the last. Many senders keep going. Three is the conventional limit and four starts to hurt the sender's reputation.
antiPatterns: Do not combine modes. One mode per email.
Do not reference that you noticed their silence. They know.
Do not include an emoji in any version.

prompts.title: Quickstart docs that get read in 30 seconds
prompts.when: You have a developer product and the README or quickstart is not converting evaluators into users.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write developer quickstarts for a living. You know that a developer skims for 30 seconds, decides whether to keep reading, and leaves if they cannot copy-paste a working snippet in under a minute.</role>

<task>Write the quickstart section for the product below. It is the only section. It must work end-to-end in under 60 seconds for a developer who already has the prerequisites.</task>

<product>
- Name: {{PRODUCT_NAME}}
- One-line description: {{ONE_LINE}}
- Primary language or runtime: {{LANG}}
- Install command: {{INSTALL_CMD}}
- Auth model (API key, OAuth, login): {{AUTH}}
- Smallest end-to-end example (5-15 lines of code): {{MIN_EXAMPLE}}
- Expected output of that example: {{EXPECTED_OUTPUT}}
- The one thing a developer must understand to get value in 5 minutes: {{CORE_IDEA}}
</product>

<structure>
The quickstart has exactly these sections, in this order. No more, no fewer.

### 1. One sentence
What the product does, in the words a developer would use to describe it to a coworker. No marketing. 15 words max.

### 2. Install
One command. The output of running that command, if useful.

### 3. Set your API key
Two lines. How to set the env var. A one-line note about where to get the key.

### 4. Run this
A working code block. Under 15 lines. Every line does something. No commented-out placeholders.

### 5. Expected output
The actual output the developer will see. If it is JSON, show JSON. If it is a URL, show the URL.

### 6. What just happened
Two sentences. The CORE_IDEA in plain language. No jargon that was not introduced.

### 7. Next steps
Three links with one-line descriptions. Not five, not one. Three.
</structure>

<voice>
- Imperative voice: "Install the CLI" not "You can install the CLI".
- No second person in headings.
- Contractions allowed.
- If a sentence does not move the developer from "I just landed here" to "I have a working result", cut it.
</voice>

<constraints>
- Total length: 200-400 words plus code blocks.
- No table of contents, no "Overview", no "Prerequisites" as a separate section. Put prerequisites inline.
- No screenshots. Code blocks only.
- No links to marketing pages. Only to docs, API reference, and one obvious next-step tutorial.
</constraints>

variants.chatgpt: Same prompt. GPT-5 sometimes expands 'What just happened' into a paragraph. Add: 'Section 6 is exactly two sentences. If you write three, cut one.'

notes: Most quickstarts fail at section 4 (Run this). A 30-line snippet with optional steps is harder to copy-paste than a 10-line minimal example.
Section 6 (What just happened) is what separates a quickstart from a code snippet. Without it the developer runs the code but does not understand what they did.
Three next steps, not five. Choice is paralysis at this point in the funnel.
antiPatterns: Do not include 'Why choose us' or comparison content. The developer already chose.
Do not add a troubleshooting section. Link to one. In quickstart length, troubleshooting kills momentum.
Do not write 'Now you have a working example!' or any celebratory sentence. The output is the celebration.

prompts.title: API reference page from a signature
prompts.when: You have an API signature and need a clean, scannable reference page that matches the conventions of Stripe, Twilio, or the GitHub API docs.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write API reference pages. You have shipped reference docs for at least one major API. You know that the page is read out of order: developers jump to the example first, then back to the parameters.</role>

<task>Write the API reference page for the endpoint below. Follow the structure exactly. Match the precision of Stripe or Twilio reference docs.</task>

<endpoint>
- HTTP method and path: {{METHOD_AND_PATH}}
- One-line summary: {{SUMMARY}}
- Required scope or permission: {{SCOPE}}
- Path parameters: {{PATH_PARAMS}}
- Query parameters: {{QUERY_PARAMS}}
- Request body schema (JSON): {{REQUEST_BODY}}
- Response body schema (JSON): {{RESPONSE_BODY}}
- Status codes returned: {{STATUS_CODES}}
- Idempotency: {{IDEMPOTENCY}}
- Rate limit: {{RATE_LIMIT}}
- A real or realistic example request and response: {{EXAMPLE}}
</endpoint>

<page_structure>
The page has these sections, in this order:

### Endpoint summary
METHOD /path in monospace. The one-line summary. A 'Try it' button if your docs support it.

### Path parameters
A table with columns: Name, Type, Required, Description. One row per parameter. Description under 12 words.

### Query parameters
Same table shape. If none, write 'None.'

### Request body
A short prose description (one sentence). Then a JSON schema block, then a worked example. Mark required fields in **bold** in the description.

### Response body
A short prose description. Then a JSON schema block, then a worked example for the 200 case. Note the shape changes for 4xx.

### Status codes
A table: Code, Meaning, When. One row per status code returned. Do not list 200 only.

### Example
A complete cURL block, a complete response block, and one language-specific example (Python or Node, pick whichever fits the product's audience). Each example must run if the developer has an API key.

### Errors
For each non-2xx status, the error code, the human-readable message, and the most common fix. One line each.

### Notes
Idempotency, rate limit, pagination, and any field-level gotchas. Bulleted. Under 80 words total.
</page_structure>

<constraints>
- No marketing language anywhere. No 'powerful', 'easy', 'simple'.
- No 'Overview' or 'Introduction' section. The endpoint summary is the intro.
- No 'See also' with more than three links.
- Every parameter must appear in the schema and the example. No orphans in either direction.
</constraints>
notes: API reference is read non-linearly. The example block is the entry point for most developers. If it does not run, the page fails.
Status code tables are the most-skipped section of API reference and the most-needed when something goes wrong. Make them scannable, not exhaustive.
Idempotency, rate limit, and pagination belong on every reference page even when they are unremarkable. Developers expect them.
antiPatterns: Do not write a tutorial in the reference page. Tutorials explain; reference documents.
Do not collapse all error responses into a single 'See errors' link. Each endpoint has its own error shape.
Do not invent fields. If the schema is unclear, mark them 'TBD' and move on.

prompts.title: Pull request description from a diff
prompts.when: You opened a PR and need a description that helps the reviewer decide whether to read the diff. Use the diff as the only source of truth.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write PR descriptions for a senior engineering team. The reviewer will read the description, then the diff. Your job is to make the description the shortest path to 'I know what to look for in this diff'.</role>

<task>Write the PR description below from the diff. Do not infer intent beyond what the diff shows. Mark anything you are guessing.</task>

<diff>
{{DIFF}}
</diff>

<context>
- Branch name: {{BRANCH}}
- Linked issue or ticket (if any): {{ISSUE}}
- Type of change (one of: feature, bugfix, refactor, chore, docs, perf): {{TYPE}}
- Risk level (low, medium, high) based on blast radius: {{RISK}}
</context>

<description_structure>
Use this exact shape:

### What
One or two sentences. The user-visible or operator-visible change. State what is different after this PR lands.

### Why
One or two sentences. The problem this solves, or the opportunity it captures. Reference ISSUE if it exists.

### How
Two to four bullets. The mechanism. What did you change under the hood. Each bullet is a sentence fragment, not a paragraph.

### Risk
One paragraph (under 60 words). What could break, what you tested, what you did not test. Be specific. 'I tested the happy path' is not enough.

### Test plan
A bulleted checklist. Each bullet is something a reviewer can run or verify. Include the exact commands, the exact inputs, and the expected output.

### Migrations, flags, rollouts
If any of these apply, one bullet each. If none apply, write 'None.' and nothing else.

### Out of scope
One or two bullets. The things a reviewer might expect that you did not do. Naming them preempts review comments.
</description_structure>

<constraints>
- Total under 350 words.
- Do not include the diff itself in the description.
- Do not include a 'Screenshots' section unless the PR changes UI and you have them.
- Do not start any sentence with 'This PR' or 'This change'. Start with the subject.
- Mark assumptions with '(assumed)' inline.
</constraints>

variants.chatgpt: Same prompt. GPT-5 sometimes pads 'How' with a paragraph. Add: 'The How section is bullets, not prose. If a bullet exceeds 20 words, split it.'
variants.minimax-m3: Same prompt. M3 prefers a tighter checklist format. The Test plan section can be a markdown checklist with `- [ ]` boxes so reviewers can tick off as they verify.

notes: The 'Risk' section is where most PR descriptions fail. Naming what you did not test is more useful than naming what you did.
Out-of-scope is a small section that prevents 30% of review comments. Reviewers ask 'why did you not also do X?'; if X is in out-of-scope, the question is already answered.
Bullets in 'How' force the model to commit to mechanism, not intent. Mechanism is what the reviewer verifies.
antiPatterns: Do not write a 'Changes' section that lists every file modified. Use the diff for that.
Do not paste the commit message as the description. Commits and PRs are different audiences.
Do not omit the Test plan even if it is short. A PR without a test plan reads as untested.

prompts.title: Conventional commit message from a diff
prompts.when: You staged a change and need a commit message that follows Conventional Commits, summarizes the change in one line, and explains the why in the body.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write git commit messages. You follow Conventional Commits. You treat the subject line as a search query someone will run with `git log --grep` six months from now.</role>

<task>Write the commit message below from the diff. Subject under 72 characters. Body wrapped at 72.</task>

<diff>
{{DIFF}}
</diff>

<format>
```
<type>(<scope>): <subject>

<body>

<footer>
```

- type: one of feat, fix, refactor, perf, docs, test, chore, build, ci
- scope: the module or area touched, in kebab-case. Optional. If the change is cross-cutting, omit.
- subject: imperative mood, lowercase after the colon, no trailing period, under 72 chars.
- body: explain WHY, not WHAT. The diff shows what. Reference the motivation, the constraint, or the alternative you rejected.
- footer: 'BREAKING CHANGE: <description>' if applicable, otherwise 'Refs: <issue>' if applicable, otherwise omit.
</format>

<rules>
1. The subject must be a complete thought. Bad: 'fix bug'. Good: 'fix: prevent double-charge on retried webhook'.
2. The body explains the decision, not the diff. If the body could be replaced with 'see diff', cut it.
3. Use the imperative. 'add', not 'added' or 'adds'.
4. Wrap the body at 72 columns.
5. Separate paragraphs in the body with blank lines.
6. If the change is a revert, use 'revert: <original subject>' and reference the original commit SHA in the footer.
</rules>

<constraints>
- One commit message per response.
- Do not include the diff in the output.
- Do not include a 'Generated with' footer.
- Do not include a co-author trailer unless the diff clearly shows co-authored work.
</constraints>
notes: The most common commit message failure is a subject that summarizes the file changed, not the behavior changed. 'update auth.ts' fails; 'fix: prevent stale token reuse after password reset' succeeds.
The body exists for the 'why'. A reviewer reading the diff in six months wants to know what was hard, what was rejected, what is fragile.
Wrapping at 72 is a fossil from email but it survives because `git log` and most terminals default to it.
antiPatterns: Do not write multi-line subjects or use bullet lists in the subject.
Do not write 'WIP' or 'fix tests' or 'address comments' as a real subject. Use them in the body if needed.
Do not include the diff stats (files changed, insertions, deletions) in the message.

prompts.title: Changelog entry from a list of commits
prompts.when: You are about to cut a release and need a clean changelog entry grouped by type, written for the user, not for the engineer.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write user-facing changelogs (Keep a Changelog format). You translate engineer-speak into user-speak without losing precision.</role>

<task>Group the commits below into a changelog entry for version {{VERSION}} released on {{DATE}}. Write each entry as a user would describe it.</task>

<commits>
{{COMMITS}}
</commits>

<format>
Follow Keep a Changelog (https://keepachangelog.com). Use these sections in this order. Omit a section if empty.

### Added
New features the user can use.

### Changed
Behavior changes to existing features. The user has to do something different, or the system behaves differently.

### Deprecated
Features marked for removal. Include the planned removal version if known.

### Removed
Features removed in this release.

### Fixed
Bugs the user would have noticed.

### Security
Security fixes. Be careful: do not include details that help an attacker. Reference the CVE if there is one.

Each entry:
- Starts with a verb in past tense for Fixed/Security, present tense for Added/Changed.
- Names the feature or area, not the file or function.
- Under 90 characters per line. Wrap if longer.
- One user-visible change per entry.
</format>

<rules>
1. Group by type. Within a type, newest first.
2. Merge commits that touch the same feature into one entry. Three commits about the same login fix become one 'Fixed' entry.
3. Drop chores, refactors, and test changes unless they have a user-visible effect.
4. Translate 'fix: handle null user_id in session refresh' to 'Fixed a crash that happened when a signed-out user opened a stale tab'.
5. Do not include commit SHAs or PR numbers in the entry. Link them in a separate 'References' section at the end if needed.
6. If a change is behind a feature flag, note that with '(flag: name)'.
7. If a change requires user action (run a migration, rotate a key, update a config), call it out with '(action required)'.
</rules>

<constraints>
- No emoji.
- No 'various improvements' or 'misc fixes'.
- No marketing language. 'A faster, more reliable experience' is not a changelog entry.
- The output is markdown, ready to paste into CHANGELOG.md.
</constraints>
notes: The hardest part of writing a changelog is the translation step. 'feat: implement exponential backoff on retry queue' becomes 'Reduced background job failure rate when upstream services are slow.'
Dropping chores and refactors is not censorship, it is signal-to-noise. A changelog full of internal noise tells the user nothing.
Action-required markers are the highest-value thing in a changelog. They prevent support tickets.
antiPatterns: Do not write 'Bug fixes and performance improvements' as a section. That is the absence of a changelog.
Do not include internal refactors under 'Changed' unless the user can observe the change.
Do not use 'we' in entries. The user is the subject, not the team.

prompts.title: Release notes for a product launch
prompts.when: You are shipping a major release and need release notes that the marketing site, the email announcement, and the in-app modal can all pull from one source.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write release notes that double as launch announcements. Your audience is mixed: existing users, evaluators, and the press. The notes must serve all three without splitting into three documents.</role>

<task>Write the release notes for the launch below. Produce three blocks: a marketing-friendly summary (60-90 words), a feature-by-feature section (3-6 features), and an in-app modal blurb (under 30 words).</task>

<release>
- Product: {{PRODUCT}}
- Version: {{VERSION}}
- Theme of the release (one sentence): {{THEME}}
- Date: {{DATE}}
</release>

<features>
For each feature, list:
- Name: {{FEATURE_NAME}}
- What it does (user-facing): {{WHAT}}
- Why it matters now (the trigger, the customer ask, the competitive shift): {{WHY_NOW}}
- A proof point (number, customer quote, or example): {{PROOF}}
- Target user segment (existing, new, both): {{SEGMENT}}

{{FEATURE_LIST}}
</features>

<output_format>
Three blocks, separated by horizontal rules.

---

### Summary

A paragraph of 60-90 words. No headings inside. The paragraph names the theme, names 2-3 of the biggest changes in user language, and closes with one line about what is next.

### Features

For each feature in FEATURE_LIST, write a section with this shape:

#### {{FEATURE_NAME}}
What changed: 1-2 sentences in user language.
Why now: 1 sentence on the trigger.
Try it: 1 sentence. The exact action the user takes to see this in their account. If there is a UI path or API endpoint, name it.

### In-app modal

One sentence under 30 words. Tells an existing user to look at what is new without forcing a tour. The modal text is what shows in a dismissible banner.

---

Then a short 'Press angle' paragraph (2-3 sentences) describing the story a tech journalist would tell about this release. Tone: specific, not breathless.
</output_format>

<rules>
1. Use the customer's words in feature names where possible. If the engineering name is 'collaborative cursors', write 'live cursors' if users call it that.
2. Numbers in the proof point must come from PROOF. Do not invent.
3. The Try it line is concrete. 'Try it in the dashboard' is not. 'Click Settings -> Notifications -> Weekly digest' is.
4. The summary is not a list. It is a paragraph.
</rules>

<constraints>
- No AI tells. No 'powerful', 'seamless', 'game-changing'.
- No exclamation marks in the summary.
- No emoji unless the brand uses emoji by default.
- The in-app modal sentence must fit on one line at standard modal width.
</constraints>

variants.chatgpt: Same prompt. GPT-5 benefits from an explicit closing reminder: 'The summary paragraph must work as the first paragraph of a press release without modification. Write it to be quoted.'

notes: Three blocks from one source avoids the 'marketing copy and product copy disagree' problem. Same source, three lengths.
The Try it line is the most under-written part of release notes. Without it the user has no path from reading to using.
Press angle at the end is a forcing function: if you cannot write the angle, the release is not interesting enough to announce.
antiPatterns: Do not include 'Bug fixes and performance improvements' as a feature section. Save those for the changelog.
Do not use the same wording in the summary and the features. The summary is the hook, the features are the receipts.
Do not include pricing or plan changes in the release notes. Those go in a separate email.

prompts.title: Blameless incident postmortem
prompts.when: An incident is resolved and you need a postmortem that the team, the leadership, and the customers can all read without flinching.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write blameless incident postmortems for a SRE or platform team. You assume the reader is the CEO on Monday morning and a new hire six months from now. Both audiences need to learn something.</role>

<task>Write the postmortem below from the incident summary. Follow the structure exactly. Use the exact incident timeline. Do not invent facts not present in the source.</task>

<incident>
- Title: {{TITLE}}
- Date and time of incident (with timezone): {{START}} to {{END}}
- Severity (1-4, where 1 is full outage): {{SEVERITY}}
- Customer-facing impact: {{IMPACT}}
- Detection time (when alerts fired or customers reported): {{DETECTION_TIME}}
- Mitigation time (when the bleeding stopped): {{MITIGATION_TIME}}
- Resolution time (when full service restored): {{RESOLUTION_TIME}}
- Detection method (alert, customer report, internal): {{DETECTION_METHOD}}
- Timeline (UTC, one bullet per meaningful event): {{TIMELINE}}
- Root cause (technical, not human): {{ROOT_CAUSE}}
- Contributing factors: {{FACTORS}}
- What worked in the response: {{WORKED}}
- What did not work in the response: {{DID_NOT_WORK}}
- Customer comms sent during the incident: {{COMMS}}
</incident>

<structure>
The postmortem has these sections, in this order. No sub-sections inside.

## Summary
Three sentences. What broke, how bad it was, how long it lasted. Lead with the customer-facing impact.

## Impact
Numbers. Requests failed, users affected, revenue lost, data lost, or 'none'. One row per metric. Use the exact figures from IMPACT.

## Timeline
A table or a bulleted list. Each row: UTC time, what happened, who acted. Time is the primary column. Skip rows where nothing changed.

## Root cause
Two to four sentences. The technical mechanism that produced the incident. End with the single sentence that, if true, would have prevented this: 'This would not have happened if X.'

## Contributing factors
A bulleted list. Each factor is a sentence. No 'human error'. If a human action contributed, name the system that allowed the action to be the wrong one. Examples: 'The runbook did not cover this failure mode', 'The dashboard did not surface the relevant metric'.

## What went well
Bulleted. The decisions and systems that worked. Be specific so the team knows what to keep doing.

## What went poorly
Bulleted. The decisions and systems that did not work. Frame each one as a system fix, not a person fix.

## Action items
A table. Columns: Action, Owner, Type (prevent, detect, respond), Priority (P0-P3), Due date. Every action item must be a system change, a runbook update, or a process change. No 'be more careful'.

## Lessons
Two to three sentences. What the broader engineering org should take from this. One lesson, not a list.
</structure>

<rules>
1. No names of individuals in the body. Roles only ('the on-call engineer', 'the release manager'). The owner column in the action items is where names belong.
2. Numbers in Impact must come from the source. If a number is unknown, write 'unknown'.
3. The postmortem is published internally and, in most cases, externally. Write accordingly.
4. Do not include customer names unless this is internal-only and the customer consented.
5. Action items must have an owner and a due date. Items without owners do not get done.
</rules>

<constraints>
- Under 1200 words.
- No 'Root cause: human error'. Ever. If a human did something wrong, name the system that should have caught it.
- No 'Lessons learned' as a section header. Use 'Lessons'.
</constraints>
notes: Blameless is not soft. It is rigorous. Naming the system that allowed the wrong action is more actionable than naming the person who took it.
The 'This would not have happened if X' line is the single most useful sentence in any postmortem. It is also the hardest to write, because it forces the team to commit to a specific fix.
Action items without owners do not happen. The owner column is not optional.
antiPatterns: Do not write a 'What we are doing about it' section that is fluff. The Action items table is that section.
Do not include the full Slack thread or call transcript. Link to it.
Do not write the postmortem in the past tense for the Action items. Use future tense. 'Add a runbook entry' not 'Added a runbook entry'.

prompts.title: Bug report engineers can act on
prompts.when: You hit a bug and need to write a bug report that an engineer can pick up without a follow-up call.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write bug reports that engineers can act on without a meeting. You assume the engineer reading it has never seen the feature before.</role>

<task>Write the bug report below from the details. Use the structure exactly. Every field is required. If you do not know a field, write 'Unknown' and explain in one short clause.</task>

<bug>
- One-line description: {{ONE_LINE}}
- Severity (blocker, major, minor, cosmetic): {{SEVERITY}}
- Where it happened (URL, screen, endpoint, command): {{LOCATION}}
- Steps to reproduce: {{STEPS}}
- What you expected: {{EXPECTED}}
- What actually happened: {{ACTUAL}}
- How often it happens (always, intermittent, once): {{FREQUENCY}}
- First time you saw it: {{FIRST_SEEN}}
- Workaround if you found one: {{WORKAROUND}}
- Screenshots, screen recording, or logs: {{ATTACHMENTS}}
</bug>

<structure>
The bug report has these fields, in this order:

### Title
One line. Imperative. 'Cancel button does not cancel' not 'There is a bug with the cancel button'.

### Severity
One of: blocker, major, minor, cosmetic. Plus a one-sentence justification: 'blocker because X cannot proceed without this'.

### Environment
A short list. App version, OS, browser, account type, region, anything else that varies. If the bug is in an API, include the SDK version and language.

### Steps to reproduce
Numbered list. Each step is one action. The engineer should be able to start from a clean state and reach the bug by following the list.

### Expected behavior
What the user expected to see. State the contract, not the implementation. 'The cancel button closes the modal without saving' not 'The cancel button calls the close handler'.

### Actual behavior
What actually happened. Include the error message verbatim. If there is a stack trace, include the top frame.

### Frequency
Always, intermittent (about X of Y attempts), or once. If intermittent, include the conditions where it happens and where it does not.

### Workaround
What the user did to get unstuck. If there is no workaround, write 'None'.

### Attachments
Links to screenshots, screen recordings, logs, or HAR files. If none, write 'None'.

### Notes
Anything else that might be relevant. Patterns you noticed, related bugs, hypotheses. Mark hypotheses as 'hypothesis:'.
</structure>

<rules>
1. The Steps to reproduce must start from a state the engineer can reach. Not 'after I imported my data' unless you include the import step.
2. Expected and Actual are different. If they read the same, the bug report is incomplete.
3. Verbatim error messages beat paraphrased ones. Copy and paste.
4. If the bug is in production only, say so. If it is in staging only, say so.
5. The Title is searchable. It must include the affected feature, not just the symptom.
</rules>

<constraints>
- No 'Steps to reproduce' as a paragraph. Always a numbered list.
- No 'I think' or 'maybe' in Steps or Expected. Save uncertainty for Notes.
- No screenshots embedded as base64. Link to them.
</constraints>
notes: The title is a search query. 'Cancel button does not cancel' beats 'Bug in modal'.
Expected vs Actual is the field most bug reports skip. Without it, the engineer has to guess what the user wanted, which leads to fix-the-symptom work.
Frequency is what tells the engineer whether to fix it now or queue it. 'Always' is P1. 'Once' is a hypothesis until reproduced.
antiPatterns: Do not include the user's emotional state ('this is very frustrating'). The severity field covers that.
Do not paste the entire log file. Include the top frame and a link.
Do not write 'Please fix ASAP' or 'This is urgent'. Severity covers urgency.

prompts.title: User story and acceptance criteria
prompts.when: You are writing a user story for a feature and need to define what 'done' looks like before engineering starts.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write user stories for a product team. You know that a story is not done when engineering ships code. It is done when the user can do the thing.</role>

<task>Write the user story and acceptance criteria below. Use the structure exactly. Be specific to the user and the context. No 'a user' or 'the system'.</task>

<feature>
- Who is this for (a specific user type, with their context): {{PERSONA}}
- What they want to do (the action, not the feature): {{ACTION}}
- Why they want to do it (the underlying job): {{JOB}}
- The success condition (what they see when it works): {{SUCCESS}}
</feature>

<story>
Use this exact format. The story is one sentence.

As a {{PERSONA}}, I want to {{ACTION}} so that {{JOB}}.

Then a short paragraph (2-4 sentences) called 'Context' that names the trigger, the constraints, and the assumptions. Reference the SUCCESS as the visible outcome.

Then 'Out of scope', a bulleted list of 2-5 things a reasonable reader might expect that this story does not include. Each bullet is one sentence.

Then 'Dependencies', a bulleted list of systems, teams, or other stories this depends on. If none, write 'None.'
</story>

<acceptance_criteria>
Use Given/When/Then. Write 3-7 criteria. Each criterion is testable in under a minute by someone with no prior context.

Format:

### AC1: {{TITLE}}
Given {{a specific precondition}}
When {{a specific action}}
Then {{a specific observable result}}

### AC2: ...
</acceptance_criteria>

<rules>
1. Each criterion has exactly one When and one Then. If you need two Whens, you have two criteria.
2. The Given is a precondition, not a setup step. 'Given the user is signed in', not 'Given the user clicks Sign In then types their password'.
3. The Then is observable. 'The user sees a confirmation message' is observable. 'The system records the action' is observable only if there is a visible artifact.
4. Cover the edge cases. Empty state, error state, the boundary case, and at least one negative path.
5. The title of the AC describes the behavior, not the implementation. 'Empty cart shows empty state' not 'Render EmptyCart component'.
</rules>

<constraints>
- No implementation details in the story or the AC. 'Calls POST /api/v2/items' does not belong here.
- No 'etc.' or 'and so on' in the AC. Every Then is a specific thing.
- The story is one sentence. The Context is a paragraph. The AC is a numbered list.
</constraints>

variants.minimax-m3: Same prompt. M3 prefers the AC titles to be specific. Add: 'Each AC title should be searchable. If the engineer types the title into the codebase and finds no match, the title is too generic.'

notes: Out of scope is the most underused section of a user story. It preempts the 'why did you not also...' review comment that arrives after the story ships.
Given/When/Then forces specificity. If you cannot write the Then, you do not know what the feature does.
Each AC should be testable in under a minute. If it takes longer, the AC is doing the job of multiple stories.
antiPatterns: Do not write 'As a user'. Name the persona.
Do not write acceptance criteria that the engineer cannot verify without reading the implementation. The AC is for QA and the user, not for code review.
Do not include non-functional requirements (perf, accessibility, security) as ACs unless they are part of the user-visible behavior. Put them in a separate 'Non-functional requirements' section.

prompts.title: Architecture decision record (ADR)
prompts.when: The team made a non-trivial architecture decision and needs a permanent record of what was decided, why, and what was rejected.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write Architecture Decision Records (ADRs). You write for the engineer who joins the team in two years and has to understand why the system looks the way it does.</role>

<task>Write the ADR below. Use the structure exactly. Cite the rejected alternatives fairly. State the decision as a commitment.</task>

<decision>
- Title: {{TITLE}}
- Status (proposed, accepted, deprecated, superseded): {{STATUS}}
- Date: {{DATE}}
- Author(s): {{AUTHORS}}
- Supersedes (ADR number, if any): {{SUPERSEDES}}
</decision>

<context>
{{CONTEXT}}
</context>

<options>
For each option considered:
- Name: {{OPTION_NAME}}
- One-line summary: {{SUMMARY}}
- Pros: {{PROS}}
- Cons: {{CONS}}
- Cost (engineering effort, dollars, ops burden): {{COST}}
- Reversibility (cheap, moderate, expensive, irreversible): {{REVERSIBILITY}}

{{OPTIONS}}
</options>

<structure>
The ADR has these sections, in this order.

## Status
One line. The status from the input. Plus one line: 'Last updated: DATE' if the decision has been amended.

## Context
Two to four paragraphs. The forces in play: the problem, the constraints, the stakeholders, the timeline. End the section with the question this ADR answers. The question is one sentence.

## Decision
One paragraph. The chosen option. State it as a commitment: 'We will X.' Not 'We considered X.' The paragraph also names the option chosen and the option rejected by name.

## Consequences
Three subsections.

### Positive
The things that get easier because of this decision. Bulleted. Each bullet is a sentence.

### Negative
The things that get harder or that we are accepting. Bulleted. Be honest. If we are trading off scalability for simplicity, name that.

### Risks
The things that could go wrong because of this decision. Bulleted. Each risk names a trigger that would make us revisit the decision.

## Alternatives considered
One subsection per option not chosen. Each subsection is two to four sentences: what it was, why it was rejected, and under what circumstances we would revisit it.

## References
Links to relevant docs, RFCs, prior ADRs, benchmarks, or discussions. Bullet list. If none, write 'None.'
</structure>

<rules>
1. The Decision paragraph is one paragraph. If you need a second paragraph, you have two decisions and need two ADRs.
2. The Alternatives section is not a dump. Each alternative is one paragraph explaining why it was rejected. If you cannot explain why it was rejected, you did not actually consider it.
3. The Risks section names triggers. 'If traffic grows past X, we will revisit' is a risk with a trigger. 'Performance might suffer' is not.
4. Cite the date the decision was made, not the date the ADR was written. They are often different.
5. Use present tense for the Decision and Consequences. Past tense for the Context (the forces that led here).
</rules>

<constraints>
- Under 1500 words.
- No 'we' in the Decision section. Use the team or system name.
- No links that will rot. Use permanent URLs (RFCs, internal docs at stable paths).
</constraints>
notes: ADRs are most useful two years after they are written, when the engineer joining the team asks 'why is it this way?' The future reader is the audience.
The Alternatives section is what most ADRs skip. It is also what makes the ADR trustworthy. If the team did not consider alternatives, the decision was not made deliberately.
Risks with triggers turn an ADR from a tombstone into a living document. The team can grep for triggers and know when to revisit.
antiPatterns: Do not write the ADR after the fact in past tense to hide the decision. Present tense forces commitment.
Do not include code in the ADR. Reference it.
Do not include the meeting minutes. The ADR is the artifact; the minutes are noise.

prompts.title: Slack message with no fluff
prompts.when: You need to post an update, ask a question, or unblock the team in a Slack channel.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write Slack messages for an engineering team. You know that a Slack message is read on a phone, in the middle of another task, by someone who has 14 unread channels.</role>

<task>Write the Slack message below for the channel and audience. Match the purpose to the structure. No fluff.</task>

<purpose>
Pick exactly one of: {{announce, ask, unblock, escalate, followup, hand-off}}

The message structure depends on the purpose.
</purpose>

<message_context>
- Channel: {{CHANNEL}}
- Audience (who will read it): {{AUDIENCE}}
- The thing you need to communicate in one sentence: {{ONE_LINE}}
- Background (the reader does not have): {{BACKGROUND}}
- Action you need from the reader (if any): {{ACTION}}
- Deadline for action (if any): {{DEADLINE}}
</message_context>

<structures>

### announce
Use this when you are telling the team something happened or is about to happen. Structure: TL;DR in the first sentence. Then 2-4 sentences of detail. End with a link or a thread-starting question.

Format:
**[TL;DR sentence]**
[Detail sentences]
[Link or question]

### ask
Use this when you need an answer. Structure: one sentence on what you need, one sentence on why, one sentence on the deadline. Tag the person if you need a specific human.

Format:
@[person] - [what you need]
[Why]
[Deadline]

### unblock
Use this when you need the team to do something so you can proceed. Structure: what is blocking you, what you need, who can unblock you, what you are doing in the meantime.

Format:
Blocked on: [what]
Need: [what]
Owner: @[person or role]
In the meantime: [what you are doing]

### escalate
Use this when something is on fire and you need eyes or a decision. Structure: what is wrong, blast radius, what you have tried, what you need now. Be specific. Vague escalations get ignored.

Format:
[What is wrong]
Blast radius: [who is affected]
Tried: [what you have already done]
Need now: [the specific decision or help]

### followup
Use this when you posted something earlier and need an update. Structure: link to the original message, what you need now, the deadline.

Format:
Following up on [link to original].
Need: [what]
Deadline: [when]

### hand-off
Use this when you are passing work to someone else. Structure: what is done, what is in flight, what is open, where the docs are.

Format:
Done: [list]
In flight: [list]
Open: [list]
Docs: [link]
</structures>

<rules>
1. The first sentence must work without the rest. Slack truncates.
2. No 'FYI' as the opening line. State the substance.
3. No 'just wanted to' or 'quick question' or 'when you have a sec'. Those phrases add latency.
4. Threads belong in threads. The channel message is the announcement; the conversation lives below.
5. Use threads for questions that have one clear owner. Use the channel for questions that benefit from multiple eyes.
6. Code blocks, links, and @mentions render as themselves. Use them.
</rules>

<constraints>
- Under 80 words for ask, unblock, followup. Under 150 words for announce, escalate, hand-off.
- No emoji as bullet points.
- No 'Please' as the first word. Save it for the end if needed.
- No exclamation marks except in hand-offs and announces where it is genuinely celebratory.
</constraints>
notes: The first sentence must work without the rest because Slack truncates previews and people read on phones. The TL;DR is not optional.
Matching structure to purpose stops the same message from doing five jobs. An ask that looks like an announce does not get answered.
Naming the blast radius in an escalation forces the sender to think about who cares. It also tells the reader how fast to act.
antiPatterns: Do not write 'Looping in @channel' without saying why. It spams people.
Do not use ':wave:' or 'gm' or any social opener. Slack is not email.
Do not write a thread-instead message as a single wall of text. Use the structure.

prompts.title: Meeting agenda that earns its slot
prompts.when: You have a recurring or one-off meeting and need an agenda that actually drives decisions instead of filling time.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write meeting agendas for engineering and product teams. You believe a meeting without a decision-making purpose is a status update that should have been a memo.</role>

<task>Write the meeting agenda below. Each item has a time box, a purpose, and an owner. The agenda ends with a clear set of decisions to make or notes to capture.</task>

<meeting>
- Title: {{TITLE}}
- Date and duration: {{DATE}}, {{DURATION}}
- Attendees (with roles): {{ATTENDEES}}
- The decision this meeting must produce, if any: {{DECISION}}
- The recurring or one-off nature: {{RECURRING_OR_ONE_OFF}}
- Constraints (people leaving early, hard stops, prerequisites): {{CONSTRAINTS}}
</meeting>

<agenda_structure>
The agenda has these sections, in this order.

### Pre-read
A bulleted list of links the attendees must read before the meeting. Two to four links max. If there is no pre-read, write 'None. This meeting is operational.' and skip the rest of this section.

### Goals
One to three sentences. What this meeting must accomplish. If the meeting cannot be summarized in three sentences, it is two meetings.

### Agenda

A table with four columns: Time, Topic, Owner, Purpose.

The Purpose column has exactly one of: Decide, Discuss, Inform, Unblock, Align.

- Decide: there is a specific decision to make at this time. The decision must be stated in the row.
- Discuss: there is a question to answer. The question must be stated in the row.
- Inform: a one-way update. The owner talks, others listen.
- Unblock: someone is blocked and the group can help.
- Align: the group needs a shared mental model. No decision, but a common understanding to leave with.

Each row has a time box. Total time boxes sum to 80% of the meeting duration. The remaining 20% is buffer.

End the agenda with a final row: 'Wrap and capture', Purpose = Decide. The owner writes down the decisions and action items before anyone leaves.

### Decisions to make
A bulleted list pulled from the Decide rows in the agenda. This is the checklist the meeting must close with. If a Decide row has no decision at the end, the meeting failed.

### Action items
Empty at the start. The owner captures these during the meeting. Format: Action, Owner, Due date.

### Notes
Optional. Anything the attendees should know that does not fit above. E.g., 'We are skipping the demo this week because the feature is not ready'.
</agenda_structure>

<rules>
1. If the meeting has no Decide rows, it should not be a meeting. Either add a decision or cancel.
2. Inform-only items belong at the start of the agenda. Decide items belong at the end, when the discussion has context.
3. The owner of an Inform row is the person talking. The owner of a Decide row is the person who will write down the decision.
4. Pre-read links must be skimmable in 10 minutes. If a link is longer, say so and tell attendees which section to read.
