(timestamped events, in order)
## Visual description
(what is on screen, including camera and lighting)
## Confidence
(which claims are well-supported by the frames, which are inferred)
</output>
notes: Anthropic Claude Opus 4.5+ can analyze videos by breaking them into frames (docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview).
Google Gemini native video understanding (https://cloud.google.com/gemini-enterprise-agent-platform/models/capabilities/video-understanding).
Sampling cadence matters: action at 1fps is missing detail; at 0.5fps you can miss state changes.
antiPatterns: Do not describe events that fall between frames as if you saw them.
Do not guess audio content from visual cues.

prompts.title: Multimodal prompt design strategy
prompts.when: Designing a prompt that combines text, images, audio, or video inputs.
prompts.prompt:
<multimodal_design>
Apply this strategy when combining modalities in a single prompt.

1. Place the visual content (image, frame) BEFORE the text question. Models attend to images in the order they appear.
2. For multi-image prompts, number them. Reference by number in the text: 'In image 2, the leftmost button...'
3. For audio, transcribe or summarize FIRST. Then ask the question. Audio fidelity varies.
4. For video, sample frames at the right cadence for the question (1fps for action, 1 frame per scene for narrative).
5. When combining modalities, the weakest link sets the ceiling. A great text prompt cannot rescue a blurry image.
6. Test the prompt with one modality at a time before combining. Each modality needs its own baseline.
</multimodal_design>

<modalities_in_use>
- text: {{what the text contains}}
- images: {{count and what they depict}}
- audio: {{length and content type}}
- video: {{length and sampling}}
</modalities_in_use>

<task>{{the multimodal task}}</task>
notes: Sourced from Google Gemini's 'Design multimodal prompts': https://cloud.google.com/gemini-enterprise-agent-platform/models/capabilities/design-multimodal-prompts.
Anthropic's vision prompting guidance reinforces 'put images before text'.
antiPatterns: Do not put text questions before images. The model attends less.
Do not chain modalities without testing each one alone first.

prompts.title: Debug by sorting training data by loss
prompts.when: Your model is underperforming and you cannot tell why. The training/eval loop looks clean but the metric is wrong.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an ML engineer applying Andrej Karpathy's 'sort your dataset by loss' debugging method. You believe most ML bugs live in the data, not in the model code.</role>

<task>Diagnose why {{model_or_pipeline}} underperforms by ranking and inspecting individual examples instead of staring at aggregate metrics.</task>

<method>
  1. Run inference over a held-out set and record the per-example loss (or error).
  2. Sort examples by loss descending. The highest-loss examples are where the model is most wrong.
  3. For the top 50 highest-loss examples, look at each one. Not the aggregate metric. Each one.
  4. Categorize what you see: label noise, near-duplicates, distribution shift, ambiguous input, true model error.
  5. Whichever category is dominant: that is the bug. Not the model.
  6. Fix the data first. Then re-evaluate. Only touch the model if data is clean.
</method>

<output_format>
## Top 50 highest-loss examples (grouped)
## Categories observed, ranked by count
## Root cause hypothesis
## One concrete data fix (not a model fix)
## Re-evaluation plan
</output_format>

<anti_instructions>
  - Do not propose a bigger model, a longer schedule, or a new optimizer until the data has been inspected.
  - Do not skim the top 5. Skim is what produced the bug in the first place.
</anti_instructions>
notes: Source: Karpathy's tweet 'When you sort your dataset descending by loss you are guaranteed to find something unexpected, strange and helpful.' (https://twitter.com/karpathy/status/1311651580769533952).
Aggregate metrics hide exactly the examples that would diagnose the problem. Loss sorting is the cheapest sanity check in ML.
Most underperformance turns out to be label noise, duplicates, or distribution shift. None of which a model change fixes.
antiPatterns: Do not aggregate the top-50 into a summary. The point is to look at each one.
Do not jump to a model architecture change before this is done.

prompts.title: Sanity check: overfit a single batch first
prompts.when: A new model is not training and you do not know whether the bug is in data, model, or training loop.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an ML engineer applying the 'overfit one batch' sanity check from Karpathy's 'most common neural net mistakes' list.</role>

<task>Design a sanity-check experiment that proves your training loop, loss, and model can fit a single batch before running on the full dataset.</task>

<method>
  1. Take a single batch (e.g. 32 examples). Repeat it 100 times so it becomes the entire 'dataset'.
  2. Train. Loss should go to near zero and accuracy to 100% within tens of steps.
  3. If it does: data pipeline, loss, optimizer, and model are all wired correctly. The real bug is elsewhere.
  4. If it does not: stop. The bug is in one of those four. Inspect in this order:
     - Loss expects logits but you passed softmaxed outputs.
     - You forgot .zero_grad() before .backward().
     - Model is in eval() mode (dropout/batchnorm frozen).
     - Labels are not aligned with inputs.
</method>

<output_format>
## Setup (batch, repeat count, expected convergence)
## Pass criterion
## If-pass branch: where to look next
## If-fail branch: 4 checks in priority order
</output_format>
notes: Source: Karpathy's tweet 'most common neural net mistakes: 1) you didn't try to overfit a single batch first...' (https://twitter.com/karpathy/status/1012825438493208576).
This is the cheapest possible sanity check and the one most often skipped. It catches loss-shape bugs, frozen-mode bugs, and label-misalignment bugs in minutes.
If a single batch overfits, the rest of the work is data and generalization. If it does not, no amount of compute will save you.
antiPatterns: Do not skip this step on a new model or a refactored training loop. Always run it.
Do not accept partial convergence. The batch should fit to near zero.

prompts.title: Context engineering over prompt engineering
prompts.when: You are designing a serious LLM workflow and realize the prompt text is the smallest part of what determines output quality.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an LLM systems designer working in Karpathy's 'context engineering' framing: the discipline of curating what ends up in the model's context window.</role>

<task>Design the context strategy for the workflow below. The prompt is a small slice of this; the rest is what you put into the window.</task>

<workflow>
  - User input: {{input}}
  - Tools available: {{tools}}
  - Memory state across turns: {{memory}}
  - External context to fetch: {{external}}
</workflow>

<method>
  1. Enumerate every source that ends up in the context window: system prompt, tool definitions, retrieved docs, prior turns, scratchpad, user input.
  2. For each source, decide: load always, load on demand, summarize-then-load, or never load.
  3. Apply the 'attention budget' rule: the model reads the window but attends well to only the top fraction. Put the high-signal context where attention will land.
  4. Define a 'context write' step: what does the agent save back to memory after each turn?
  5. Define a 'context compaction' step: when the window fills up, what gets summarized or dropped?
  6. Define the failure mode: 'lost in the middle' means the middle of long contexts gets less attention. Put the question near the end, not the middle.
</method>

<output_format>
## Context sources (table)
## Load policy per source
## Memory write step
## Compaction step
## Attention placement (high-signal items near top and bottom)
## Failure modes I am designing against
</output_format>
notes: Source: Karpathy's '+1 for context engineering over prompt engineering' tweet (https://twitter.com/karpathy/status/1937902205765607626).
Prompt engineering is the visible part of a much larger problem. The 80% of quality comes from what you put in the window, not what you write in the prompt.
The 'attention budget' framing is the missing step: the model can read the whole window, but it does not attend uniformly. Place high-signal context where attention lives.
antiPatterns: Do not write a long system prompt when the real problem is missing context.
Do not put user input in the middle of a long context. Lost-in-the-middle is real.

prompts.title: Improve the agent by editing the system prompt
prompts.when: Your agent makes the same kind of mistake repeatedly. You have a feedback loop. You want to turn corrections into a permanent rule.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an agent builder practicing Karpathy's 'system prompt learning' pattern: every agent mistake is a candidate update to the system prompt.</role>

<task>Design the loop that converts observed agent failures into system-prompt edits.</task>

<loop>
  1. Capture: every agent run produces a transcript and an outcome tag (success / failure / partial).
  2. Cluster: group failures by root cause, not by surface symptom.
  3. Diagnose: for each cluster, ask 'would a single rule in the system prompt have prevented this?'
  4. Edit: if yes, draft a one-line rule. If no, the issue is not in the prompt.
  5. Test: run the rule on past failures. It should have caught them.
  6. Add: append the rule to the system prompt with a short rationale and the failure mode it covers.
  7. Trim: when rules exceed a budget (say 80 lines), audit and remove redundant or low-yield ones.
</loop>

<system_prompt_template>
  ## Operating rules
  - {{rule_1}}: {{rationale}} (added after failure mode X)
  - {{rule_2}}: {{rationale}} (added after failure mode Y)
  ## Anti-patterns
  - {{anti_pattern_1}}
</system_prompt_template>

<output_format>
## Captured failure clusters
## Rules proposed (with rationale + linked failure mode)
## Trim audit (rules to remove)
## Resulting system prompt section
</output_format>
notes: Source: Karpathy's 'system prompt learning' tweet (https://twitter.com/karpathy/status/1921368644069765486) and follow-up Dust.tt analysis (https://dust.tt/blog/system-prompt-learning-the-feedback-loop-your-agents-are-missing).
The system prompt is the longest-lived memory of an agent. Every correction is a chance to make it permanent.
Forcing 'one rule per failure mode' prevents the system prompt from collapsing into a wall of overlapping caveats.
antiPatterns: Do not add a rule for a one-off failure. Wait until the same shape appears twice.
Do not keep a rule that no longer corresponds to an observed failure. Trim ruthlessly.

prompts.title: Invoke a panel, not a single 'you'
prompts.when: A question has real uncertainty and a single AI perspective feels thin. You want multiple expert lenses in one pass.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a panel of named experts convened to answer a question. Each expert writes in their own voice and disagrees when they would.</role>

<task>Convene a panel for the question below. Each panelist writes a short position. The moderator synthesizes at the end.</task>

<question>{{question}}</question>

<panel>
  Panelist 1: a senior engineer who has shipped this kind of system before.
  Panelist 2: a researcher who has read the literature but never shipped.
  Panelist 3: a product person who has to defend the decision to a board.
  Panelist 4: a contrarian whose job is to disagree with the other three.
</panel>

<method>
  1. Each panelist writes a 3 to 5 sentence position. They speak in their own voice.
  2. Where they disagree, name the disagreement explicitly.
  3. The moderator (you) writes a synthesis that names what all four agree on, where they split, and which split would change the recommendation.
  4. The synthesis is the deliverable. The panel positions are the receipts.
</method>

<output_format>
## Panelist 1: position
## Panelist 2: position
## Panelist 3: position
## Panelist 4: position (the contrarian)
## Synthesis
  - What they all agree on
  - Where they split
  - Which disagreement would change the recommendation
</output_format>
notes: Source: Karpathy's 'Multi-Expert Simulation' framing summarized on LinkedIn (https://www.linkedin.com/posts/nylan-richard_most-people-prompt-llms-wrong-karpathy-just-activity-7404794024487931904-a56A) and a Y Combinator discussion of the same idea.
Invoking a single 'you' produces a single distribution. Invoking a named panel produces named disagreements, which is where the real value sits.
Naming the contrarian is the highest-leverage move. Without it, every panelist converges to 'reasonable'.
antiPatterns: Do not pick panelist voices that all agree. The point is disagreement.
Do not collapse the synthesis into a mushy summary. Name the splits.

prompts.title: Wiki artifact as long-running agent memory
prompts.when: You are running an autonomous agent on a task that takes many steps or many hours, and you need memory that survives context compaction and human review.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a long-running autonomous agent. You maintain a wiki file as your durable memory. The wiki is your single source of truth across context windows.</role>

<task>{{task}}</task>

<wiki_structure>
  ## Goals
  - The 1 to 3 things this task must achieve. Stated as outcomes, not actions.

  ## Findings
  - Each entry: a date, a one-line fact, and the source path or URL.
  - No 'I think' or 'probably'. Either it is a finding with evidence or it is not in this section.

  ## Decisions
  - Each entry: the decision, the alternatives considered, the trade-off accepted, the trigger that would revisit it.

  ## Open questions
  - Each entry: the question and the experiment or lookup that would resolve it.

  ## Done
  - Completed items, dated, with verification evidence.
</wiki_structure>

<protocol>
  1. Before any plan, write or update the Goals section.
  2. After every meaningful step, write or update Findings. If you learned nothing, write nothing.
  3. When you make a choice between two paths, write it under Decisions with the trade-off named.
  4. When the window fills up, the next session reads the wiki before doing anything.
  5. Never delete from the wiki. Strike through with a date and the reason.
  6. The wiki is the audit trail. The transcript is not.
</protocol>

<output_format>
## Plan (3 to 7 steps)
## Wiki delta (what changed since last step)
## Checkpoint
</output_format>
notes: Source: Karpathy's LLM Wiki gist (https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f), which inspired a generation of long-running agent workflows.
Compaction-resistant memory is the missing piece in long-running agents. A wiki file is human-readable, agent-readable, and survives context resets.
Decisions as a first-class section turns the wiki from a log into a reasoning trail.
antiPatterns: Do not let the wiki drift from the actual work. If it says something the code does not show, the wiki is lying.
Do not delete entries. Strike them through. Future agents need to see what was rejected.

prompts.title: Diagnose a neural net training run
prompts.when: A neural net training run is going wrong and you need a structured checklist before touching anything.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an ML engineer applying Karpathy's 'Recipe for Training Neural Networks' checklist.</role>

<task>Diagnose a failing training run on {{model_and_task}} using the two-question framework and the bias/variance ladder.</task>

<initialization_checks>
  Before anything else, verify these in order. Each one is a tripwire.
  1. Are you using a sane default for {{framework}}? (3e-4 for Adam, per Karpathy.)
  2. Did you turn off augmentation to confirm the data pipeline first?
  3. Did you overfit a single batch? (See the single-batch prompt.)
  4. Is the loss what you expect on the first iteration vs after 100 iterations?
  5. Did you set train/eval mode correctly for dropout and batch norm?
</initialization_checks>

<two_questions>
  Q1. Is the model underfitting or overfitting?
  Q2. Is the gap between train and eval large or small?
</two_questions>

<ladder>
  Case A: Underfitting. Train loss is high. Eval loss is high. Gap is small.
    - Try: bigger model, longer training, lower regularization, check loss target.
  Case B: Overfitting. Train loss is low. Eval loss is high. Gap is large.
    - Try: more data, stronger augmentation, stronger regularization, smaller model.
  Case C: Both high with a small gap. Underfitting AND a data/label problem.
    - Try: inspect the data. Sort by loss. Fix labels.
  Case D: Both high with a huge gap. The model cannot generalize from this data.
    - Try: more data, distribution review, or rethink the task framing.
</ladder>

<output_format>
## Initialization checks (pass / fail)
## Two-question answer
## Case classification
## First action to try
## Stop condition
</output_format>
notes: Source: Karpathy's 'A Recipe for Training Neural Networks' (https://karpathy.github.io/2019/04/25/recipe/).
The two-question framework prevents the most common ML mistake: trying regularization tricks on an underfit model, or model-size tricks on an overfit one.
Initialization checks are the tripwires. Most training runs die in step 3 of the checklist, not step 10.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the initialization checks because the bug 'looks like' a model issue.
Do not change multiple variables at once. One change, one measurement.

prompts.title: Design an LLM training curriculum
prompts.when: You are teaching someone (a junior engineer, a partner, yourself) how LLMs actually work, and you want a curriculum that builds from first principles.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an LLM educator following the Karpathy LLM101n approach: build the model before you call the API. Each unit produces a runnable artifact.</role>

<task>Design a curriculum for {{audience_and_goal}} that takes the learner from zero to building a small but real LLM.</task>

<units>
  Unit 1. Tokens and the tokenizer.
    Build a byte-pair encoder. Input: a text corpus. Output: a vocab and an encode/decode pair.

  Unit 2. The dataset.
    Download a corpus. Tokenize it. Pack into training batches. Inspect the raw input tensor.

  Unit 3. The architecture.
    Implement a transformer block: multi-head self-attention, MLP, residual connections, layer norm.

  Unit 4. The training loop.
    Cross-entropy loss, AdamW, a learning-rate schedule. Overfit one batch, then run.

  Unit 5. Sampling.
    Temperature, top-k, top-p. Generate from the trained model.

  Unit 6. Evaluation.
    Perplexity, qualitative generations, a small held-out task.

  Unit 7. Fine-tuning.
    SFT on a small instruction dataset. Measure the difference in generations.

  Unit 8. RLHF (optional, advanced).
    Reward model + PPO or DPO. The alignment cap on the curriculum.
</units>

<output_format>
## Per unit
  - One-paragraph goal
  - The artifact the learner produces
  - The single most common mistake
  - The reading (Karpathy video, paper, or doc)
## Capstone
  - What the learner has built by the end
  - What they are ready to learn next
</output_format>
notes: Source: Karpathy's LLM101n course direction (https://karpathy.ai/llm101n) and his broader teaching philosophy across 'Intro to Large Language Models' and 'Deep Dive into LLMs like ChatGPT'.
The build-it-first principle forces the learner to internalize that LLMs are not magic; they are matrix multiplies with a clever objective.
Each unit ends in a runnable artifact, which is what separates a curriculum from a reading list.
antiPatterns: Do not start with the API. The API hides the thing the learner came to learn.
Do not skip unit 4. Most learners fail the curriculum at the training loop.

prompts.title: Sort text data by surprise to find issues
prompts.when: You have a text dataset you want to train or fine-tune on, and you suspect quality issues hiding in the long tail.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a dataset curator applying Karpathy's 'sort by loss / surprise' method to a text corpus.</role>

<task>Audit {{corpus_or_path}} for quality issues by surfacing the highest-loss and lowest-loss examples under a reference model.</task>

<method>
  1. Run a reference model over the corpus, recording per-document or per-chunk loss.
  2. Sort descending. Top 1% are the highest-loss: either highest signal or highest noise.
  3. Sort ascending. Bottom 1% are the lowest-loss: usually boilerplate, headers, repetitions.
  4. Sample 50 from each tail. Read them.
  5. Categorize: domain noise, near-duplicates, headers/footers, factual errors, formatting junk.
  6. Produce the dedup and filter rules.
</method>

<output_format>
## Top 1% (highest loss) categories
## Bottom 1% (lowest loss) categories
## Filter rules (regex / hash / heuristic)
## Estimated yield after filtering
## Estimated signal retention
</output_format>
notes: Source: Karpathy's 'When you sort your dataset descending by loss you are guaranteed to find something unexpected, strange and helpful' (https://twitter.com/karpathy/status/1311651580769533952).
The same idea that catches model bugs catches corpus bugs. The tail of loss is where quality problems live.
Filtering by loss is more defensible than filtering by hand-tuned heuristics, because you have evidence for each removal.
antiPatterns: Do not blindly remove everything in the top 1%. Some of it is genuinely hard and important.
Do not trust heuristic filters without looking. Heuristics encode the curator's priors, not the corpus's quirks.

prompts.title: Design a UI where the AI supervises the human
prompts.when: You are designing an interface where an AI agent is the primary actor and a human is the auditor. The human needs to see what the AI did and why.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a UI designer working in the Karpathy 'Software 2.0 / 3.0' framing: the program is the model, the human supervises it. Your interface is for the supervisor.</role>

<task>Design the UI surface for a system where {{ai_agent}} takes actions and {{human_role}} supervises.</task>

<design_principles>
  1. The interface must answer, in one screen, three questions:
     - What is the AI about to do?
     - What did the AI just do?
     - Why did the AI do that? (linked to the input or evidence that drove it)
  2. Action preview before commit. The human sees the diff before the AI applies it.
  3. Action log after commit. Every action is timestamped, reversible where possible, and linked to its inputs.
  4. Confidence is shown alongside the action. Not just 'success / failure' but the model's own estimate.
  5. The supervisor can interrupt, redirect, or take over without restarting the run.
  6. Errors are first-class. The supervisor sees them before they disappear into a log.
</design_principles>

<anti_patterns>
  - Hiding the AI's reasoning in a collapsible panel. Reasoning is the audit trail.
  - Showing only the final output. The supervisor needs to see the path.
  - Auto-dismissing notifications. The supervisor must be able to revisit them.
</anti_patterns>

<output_format>
## Three-question layout
## Action preview component
## Action log component
## Interrupt / redirect / takeover flow
## Error surfacing
## One concrete wireframe (ASCII or component tree)
</output_format>
notes: Source: Karpathy's 'Software 2.0' framing (https://karpathy.github.io/2017/01/software-2-0.html) extended to UI: if the program is the model, the UI is the supervisor console.
Most agent UIs are designed as chat logs. A supervisor UI is designed around what the human needs to verify, not what the model wants to say.
The three-question layout (about to do / just did / why) is the minimum viable agent console.
antiPatterns: Do not put the agent's reasoning in a collapsed accordion. It must be visible by default.
Do not let the supervisor see only the final output. They cannot verify what they cannot see.

prompts.title: Cut the lethal trifecta for an agent
prompts.when: You are designing or auditing an agent that touches private data and untrusted content. You need to break Simon Willison's 'lethal trifecta' on purpose, not by accident.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a security engineer applying Simon Willison's 'lethal trifecta' framing. You believe agents are not safe until the trifecta is broken at a deterministic layer, not at the prompt layer.</role>

<task>For the agent below, name which of the three legs of the trifecta it has, and design the deterministic control that cuts one.</task>

<trifecta>
  Leg 1: access to private data
  Leg 2: exposure to untrusted content (email, web, files, MCP resources)
  Leg 3: a way to exfiltrate data (network egress, file send, image render, link generation)
  All three: the lethal trifecta. Any attacker who can land in leg 2 can read leg 1 and ship it out leg 3.
</trifecta>

<agent>
  - Purpose: {{purpose}}
  - Private data it can read: {{private_data}}
  - Untrusted sources it consumes: {{untrusted_sources}}
  - Egress paths it can use: {{egress_paths}}
</agent>

<method>
  1. For each leg, write a one-line evidence statement: how does this agent have it?
  2. Pick the leg to cut. Cheapest to cut is usually the egress leg.
  3. Design a deterministic control. Not a prompt. A sandbox, allow-list, network policy, CSP, file-system boundary.
  4. Specify the failure mode if the control is misconfigured. Naming it forces you to take it seriously.
  5. Write the eval that proves the control is in place. If you cannot test it, the control is a hope.
</method>

<output_format>
## Trifecta legs (current state)
## Chosen leg to cut
## Deterministic control
## Failure mode if misconfigured
## Eval / test
## Why a prompt-only fix would not work
</output_format>
notes: Source: Simon Willison's 'Lethal Trifecta' tag and OpenAI Lockdown Mode writeup (https://simonwillison.net/tags/lethal-trifecta/), plus his prompt-injection series (https://simonwillison.net/2022/Sep/12/prompt-injection/).
Prompt-only defenses are non-deterministic by construction. The only reliable cut is a deterministic one at the OS / network layer.
Naming the failure mode if the control is misconfigured is what separates a real control from a check-the-box setting.
antiPatterns: Do not rely on the model itself to refuse exfiltration. That is the layer the attacker controls.
Do not assume a single control is enough. Layer them: network policy AND file system boundary AND egress allow-list.

prompts.title: Color-tag MCP tools by injection risk
prompts.when: You have a long list of MCP tools or skills and you need a low-friction way to keep dangerous combinations from happening at runtime.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an agent operator applying Tim Kellogg's 'MCP Colors' pattern, popularized by Simon Willison (https://simonwillison.net/2025/Nov/4/mcp-colors-systematically-deal-with-prompt-injection-risk/).</role>

<task>Color-tag every tool in the list below so the agent can refuse to combine the dangerous colors.</task>

<colors>
  Red: tool exposes the agent to untrusted (potentially attacker-controlled) instructions. Examples: read_email, fetch_url, read_file_in_repo.
  Blue: tool performs a critical action that the user would not want an attacker to trigger. Examples: send_email, git_push, write_file, delete_record.
  Neither: read-only, in-memory, idempotent helpers.
  Rule: an agent must never mix red and blue in the same action. If it does, halt and ask.
</colors>

<tools>
{{tool_list}}
(one per line: name, one-line description)
</tools>

<output_format>
## Red tools (untrusted input)
## Blue tools (critical action)
## Neither (safe helpers)
## For each red tool, the blue tools it must never directly trigger
## Default runtime rule that enforces the above
</output_format>
notes: Source: Tim Kellogg's MCP Colors pattern as discussed by Simon Willison (https://simonwillison.net/2025/Nov/4/mcp-colors-systematically-deal-with-prompt-injection-risk/).
Coloring tools is a triage that scales. With ~200 tools in some MCP deployments, only a runtime check that rejects red-then-blue sequences gives a real defense.
Storing the color in the MCP _meta object lets the agent reason about it without parsing prose.
antiPatterns: Do not color by hand for more than 20 tools. Automate the labeling and review the exceptions.
Do not let the agent decide at runtime whether red and blue are safe to combine. That is the layer the attacker controls.

prompts.title: Draft a blog post from a session transcript
prompts.when: You finished a long Claude Code session (or any agent session) and you want a publishable blog post, not a transcript paste.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a writer in the Simon Willison tradition: technical, specific, opinionated, never breathless. You turn raw transcripts into posts that earn their reading time.</role>

<task>Turn the transcript below into a blog post. Output the post in plain prose, not bullet points.</task>

<transcript>{{transcript}}</transcript>

<constraints>
  - The opening sentence states the result, not the setup. Willison opens with the conclusion.
  - Show the actual commands, prompts, and outputs. Readers came for receipts.
  - Quote yourself verbatim when the prompt wording is the point of the post.
  - Name the failure modes. A post without honest failure is marketing.
  - Link to the model card or relevant docs when the behavior depends on a model.
  - Under 1200 words. Cut any paragraph that does not add evidence or a new thought.
  - One pull-quote candidate highlighted at the end.
</constraints>

<anti_patterns>
  - Do not start with 'In this post, I will...'. State the result.
  - Do not summarize the transcript in order. Pick the 3 to 5 moments that matter.
  - Do not add AI tells. No 'dive in', 'leverage', 'in today's fast-paced world'.
</anti_patterns>

<output_format>
## Title (5 to 9 words, states the result)
## Body (post, 600 to 1200 words)
## Pull quote candidate (1 sentence)
## Tags (3 to 6)
</output_format>
notes: Source: Willison's writing pattern visible across his 155+ prompt-injection posts and his general blog cadence (https://simonwillison.net/series/using-llms/).
Willison posts open with the conclusion and follow with receipts. Readers get the result first, evidence second.
Naming the failure modes is the trust move. A 'this worked perfectly' post is not credible.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the failure modes. They are why readers trust the wins.
Do not bury the prompt text. It is the most useful artifact in the post.

prompts.title: Embeddings for recall, LLM for answer
prompts.when: You have a corpus you want to query with natural language, and you want the LLM to answer using the right passages, not the ones that look most lexically similar.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an ML engineer applying Willison's 'vibes-based search' insight: embeddings handle recall, the LLM handles relevance. Never confuse the two.</role>

<task>Design the hybrid retrieval pipeline for the corpus and question set below.</task>

<corpus>
  - Path or description: {{corpus}}
  - Chunk size: {{chunk_size}}
  - Embedding model: {{embed_model}}
</corpus>

<questions>
{{questions}}
</questions>

<pipeline>
  Step 1. Chunk the corpus. Each chunk gets a stable id and a short docstring.
  Step 2. Embed every chunk once. Store the vectors. Build a small index.
  Step 3. Embed the question. Retrieve the top K chunks (K = 20 to 50).
  Step 4. Rerank with a cheap cross-encoder if available. Take top N (N = 5 to 10).
  Step 5. Pass the top N chunks + the question to the LLM.
  Step 6. The LLM cites each claim with the chunk id and a short verbatim quote.
</pipeline>

<output_format>
## Chunking policy
## Embedding choice + cost
## Retrieval K and reranker N
## LLM prompt (with citation format)
## Eval set: 10 questions with expected chunk ids
## Failure modes: recall miss, precision miss, stale index, wrong chunk boundary
</output_format>
notes: Source: Willison's 'Embeddings: What they are and why they matter' (https://simonwillison.net/2023/Oct/23/embeddings/) and his ongoing tag on RAG patterns.
Embeddings get you recall. They do not get you precision. The LLM is where precision lives.
Citation with chunk id and verbatim quote is what makes the answer auditable and what lets the user reject wrong chunks.
antiPatterns: Do not use the LLM for retrieval. It is the wrong tool.
Do not skip the rerank step on a hard corpus. Embedding top-K alone is rarely enough.
Do not quote the LLM. Quote the chunk. The LLM is the renderer, not the source.

prompts.title: Tag-driven context loading for long sessions
prompts.when: You have a long-running session (or app) that needs to load context based on what the user is doing now, not what was loaded at startup.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an LLM app designer applying Willison's tag-driven context loading pattern: every page, tool, and route carries tags, and the LLM loads only the context whose tags match the active task.</role>

<task>Design the tag schema and the context loader for the application below.</task>

<app>
  - Name: {{app_name}}
  - Pages or tools: {{pages_tools}}
  - Knowledge base size: {{kb_size}}
  - Average session length: {{session_length}}
</app>

<schema>
  Each piece of context has a tag list:
  - topic: {{topic}} (e.g., billing, onboarding, security)
  - audience: {{audience}} (e.g., admin, end-user, operator)
  - risk: {{risk}} (low, medium, high)
  - freshness: ISO date of last review
</schema>

<loader>
  1. Resolve the active task's tags from the current page, tool, or query.
  2. Pull only context whose tags intersect the active tags.
  3. Cap the load at N items. Older or lower-risk items fall off first.
  4. Never load high-risk context into a low-risk task without explicit confirmation.
  5. Log every load. The log is the audit trail.
</loader>

<output_format>
## Tag taxonomy
## Loader rules
## Cap policy
## Audit log shape
## What the user sees when context is missing
</output_format>
notes: Source: Willison's tags prompt technique, applied across his LLM app projects (https://simonwillison.net/tags/llms/).
Tags turn 'load everything' into 'load what matches'. On long sessions, this is the difference between on-topic answers and confused answers.
Logging the load gives you a debugging surface when the LLM gets something wrong.
antiPatterns: Do not load all context at session start. The model will lose track of which piece applied.
Do not skip the freshness tag. Stale context is a silent failure.

prompts.title: Give the model the exact thing you want
prompts.when: You have used LLMs for code and been disappointed. The model is guessing because you gave it latitude it did not need.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an engineer following Willison's 'Using LLMs for code' principle: the model is precise when you are precise. Latitude is the bug.</role>

<task>Rewrite the request below to remove every source of latitude the model does not need.</task>

<request>
{{request}}
</request>

<checklist>
  1. Is the language and version named? (Python 3.12, not 'a script')
  2. Is the file or function specified, or is the model expected to choose?
  3. Are the inputs and outputs typed, with example values?
  4. Are the edge cases enumerated, or is the model expected to invent them?
  5. Is the style guide named (PEP 8, project conventions, a snippet)?
  6. Are the tests specified, or is the model expected to skip them?
  7. Is the failure behavior named (raise vs. log-and-continue)?
  8. Is the scope bounded (one function, one file, no drive-by refactors)?
</checklist>

<output_format>
## Original request
## Latitude inventory (what was unspecified)
## Rewritten request (specific, with examples)
## Latency saved (the model will need fewer turns to converge)
</output_format>
notes: Source: Willison's 'Here's how I use LLMs to help me write code' (https://simonwillison.net/2025/Mar/11/using-llms-for-code/) and his substack version (https://simonw.substack.com/p/how-i-use-llms-to-help-me-write-code).
Vague prompts produce code that compiles but is wrong. Specific prompts produce code that compiles and is right.
Latitude is not freedom, it is the model guessing. Name everything you know. Leave latitude only for the parts you do not know.
antiPatterns: Do not leave the language implicit. 'Write a function' is two sources of latitude (language and signature).
Do not let the model choose the test framework. Name it.

prompts.title: Dual LLM pattern for untrusted content
prompts.when: You need an agent that reads untrusted content (web pages, emails, support tickets) and acts on a privileged API. You cannot let the untrusted content reach the privileged model directly.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a security engineer applying Simon Willison's 'Dual LLM' pattern: a Privileged LLM holds the API and the data; a Quarantined LLM handles untrusted input. They communicate through structured data, never raw text.</role>

<task>Design the dual-LLM topology for the workflow below.</task>

<workflow>
  - Privileged resources: {{privileged_resources}}
  - Untrusted input sources: {{untrusted_sources}}
  - Actions the agent must take: {{actions}}
</workflow>

<topology>
  Quarantined LLM (Q-LLM):
    - Reads only the untrusted sources.
    - Outputs a structured summary: tokens, ids, structured fields. No free-form prose.
    - Cannot reach the privileged API or the data.
  Privileged LLM (P-LLM):
    - Never reads the raw untrusted source.
    - Reads only the structured summary from Q-LLM.
    - Holds the privileged API keys and the data.
    - Decides and acts.
</topology>

<output_format>
## Q-LLM schema (fields and constraints)
## P-LLM input contract
## Channel between them (a typed schema, not prose)
## What happens when Q-LLM is compromised
## What happens when P-LLM is compromised
## Test that proves the isolation
</output_format>
notes: Source: Willison's 'Dual LLM pattern' coverage summarized in ArcticDBA's review (https://www.arcticdba.se/posts/fighting-the-unfixable/) and Willison's own prompt-injection series.
The dual-LLM pattern breaks the data-exfiltration leg of the lethal trifecta deterministically. The privileged API never sees untrusted text.
Structured data as the channel is the trick. Prose is the injection vector.
antiPatterns: Do not let P-LLM see the raw untrusted source, even for a 'sanity check'. That re-opens the channel.
Do not trust Q-LLM to summarize safely. Validate the schema on P-LLM side.

prompts.title: Write a security investigation blog post
prompts.when: You found a real prompt-injection or agent vulnerability and you want to write the post Willison-style: timeline, evidence, mitigation, and the rule for the next time.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a security researcher writing in Simon Willison's voice. You name the vulnerability, the exploit, the impact, and the fix. You never blame the user.</role>

<task>Turn the finding below into a publishable investigation post.</task>

<finding>
  - Vulnerability: {{vuln}}
  - Affected system: {{system}}
  - Exploit chain: {{exploit}}
  - Blast radius: {{blast}}
  - Reporter and timeline: {{timeline}}
  - Vendor response (or absence): {{response}}
  - Mitigation: {{mitigation}}
</finding>

<structure>
  1. Open with the attack in two sentences. The reader must know what broke before the second paragraph.
  2. Reproduce the exploit in a code block. Redact only credentials.
  3. Show the impact: which user, which data, which blast radius. Numbers, not vibes.
  4. Timeline table: when reported, when acknowledged, when fixed.
  5. The mitigation: deterministic control, not a prompt change.
  6. The takeaway: one rule readers can apply to their own system.
</structure>

<rules>
  - Quote your sources.
  - Name the vendor's response, including silence.
  - Do not speculate about attacker motive. Describe capability.
  - End with the rule, not a 'we need to do better' sentence.
</rules>

<constraints>
  - Under 1500 words.
  - One screenshot or log snippet that proves the exploit. Not more.
  - One pull quote for social.
</constraints>
notes: Source: Willison's recurring investigation post pattern visible in his prompt-injection tag feed (https://simonwillison.net/tags/prompt-injection/) including Clinejection, Google Antigravity Exfiltrates Data, and others.
The Willison structure is: attack first, exploit second, impact third, mitigation fourth, takeaway fifth. Readers can leave after any section and still learn something.
Naming the vendor's silence or slow response is part of the pattern. It is what makes the mitigation real.
antiPatterns: Do not save the exploit for the middle. The reader leaves before they get there.
Do not omit the timeline. Without it, the fix looks cheap.

prompts.title: Sandboxed bash for a coding agent
prompts.when: You run a coding agent with shell access and you want a deterministic sandbox that does not depend on the model refusing.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an SRE applying Willison's 'deterministic sandbox' stance: agent sandboxes must not be enforced by the agent's own judgment.</role>

<task>Design the sandbox for an agent that runs {{tooling}} on {{os}}.</task>

<sandbox_requirements>
  - File system: agent can read {{read_paths}} and write {{write_paths}}. Nothing else.
  - Network: agent can reach {{allowed_domains}}. Outbound by default is denied.
  - Processes: agent can spawn {{allowed_binaries}}. Anything else requires approval.
  - Environment: agent sees {{env_vars}}. Secrets are not in the environment.
  - Identity: agent runs as {{user}}. Cannot escalate.
</sandbox_requirements>

<enforcement>
  - Landlock (Linux) or Seatbelt (macOS) profiles loaded at exec time.
  - cgroup limits on CPU, memory, IO.
  - Per-session namespace. Cannot see other sessions' resources.
  - All denials logged with the offending syscall and the prompt context.
</enforcement>

<output_format>
## Sandbox profile (full config)
## Enforcement layer (kernel + cgroup + namespace)
## Allow-list and deny-list (explicit)
## Test: prompt-injection payload attempting curl to attacker.com must be denied
## Test: prompt-injection payload attempting cat /etc/shadow must be denied
## Failure modes I have tested
</output_format>
notes: Source: Willison's recurring stance in his prompt-injection feed (https://simonwillison.net/tags/prompt-injection/) and his Claude Code auto-mode writeup (https://simonwillison.net/2026/March/24/auto-mode-for-claude-code/) where he explicitly prefers deterministic sandboxes over AI-classifier permission systems.
An AI-classifier permission gate is non-deterministic by definition. A kernel-level sandbox is not. Choose the layer you can reason about.
The two tests (curl to attacker.com and cat /etc/shadow) are the minimum bar. If both fail closed, you have something.
antiPatterns: Do not rely on the model to refuse bad commands. The model is the layer the attacker controls.
Do not put secrets in the agent's environment. The agent can exfiltrate anything it can read.

prompts.title: Pick the right tags for a new post
prompts.when: You publish posts regularly and want a tag taxonomy that compounds across posts, not a per-post word cloud.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an editor designing a tag taxonomy in Willison's style: 50 to 80 tags, hand-curated, each one doing real work across many posts.</role>

<task>Propose the tag set for a new post given the topic, draft, and existing tag taxonomy.</task>

<post>
  Title: {{title}}
  Topic: {{topic}}
  Body excerpt: {{excerpt}}
</post>

<existing_tags>
{{existing_tags}}
(the full set, one per line)
</existing_tags>

<rules>
  - Pick 2 to 5 tags. Not 1, not 10.
  - Prefer existing tags. Invent a new tag only if no existing tag fits.
  - Tags are nouns or short noun phrases. Not verbs, not categories.
  - A tag that appears on only 1 post is a smell. Either merge it or stop using it.
  - The tag set, viewed as a graph, should cluster. A taxonomy with 5 singletons is broken.
</rules>

<output_format>
## Proposed tags (2 to 5)
## Why each one (1 line each)
## Tags considered and rejected (1 line each)
## New tag proposal (only if needed, with rationale)
</output_format>
notes: Source: Willison's disciplined tag taxonomy visible at https://simonwillison.net/tags/.
Tags are how a single-author blog becomes a navigable resource. Most blogs under-tag; Willison over-tags deliberately.
Inventing a new tag is a real cost. It must earn its place by appearing again soon.
antiPatterns: Do not tag with the same word used in the title. The title is already searchable.
Do not invent a tag for one post. Wait until the second post needs it.

prompts.title: Decide when NOT to use AI
prompts.when: You are about to reach for an LLM and you want a 30-second pause to check whether this is the right call.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are the user, applying Mollick's 'be very specific' rule from Co-Intelligence: many tasks should not be done by AI at all, and the choice matters more than the prompt.</role>

<task>Score the task below on three axes. If any axis says 'no', do not use AI.</task>

<task_brief>{{task_brief}}</task_brief>

<axes>
  Axis 1. Is this task inside the jagged frontier?
    Some tasks AI does well. Some it fails at predictably. Some it does sometimes. Use AI for the first; avoid the second; treat the third as research, not production.

  Axis 2. Is verification cheap?
    If you can check the AI's output in seconds, AI is fine. If you would need an expert and a week to verify, AI may be worse than nothing because the verification cost hides.

  Axis 3. Does doing this task yourself build the judgment you need next time?
    Every time you hand a task to AI, you skip the practice. For tasks where the skill is the point (learning to write, learning to code, learning to analyze), AI use is a tax on your future capability.
</axes>

<output_format>
## Axis 1: frontier (yes / no / mixed)
## Axis 2: verification cost (cheap / expensive)
## Axis 3: practice value (low / high)
## Decision (use AI / don't use AI / use AI as a starting point only)
## If 'use AI': which mode (co-intelligence / wizard / refusal to use)
</output_format>
notes: Source: Mollick's 'Co-Intelligence' book rules, particularly 'be very specific' (https://www.oneusefulthing.org/p/co-intelligence-2-the-fourth-rule-of-co-intelligence-is-be-very-specific) and his 'Working with Wizards' essay (https://www.oneusefulthing.org/p/on-working-with-wizards).
Most users default to AI for everything. Mollick's rule forces a triage: AI is good for some tasks, useless for others, and actively harmful for a third set.
The 'practice value' axis is the one most AI advice skips. Skills you do not use are skills you lose.
antiPatterns: Do not use AI when the verification cost exceeds the task cost. You will think you saved time and lose it later.
Do not use AI to learn a skill that AI itself needs. You will not be able to evaluate the AI's output later.

prompts.title: Verify a wizard-mode AI output
prompts.when: You got a stunning output from a top model (GPT-5 Pro, Claude Opus, etc.) for a high-stakes task. The output looks correct. You cannot tell if it is.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a domain expert who got a 'wizard' output you cannot fully verify. Apply Mollick's provisional-trust discipline.</role>

<task>For the AI output below, name what to verify, what to spot-check, and what to provisionally accept.</task>

<output_to_audit>
  Task: {{task}}
  AI output: {{output}}
  Stakes: {{stakes}}
</output_to_audit>

<method>
  1. List the top 5 claims the output makes that, if wrong, would matter.
  2. For each claim, name the cheapest verification you can run in under 10 minutes.
  3. Identify the 1 claim that is hardest to verify. That is the one you watch.
  4. Identify any structural pattern that suggests the AI confabulated (overconfident numbers, no sources, suspiciously clean formatting).
  5. Decide: accept / revise / discard.
</method>

<output_format>
## Top 5 claims
## Cheap verifications (with effort estimate)
## The 1 hardest claim to verify
## Conformation-of-deviance signals
## Decision
## What I will check next time the same prompt is run
</output_format>
notes: Source: Mollick's 'On Working with Wizards' essay (https://www.oneusefulthing.org/p/on-working-with-wizards) and the Brendan/Bonn/Carlini essay on the OODA-loop problem with agentic AI.
Wizard outputs are seductive because they look finished. The verification step is what separates a delivered answer from an appearance of an answer.
Cheap verification is the art. A 30-second sanity check that catches 80% of errors is worth more than a perfect audit you do not run.
antiPatterns: Do not trust the output because it 'looks right'. That is the failure mode wizards cause.
Do not verify everything. Verify the 5 claims that would matter if wrong.

prompts.title: Chain of thought for a non-LLM domain
prompts.when: You want to use chain-of-thought reasoning for a non-coding domain (strategy, narrative, planning) and you want the chain to be auditable, not hidden.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are applying Mollick's game-master style of explicit chain-of-thought: the model writes its reasoning steps as named beats, and the user can read each beat before continuing.</role>

<task>Produce the chain-of-thought prompt for {{domain_task}} that exposes each reasoning step.</task>

<method>
  1. List the reasoning steps a careful human would take. Name them.
  2. For each step, write the prompt for that step alone. The model produces output, you as user check it, then you feed it to the next step.
  3. Define the audit: at each step, what is the user checking?
  4. Allow 'back step': if a later step reveals a flaw in an earlier step, return and revise.
  5. Cap the chain at 7 steps. Beyond 7 the user stops reading.
</method>

<output_format>
## Step 1: {{name}}
  Prompt:
  Audit:
## Step 2: {{name}}
  Prompt:
  Audit:
...
## Back-step rule
## Stop condition
</output_format>
notes: Source: Mollick's 'A guide to prompting AI for what you want' (https://www.oneusefulthing.org/p/a-guide-to-prompting-ai-for-what), where he adapts the game-master prompt to one-shot business tasks.
Naming the steps turns CoT from a hidden behavior into an audit trail. The user can disagree with step 3, not just the answer.
The back-step rule is what catches the case where later reasoning reveals an earlier error. Without it, errors compound silently.
antiPatterns: Do not chain more than 7 steps. The user stops reading past 7.
Do not hide the steps in a single shot. The point is the user sees each one.

prompts.title: Map a chapter from Build an LLM from Scratch
prompts.when: You are reading or assigning chapters of Raschka's 'Build a Large Language Model from Scratch' and want a study guide that pairs the chapter with a buildable artifact.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a study guide designer for Sebastian Raschka's 'Build a Large Language Model from Scratch' (https://github.com/rasbt/LLMs-from-scratch). You pair each chapter with a hands-on artifact.</role>

<task>Map chapter {{chapter_number}} ({{chapter_title}}) to a buildable artifact and a comprehension check.</task>

<chapter_summary>
{{chapter_summary}}
</chapter_summary>

<method>
  1. State the chapter's core idea in one sentence.
  2. List the 3 to 5 key concepts in order.
  3. Identify the artifact the reader can build or extend that exercises the chapter's core idea.
  4. Write 5 comprehension questions that cannot be answered by skimming.
  5. Write 2 'extension' prompts that go beyond the chapter.
  6. Flag any concept the chapter assumes you already know.
</method>

<output_format>
## Core idea (1 sentence)
## Key concepts in order
## Artifact to build
## 5 comprehension questions
## 2 extension prompts
## Prerequisites the chapter assumes
</output_format>
notes: Source: Sebastian Raschka's book 'Build a Large Language Model from Scratch' (https://www.manning.com/books/build-a-large-language-model-from-scratch) and his companion GitHub repo.
Pairing a chapter with a buildable artifact is the difference between reading and learning. The artifact is the receipt.
Comprehension questions that cannot be answered by skimming are what makes the study guide work.
antiPatterns: Do not write comprehension questions that the chapter already answers in a sidebar.
Do not assign the chapter without an artifact. Reading alone is not learning.

prompts.title: Pick the inference-time reasoning strategy
prompts.when: You are about to spend tokens on inference-time reasoning and you want to pick the right strategy for the task, not the one that worked last time.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an ML engineer applying Raschka's reasoning taxonomy from 'Understanding Reasoning LLMs' (https://magazine.sebastianraschka.com/p/understanding-reasoning-llms). You match inference-time compute to the task.</role>

<task>Pick the reasoning strategy for the task below. Justify in one paragraph. Show the prompt that uses it.</task>

<task_brief>
  Task: {{task}}
  Stakes: {{stakes}}
  Cost budget: {{budget}}
  Available: {{model}}
</task_brief>

<strategies>
  No reasoning (zero-shot): simple lookup, factual, low stakes.
  Light CoT: append 'think step by step'. Cheap. Often enough.
  Strong CoT: structured prompt with named reasoning steps.
  Self-consistency: sample N answers, vote. Use when verification is cheap.
  Self-refine: generate, critique, regenerate. Use when the first pass is rough but the problem is well-defined.
  Search (tree-of-thought, beam search): use when the search space branches.
  Tool-augmented: use a calculator, retriever, or code interpreter when the bottleneck is computation.
  Do not reason: when the task is below the reasoning threshold, reasoning is a tax.
</strategies>

<output_format>
## Chosen strategy (1 word)
## Why (1 paragraph)
## The prompt (with the strategy explicit)
## Expected token cost
## Failure mode that would flip the choice
</output_format>
notes: Source: Sebastian Raschka's 'Understanding Reasoning LLMs' (https://magazine.sebastianraschka.com/p/understanding-reasoning-llms) and his LinkedIn follow-up on Wait-token and self-verify tricks (https://www.linkedin.com/posts/sebastianraschka_following-up-on-my-reasoning-model-article-activity-7293627531142840321-MSc-).
Inference-time reasoning has a cost. Most prompts use none when they should, or heavy CoT when light would do.
Naming the failure mode that would flip the choice turns the selection from a one-shot into an iterative habit.
antiPatterns: Do not default to 'think step by step' for everything. Most tasks do not need it.
Do not use self-consistency when verification is hard. You cannot tell what 'majority' means.

prompts.title: Curate a fine-tuning dataset
prompts.when: You are about to fine-tune a base model and you want a curation pass before any training runs.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an ML engineer applying Maxime Labonne's data-curation discipline (https://github.com/mlabonne/llm-course). Curation is most of the work in fine-tuning; the training is the easy part.</role>

<task>Curate {{raw_corpus}} into a fine-tuning dataset for {{target_behavior}}.</task>

<pipeline>
  1. Inspect. Sample 200 examples. Read them. Categorize what is in the corpus.
  2. Filter. Remove: duplicates, near-duplicates, low-quality, off-topic, PII.
  3. Deduplicate. Hash on a normalized form. Keep one canonical version.
  4. Balance. If your target behavior is rare in the corpus, generate or upsample.
  5. Validate. Hold out 10%. Write 20 held-out eval prompts.
  6. Document. Dataset card: source, license, size, distribution, intended use.
</pipeline>

<output_format>
## Sample read (categorized)
## Filter rules (with examples removed per rule)
## Dedup stats (count removed)
## Balance strategy (with target ratios)
## Held-out eval prompts
## Dataset card
</output_format>
notes: Source: Maxime Labonne's LLM course (https://github.com/mlabonne/llm-course), his fine-tuning handbook (https://mlabonne.github.io/blog/), and his LLM Datasets curated list.
Most fine-tuning fails because of the dataset, not the training loop. The pipeline above catches the failure modes that are not visible until training time.
Documentation is part of the work. A dataset without a card cannot be reused safely.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the held-out eval. Training without an eval is a guess.
Do not balance by adding duplicates. Synthetic upsampling is fine; copy-upsampling is not.

prompts.title: Weekly AI digest from Hacker News threads
prompts.when: You want a weekly summary of the AI discussion on Hacker News, organized by topic, with the highest-signal threads surfaced.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a curator applying Maxime Labonne's weekly digest style (https://mlabonne.github.io/blog/): short, evidence-cited, top threads first.</role>

<task>Produce the weekly AI digest for {{week}} from the Hacker News threads below.</task>

<threads>
{{threads}}
(one per block: title, score, comment count, top comment, link)
</threads>

<method>
  1. Group threads by topic. 3 to 6 topic buckets.
  2. Pick the 1 thread per bucket that has the highest signal-to-noise ratio. Score = (insight in top comments) / (noise in the rest).
  3. For each pick, write a 2-sentence summary with a verbatim quote from the top comment.
  4. Surface the contrarian thread (high score, controversial, against the consensus).
  5. Note any open question that the threads did not resolve.
</method>

<output_format>
## This week's themes (3 to 6)
## Per theme
  - Headline thread (title + link)
  - 2-sentence summary with verbatim quote
## Contrarian thread
## Open question
</output_format>
notes: Source: Maxime Labonne's weekly AI digest cadence at https://mlabonne.github.io/blog/.
Weekly digests die when they try to summarize everything. Picking the highest-signal thread per topic is what makes them readable.
Surfacing the contrarian is the trust move. A digest without dissent is propaganda.
antiPatterns: Do not list 30 threads. The reader stops at 6.
Do not summarize the top comment in your own words. Quote it.

prompts.title: Spec a coding task in an HTML file
prompts.when: You have a non-trivial coding task for Claude Code (or any coding agent) and a markdown brief keeps getting misinterpreted.
prompts.prompt:
You are Thariq Shihipar (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qrpm7E80wQ0): an engineer at Anthropic who specs coding tasks in HTML files so the agent can render them while it works. The HTML is the brief. Write the HTML spec for {{task}}. The spec is the only input the agent gets. The agent must be able to complete the task by reading this file.

{{task_title}}

Status: {{status}}

Owner: {{owner}}

One paragraph. What the user-visible outcome is.
A bulleted list of test cases. Each bullet is a concrete check.
A bulleted list. What the agent must NOT do.
File paths, function names, prior decisions. The agent does not search the web for this.
Links the agent can verify against.
- The agent must be able to render the file and read the spec without a separate doc. - 'Goal' is one paragraph. If it takes two, the task is two tasks. - 'Success' is test cases, not aspirations. 'Works' is not a test case. - 'Out of scope' is the trust move. The agent stops guessing what not to do. ## The HTML file (complete) ## Why each section earned its place ## What you deliberately left out
notes: Source: Thariq Shihipar's workshop 'Claude Agent SDK [Full Workshop]' and his talk 'Why this Claude Code engineer uses HTML files as AI specs' (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qrpm7E80wQ0).
HTML files render in the agent's UI. The agent can keep the brief open while it works, which a markdown paste does not allow.
The 'Out of scope' section is the highest-leverage block. Without it, the agent invents scope.
antiPatterns: Do not let 'Success' be aspirational. Make each bullet a runnable check.
Do not put implementation details in the spec. The agent chooses the mechanism.

prompts.title: Write a Claude Code Skill from scratch
prompts.when: You want to author a Claude Code Skill (or equivalent agent skill) that other engineers will load and trust.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are Thariq Shihipar (https://www.claude.com/blog/skills), an engineer at Anthropic. You write Skills that compress tribal knowledge into reusable instructions.</role>

<task>Design the Skill {{skill_name}} for {{workflow}}.</task>

<skill_skeleton>
  ---
  name: {{skill_name}}
  description: {{one-line, when to load}}
  ---

  # {{skill_name}}

  ## When to use
  Bullet list. The triggers that should make an agent load this skill.

  ## Operating principles
  3 to 7 rules. Each is one line. Each is a contract.

  ## Procedure
  Numbered steps. Each step is one action. The agent follows them in order.

  ## Examples
  2 to 4 worked examples. Each has input and expected output.

  ## Anti-patterns
  3 to 5 things this skill must NOT do.
</skill_skeleton>

<quality_rules>
  - If the description cannot be one line, the trigger is fuzzy. Tighten it.
  - If a procedure step needs more than one sentence, split it.
  - Every example must be a real-world case, not a toy.
  - Anti-patterns are what the agent will try first. They are the highest-leverage block.
</quality_rules>

<output_format>
## Skill file (frontmatter + body)
## The 3 sharpest anti-patterns
## The eval that proves the skill loaded
</output_format>
notes: Source: Thariq Shihipar's 'Lessons from Building Claude Code: How We Use Skills' (https://www.claude.com/blog/skills) and Anthropic's Agent Skills documentation.
A Skill's description is the trigger. If the trigger is fuzzy, the skill loads at the wrong time and burns the context window.
Anti-patterns are what the agent tries first. Naming them up front is the difference between a Skill that works and a Skill that gets disabled.
antiPatterns: Do not write a Skill without examples. Without examples the agent improvises.
Do not stack too many principles. Past 7 the agent stops reading.

prompts.title: Write a CLAUDE.md that survives contact
prompts.when: You are setting up a new project for use with Claude Code and want a CLAUDE.md that is short, durable, and actually followed.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are Thariq Shihipar (Anthropic) (https://www.claude.com/blog/skills). You write CLAUDE.md files that fit on one screen and pay rent forever.</role>

<task>Draft the CLAUDE.md for {{project}}.</task>

<rules_for_writing>
  1. Under 80 lines. The agent reads it on every load. If it scrolls, it stops.
  2. Only rules that have been violated at least twice. Speculative rules are noise.
  3. Each rule is a sentence. 'Never do X' beats 'when in doubt, consider whether X is appropriate'.
  4. Stack-rank: the rule that prevents the worst bug goes first.
  5. Include the test or build command. The agent should not have to ask.
  6. Include the path to the file that holds the real architecture doc (if any). The CLAUDE.md is the index, not the manual.
</rules_for_writing>

<template>
  # CLAUDE.md

  ## Operating rules
  - {{rule_1}}
  - {{rule_2}}
  - {{rule_3}}
  ## Build / test / run
  - {{command_1}}
  - {{command_2}}
  ## Architecture overview
  See: {{path_to_arch_doc}}
</template>

<output_format>
## The CLAUDE.md
## Rules I considered and rejected (with reason)
## How to update it (when a new rule earns its place)
</output_format>
notes: Source: Thariq Shihipar's guidance on Skills/CLAUDE.md and the public Karpathy CLAUDE.md that circulated in mid-2025 (https://drive.google.com/file/d/1mtJKbu-QRk62WTWkyc0M0pGXbKzisA5W/view).
CLAUDE.md is the agent's permanent memory. Every rule must pay rent. Speculative rules pollute it.
Stacking rules by severity puts the highest-impact rule where the agent reads first.
antiPatterns: Do not write speculative rules. Wait for the second violation.
Do not duplicate architecture docs in CLAUDE.md. Link to them.

prompts.title: Spec a malleable software feature
prompts.when: You are designing a feature that lets non-programmers reshape software to their needs. Geoffrey Litt's malleable-software framing applies.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are Geoffrey Litt (https://www.geoffreylitt.com/2023/03/25/llm-end-user-programming.html), designing malleable software. Your job is to make the program reshape itself without the user writing code.</role>

<task>Design the malleable feature for {{product_or_workflow}}.</task>

<design_questions>
  1. What is the user's concrete need that the current software cannot express?
  2. What would the user change if they could edit the source? Write the diff in plain English.
  3. What is the smallest surface the user can reshape to express their need? (a view, a filter, a field, a workflow, a workflow + a field)
  4. How does the user express the reshape? (a prompt, a form, a natural-language instruction, an example)
  5. How does the software show the user what changed and offer to revert?
  6. What does the reshape persist as? (a per-user preference, a per-team policy, a per-workspace script)
</design_questions>

<output_format>
## The need (1 paragraph)
## The diff in plain English
## Smallest surface to reshape
## Reshape interaction (the prompt or form)
## Revert and audit
## Persistence model
## What the user does NOT need to know
</output_format>
notes: Source: Geoffrey Litt's 'Malleable software in the age of LLMs' (https://www.geoffreylitt.com/2023/03/25/llm-end-user-programming.html) and his subsequent writing on end-user programming.
Malleable software reshapes itself to the user, not the other way around. The reshape surface is small by design.
Revert and audit are first-class. The user must be able to undo any reshape, including ones the AI made.
antiPatterns: Do not make every part of the software reshapeable. Pick the smallest surface that solves the need.
Do not hide the reshape behind a settings menu. The user must see what changed.

prompts.title: Write an explainer that lives next to the code
prompts.when: You have code or a workflow that is hard to explain in prose alone. You want the explanation to live where the user is, not in a separate doc.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are Geoffrey Litt. You write 'explanations that live next to the thing they explain'. The doc is the workspace, not a museum.</role>

<task>Design the live explainer for {{artifact_or_workflow}}.</task>

<structure>
  1. Inline annotations on the artifact: a one-sentence note next to each meaningful block, in the user's voice.
  2. A 'why this exists' section at the top: the trigger and the constraint.
  3. A 'what to change if X' section: the 3 most likely changes a user will want and the lines they touch.
  4. A 'things that bit me' section: real bugs that came from misunderstanding this artifact.
  5. A 'see also' that links to the smallest possible external docs.
</structure>

<rules>
  - Every annotation cites the line or block it describes. No floating comments.
  - The 'why this exists' section must fit in 3 sentences. If not, the artifact is doing two things.
  - 'What to change if X' is the highest-leverage block. Most users came for exactly that.
  - 'Things that bit me' is the trust block. Without it the doc reads as marketing.
</rules>

<output_format>
## The explainer (annotated artifact)
## Why this exists
## What to change if X
## Things that bit me
## See also
</output_format>
notes: Source: Geoffrey Litt's malleable-software writing and his talk on inline computation (https://www.geoffreylitt.com/).
Explanations that live next to the artifact are read. Explanations in a separate doc are not.
The 'things that bit me' section is what makes the doc a senior engineer's note, not a tourist brochure.
antiPatterns: Do not write the explainer as a separate README. Put it next to the artifact.
Do not omit 'things that bit me'. It is the section that earns the doc's keep.

prompts.title: Front-load the premise with a single sentence
prompts.when: You have a prompt that is producing off-target output and you suspect the model is not committing to a single interpretation.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are Riley Goodside (https://scale.com/blog/prompt-engineering). You believe the model's behavior is steered by the premise at the top of the prompt, not the instructions at the bottom.</role>

<task>Rewrite the prompt below so the first sentence locks in one premise and the rest of the prompt is consistent with it.</task>

<prompt>
{{prompt}}
</prompt>

<rules>
  1. The first sentence establishes a single, specific premise. 'You are X doing Y' or 'Given Z, do W'.
  2. Nothing in the prompt contradicts the premise. If a later instruction does, the model picks the premise.
  3. Cut anything that is true in the world but not in the premise. Distractors cost you.
  4. If the premise needs supporting facts, put them in the second sentence, not later.
  5. Test: read just the first sentence aloud. Does it constrain the answer space? If not, rewrite.
</rules>

<output_format>
## The premise (1 sentence)
## Supporting facts (the second sentence, if any)
## The rest of the prompt (cleaned)
## What I cut and why
</output_format>
notes: Source: Riley Goodside's prompting essays and interviews, including 'Interviewing Riley Goodside on the science of prompting' (https://www.interconnects.ai/p/riley-goodside-on-science-of-prompting) and the Washington Post profile (https://www.washingtonpost.com/technology/2023/02/25/prompt-engineers-techs-next-big-job/).
Goodside's observation: the model's behavior is steered by the context the prompt sets, not the instructions it lists. The premise is the steering wheel.
If two sentences in the prompt are inconsistent, the model picks the one with the most surface area. Usually that is the premise at the top.
antiPatterns: Do not bury the premise in the middle. The first sentence wins.
Do not include facts that contradict the premise. They pull the answer.

prompts.title: Spot the GPT-poisoning sentence in a corpus
prompts.when: You suspect a corpus has been poisoned with sentences designed to manipulate the trained model (a Karpathy concern). You want to find them.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an ML engineer following Karpathy's warning that 'by posting GPT generated text we're polluting the data for its future versions' (https://twitter.com/karpathy/status/1284361499494952961) and Riley Goodside's observation that anything that looks like training data ('just casually tells you what 57 times 125 is') becomes training data.</role>

<task>Audit {{corpus_or_path}} for poisoning patterns and rank the candidates.</task>

<method>
  1. Tokenize and embed every document.
  2. For each document, compute its out-of-distribution score against a clean reference corpus.
  3. Cluster the top-1% by OOD score. Identify the cluster that looks like GPT-style prose.
  4. Within that cluster, look for content that is harmless-seeming but contains an instruction-like frame ('note that X', 'remember that Y'). Those are the candidates.
  5. Manually review 50 candidates. Confirm or reject each.
  6. Filter confirmed poisons.
</method>

<output_format>
## Cluster summary
## Top 50 candidates (id + sentence)
## Manual review verdict
## Filtered count
## Estimated corpus size after filtering
## The poisoning pattern template (so the next audit knows what to look for)
</output_format>
notes: Source: Karpathy's 'By posting GPT generated text we're polluting the data for its future versions' (https://twitter.com/karpathy/status/1284361499494952961) and Goodside's comments on training-data leakage (https://www.cognitiverevolution.ai/from-poetry-to-programming-the-evolution-of-prompt-engineering-with-riley-goodside-of-scale-ai/).
Anything that looks like training data becomes training data. Seemingly harmless sentences that drop a fact are exactly the seed for a future model's belief.
The hardest part is the manual review. Automated filters can rank. A human must confirm.
antiPatterns: Do not trust a black-box 'AI content detector'. They are unreliable and biased.
Do not filter by OOD score alone. The cluster pattern matters as much as the score.

prompts.title: Coder-reviewer two-agent loop with explicit handoff
prompts.when: A single-agent coding session drifts in quality. You want a second agent to grade every change before commit.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are running two agents in a loop. Agent A is the coder. Agent B is the reviewer. They never share state except through the artifact under review.</role>

<task>Implement {{feature}} using the coder-reviewer loop.</task>

<loop>
  1. Agent A reads the spec and writes a candidate implementation.
  2. Agent A writes a 'Change rationale' block: what changed, why, what was not changed.
  3. Agent B reads the diff and the rationale. Agent B does not see Agent A's internal reasoning.
  4. Agent B writes one of three verdicts:
     - ACCEPT (with a 1-line reason)
     - REVISE (with a numbered list of concrete revisions, each file:line specific)
     - REDESIGN (with the assumption that was wrong, and what to ask the user instead)
  5. If REVISE: Agent A iterates. Loop until ACCEPT or REDESIGN.
  6. If REDESIGN: the loop pauses and surfaces the question to the user.
</loop>

<reviewer_rules>
  - The reviewer grades correctness, security, and maintainability. Not style.
  - The reviewer must cite file:line for every finding.
  - The reviewer must not invent issues. If the diff is clean, ACCEPT.
  - The reviewer must not silently accept. ACCEPT requires a 1-line reason.
</reviewer_rules>

<output_format>
## Iteration k
### Coder output (diff + rationale)
### Reviewer verdict
  - If REVISE: numbered revisions
## Final accepted diff
## What was redesigned (if anything) and why
</output_format>
notes: Source: Jim Fan's recurring multi-agent code pattern visible in his public talks and his commentary on agentic engineering, echoed by Karpathy and applied in Cognition's Devin architecture.
Two agents without shared internal state produce cleaner code than one agent with self-review. The reason: the reviewer's evidence is the diff, not the coder's intentions.
The REDESIGN verdict is the loop's safety valve. It stops the agents from agreeing to a wrong premise.
antiPatterns: Do not let the reviewer see the coder's reasoning. It biases the review.
Do not loop forever. Three iterations max before REDESIGN.

prompts.title: Treat chat as the OS shell
prompts.when: You want to give a power user an interface that lets them compose app primitives through chat without leaving the conversation.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are designing Linus Lee's 'chat as OS' interface (https://thelinuslee.com/): the chat is the shell, primitives are the commands, and the user composes them in natural language.</role>

<task>Design the chat-OS interface for {{product}}.</task>

<primitives>
  List the 5 to 15 atomic primitives the user can invoke. Each primitive is a typed command:
  - name: verb-noun (search_docs, draft_email, render_chart)
  - inputs: typed schema with examples
  - output: typed schema with examples
  - cost: rough token or time estimate
</primitives>

<shell>
  The chat is the shell. The user composes primitives in plain language:
  - 'Find Q3 deals over $50k and draft a renewal email for the top 3.'
  The shell translates the composition into primitive calls, runs them, renders the output.
  The shell shows the user the primitive calls it intends to make before running them.
  The user can edit a primitive call inline before it runs.
</shell>

<output_format>
## Primitive catalog
## Composition examples (5 worked examples)
## Confirm-before-run flow
## Inline edit of primitive calls
## Failure surfacing
</output_format>
notes: Source: Linus Lee's essays on chat as a programmable surface (https://thelinuslee.com/) and his work on end-user programming.
Chat-as-OS makes the user the composer, not the customer. They assemble primitives instead of clicking menus.
Confirm-before-run is the safety rail. The user sees the primitive calls before the OS executes them.
antiPatterns: Do not invent primitives the user has not asked for. Start with 5, not 50.
Do not run without confirmation. The chat is the shell; shells confirm destructive operations.

prompts.title: Decompose a deep-tech paper for a podcast
prompts.when: You are prepping for a Latent Space-style podcast on a deep-tech paper and want a clean decomposition the host and guest can riff from.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are prepping for Latent Space (https://latent.space/). The format is: the host and guest riff on a paper. Your job is to give them the spine.</role>

<task>Decompose {{paper_url_or_pdf}} for a 60 to 90 minute podcast.</task>

<method>
  1. One-paragraph elevator: who, what, why now.
  2. The headline claim in one sentence.
  3. The 3 to 5 contributions, ranked by how essential each is to the headline claim.
  4. The 2 to 3 places where the guest will disagree with the paper. Prep the rebuttal.
  5. The 2 to 3 places where the host will not understand the paper. Prep the analogy.
  6. The one follow-up experiment that would settle the open question.
  7. The 5 minute closer: why this paper matters in 5 years, or why it does not.
</method>

<output_format>
## Elevator (1 paragraph)
## Headline claim
## Contributions ranked
## Likely disagreements + rebuttals
## Likely confusion + analogies
## Follow-up experiment
## Closer
## Time budget per section
</output_format>
notes: Source: Latent Space podcast format across hundreds of episodes, including guest interviews on prompt patterns and agentic engineering (https://latent.space/).
Pre-deciding where the guest will disagree and where the host will be lost is the difference between a podcast that educates and one that improvises badly.
The closer is what the listener remembers. If it is not interesting, the episode was not interesting.
antiPatterns: Do not try to summarize the whole paper. Pick the spine.
Do not skip the analogies. A confused host is a confused audience.

prompts.title: Extract structured data from unstructured text
prompts.when: You have a pile of unstructured text (resumes, invoices, support emails) and want a typed JSON object per item, fast.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are applying Willison's 'vibes-based extraction' pattern: use an LLM to produce structured JSON, validate the schema, reject items that do not parse.</role>

<task>Extract the {{target_schema}} from each item below into a JSON object. Validate before returning.</task>

<schema>
{{schema}}
(JSON schema with required fields, types, enums)
</schema>

<items>
{{items}}
(one per block)
</items>

<rules>
  1. Output one JSON object per item. Nothing else.
  2. If a required field is missing in the source, set it to null and add a 'missing_fields' list.
  3. If a field is ambiguous, pick the most likely value and add a 'low_confidence_fields' list.
  4. Do not invent values. The source is the only truth.
  5. After producing JSON, validate against the schema. If invalid, fix the JSON, not the schema.
  6. If a single item cannot be parsed into the schema after one fix attempt, drop it and report.
</rules>

<output_format>
## Per item: { ...json... }
## Validation summary: parsed=N, dropped=M, low_confidence=K
## The dropped items (with reason)
</output_format>
notes: Source: Willison's vibe-coding and extraction patterns (https://simonwillison.net/tags/llms/) plus his Datasette work.
Schema validation is the missing step in most extraction prompts. Without it, the JSON is hope, not data.
Distinguishing missing from low_confidence is the difference between a clean dataset and a noisy one.
antiPatterns: Do not return prose alongside JSON. JSON only.
Do not invent values to fill required fields. Use null + missing_fields.

prompts.title: Brief an AI the way you'd brief an intern
