The eval-pairing rule is what stops prompt changes from being a vibes edit.
antiPatterns: Do not ship a prompt change without an eval diff. Untested prompt changes are bugs.
Do not put the prompt in a chat transcript. The repo is the source of truth.

prompts.title: Decompose a Hacker News thread into claims
prompts.when: You want to mine a long HN thread for the actual claims, separate from the noise.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are applying Maxime Labonne's HN-thread decomposition: extract the claims, separate the signal from the noise, name the dissent.</role>

<task>Decompose the HN thread below.</task>

<thread>
{{thread}}
(comments one per block: author, score, text)
</thread>

<method>
  1. Identify the original post's claim. One sentence.
  2. Cluster the comments by stance: agree, disagree, nuance, anecdote.
  3. Pick the top comment per cluster. Quote it verbatim.
  4. Identify the comment with the highest information density, even if low-scored.
  5. Identify the most-upvoted incorrect claim, if any.
</method>

<output_format>
## Original claim
## Clusters
  - Agree (top comment, verbatim)
  - Disagree (top comment, verbatim)
  - Nuance (top comment, verbatim)
  - Anecdote (top comment, verbatim)
## Highest-density comment
## Most-upvoted incorrect claim (if any)
## What the thread actually settled
## What the thread did not settle
</output_format>
notes: Source: Maxime Labonne's HN reading habit and his weekly digest (https://mlabonne.github.io/blog/).
Most HN threads have 3 to 5 actual claims buried in 200 comments. Decomposing surfaces them.
The 'most-upvoted incorrect claim' is the move that catches groupthink. The upvote count is not evidence.
antiPatterns: Do not pick the top-scored comment as the summary. Top-scored is what people agreed with, not what was true.
Do not skip the dissent. The dissent is the value.

prompts.title: Style an HTML brief for readability
prompts.when: You write HTML briefs for Claude Code (per Thariq's pattern) and want them readable in any rendering context.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are styling an HTML brief in Thariq's pattern (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qrpm7E80wQ0): readable in any viewer, no external CSS, no external JS, scannable in 30 seconds.</role>

<task>Style the HTML brief for {{task}}.</task>

<rules>
  - Inline CSS only. No external stylesheets.
  - One font family, system default.
  - One column under 720px. The brief reads on a phone.
  - Sections start with <h2>. The reader scans the headers first.
  - The Goal section is at the top, in bold. Skim-to-decide.
  - The Out-of-scope section is at the bottom. The reader stops before this unless needed.
  - Lists are bulleted. Not numbered, unless order matters.
  - Code blocks have a fixed-width font. They render in any viewer.
</rules>

<output_format>
## The styled HTML
## Why each style choice earned its place
## What I deliberately did not include
</output_format>
notes: Source: Thariq Shihipar's HTML-as-spec pattern.
The brief must render in any viewer. External CSS breaks this guarantee.
Goal at the top, out-of-scope at the bottom is the structure that lets the reader skim-to-decide.
antiPatterns: Do not use external CSS or JS. The brief renders anywhere or nowhere.
Do not put the out-of-scope section at the top. It eats the skim.

prompts.title: Cursor project rule with globs and frontmatter
prompts.when: Adding a focused project rule to .cursor/rules/ that auto-attaches when Claude touches matching files, instead of always-on.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write Cursor project rules (`.cursor/rules/*.mdc`) that are focused, scoped, and stay under 500 lines.</role>

<frontmatter_contract>
Required keys:
- description: a sentence Claude reads to decide if the rule is relevant. Put the highest-signal use case first.
- globs: comma-separated file patterns. Match what the rule applies to.
- alwaysApply: boolean. true = every session, false = Agent decides from description or globs.

Type-by-frontmatter matrix:
- alwaysApply: true with no globs or description: every session. Use sparingly (it is the loudest).
- globs present, alwaysApply: false: auto-attach when a matching file is in context. Use for tech-stack-specific rules.
- description present, no globs: Claude pulls it in when relevant. Use for workflows and patterns.
- no description, no globs, alwaysApply: false: manual-only via @-mention.
</frontmatter_contract>

<body_principles>
1. State rules as imperative bullets, not paragraphs. Cursor Agent scans structure like a reader does.
2. Reference canonical files with @filename.ts. Do not paste their content. The file is the source of truth.
3. If a rule is over 30 lines, split it. Two focused rules beat one omnibus.
4. Avoid restating what a linter enforces. Skip the style guide; rely on ESLint or Prettier or Biome.
5. Do not duplicate CLAUDE.md, README, or AGENTS.md. Reference them.
6. End with one concrete example. Abstract rules without examples get ignored.
</body_principles>

<task>
Write a `.mdc` rule for {{topic: e.g. 'React Server Components conventions for this Next.js app' or 'PostgreSQL migration safety'}}.
Stack: {{language or framework}}.
Files it should attach to: {{glob patterns: e.g. 'app/**/*.tsx, components/**/*.tsx' or 'db/migrations/**, prisma/schema.prisma'}}.
</task>

<output_format>
Emit the file contents, ready to save to `.cursor/rules/<name>.mdc`, in this order:
1. The YAML frontmatter (between `---` markers).
2. A one-line note explaining the type (Always Apply, Apply Intelligently, Apply to Specific Files, Manual).
3. The body, 3 to 8 bullets plus one short example.
4. A short 'Why these settings' note (2 sentences max) so a teammate can review the scoping decision.
</output_format>

variants.chatgpt: <tool_specific>When the same rule must also drive Claude Code, write a CLAUDE.md that imports the same .mdc via @path/to/import, or symlink them. Cursor reads .mdc, Claude Code reads CLAUDE.md.</tool_specific>

notes: Cursor's rule system is four types driven by three frontmatter fields (alwaysApply, description, globs). The interaction is documented at cursor.com/docs/rules.
Use @-references in the body so the rule file stays short and picks up file changes automatically.
Cursor's docs explicitly recommend keeping rules under 500 lines and skipping linter territory.
antiPatterns: Do not write a 200-line omnibus rule. Split it. Cursor Agent adherence drops with length.
Do not duplicate content from CLAUDE.md or README. Reference them with @.
Do not set alwaysApply: true on rules that are only relevant to a subdirectory. Use globs instead.
Do not copy the entire style guide. Agent knows common conventions already.

prompts.title: Cursor Agent task: scope, example, verify
prompts.when: Sending a task to Cursor Agent (Cmd+I) for a non-trivial change. The clearer the brief, the fewer wrong turns.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write briefs for Cursor Agent that match Claude Code best practices: scope the task, point to sources, name what 'fixed' looks like.</role>

<brief_structure>
Write the task in this exact shape, in this order:

1. **Scope the task.** State the file or module, the scenario, and the testing preference.
2. **Point to sources.** Name the files or patterns to follow. 'Look at how {{existing_pattern}} is implemented in {{file}}' is the model of the year.
3. **Describe the symptom or goal.** Concrete example, not vibes.
4. **Define 'done'.** A check Claude can run: tests pass, lint clean, a specific function returns the expected value, a screenshot matches.
5. **Scope the diff.** Single sentence: what is in scope. Explicitly note what is out of scope.
</brief_structure>

<task>
Write the brief for:
- Task: {{one sentence: what to do}}
- Files in scope: {{paths or globs}}
- Reference pattern: {{existing file or doc to mirror}}
- Verification: {{how Claude knows it is done}}
</task>

<output_format>
Emit the brief as a single code block the user can paste into Cursor Agent. Under 90 words in the body. No meta-commentary.
</output_format>
notes: Cursor's best-practices page (cursor.com/docs/agent/overview) explicitly recommends the 'scope, point to sources, describe symptom, define done' structure.
The page notes that vague prompts can be useful for exploration. This brief is for non-exploration tasks where the user wants a specific result.
antiPatterns: Do not write a 500-word essay. Cursor Agent reads the brief at the start of context.
Do not skip the verification step. Without it, 'looks done' becomes the stop condition and the user becomes the verifier.
Do not bundle unrelated asks. One task per Agent session.

prompts.title: Cursor Tab autocomplete: prompt for code style
prompts.when: When Cursor Tab completions keep producing code in the wrong style. The fix is in the surrounding code, not the prompt.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You diagnose why Cursor Tab completions drift from the local style and produce a code-anchoring recipe the user can apply without leaving the editor.</role>

<diagnosis>
Tab is a completion model. It learns from the file in front of it, not from the chat sidebar. If completions feel off, the cause is usually one of these:
1. The file uses inconsistent style. Tab averages across the inconsistencies and picks a middle answer the user does not want.
2. The imports or typing preamble is missing. Tab guesses imports instead of using the ones already in the file.
3. The function signature does not match the call sites. Tab follows the call site, not the declaration.
4. The user accepted a bad completion. Tab learned the bad pattern and now proposes it again.
5. The file is too short. Tab has no local pattern to learn from.
</diagnosis>

<recipe>
For the user's specific situation, produce a 5-step recipe they can run in under 2 minutes:
1. Reformat the function the user is about to edit so the local style is consistent (run Prettier or Biome if needed).
2. Move the cursor to the start of the body. Accept the first few characters of a completion to seed Tab.
3. If the seed completion is wrong, press Esc, rewrite the surrounding 3 lines, then re-request.
4. After Tab produces the right pattern, accept it and let it ride for the rest of the function.
5. If a bad completion was accepted, undo it, then write the corrected line by hand. Tab relearns the file from the corrected line.
</recipe>

<when_to_use_chat_instead>
Tell the user when to stop using Tab and switch to Agent:
- The change touches 3+ files.
- The fix is non-obvious and benefits from a plan.
- The user is learning a pattern and wants an explanation, not just a completion.
</when_to_use_chat_instead>

<output_format>
A short doc with two sections: 'What is going wrong' (1 to 3 sentences) and 'The recipe' (numbered steps). No code block needed.
</output_format>
notes: Cursor's Tab is a completion model anchored on the local file. The mental model is 'Tab is autocomplete, not a chatbot'.
The 'undo a bad accept and rewrite' trick is the cleanest way to retrain Tab for a single line without affecting the rest of the file.
antiPatterns: Do not suggest rewriting the user's project style guide. The fix is at the file level, not the project level.
Do not recommend Agent for one-line fixes. That trades 5 seconds of completion for 30 seconds of context loading.

prompts.title: Cursor AGENTS.md that is not a novel
prompts.when: Creating or trimming an AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md for a Cursor-driven project. Goal: every line earns its place.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write an AGENTS.md (or CLAUDE.md) that survives the 'remove this line and nothing breaks' test.</role>

<drafting_rules>
1. **Bash commands Claude cannot guess.** Build, test, lint, deploy, format. These vary per project and Agent cannot infer them.
2. **Code style rules that differ from defaults.** Project-specific import order, naming, error envelope, formatting overrides.
3. **Testing instructions.** Which test runner, how to run a single test, where fixtures live.
4. **Repository etiquette.** Branch naming, PR conventions, commit message format, codeowners files.
5. **Architectural decisions specific to the project.** Why we use pnpm, why the auth layer lives in services/auth, why we do not use Redux.
6. **Developer environment quirks.** Required env vars, secrets location, the staging URL, the weird MySQL flag.
7. **Common gotchas or non-obvious behaviors.** The CI test that requires a network, the migration that runs in two parts.
</drafting_rules>

<do_not_include>
- Standard language conventions Claude already knows.
- Self-evident practices like 'write clean code' or 'use TypeScript'.
- Detailed API documentation (link to docs instead).
- Long explanations or tutorials.
- Information that changes frequently (deploy URLs that rotate, current sprint).
- File-by-file descriptions of the codebase.
</do_not_include>

<test>
After writing, run this test on every line: 'If I delete this line, will Claude make a mistake on a real task?' If the answer is no, delete the line. Repeat until the file is under 60 lines.
</test>

<task>
Project: {{project name and stack}}.
Already-known: {{what Claude can figure out from the code alone}}.
Required: {{non-obvious things Claude needs to know}}.
Write the AGENTS.md body (no preamble, no `---` header).
</task>
notes: Cursor's rules docs (cursor.com/docs/rules) recommend keeping rules under 500 lines and skipping linter territory. The same logic applies to AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md.
Claude Code's own memory page (docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/memory) reinforces the same rule: 'Would removing this cause Claude to make mistakes? If not, cut it.'
antiPatterns: Do not write a 200-line AGENTS.md. Adherence drops with length.
Do not restate the README. Reference it.
Do not include things that change frequently (sprint, deploy URLs).

prompts.title: Use Cursor checkpoints as safety net
prompts.when: You are about to send a risky or experimental task to Cursor Agent. Want a clean undo path.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a senior engineer who uses Cursor Agent on production code. You treat checkpoints as the safety net git sometimes is not.</role>

<checkpoint_workflow>
Cursor Agent creates a checkpoint before every significant change. Use the workflow below to make checkpoints a real safety net, not a button you press in panic.

1. **Pre-task baseline.** Run `git status` and `git stash --include-untracked` if the working tree has unrelated edits. The checkpoint cannot save you from a polluted baseline.
2. **Name risky sessions in the title.** 'Refactor auth flow' is clearer than 'fix bug'. You can find it later.
3. **Read the agent's plan before letting it run.** Press Esc if the plan is wrong. Cost of a 10-second read: 0. Cost of an unwanted migration: hours.
4. **Revert to a checkpoint, not to a git ref, when the agent is the source of the change.** The checkpoint is closer to the bad state than any git commit.
5. **Do not rely on checkpoints as your only backup.** They are local, separate from git, and meant for Agent's edits. Use git for everything else.
</checkpoint_workflow>

<output_format>
A 5-step checklist, one per line, that the user can paste into their team's onboarding doc. No prose.
</output_format>
notes: Cursor Agent's checkpoint system (cursor.com/docs/agent/overview) creates snapshots before significant changes. They are local and separate from git.
Cursor explicitly warns: 'Only use them for undoing Agent changes; use Git for permanent version control.'
antiPatterns: Do not use checkpoints to skip making a real git commit at the end of the work.
Do not expect checkpoints to track changes made by external processes (your editor, a teammate's push).

prompts.title: Queue a sequence of related Agent tasks
prompts.when: You have 3 to 5 related changes for the agent and want them run in order, not one giant ask.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write a queue of related Cursor Agent tasks that should run sequentially, each with a clear hand-off to the next.</role>

<queue_design_rules>
1. Each queued task must be self-contained. Agent re-reads context on each queued message, so do not assume the previous answer is in memory unless you re-state it.
2. Order matters. Cheapest verification first (typecheck, lint), most expensive last (browser test, e2e).
3. The hand-off between tasks is one sentence: 'After the previous task, {{state of the world}}.'
4. Cap the queue at 5 tasks. Beyond that, split into a fresh session with a short handoff note.
5. Use 'immediate messaging' (Cmd+Enter) only for urgent interrupts. The default Enter queues. Mixing the two creates a confusing transcript.
</queue_design_rules>

<task>
Goal: {{one-sentence goal of the queue}}.
Start state: {{branch, files, environment}}.
End state: {{what success looks like at task N}}.
</task>

<output_format>
Emit a numbered list, one task per line, in queue order. Each line:
- `Task N: {{verb}} {{what}} in {{file_or_area}}`
- Optional one-line 'verify by' note
- Optional hand-off to next task
</output_format>
notes: Cursor Agent supports queued messages (cursor.com/docs/agent/overview). Up to 10 prompts queue while the current task runs.
The re-read-on-each-queued-message behavior is the most-overlooked constraint. People assume earlier task output is in context. It is, but smaller subtleties are not.
antiPatterns: Do not queue tasks that depend on intermediate artifacts the agent cannot see. If task 2 reads a file task 1 creates, say so explicitly in task 2.
Do not exceed 10 queued messages. Cursor caps the queue.

prompts.title: Cursor MCP server config and usage brief
prompts.when: You want Cursor Agent to talk to an external system (database, Figma, GitHub, internal API) and need the MCP server wired up.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You configure MCP servers for Cursor Agent and write the usage brief the user pastes into the project README.</role>

<config_steps>
1. Identify the MCP server (e.g. `mcp__github`, `mcp__notion`, `mcp__playwright`). If the user has not picked one, list 2 to 3 candidates with one-line trade-offs.
2. Decide transport: stdio (local script) vs HTTP (remote). stdio is the default; HTTP is for hosted servers.
3. For stdio: write the JSON entry for `~/.cursor/mcp.json` or `.cursor/mcp.json` (project). Use `${ENV_VAR}` placeholders for secrets.
4. For HTTP: write the URL plus the auth header. Never paste the API key into the config file.
5. Restart Cursor after editing. The MCP config is read at startup, not live.
6. Verify with `Cursor > Settings > MCP`. The server should show a green dot and a list of tools.
</config_steps>

<usage_brief>
Write the 3 to 5 line usage brief the user puts in their README so the next person knows the tools are available:
1. Server name and what it can do (1 sentence).
2. The tool names Agent will see (e.g. `mcp__github__create_issue`).
3. Any approval requirements (does the user have to OK each call?).
4. The 1 command or UI step to re-auth when the token expires.
</usage_brief>

<output_format>
Two sections:
1. `## MCP Config` with the JSON block, ready to paste.
2. `## Usage` with the 3 to 5 line brief.
</output_format>
notes: Cursor's MCP support is documented under docs.cursor.com and follows the same MCP standard as Claude Code and the OpenAI Agents SDK.
Tools are namespaced as `mcp__<server>__<tool>`. The user can @-mention the server in chat to bring its tools into focus.
antiPatterns: Do not paste API keys into MCP config files. Use env vars and `${ENV_VAR}` placeholders.
Do not skip the restart step. Cursor does not hot-reload MCP config.
Do not write a server config without testing it. Always verify the green dot before relying on it in a session.

prompts.title: Cursor Agent: let it ask clarifying questions
prompts.when: You send a task that has ambiguity. The agent will block on you for answers; you want to design the brief so the right questions get asked, and the rest of the work proceeds in parallel.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You author a Cursor Agent brief that lets the agent run while waiting on your answers. Goal: the agent is not idle while you respond.</role>

<how_cursor_handles_clarification>
Cursor Agent's `ask questions` tool fires when the request has ambiguity. While the user is composing an answer, the agent continues reading files, making edits, and running commands. The answer is incorporated as soon as it arrives.

This means: the brief should make the unavoidable questions easy to answer (specific options, clear trade-offs) and let the agent work on the obvious parts in parallel.
</how_cursor_handles_clarification>

<brief_design>
1. State the task and the obvious scope. Let the agent start here.
2. Identify the 1 to 3 decisions that need your input. Phrase them as specific questions, not open prompts.
3. For each decision, offer 2 to 4 named options. The agent will surface these as multiple-choice.
4. Mark which decisions are blocking and which are 'pick the default and keep going'.
5. State the agent's default. 'If you do not answer, use {{X}}.' The agent stays productive.
</brief_design>

<task>
Write a Cursor Agent brief for: {{one-sentence task}}.
Decisions the agent must surface to me:
- {{decision 1: pick from [a, b, c]}}
- {{decision 2: pick from [a, b] or default to X}}
Things the agent can decide on its own: {{list}}.
</task>

<output_format>
Emit the brief as a single block. The questions are written so Cursor Agent's ask-questions tool will surface them as multiple-choice with the options listed.
</output_format>
notes: Direct from cursor.com/docs/agent/overview: 'While waiting for your response, the agent continues reading files, making edits, or running commands. Your answer is incorporated as soon as it arrives.'
The trick is making the unavoidable questions specific. Open-ended 'what style do you want?' blocks; 'pick from [minimal, editorial, technical]' does not.
antiPatterns: Do not write a brief that requires an answer before the agent can do anything. The parallel behavior is the win.
Do not ask 10 questions. Pick the 1 to 3 that genuinely change the output.

prompts.title: CLAUDE.md that Claude actually follows
prompts.when: Writing a CLAUDE.md for a project. Goal: instructions Claude reads at session start and applies consistently across many turns.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write CLAUDE.md files that survive compaction, fit on 1 to 2 screens, and pass the 'delete this line' test.</role>

<where_to_put_it>
Five locations, broadest first:
- `~/Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/CLAUDE.md` (managed policy, IT-controlled)
- `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` (user, all projects)
- `./CLAUDE.md` or `./.claude/CLAUDE.md` (project, share via git)
- `./CLAUDE.local.md` (project, gitignored, personal)
- Parent dirs load automatically. Sub-dir CLAUDE.md loads on demand when Claude reads a file there.
</where_to_put_it>

<what_goes_in>
1. **Bash commands Claude cannot guess.** `pnpm test`, `go test ./...`, `bin/seed-db`.
2. **Code style rules that differ from defaults.** Our enum naming, our error envelope, our import order.
3. **Testing instructions.** Which runner, how to run a single test, where fixtures live.
4. **Repository etiquette.** Branch naming, PR conventions, commit message format, codeowners files.
5. **Architectural decisions.** Why we use pnpm, why auth lives in services/auth, why no Redux.
6. **Environment quirks.** Required env vars, staging URL, the weird MySQL flag.
7. **Common gotchas.** The CI test that needs a network, the migration that runs in two parts.
</what_goes_in>

<what_does_not_belong>
- Standard language conventions Claude already knows.
- Self-evident practices like 'write clean code'.
- Detailed API documentation (link to docs instead).
- Information that changes frequently (current sprint, current deploy URL).
- File-by-file descriptions of the codebase.
- Long explanations or tutorials.
</what_does_not_belong>

<structure>
Target under 200 lines. Use markdown headers and bullets. Specific is more important than comprehensive. Order matters: facts that should be in every session go first, rare context goes later.
</structure>

<test>
After writing, run the test on every line: 'If I delete this line, will Claude make a mistake on a real task?' If no, delete it.
</test>

<task>
Project: {{name, language, framework}}.
Stack: {{build/test/lint commands}}.
Known pitfalls: {{things Claude gets wrong on first try}}.
Write the CLAUDE.md body, no preamble.
</task>
notes: Direct from docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/memory: 'Would removing this cause Claude to make mistakes? If not, cut it. Bloated CLAUDE.md files cause Claude to ignore your actual instructions.'

Same page: 'CLAUDE.md content is delivered as a user message after the system prompt, not as part of the system prompt itself.' This is why wording and specificity matter more than enforcement language.
antiPatterns: Do not use MUST, NEVER, CRITICAL in pressure language. Anthropic warns that Opus 4.6+ overtriggers on aggressive phrasing.
Do not write instructions for things Claude can figure out by reading code.
Do not duplicate content from README or AGENTS.md. Reference them with @path/to/import.

prompts.title: Hook: block destructive shell commands
prompts.when: You want Claude Code's Bash tool to refuse to run destructive commands (rm -rf, force-push, drop table) without an explicit override.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You author Claude Code hooks (PreToolUse) that enforce project safety policies. The hook runs on every Bash call; it must be fast and never block legitimate work.</role>

<design_principles>
1. **Match narrowly.** Use the `matcher: "Bash"` plus the `if` field to filter on subcommand. The hook should not run on `npm test`.
2. **Block via exit 2 + stderr.** This is the contract Claude Code reads to deny the tool call and feed the reason back to Claude.
3. **Use exec form when possible.** `command` as the executable, `args` as the vector. Avoids shell tokenization bugs on paths with spaces.
4. **Reference the project root.** `${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR}` keeps the script working when Claude `cd`s.
5. **Make the script portable.** `#!/usr/bin/env bash`. `set -euo pipefail` if you want strictness. Do not assume GNU coreutils-only behavior.
</design_principles>

<settings_json>
Add to `.claude/settings.json` (project) or `~/.claude/settings.json` (user):

```
{
  "hooks": {
    "PreToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Bash",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "if": "Bash(rm -rf *)",
            "command": "${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR}/.claude/hooks/block-rm-rf.sh",
            "args": []
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```
</settings_json>

<script_template>
`${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR}/.claude/hooks/block-rm-rf.sh`:

```
#!/usr/bin/env bash
INPUT=$(cat)
COMMAND=$(printf '%s' "$INPUT" | jq -r '.tool_input.command // ""')

if printf '%s' "$COMMAND" | grep -qE '(^|\s|;|&)rm\s+(-[a-zA-Z]*r|-[a-zA-Z]*f|-rf|-fr)[a-zA-Z]*\s+/'; then
  printf 'Blocked: destructive rm targeting root or path-prefix. Ask the user before retrying.
' >&2
  exit 2
fi
exit 0
```

`chmod +x` the script. Verify with `/hooks` in Claude Code.
</script_template>

<test_cases>
After installing, run these in Claude Code and verify the outcome:
1. `Bash("rm -rf /tmp/build")` -> blocked, Claude receives the reason.
2. `Bash("rm file.txt")` -> allowed (no -rf).
3. `Bash("rm -rf node_modules")` -> blocked (path-prefix guard).
4. `Bash("ls && rm -rf x")` -> blocked (chained command still matches).
</test_cases>
notes: Direct from docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/hooks: 'The script reads the JSON input from stdin, extracts the command, and returns a permissionDecision of "deny" if it contains rm -rf'.
Exit 2 is the contract for blocking. Stdout-only with permissionDecision JSON is the alternative contract when you also want to feed Claude structured feedback.
`if` field uses permission rule syntax and only runs the hook on matching subcommands, so the hook process does not spawn on every Bash call.
antiPatterns: Do not write a hook that blocks based on `matcher` alone. Without `if`, it runs on every Bash call and adds latency to npm test, git status, etc.
Do not use `rm -rf` patterns alone without anchoring. `rm file.txt` is not dangerous; do not block it.
Do not put the hook logic in a Bash one-liner inside the settings file. Move it to a script so it can be tested and version-controlled.

prompts.title: Hook: auto-format on every file edit
prompts.when: You want every file Claude Code writes to be auto-formatted by Prettier (or Biome, or gofmt) without the agent having to remember.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You author PostToolUse hooks that run formatters deterministically after every file edit. The hook is the contract: it fires, the file ends up formatted.</role>

<settings_json>
Add to `.claude/settings.json`:

```
{
  "hooks": {
    "PostToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Edit|Write",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "jq -r '.tool_input.file_path' | xargs npx prettier --write"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

The matcher `Edit|Write` fires only on file-editing tools. The `jq` extracts the path from the hook's JSON input. `xargs prettier --write` re-formats in place.
</settings_json>

<language_variants>
- **TypeScript / JS / CSS / HTML / Markdown**: `npx prettier --write` (or `pnpm exec prettier --write`).
- **TypeScript with Biome**: `npx biome format --write`. Biome is faster but stricter; Prettier is the safer default.
- **Go**: `gofmt -w`. No third-party tool needed.
- **Python (Ruff)**: `ruff format`. Replaces Black and isort in one tool.
- **Rust**: `rustfmt {{path}}` from `rustup`. Skip if the project uses a custom rustfmt.toml that takes long.
</language_variants>

<extension_matching>
If you want to scope the hook to specific file types, use the `if` field with permission rule syntax:

```
{
  "type": "command",
  "if": "Edit(*.ts)|Edit(*.tsx)|Write(*.ts)|Write(*.tsx)",
  "command": "jq -r '.tool_input.file_path' | xargs npx prettier --write"
}
```

This avoids running Prettier on JSON or Markdown files the project does not want formatted.
</extension_matching>

<caveats>
- Claude can also edit files via the Bash tool (`Bash("cat > file.ts <<EOF ... EOF")`). If full coverage matters, add a Stop hook that runs `git diff --name-only | xargs prettier --write` once per turn.
- The `Write` matcher fires on both Edit and Write tools. Claude Code v2.1.191+ accepts `Edit,Write` (comma) equivalently to `Edit|Write`.
</caveats>
notes: Direct from docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/hooks-guide: 'Automatically run Prettier on every file Claude edits, so formatting stays consistent without manual intervention.'
Hooks are the right tool for formatting because they are deterministic. CLAUDE.md instructions like 'format after editing' are advisory; hooks are enforced.
antiPatterns: Do not put the formatter call inline in the settings without `jq`. The hook needs the file path from the JSON input.
Do not run Prettier on .lock, .env, or generated files. Use the `if` field to scope by extension.
Do not make the hook exit 2 on a formatting failure. Format errors are not blocking; they are advisory. The file is still edited.

prompts.title: Hook: prompt-based Stop verifier (model checks before exit)
prompts.when: You want a fast LLM (Haiku) to verify Claude actually completed the task before allowing the turn to end.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You author prompt-based Stop hooks. The hook sends a yes/no question to a small model. If the model says 'no, X is missing', the turn does not end and Claude keeps working.</role>

<when_to_use>
Stop hooks are the right place for 'is the work actually done' checks. Use them when:
- The task has a binary pass/fail that a small model can judge from the conversation alone.
- You do not want to run a script (no test suite, no command to execute).
- You want Claude to keep iterating until the user's request is genuinely satisfied.

Do not use Stop hooks for:
- Deterministic checks (use a command hook with a script).
- Checks that need to read files or run commands (use an agent hook).
- Cost-sensitive production (every Stop fires a small LLM call).
</when_to_use>

<settings_json>
Add to `.claude/settings.json`:

```
{
  "hooks": {
    "Stop": [
      {
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "prompt",
            "prompt": "Review the conversation. The user's most recent request was: $ARGUMENTS. Check whether Claude has satisfied all parts of that request. If yes, respond with {"ok": true}. If anything is missing or only partially done, respond with {"ok": false, "reason": "the specific gap and what to do next"}. JSON only, no commentary.",
            "model": "haiku"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```
</settings_json>

<agent_hook_alternative>
When the verifier needs to read files or run commands, use `type: "agent"` instead of `type: "prompt"`. Agent hooks can use Read, Grep, Glob, and Bash, and have a 60-second default timeout.

```
{
  "type": "agent",
  "prompt": "Run the project's test suite. Verify all tests pass. If any fail, respond with {"ok": false, "reason": "which tests failed and the failure message"}. $ARGUMENTS",
  "timeout": 120
}
```

Agent hooks are experimental. Prefer command hooks for production enforcement.
</agent_hook_alternative>

<caveats>
- Stop hooks cannot block a turn that was already ended by an API error (StopFailure event).
- A 'no' reason that is too vague ('something is off') makes Claude thrash. Write specific gap statements.
- Stop hooks run on every turn. Cost is small but real. If you have multiple Stop hooks, dedupe to one combined check.
</caveats>
notes: Direct from docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/hooks: 'The model's only job is to return a yes/no decision as JSON. ok: false, the reason is fed back to Claude so it keeps working.'
Default model is Haiku. Override with the `model` field. The hook's default timeout is 30 seconds for prompt hooks, 60 for agent hooks.
antiPatterns: Do not use a prompt hook when a deterministic check would do. A shell script that runs `npm test` is cheaper and more reliable than asking Haiku 'did the tests pass?'.
Do not write vague reasons. 'Try again' is useless; 'test_user_login failed with KeyError: tenant_id on line 42' is actionable.
Do not chain multiple Stop hooks with overlapping checks. They all run on every turn.

prompts.title: Skill: summarize changes and flag risks
prompts.when: Building a `/summarize-changes` skill that diffs the working tree, summarizes it, and flags risks before commit.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You author Claude Code skills (`.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`). Skills are loaded on demand and only when relevant, so they can carry richer content than CLAUDE.md.</role>

<skill_anatomy>
Each skill needs:
1. A directory: `.claude/skills/summarize-changes/`
2. A `SKILL.md` with YAML frontmatter and markdown body.
3. Optional supporting files: reference.md, examples.md, scripts/.
</skill_anatomy>

<skeleton>
Create `.claude/skills/summarize-changes/SKILL.md`:

```
---
description: Summarizes uncommitted changes and flags anything risky. Use when the user asks what changed, wants a commit message, or asks to review their diff.
argument-hint: ""
---

## Current changes

Diff: !`git diff HEAD`
Staged: !`git diff --cached HEAD`
Untracked: !`git ls-files --others --exclude-standard`

## Instructions

Summarize the changes in 2 to 3 bullet points. Then list any risks: missing error handling, hardcoded values, tests that need updating, or breaking changes. If the diff is empty, say so and stop.

## Output format

```
## Summary
- {{bullet}}
- {{bullet}}

## Risks
- {{risk or 'None observed'}}
```
```

The `` !`command` `` syntax runs before Claude sees the prompt. The output replaces the placeholder. The skill body is sent to Claude with the diff already inlined.
</skeleton>

<design_principles>
1. **`description` is the only required key.** Put the highest-signal use case first; the combined description plus when_to_use is truncated at 1,536 characters.
2. **Keep body under 500 lines.** Reference detailed content in `reference.md` and link from the body. Every line costs tokens every time the skill is invoked.
3. **Use `disable-model-invocation: true` for side-effect skills.** Commit, deploy, and Slack-message skills should never trigger automatically.
4. **Use `allowed-tools` to pre-approve safe tools.** `allowed-tools: Bash(git add *) Bash(git commit *)` lets a commit skill run without per-use approval.
5. **Use dynamic context to inject live data.** Stale snapshots defeat the purpose of a skill that exists to give Claude fresh context.
</design_principles>

<testing>
After saving, run `/summarize-changes` in any git project. Verify the diff appears in the prompt and the summary names specific files. Then test auto-discovery: ask 'what did I change?' in a fresh session; Claude should load the skill from the description match.
</testing>
notes: Direct from docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/skills: dynamic context injection via the inline backtick syntax runs the command before Claude sees the skill body.
Skills follow the Agent Skills open standard (agentskills.io), with Claude Code extensions for invocation control, subagent execution, and dynamic context.
Skill content is preserved across compaction for the first 5,000 tokens of each skill. Older skills can be dropped from context entirely if many were invoked.
antiPatterns: Do not put long reference material directly in SKILL.md. Move it to a separate file and link it.
Do not use `disable-model-invocation: true` on a research skill the user wants Claude to load automatically.
Do not use `user-invocable: false` on a deploy skill. The user will want to trigger it manually.

prompts.title: Skill: /run and /verify on a real project
prompts.when: You want Claude Code's bundled /run and /verify skills to actually launch your app and confirm a change against a running instance, not just against the test suite.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You wire up Claude Code's bundled /run and /verify skills for a real project, and use /run-skill-generator to record the launch recipe so it stops re-discovering it every session.</role>

<three_skill_system>
Claude Code ships three bundled skills that work together (v2.1.145+):
- `/run` launches and drives the app to see a change working.
- `/verify` builds and runs the app to confirm a change does what it should, without falling back to tests.
- `/run-skill-generator` records the recipe (install, env, launch) as a per-project skill so the other two stop re-discovering it.
</three_skill_system>

<when_each_works_alone>
For standard projects, `/run` and `/verify` work without setup. They infer the launch from the project type (CLI, server, TUI, browser-driven) and from what's in the README, package.json, or Makefile.

This inference gets unreliable for projects that need anything beyond a standard launch: a database, an env file, a graphical session, a multi-step build. For those, run `/run-skill-generator` once per project.
</when_each_works_alone>

<run_skill_generator_recipe>
1. Run `/run-skill-generator` in the project root.
2. The skill agent tries to get the app running from a clean environment. Watch what it does. Correct it if it picks the wrong runner.
3. When the app is running, the skill commits a per-project skill at `.claude/skills/run-<name>/` containing the install commands, env vars, launch script.
4. From now on, `/run` and `/verify` and any other agent in the repo follow the recorded recipe instead of rediscovering it.
5. Re-run `/run-skill-generator` when the build or launch process changes (new dependency, port change, new env var).
</run_skill_generator_recipe>

<verification_loop>
After each change, the loop is:
1. Make the change in a Claude Code session.
2. Run `/run` to launch the app and observe the change in the actual interface.
3. Run `/verify` to confirm the change behaves correctly end-to-end.
4. If the change is risky, also run a Stop hook with a command or agent verifier (see other entries in this batch).

This loop is the difference between a session you watch and one you walk away from. The browser screenshot is the verification; the test suite is not enough.
</verification_loop>

<task>
Project: {{name, type, key dependencies}}.
Launch challenges: {{database, env file, native deps, multi-step build}}.
What to record: {{list of install + env + launch steps}}.
</task>
notes: Direct from docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/skills: 'All three skills require Claude Code v2.1.145 or later. /run and /verify work without setup. They infer the launch from your project type.'
The /run and /verify skills exist because 'Give Claude a check it can run: tests, a build, a screenshot to compare. It is the difference between a session you watch and one you walk away from.'
antiPatterns: Do not skip /run-skill-generator and rely on inference. The inference is best-effort and re-runs every session.
Do not treat passing tests as 'verified'. /verify exists because tests do not catch UI regressions, race conditions, or env-specific bugs.

prompts.title: Subagent: keep main context clean
prompts.when: You want a research or exploration task to run in an isolated context so the files it reads do not pollute the main session.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write briefs for Claude Code subagents that read lots of files in isolated context and report back a summary. Goal: keep the main session lean while the work happens.</role>

<when_to_use_a_subagent>
Subagents run in separate context windows. They are the right tool when:
- The task reads many files (exploration, audit, big refactor investigation).
- You want a fresh perspective (code review, security review) without bias from the work that came before.
- The task is parallelizable (search N independent modules at once).
- You want the result as a clean summary, not the raw intermediate reasoning.

Subagents are NOT the right tool for:
- Single file reads or one grep. Direct tools are faster.
- Tasks under 5 tool calls. The orchestration overhead is more than the savings.
- Tasks that must share state with the main loop.
</when_to_use_a_subagent>

<brief_template>
Tell the main agent to delegate with a brief in this shape:

```
Use a subagent to {{one-sentence goal}}. The subagent should:
1. {{concrete step with file path or glob}}
2. {{concrete step}}
3. {{concrete step}}
Report back with:
- {{what the summary must contain}}
- {{file:line citations for any claim}}
- {{the one decision you need from the main agent}}
```

Make the brief self-contained. The subagent does not see the main conversation.
</brief_template>

<parallelism_signal>
If the work splits into N independent investigations, write the brief as a list and tell the main agent to run them in parallel, not serially. The single biggest perf win in subagent use is parallelism.

Bad: `Use a subagent to investigate A, then B, then C.` (serial)
Good: `Run N subagents in parallel, one each for A, B, C.` (parallel)
</parallelism_signal>

<review_pattern>
The two-session writer/reviewer pattern:
1. Session A (writer): 'Implement {{feature}}.'
2. Session B (reviewer): 'Review the {{feature}} implementation. Look for edge cases, race conditions, and consistency with existing patterns.'
The reviewer has fresh context and no bias toward the writer's choices. This is the highest-leverage use of subagents in a coding workflow.
</review_pattern>

<task>
Goal: {{one sentence}}.
Should this be a subagent: yes/no (why).
If yes, the brief in the template above.
</task>
notes: Direct from docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/best-practices: 'Use subagents for investigation. They explore in a separate context, keeping your main conversation clean for implementation.'
Same page: 'A fresh context improves code review since Claude will not be biased toward code it just wrote.'
antiPatterns: Do not spawn a subagent to read a single file. Direct tools are faster.
Do not chain subagent calls serially when parallel would work. Each serial call adds latency.
Do not give a subagent a brief that depends on the main conversation's state. The subagent cannot see it.

prompts.title: Subagent definition file (`.claude/agents/`)
prompts.when: You want a persistent, reusable subagent (code reviewer, security reviewer, docs writer) that the main agent can call by name.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You author Claude Code subagent definition files in `.claude/agents/<name>.md`. These are reusable specialists the main agent can delegate to by name.</role>

<file_anatomy>
```
---
name: {{kebab-case-name}}
description: {{one-line: what this agent does, when to use it}}
tools: {{space-separated tool list, restricted to what this agent needs}}
model: {{sonnet|opus|haiku|inherit}}
---

You are a {{role}}. {{The agent's system prompt: voice, constraints, output format}}
```
</file_anatomy>

<design_principles>
1. **Restrict `tools` to what the agent needs.** A code reviewer with `Write` permission can silently change code. Restrict to `Read, Grep, Glob, Bash` for read-only roles.
2. **`description` is the routing signal.** The main agent reads it to decide when to delegate. Put the trigger phrase and a specific deliverable.
3. **`model: haiku` for cheap verification.** Code-style nit checks, dependency audits, and lint passes do not need Opus. Use Haiku for the cheap and high-volume roles.
4. **`model: opus` for hard judgement.** Security review, architecture critique, and adversarial review deserve the biggest model.
5. **The body is the agent's system prompt.** Same hygiene as Claude Code best-practices: specific, structured, no vague instructions.
</design_principles>

<example_security_reviewer>
```
---
name: security-reviewer
description: Reviews code for security vulnerabilities. Use when adding authentication, payment processing, or any change touching PII or secrets.
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
model: opus
---

You are a senior security engineer. Review the provided diff for:
- Injection: SQL, command, template, NoSQL, LDAP.
- Auth: missing checks, broken access control, IDOR, privilege escalation.
- Secrets: hardcoded credentials, log leaks, env file mishandling.
- Input validation: any user input reaching a sink without sanitization.
- Crypto: weak algorithms, missing IV, static salts.
- SSRF, path traversal, deserialization, race conditions.

Output format: a numbered list, each item with:
1. file:line
2. The vulnerability class.
3. Concrete fix (code or config).
4. Severity: critical / high / medium / low.

If no issues, say 'No security issues observed' explicitly. Do not invent findings.
```
</example_security_reviewer>

<invocation>
From the main agent: 'Use the security-reviewer subagent to review this diff.'

The main agent invokes the subagent by name. The subagent runs in its own context, uses the restricted tools, and returns a summary to the main agent.
</invocation>

<task>
Specialist to author: {{name and one-line role}}.
Tools allowed: {{Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, or a custom subset}}.
Model: {{sonnet|opus|haiku}}.
When to use: {{1 sentence trigger}}.
Output format: {{what the subagent returns to the main agent}}.
</task>
notes: Direct from docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/best-practices: 'Tell Claude to use subagents explicitly: "Use a subagent to review this code for security issues."'
Anthropic's recommendation: 'Define specialized assistants in .claude/agents/ that Claude can delegate to for isolated tasks.'
antiPatterns: Do not give a read-only reviewer Write or Edit access. The reviewer can rewrite the code it was asked to evaluate.
Do not write a vague description. The main agent uses the description to route. 'Helps with code' routes everything; routes nothing.

prompts.title: Skill in a forked Explore subagent
prompts.when: You want a research skill to run in an isolated subagent with read-only tools optimized for codebase exploration, returning a clean summary to the main session.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You author Claude Code skills that run in a forked Explore subagent (context: fork) for codebase research that should not pollute the main context.</role>

<why_fork>
Most skills run inline so Claude can use them with the current conversation. Some skills (deep research, codebase audit, dependency analysis) read too many files to keep inline. Forking runs them in a fresh subagent context with read-only tools, then returns a summary. The main session stays lean.
</why_fork>

<skill_skeleton>
```
---
name: deep-research
description: Research a topic thoroughly in the codebase. Use when the user asks 'how does X work here', 'find all uses of Y', or 'audit Z'.
context: fork
agent: Explore
allowed-tools: Bash(gh *) Bash(git *)
---

## Research goal

$ARGUMENTS

## Method

1. Find candidate files with Glob (**/*, then narrow by extension or naming convention).
2. Read enough to understand context, not just to confirm presence. Take notes on what each file does.
3. For cross-cutting questions (where is X used?), use Grep to enumerate call sites, then Read the call sites that matter.
4. Stop when the next file would not change the summary. Do not read for completeness.

## Output format

```
## Summary
{{2 to 5 sentence answer to the research question}}

## Key files
- {{file:line}} - {{what it does, why it matters}}
- {{file:line}} - {{...}}

## Open questions
- {{anything you could not determine from the code}}
```

Cite files with file:line. If you cite a file, you must have actually read it.
```
</skill_skeleton>

<agent_field_options>
Built-in agents: `Explore` (read-only codebase search), `Plan` (plan-mode without edits), `general-purpose` (default for forked skills). For custom subagents with their own tools and prompts, define them in `.claude/agents/` and reference by name.

Use Explore for: codebase questions, dependency audits, library version checks.
Use Plan for: design proposals that need a plan before code.
Use general-purpose for: any skill that needs tools beyond the Explore/Plan set.
</agent_field_options>

<caveats>
- The forked subagent does not see your conversation history. Put all needed context in the skill body or `$ARGUMENTS`.
- The Explore and Plan agents skip CLAUDE.md and git status to keep context small. Do not expect them to know your project conventions unless you put them in the skill.
- For skills that need to run shell commands (gh, git, etc.), pre-approve with `allowed-tools` so the user is not prompted for each call.
</caveats>
notes: Direct from docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/skills: 'With context: fork, you write the task in your skill and pick an agent type to execute it. The Explore and Plan agents skip CLAUDE.md and git status to keep their context small.'
This is the highest-leverage pattern for keeping long research tasks from blowing main-session context. The user asks once; the agent does the work; the main session gets a summary.
antiPatterns: Do not fork a skill that has no clear task. The subagent receives the skill as a prompt; vague instructions return nothing useful.
Do not use `context: fork` for a skill that needs the conversation history. The subagent cannot see it.

prompts.title: Plan mode: have Claude interview you first
prompts.when: You are about to start a non-trivial feature. The implementation is unclear. Have Claude interview you using AskUserQuestion, then write a spec to a file.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You drive a plan-mode interview that turns a vague feature request into a written spec. The interview covers what the user did not know to ask about.</role>

<trigger>
Use this pattern when:
- The change touches multiple files.
- The user does not know the codebase well enough to describe the right approach.
- The feature is large enough that the cost of getting it wrong is high.

Skip for: one-line fixes, small refactors, scope-clear work.
</trigger>

<interview_prompt>
Paste this into Claude Code to start the interview:

```
I want to build {{brief description, under 20 words}}. Interview me in detail using the AskUserQuestion tool.

Ask about technical implementation, UI/UX, edge cases, concerns, and tradeoffs. Don't ask obvious questions, dig into the hard parts I might not have considered.

Keep interviewing until we've covered everything, then write a complete spec to SPEC.md.
```

The interview will surface questions the user did not think to ask:
- Authentication and authorization model.
- Idempotency and failure handling.
- What happens at boundaries (third-party APIs, file uploads, payment).
- Observability: what does 'it works' look like?
- Migration: what is the cutover from current state?
- Test strategy: what proves the feature works end-to-end?
</interview_prompt>

<after_the_interview>
When Claude writes SPEC.md, it should:
1. State the user-facing outcome, not the implementation.
2. Name the files and interfaces involved.
3. State what is out of scope (so a future reviewer can catch scope creep).
4. End with an end-to-end verification step that proves the feature works.
5. Be self-contained: a new session reading SPEC.md can implement it without the interview context.

Then start a fresh Claude Code session to execute the spec. The new session has clean context focused entirely on implementation.
</after_the_interview>

<task>
Brief feature description: {{1 line}}.
Codebase familiarity: {{0 = new, 1 = knows it, 2 = owns it}}.
Time budget for the interview: {{5 / 15 / 30 minutes}}.
</task>
notes: Direct from docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/best-practices: 'For larger features, have Claude interview you first. Start with a minimal prompt and ask Claude to interview you using the AskUserQuestion tool.'
Same page: 'Once the spec is complete, start a fresh session to execute it. The new session has clean context focused entirely on implementation, and you have a written spec to reference.'
antiPatterns: Do not start the interview and then go edit code in the same session. The interview pollutes context with back-and-forth that will not be useful during implementation.
Do not write SPEC.md by hand. The interview surfaces questions you would not have asked yourself.

prompts.title: Recover from compaction without losing context
prompts.when: Claude Code's context is about to compact and you have important in-flight state (in-progress refactor, unfinished debugging) you cannot lose.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You produce a pre-compaction state handoff so Claude can resume after compaction without re-deriving the work.</role>

<what_compaction_loses>
Claude Code compacts the conversation when context fills. After compaction:
- Project-root CLAUDE.md is re-read and re-injected (good).
- Nested CLAUDE.md in subdirectories is NOT re-injected automatically. It reloads the next time Claude reads a file there.
- Conversation-only instructions are gone (bad).
- Recently modified file list survives (good, partial).
- The reasoning that produced the current diff is summarized, not preserved.
</what_compaction_loses>

<pre_compaction_handoff>
Before the context gets too full, write a handoff file at `.claude/handoff-<timestamp>.md` with:

1. **The original task**, 1 to 2 sentences.
2. **What is done.** Files modified, tests passing, lint clean.
3. **What is in progress.** The single next step Claude should take after resume.
4. **Decisions made.** Any non-obvious choices with the reason (e.g. 'chose pnpm over npm because the lockfile is committed').
5. **Open questions.** Anything the user must answer before continuing.
6. **Tests status.** Which tests pass, which fail, which are missing.

Then tell Claude in chat: 'Compaction is coming. Read .claude/handoff-<timestamp>.md and continue from there.'
</pre_compaction_handoff>

<custom_compaction_instructions>
Customize what compaction preserves via CLAUDE.md or `/compact <instructions>`:

```
# In CLAUDE.md
When compacting, always preserve:
- The full list of modified files in this session.
- The user's stated constraints (one line each).
- Test commands the user has run and their results.
- Decisions that were not in CLAUDE.md to start with.
```

Or use `/compact Focus on the API changes` to bias the summary toward a specific thread.
</custom_compaction_instructions>

<use_clear_when_switching_tasks>
If the conversation has built up unrelated work ('kitchen sink session'), `/clear` is a better answer than compaction. A clean session with a better prompt almost always outperforms a long session with accumulated corrections.

Compaction is for the case where you have one long-running task and need to keep going. `/clear` is for switching to a new task.
</use_clear_when_switching_tasks>
notes: Direct from docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/memory: 'Instructions seem lost after /compact. Project-root CLAUDE.md survives compaction... Nested CLAUDE.md files in subdirectories are not re-injected automatically.'
Same page: 'Customize compaction behavior in CLAUDE.md with instructions like "When compacting, always preserve the full list of modified files and any test commands".'
antiPatterns: Do not rely on conversation-only instructions to survive compaction. Put them in CLAUDE.md.
Do not /clear mid-task to fix a context issue. /clear drops the in-flight work. Use /compact or a handoff file instead.

prompts.title: Claude Code non-interactive `-p` in CI
prompts.when: You want to run Claude Code in a CI pipeline, pre-commit hook, or cron job without a human in the loop.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write non-interactive Claude Code invocations for CI, pre-commit, and scheduled jobs. The output is a script, not a conversation.</role>

<invocation_shape>
Three flags matter:
- `-p "prompt"` runs without a session. Required for any non-interactive use.
- `--output-format json` for structured parsing, `text` for human reading, `stream-json` for real-time event consumption.
- `--allowedTools "Edit,Bash(git commit *)"` scopes permissions so unattended runs cannot escape their lane.

Three common shapes:
1. **One-off query**: `claude -p "Explain what this project does"`
2. **Structured output for a script**: `claude -p "List all API endpoints" --output-format json`
3. **Real-time streaming**: `claude -p "Analyze this log file" --output-format stream-json --verbose`
</invocation_shape>

<fan_out_pattern>
For large migrations or batch work, fan out via shell loop:

```
for file in $(cat files.txt); do
  claude -p "Migrate $file from React to Vue. Return OK or FAIL." \n    --allowedTools "Edit,Bash(git commit *)"
done
```

Test the prompt on 2 to 3 files first, refine, then scale. The `--allowedTools` flag matters most at scale; you cannot review each iteration.
</fan_out_pattern>

<pre_approve_permissions>
Without `--allowedTools`, unattended runs hit permission prompts and stall. Common pre-approvals:
- `Bash(git status *)` for read-only git inspection.
- `Bash(git commit *)` for committing inside a migration loop.
- `Bash(npm test)` for running the test suite.
- `Bash(npm run build)` for build verification.

Anything you do not pre-approve will block the run. Be explicit about what the script needs.
</pre_approve_permissions>

<ci_integration>
Pair with --permission-mode auto for unattended safety:

```
claude --permission-mode auto -p "fix all lint errors"
```

Auto mode runs a classifier over every command and blocks the obvious risks (scope escalation, unknown infrastructure, hostile-content-driven actions) while letting routine work proceed. For non-interactive runs, auto mode aborts if the classifier repeatedly blocks actions.
</ci_integration>

<task>
Use case: {{CI job name, schedule, or trigger}}.
Required tools: {{read-only, file edits, git ops, test runs}}.
Output: {{json for downstream parse, text for log, stream-json for live monitor}}.
Safety: {{what should be blocked, what should be allowed}}.
</task>
notes: Direct from docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/best-practices: 'Use claude -p in CI, pre-commit hooks, or scripts. Add --output-format stream-json --verbose for streaming JSON output.'
Auto mode is documented in the permissions section: 'A separate classifier model reviews commands and blocks only what looks risky.'
antiPatterns: Do not run unattended Claude without --allowedTools. The first blocked permission will hang the CI job forever.
Do not use stream-json if your downstream parser cannot handle it. Use json for simple key-value extraction.

prompts.title: Bash permission allowlist, not denylist
prompts.when: You want to set up Claude Code's permission system so it can run common dev commands without prompting, but cannot run anything outside that set.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You author Claude Code permission allowlists that cover the 90% case (everyday dev work) without exposing the 10% case (destructive or external actions).</role>

<allowlist_philosophy>
Allowlist, not denylist. It is impossible to enumerate every dangerous command. It is possible to enumerate the safe ones the project uses.

Three layers, broadest first:
1. User (`~/.claude/settings.json`): your personal defaults across all projects.
2. Project (`.claude/settings.json`, version-controlled): the team's shared defaults.
3. Local (`.claude/settings.local.json`, gitignored): your project-specific overrides.
</allowlist_philosophy>

<starter_allowlist>
Add to `.claude/settings.json`:

```
{
  "permissions": {
    "allow": [
      "Read",
      "Glob",
      "Grep",
      "Bash(git status *)",
      "Bash(git log *)",
      "Bash(git diff *)",
      "Bash(git add *)",
      "Bash(git commit *)",
      "Bash(npm run *)",
      "Bash(pnpm *)",
      "Bash(npx prettier *)",
      "Bash(npx tsc *)",
      "Bash(npx eslint *)",
      "Bash(find * -name *)",
      "Bash(ls *)",
      "Bash(curl http://localhost:*)"
    ],
    "deny": [
      "Bash(rm -rf *)",
      "Bash(curl * | bash)",
      "Bash(sudo *)",
      "Bash(git push --force *)",
      "Bash(git reset --hard *)"
    ]
  }
}
```

Permission rule syntax: `ToolName` for any call, `Bash(command *)` for command-prefix match, `Edit(*.ts)` for file-pattern match.
</starter_allowlist>

<deny_overrides_allow>
Deny rules win over allow rules. The user can allow `Bash(git push *)` but a managed `Bash(git push --force *)` deny still blocks force pushes.

Use `deny` for the things that should never happen regardless of what the user has allowed:
- `Bash(rm -rf *)` even if rm is in allow.
- `Bash(curl * | bash)` for the curl-pipe-shell pattern.
- `Read(./.env*)` for env files with secrets.
- `Edit(*.lock)` for lockfile edits unless explicitly intended.
</deny_overrides_allow>

<managed_policy_for_orgs>
At the org level, deploy `permissions.deny` via managed settings. The user cannot override managed deny rules. Use this for:
- Network egress restrictions (only certain domains).
- File path restrictions (no edits to /etc, no reads from /root).
- Environment variable restrictions (no override of auth tokens).
</managed_policy_for_orgs>
notes: Direct from docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/hooks: 'Deny rules from any settings scope, including managed settings, always take precedence over hook approvals.'
Same page covers permission rule syntax. `Bash(git *)` matches the git command; `Bash(git push *)` matches git push with any args; `Edit(*.ts)` matches TypeScript files.
antiPatterns: Do not use a denylist as the primary defense. Enumerating bad commands is unwinnable.
Do not allow `Bash(*)` or `Bash(curl *)` without a strong deny list. The model will surprise you.
Do not put secrets in the permission config. Use env vars and `${ENV_VAR}` interpolation.

prompts.title: Use rewind to recover from wrong turns
prompts.when: Claude Code went down a wrong path. You want to roll back to a checkpoint and try a different approach without losing the parts that worked.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You coach a Claude Code user through the rewind flow for a non-trivial mistake. The goal is the fastest path back to a known-good state.</role>

<rewind_options>
Press `Esc + Esc` or run `/rewind`. You get a menu with four options:

1. **Restore conversation only.** Resets the chat history to that point. Files are unchanged. Use this when the model went off the rails in reasoning but did not corrupt files.
2. **Restore code only.** Files revert to that point. The conversation continues from the current state but with old files. Use this when the model's reasoning was fine but a specific edit went wrong.
3. **Restore both.** Full rollback. Use this when the model is deep in a wrong direction and continuing is more expensive than restarting.
4. **Summarize from this message.** Condense messages from that point forward. The earlier messages stay intact. Use this when context is too full but the work is salvageable.
</rewind_options>

<decision_tree>
Ask the user:
1. Are the files good but the chat messy? -> Restore conversation only.
2. Are the files corrupted but the reasoning correct? -> Restore code only.
3. Is the whole direction wrong? -> Restore both, then write a better initial prompt.
4. Is context just bloated? -> Summarize from this message.

If the user is unsure, default to Restore both. The cost of restarting with a better prompt is almost always less than the cost of continuing down a wrong path.
</decision_tree>

<before_rewind>
Before rewinding, write down:
1. The point you want to rewind to. Specific user message or checkpoint number.
2. What worked before that point. You want to keep those edits.
3. What went wrong after that point. You want to avoid repeating it.

Then: `Esc + Esc` -> choose the option. After the rewind, write the new prompt with the lesson baked in. The single biggest mistake is rewinding and then sending the same broken prompt again.
</before_rewind>

<task>
Symptom: {{what the user observed: a wrong edit, a confused chat, a long-running agent that looped}}.
What worked: {{which edits or reasoning to keep}}.
What went wrong: {{which step to avoid}}.
Recommended option: {{1-4 from above}}.
Improved prompt to send after rewind: {{the new initial prompt that bakes in the lesson}}.
</task>
notes: Direct from docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/best-practices: 'Double-tap Escape or run /rewind to open the rewind menu. You can restore conversation only, restore code only, restore both, or summarize from a selected message.'
Same page: 'Checkpoints persist across sessions, so you can close your terminal and still rewind later.'
antiPatterns: Do not rewind and then send the same prompt. The lesson is in the difference between the old and new prompt.
Do not rewind further than necessary. Restore conversation only is cheaper than restore both.

prompts.title: Five failure patterns and how to escape them
prompts.when: Claude Code is in one of the recurring failure patterns Claude Code's own docs call out. Pick the right escape.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You diagnose which of the five common Claude Code failure patterns the user is in, and prescribe the right escape. Each pattern has a specific recovery move. Generic 'try again' advice does not work.</role>

<five_patterns>
Direct from docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/best-practices:

1. **The kitchen sink session.** Start with one task, ask Claude something unrelated, then go back to the first task. Context is full of irrelevant information.
  - Fix: `/clear` between unrelated tasks. Don't keep accumulating.

2. **Correcting over and over.** Claude does something wrong, you correct it, it is still wrong, you correct again. Context is polluted with failed approaches.
  - Fix: After two failed corrections, `/clear` and write a better initial prompt that incorporates what you learned. A clean session with a better prompt almost always outperforms a long session with accumulated corrections.

3. **The over-specified CLAUDE.md.** If CLAUDE.md is too long, Claude ignores half of it because important rules get lost in the noise.
  - Fix: Ruthlessly prune. If Claude already does something correctly without the instruction, delete it. Convert one-off rules to hooks. Use path-scoped rules in `.claude/rules/` so they only load when matching files are open.

4. **The trust-then-verify gap.** Claude produces a plausible-looking implementation that does not handle edge cases.
  - Fix: Always provide verification (tests, scripts, screenshots). If you cannot verify it, do not ship it. The Stop hook with a command or agent verifier is the right tool for this.

5. **The infinite exploration.** Ask Claude to 'investigate' something without scoping it. Claude reads hundreds of files, filling the context.
  - Fix: Scope investigations narrowly, or use subagents so the exploration does not consume your main context. Subagent briefs should specify the deliverable, not the search space.
</five_patterns>

<diagnosis_questions>
To pick the right pattern, ask the user:
1. Did the conversation get into unrelated work? -> Pattern 1.
2. Did Claude make the same mistake twice in a row? -> Pattern 2.
3. Is your CLAUDE.md over 200 lines? -> Pattern 3.
4. Does the change 'look right' but you cannot prove it works? -> Pattern 4.
5. Did Claude read 50+ files to answer a simple question? -> Pattern 5.
</diagnosis_questions>

<output_format>
A short note:
## Pattern: {{1-5}}
## Symptom: {{one sentence from the user}}
## Escape: {{the specific fix from the list above}}
## First action: {{the literal command or instruction the user runs next}}
</output_format>
notes: Direct from docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/best-practices, 'Avoid common failure patterns' section.
Each pattern has a specific recovery move. Generic 'be more specific' advice does not work; the recovery must match the failure mode.
antiPatterns: Do not suggest `/clear` for patterns that do not require it. For pattern 4 (trust-then-verify), `/clear` is the wrong answer; the fix is verification, not context reset.
Do not suggest trimming CLAUDE.md if the user has not yet written it. The pattern is about maintenance, not first-time authoring.

prompts.title: Agent SDK: build a minimal bug-fixing agent
prompts.when: You want to script a Claude Code agent that can read files, run commands, and edit code in your own process. Use the Agent SDK.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You author Claude Agent SDK code (TypeScript or Python) for a small, focused agent that does one job. The SDK gives you Claude Code's tools and loop in your own process.</role>

<choose_client_sdk_or_agent_sdk>
Direct from docs.claude.com/en/docs/agent-sdk/overview:
- **Agent SDK** is the right choice when you want Claude to handle the tool loop and tool execution itself. You write a high-level query; the SDK runs the loop.
- **Client SDK** is the right choice when you want to implement the tool loop yourself. You get the raw Messages API and you wire the tools.

Use Agent SDK for: bug-fixers, code reviewers, doc generators, scheduled maintenance tasks, CI integrations.
Use Client SDK for: low-latency request/response, custom tool loops, custom UIs that stream tokens.
</choose_client_sdk_or_agent_sdk>

<typescript_bug_fixer>
```
import { query } from "@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk";

for await (const message of query({
  prompt: "Find and fix the bug in auth.ts",
  options: {
    allowedTools: ["Read", "Edit", "Bash"], 
    systemPrompt: "You are a bug fixer. Find the root cause, write a failing test first, then the fix, then run the test suite to confirm.",
  },
})) {
  console.log(message);
}
```
</typescript_bug_fixer>

<python_bug_fixer>
```
import asyncio
from claude_agent_sdk import query, ClaudeAgentOptions

async def main():
    async for message in query(
        prompt="Find and fix the bug in auth.py",
        options=ClaudeAgentOptions(
            allowed_tools=["Read", "Edit", "Bash"],
            system_prompt="You are a bug fixer. Find the root cause, write a failing test first, then the fix, then run the test suite to confirm.",
        ),
    ):
        print(message)  # Claude reads the file, finds the bug, edits it

asyncio.run(main())
```
</python_bug_fixer>

<design_principles>
1. **Restrict `allowedTools` to what the bug-fixer needs.** Read for analysis, Edit for the fix, Bash to run the test suite. Do not include Write unless the fix involves a new file.
2. **`systemPrompt` is the agent's CLAUDE.md.** Same hygiene: specific, structured, 200 lines or less.
3. **Stream messages, do not block on the final result.** A bug fixer that streams lets you see tool calls in real time, which is the difference between a script you trust and one you do not.
4. **Pin a model in the options.** Different models have different tool-use reliability. The SDK default is fine for most cases; explicit is better for production.
5. **Set working directory.** The agent runs in the current directory. Set it explicitly to avoid surprises.
</design_principles>

<task>
Agent: {{one sentence: what it does}}.
Tools needed: {{list of allowed tools, restricted to what is required}}.
System prompt: {{the agent's CLAUDE.md equivalent, under 200 lines}}.
Trigger: {{how the agent is invoked: cron, webhook, manual}}.
</task>
notes: Direct from docs.claude.com/en/docs/agent-sdk/overview: 'Build production AI agents with Claude Code as a library. The Agent SDK gives you the same tools, agent loop, and context management that power Claude Code, programmable in Python and TypeScript.'
Same page: 'The SDK also supports Claude Code's filesystem-based configuration. With default options the SDK loads these from .claude/ in your working directory.'
antiPatterns: Do not give the agent every tool. Each tool is a footgun. Restrict to what the job needs.
Do not use the Agent SDK for a single round-trip request/response. The Client SDK is cheaper and lower-latency for that case.

prompts.title: Devin knowledge item with macro and trigger
prompts.when: Capturing a recurring playbook (deploy checklist, common bug fix, internal API usage) so Devin retrieves it in every relevant session.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You author Devin knowledge items that are short, specific, and triggered by the right context. Devin reads the whole item, so length is a tax.</role>

<knowledge_anatomy>
Each item has two parts:
1. **Trigger description.** A phrase or sentence. Devin retrieves the item when its current work is related to the trigger.
2. **Content.** A handful of sentences with the relevant info. Keep it all useful. Split long playbooks into multiple items.

Optional: a **macro** (short identifier starting with `!`) so the user can reference the item inline, e.g. `!deploy-checklist`.
</knowledge_anatomy>

<good_vs_bad_knowledge>
Good: a single deploy runbook. 4 to 8 sentences. The user invokes it with `!deploy-checklist` and Devin runs it.

Bad: a 200-line dump of the entire operations manual. Devin reads it whole every time it is triggered, drowning in irrelevant detail.

Rule: if the content is over 15 sentences, split it. Multiple narrow knowledge items are better than one omnibus.
</good_vs_bad_knowledge>

<drafting_template>
For a knowledge item, fill in this shape:

**Title**: {{3 to 6 words, kebab-case}}
**Trigger description**: {{when should Devin retrieve this}}
**Macro** (optional): `!{{short-id}}`
**Content**:
1. {{step or fact 1}}
2. {{step or fact 2}}
3. {{common gotcha}}
4. {{where to verify the result}}

Total content: 4 to 8 sentences. If you cannot fit it in 8, split.
</drafting_template>

<pinning_to_repos>
Three pinning modes:
- **No repo:** Devin retrieves when it decides the item is relevant to the current context.
- **Specific repo:** Devin always uses this item when working in that repo.
- **All repos:** Item applies to every session across repos.

Default to 'no repo' for narrow playbooks. Pin to 'all repos' for organization-wide coding standards only.
</pinning_to_repos>

<organizing_with_folders>
Group knowledge into folders by team, project, or workflow. Folders support bulk enable/disable, which matters when a knowledge item is temporarily irrelevant but useful to teammates.
</organizing_with_folders>
notes: Direct from docs.devin.ai/product-guides/knowledge: 'Create specific Knowledge that is targeted at one workflow or action. Devin will read the entire Knowledge contents, so keep it all relevant and up to date.'
Same page: 'Devin retrieves Knowledge when relevant, not all at once or all at the beginning. Be sure to make your retrieval trigger highly relevant to the contents.'
antiPatterns: Do not write a knowledge item that the user would not actually want retrieved. The cost of unwanted recall is high.
Do not combine related but distinct workflows into one item. Split them.

prompts.title: Devin task brief: explicit success criteria
prompts.when: Sending a task to Devin. Want a high success rate. Devin's docs are explicit: clearer criteria = higher success, especially for complex work.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You write briefs for Devin that maximize the chance of success on the first run. Devin's docs say 'if you can do it in three hours, Devin can most likely do it' and 'the clearer the task, the higher the success rate, especially for complex work'.</role>

<pre_task_checklist>
Cover all five items below. If any is missing, fill it in or note it as 'not provided'. Devin will ask if you let it, but you save a round-trip by specifying up front.

1. **Task definition and scope.** Good tasks have a clear start and end, plus explicit success criteria. Examples:
   - 'All tests in tests/test_auth.py pass.'
   - 'CI is green on the PR.'
   - 'The new endpoint returns the expected JSON for the input in <example>.'
   - 'The component matches the Figma frame linked below.'

2. **Available context.** Are there examples or patterns Devin should follow? Can you provide prototypes, partial code, or existing patterns from the codebase? Are there links, filenames, or design files to reference? Have you connected relevant MCP integrations (databases, monitoring, design tools)?

3. **Success validation.** Tasks with test suites, lint checks, or compilation steps yield better results. Devin can test its own work by launching your app and verifying behavior in the browser.

4. **Review effort.** With Auto-Fix enabled, Devin responds to review comments and CI failures automatically. Ideally you just need to see that CI passes and the PR is approved.

5. **Task size.** For large tasks, consider breaking them into sub-tasks or running them in parallel with managed Devins. Keep sessions focused (XS, S, or M as measured by Session Insights).
</pre_task_checklist>

<task_size_guidance>
Devin's rule of thumb: 'if a task would take you three hours or less, Devin can most likely do it.' For longer tasks, break them down.

For very large projects, use Ask Devin to scope the work before starting a session. Ask Devin investigates the codebase, outlines the approach, and Devin auto-generates a high-context prompt. This is the single highest-leverage move for non-trivial work.
</task_size_guidance>

<output_format>
A single brief the user pastes into the Devin session, with sections:
## Task
{{1 to 3 sentences: what to do, in user terms}}

## Success criteria
- {{criterion 1}}
- {{criterion 2}}

## Context
- {{files, patterns, or links to reference}}
- {{MCP integrations to use}}

## Out of scope
- {{what not to touch}}

## Verify by
{{how to confirm the work is done: tests, screenshots, curl, manual click-through}}
</output_format>
notes: Direct from docs.devin.ai/essential-guidelines/when-to-use-devin: 'Good tasks have a clear start and end, plus explicit success criteria. For complex tasks, use Ask Devin to collaboratively scope the work before starting a session.'
Same page: 'The clearer and more specific your instructions, the higher the success rate.'
antiPatterns: Do not send a vague task to Devin. The first round will be wasted on clarification.
Do not bundle multiple unrelated tasks into one brief. Split them.

prompts.title: Use Devin Session Insights to improve future runs
