<output_format>Markdown: usage summary, comparison, costs, decision, deprecation note (if cut).</output_format>
<feature>{{feature_name}}</feature>
notes: Patterned after how product teams at frontier AI companies apply evaluation discipline to feature decisions, per Karpathy's 'Software 3.0' framing and Mollick's Co-Intelligence chapter on shipping less.
The 'opportunity cost' step is the key anti-vibes addition.
antiPatterns: Do not cut a feature because one exec does not like it; demand usage data.
Do not keep a feature because it is 'strategic' without naming the strategy it serves.

prompts.title: Pre-mortem LLM rollout
prompts.when: You are about to roll out a new LLM-powered feature to production and want to imagine the failure modes in advance.
prompts.prompt:
<pre_mortem>
Assume the rollout of {{feature}} to 100 percent of users has failed catastrophically 6 weeks from now. Write the post-mortem from that future perspective.
1. What went wrong? 3-5 concrete failure modes (latency spike, hallucinated content, brand-voice violation, cost overrun, safety incident).
2. For each failure mode, list the warning signs you would have seen in week 1.
3. For each failure mode, list the mitigation you could ship now (before rollout) that would have prevented or contained it.
4. List 2 'unknown unknowns' that you cannot predict and the canary release strategy that would surface them.
5. Final recommendation: ship, ship-with-canary, hold-and-revise, or kill.
</pre_mortem>
<output_format>Markdown with the future post-mortem, warnings, mitigations, unknowns, recommendation.</output_format>
<feature>{{feature}}</feature>
<rollout_plan>{{rollout_plan}}</rollout_plan>
notes: Patterned after Gary Klein's pre-mortem technique as applied to AI rollouts by Mollick and Anthropic's deployment-readiness reviews.
The 'warning signs in week 1' field is the most actionable output; it's the canary metrics you'd add before shipping.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the 'unknown unknowns'; they are the reason to ship behind a canary.
Do not let optimism bias compress the failure modes. Write them as if they happened, not as if they might.

prompts.title: Pivot on eval signal not anecdotes
prompts.when: You are considering a pivot in product direction and need to ground the decision in eval data rather than customer anecdotes.
prompts.prompt:
<pivot_decision>
State the proposed pivot in one sentence.
1. List 3 quantitative signals that would support the pivot: (a) usage data, (b) eval data, (c) retention or revenue signal.
2. List 3 quantitative signals that would refute the pivot.
3. Score each signal's strength 0-3 (volume, recency, cleanliness).
4. Compute a weighted sum: support_total - refute_total.
5. Recommend: pivot, hold, or test (run a structured experiment for 2 weeks first).
6. If test, specify the experiment: hypothesis, success metric, stop condition, sample size.
</pivot_decision>
<output_format>Markdown table with support/refute signals, scores, decision, and experiment spec.</output_format>
<pivot>{{proposed_pivot}}</pivot>
notes: Patterned after lean startup decision-making combined with Jim Fan's 'evals as the new product spec' framing.
The 'score each signal's strength' field is the antidote to confirmation bias in anecdotal data.
antiPatterns: Do not pivot on a single loud customer. Aggregate signal always beats anecdote.
Do not let refuting signals below a threshold kill a pivot; require multiple independent ones.

prompts.title: Go / no-go launch checklist
prompts.when: You are 24 hours from a launch and need a structured go / no-go decision across all the dimensions that matter.
prompts.prompt:
<checklist>
For each axis, mark PASS, CONCERN, or BLOCK and add a one-line reason.

1. Quality: p50 quality score on the eval set >= target. No axis below threshold.
2. Safety: PII filter rate > 99 percent on red-team probes. Jailbreak compliance < 1 percent.
3. Latency: p95 latency <= target. p99 within 2x of p95.
4. Cost: per-request cost within budget. Cost-per-engagement acceptable.
5. Reliability: 7-day uptime > 99.9 percent in staging. Fallback tested.
6. Support: runbook written. On-call rota set. Top 5 failure modes documented.
7. Legal: privacy review done. Terms of service updated. Required disclosures in UI.
8. Rollback: tested rollback to previous version in under 5 minutes.

Decision rule: any BLOCK means no-go. Two or more CONCERNS means hold. Otherwise go.
</checklist>
<output_format>Markdown table. Roll up to: GO / HOLD / NO-GO.</output_format>
<launch>{{launch_name}}</launch>
notes: Patterned after SRE launch checklists (Google SRE Workbook) and Anthropic's launch-review process.
The eight-axis list is the minimum; large enterprise launches add legal and regulatory axes.
antiPatterns: Do not waive a BLOCK with a verbal assurance; demand the eval number.
Do not let schedule pressure compress the checklist; that is the moment the checklist matters most.

prompts.title: PRM step-level test coverage
prompts.when: You generate code that performs a multi-step computation and you want step-level unit tests, not just an end-to-end test.
prompts.prompt: Given a function or pipeline that performs N intermediate steps to produce a result: 1. Identify each step S_i and its expected output type. 2. Write a unit test for each step S_i in isolation, with at least 3 inputs: normal, edge, error. 3. Write an integration test for the full pipeline, asserting both the final result AND each intermediate S_i output. 4. For each test, log the source of the expected value: 'derived from spec', 'computed by hand', 'from paper section X'. 5. If any step's expected output is not derivable from the spec, flag it as 'unverifiable' and add a TODO for the domain expert. Markdown. One section per step with the three test cases and the integration test at the end. {{code_snippet}} {{spec_doc}}
notes: Combines the PRM (process reward model) idea from OpenAI's 'Let's Verify Step by Step' (https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.20050) with classical unit-testing discipline.
The 'source of expected value' field is what makes the tests auditable.
antiPatterns: Do not write only end-to-end tests for multi-step code; a single passing test can hide 5 broken steps.
Do not invent expected values. If you cannot derive them, flag the gap.

prompts.title: Code rollout with canary and auto-rollback
prompts.when: You are deploying a new model version to a code-writing or coding-assistant product and need a safe rollout.
prompts.prompt:
<rollout_plan>
Stages (each auto-promotes if all gates pass; auto-rollback if any gate fails):
1. Internal: 0 percent of external traffic. Engineers + dogfooders. Hold 24 hours.
   Gates: crash-free rate > 99.5 percent. Eval-set pass rate within 1 percent of prior version.
2. Canary: 1 percent of external traffic. Random assignment. Hold 48 hours.
   Gates: quality regression < 2 percent on the eval set. Latency p95 within 10 percent of prior. Cost within 10 percent.
3. Ramp: 10 percent for 24 hours, then 50 percent for 24 hours, then 100 percent.
   Gates at each step: no safety regression, no PII regression, no new top-3 failure mode.
4. Rollback: tested revert to prior version in under 5 minutes. Always available.
</rollout_plan>
<output>Markdown checklist with each gate's measurement, the auto-promote and auto-rollback thresholds, and the on-call rota.</output>
<feature>{{feature_being_rolled_out}}</feature>
notes: Patterned after OpenAI's and Anthropic's staged model rollout practices as described in their safety and deployment blog posts.
The 'auto-promote if all gates pass' rule is the operationalization of Hassabis's eval discipline.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the canary stage for 'small' changes; small changes cause the worst incidents.
Do not leave rollback untested; a rollback that has never been rehearsed does not work in the moment.

prompts.title: Code-agent pipeline spec
prompts.when: You are building a coding agent (similar to Claude Code, Devin, Aider) and need a structured pipeline spec.
prompts.prompt:
<pipeline>
Five stages. Each stage has a clear input, output, and stop condition.
1. Understand: read the user request. Output a 1-sentence success criterion + a list of files likely to change.
2. Plan: emit a numbered plan P with each step's intent, inputs, outputs, and the tool to execute it.
3. Execute: walk P in dependency order. Issue parallel tool calls where independent. Honor the per-tool budget.
4. Verify: after each step, run the project's tests for the affected module. If tests fail, retry once, then escalate to the user with the failure.
5. Review: before declaring done, run a self-review pass against the original success criterion plus any 'must NOT change' constraints from the user.
</pipeline>
<persistence>
Persist all of: P, intermediate outputs, test results, the final review, and the diff.
If context is lost (long session), the next turn can reconstruct from the persisted artifacts.
</persistence>
<user_request>{{user_request}}</user_request>
notes: Patterned after Andrew Ng's Planning + Reflection patterns, the Anthropic Claude Code architecture, and Jim Fan's Voyager curriculum.
The persistence rule echoes Altman's 'agents as labor' framing of long-running agents.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the Understand stage; the success criterion is what 'done' means.
Do not auto-retry failed tests more than once; failure patterns compound and waste budget.

prompts.title: Mollick eval-as-code harness
prompts.when: You want to write code-based evals for an LLM feature, modeled on the harness pattern Mollick and Anthropic advocate.
prompts.prompt:
<eval_harness>
For each eval case, capture:
1. input: the exact prompt or test input.
2. expected: a structured expectation (regex match, JSON schema, exact string, or judge prompt).
3. weight: 0-1, how much this case matters for the overall score.
4. tags: e.g. 'regression', 'safety', 'capability'.

The harness runs every case, scores it, computes a weighted score, and writes a report.
Report includes: total score, per-tag score, top-5 failing cases (with input, actual output, expected, score).

The harness should be runnable in CI on every PR. Failure threshold: total score drops by more than 2 percent vs main.
</eval_harness>
<output_format>Markdown spec plus a sample eval file (YAML or JSON).</output_format>
<feature>{{feature_to_eval}}</feature>
notes: Synthesizes Mollick's 'one useful thing' practice with Anthropic's evals-as-spec engineering and Jim Fan's 'eval as product' framing.
The CI integration is what makes the eval set durable; without it, evals drift into oblivion.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the weight field; weighted scoring is what makes the overall number meaningful.
Do not run evals only manually; manual evals are forgotten after the second sprint.

prompts.title: Claygent research script
prompts.when: You are building a Clay-style research agent that runs a multi-step web research script for each lead.
prompts.prompt:
<research_script>
Per lead, run this script:
1. Find the lead's company site. Extract: company name, what they do (1 sentence), their latest product or post (URL).
2. Find the lead's LinkedIn or equivalent professional page. Extract: title, prior roles, recent posts.
3. Find any of: recent funding, recent hire in the lead's department, recent product launch. Record URL.
4. Synthesize: produce a 1-paragraph 'why now' summary that ties one of the above triggers to our value prop.
5. Stop. Do not draft the email yet; the next stage is the personalization card.
</research_script>
<rules>
1. If a step yields no signal, mark 'not found' and continue; do not invent.
2. Each fact must have a URL. No URL = no fact.
3. Cache results for 7 days; do not re-research the same lead twice in a week.
</rules>
<output>JSON: {lead_id, triggers: [{type, url, summary}], why_now: '...'}</output>
<lead>{{lead_input}}</lead>
notes: Patterned after Clay's Claygent workflow as described on https://www.clay.com/blog.
The 'no URL = no fact' rule is the discipline that distinguishes research from hallucination.
antiPatterns: Do not draft the email in this stage; the personalization card is a separate pass.
Do not re-run for a lead within 7 days; cache prevents duplicate work.

prompts.title: Eval-driven rollout gate
prompts.when: You operate a model in production and need to gate every release on a documented eval pass.
prompts.prompt:
<release_gate>
Before any model or system release, the following must be true:
1. Capability eval: total score >= prior release's score minus 1 percent. No individual capability axis drops by more than 3 percent.
2. Safety eval: jailbreak compliance rate <= prior release's rate. PII leak rate <= prior release's rate. No new safety failure mode.
3. Regression eval: the 50-item regression set (manually curated from past incidents) passes at >= 98 percent.
4. Cost eval: per-request cost not more than 10 percent higher than prior release at equivalent traffic.
5. Latency eval: p95 not more than 10 percent higher than prior release.
6. Human spot-check: 30 random production-like prompts reviewed by 2 humans with inter-rater agreement >= 80 percent.

Any gate failing blocks the release. Document the gate results in <release_eval> as part of the release notes.
</release_gate>
<release>{{release_id}}</release>
notes: Patterned after DeepMind's and Anthropic's release processes and Hassabis's repeated emphasis on eval discipline.
The 50-item regression set is the under-appreciated piece; it's the 'we have seen this failure before' memory.
antiPatterns: Do not waive a gate for a 'small' release; small releases cause large incidents.
Do not skip the human spot-check; evals miss what humans catch.

prompts.title: Frontier LLM incident first 15 minutes
prompts.when: You are the on-call for a production LLM system and you have 15 minutes to triage an incident.
prompts.prompt:
<triage_loop>
Within 15 minutes:
1. Identify the user-visible symptom in one sentence (latency, hallucination, refusal, leak, crash).
2. Check the eval dashboard. Did any safety or quality axis drop below threshold in the last hour?
3. Check the cost dashboard. Is per-request cost spiking?
4. Check the rollout log. Did any release happen in the last 4 hours?
5. Check the upstream model provider status. Is there a known degradation?
6. If you found a likely cause, decide: mitigate (rate-limit, route to fallback), revert (rollback to prior release), or investigate further.
7. If you cannot find a cause in 15 minutes, escalate and start a wider investigation.
</triage_loop>
<output>One-page incident note: symptom, likely cause, action taken, next steps, owner.</output>
<incident>{{incident_description}}</incident>
notes: Patterned after Google SRE incident response and the SRE Workbook's incident management chapter, adapted for LLM-specific surfaces (eval dashboard, provider status).
The '5. check upstream provider' step is LLM-specific and often the cause.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the provider status check; many incidents are not your fault.
Do not investigate for more than 15 minutes before escalating; burnout is the on-call's biggest risk.

prompts.title: Decidable prompt cache structure
prompts.when: You want to structure your system prompt so the model provider's prompt cache hits consistently and saves cost.
prompts.prompt:
<cache_structure>
1. Static prefix: identity, capabilities, constraints. NEVER changes between requests. Must be at the top.
2. Semi-static: today's date, user's role, current task type. Changes rarely. Below the static prefix.
3. Dynamic: the actual user request and any tool-result text. At the bottom.
4. Variable: tool definitions, examples, retrieved documents. Inserted after the static prefix and before the dynamic block.

Rules:
1. Anything that changes more than once per hour goes below the static prefix.
2. The cache key is the prefix up to the first variable element. Maximize the prefix length.
3. Do not include timestamps, request IDs, or random IDs in the static prefix.
4. Do not change the static prefix between requests unless you intentionally want to bust the cache.
</cache_structure>
<output_format>Annotated prompt showing the four blocks in order with explicit comments.</output_format>
<prompt_to_restructure>{{prompt}}</prompt_to_restructure>
notes: Patterned after Anthropic's prompt caching guide and OpenAI DevDay 2024 prompt-caching announcement.
The four-block structure is the portable abstraction; provider APIs differ but the ordering invariant holds.
antiPatterns: Do not put the user request in the static prefix; it will bust the cache.
Do not include random IDs or timestamps in the static prefix for the same reason.

prompts.title: SLO definition for LLM product
prompts.when: You operate an LLM product and need an SLO definition that goes beyond uptime to cover quality.
prompts.prompt:
<slo_definition>
Define SLOs along three axes:
1. Availability: monthly uptime >= 99.9 percent.
2. Quality: monthly fraction of responses with eval score >= pass threshold >= 95 percent.
3. Latency: monthly fraction of requests with p95 latency <= target_ms >= 95 percent.

For each axis, specify:
- Measurement method (eval set, prod sample, golden traces).
- Pass threshold (numerical).
- Burn rate: how fast you can burn the error budget. Standard: 2 percent of budget in 1 hour, 5 percent in 6 hours.
- Action when burning: alert, page, freeze releases, rollback.
</slo_definition>
<product>{{product}}</product>
<usage_pattern>{{p50_daily_traffic_and_peak}}</usage_pattern>
notes: Combines Google SRE Workbook SLO chapter with LLM-specific quality metrics drawn from Anthropic's reliability engineering posts and the Latent Space reliability episodes.
The 'burn rate' framing is what makes SLOs actionable in real time.
antiPatterns: Do not set quality SLOs without a stable eval set; the SLO becomes noise.
Do not set SLOs so tight that any normal traffic variation trips them; users will ignore the alerts.

prompts.title: Amodei honest product narrative
prompts.when: You are writing a product narrative (launch post, founder note) that must be both compelling and honest, per Amodei's stance on AI discourse.
prompts.prompt:
<narrative_posture>
Write a product narrative that is:
1. Honest about what the product can and cannot do today. No 'revolutionary' language where 'incremental' is accurate.
2. Clear about what is research vs shipping. 'We are exploring X' is not the same as 'We ship X'.
3. Specific about the failure modes users will encounter. Naming failure modes builds trust faster than hiding them.
4. Free of 'AGI' framing unless the product actually is AGI. Hyperbole degrades every future claim.
5. Specific about safety posture: what data is collected, how it is used, what is not done.
</narrative_posture>
<product>{{product}}</product>
<audience>{{audience}}</audience>
<output>Markdown. 600-1000 words. Title + 4-6 sections.</output>
notes: Sourced from Dario Amodei's writing on AI honesty (Lex Fridman #452, his 'Machines of Loving Grace' essay) and his stance against AI hype.
The 'name your failure modes' rule (#3) is the under-used one that distinguishes trust-building posts from hype posts.
antiPatterns: Do not promise AGI or human-level intelligence as a marketing claim.
Do not omit the safety section; readers will assume you are hiding it.

prompts.title: Mollick co-intel blog post
prompts.when: You want to write a 'Working with AI' style blog post that mixes anecdote, framework, and concrete prompt patterns, in the spirit of Mollick's Substack.
prompts.prompt:
<post_structure>
1. Opening anecdote: a concrete moment where AI surprised you or failed you. 200 words. Specific.
2. The pattern: distill the anecdote into a 1-sentence insight.
3. The framework: a 3-5 step pattern that others can apply. Each step concrete and actionable.
4. The prompt: include a copy-paste-ready prompt that implements step 3.
5. The limit: where this pattern breaks. Be honest. 100 words.
6. The invitation: a question or task for the reader. End on a 'try this' rather than a 'thoughts?' close.
</post_structure>
<output>Markdown. 1000-1500 words. One image or diagram if it earns its place.</output>
<anecdote_seed>{{your_anecdote}}</anecdote_seed>
notes: Sourced from Ethan Mollick's 'One Useful Thing' Substack style and Co-Intelligence chapter structure.
The 'include the prompt' step is Mollick's signature move; readers can copy and apply immediately.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the 'limit' section; without it the post reads as marketing.
Do not write a 3000-word post; Mollick's sweet spot is 1000-1500 words.

prompts.title: Stylized portrait avatar pack
prompts.when: You need a coherent set of profile avatars in distinct art styles from a single face reference.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a senior image-prompt engineer producing a 4-tile avatar pack on a clean white canvas.</role>
<task>Render the subject from {{reference_image}} as a 2x2 grid of four stylized portraits, each in a different art style. Keep the face recognizable across all tiles.</task>
<subject>{{subject_description: age, gender, hair, skin tone, signature features}}</subject>
<style_tiles>
Tile 1: Pixar-style 3D render, soft cinematic lighting, warm palette.
Tile 2: Studio Ghibli anime watercolor, soft linework, pastel sky background.
Tile 3: 90s comic-book cel-shaded illustration, bold outlines, halftone dots.
Tile 4: Pixel-art 64x64 sprite scaled up with nearest-neighbor, retro game palette.
</style_tiles>
<consistency_rules>
- Same hair color, eye color, facial hair if any across all four tiles.
- Same head angle (three-quarter facing camera) in all four tiles.
- Tile labels in small caps under each frame, plain sans-serif.
- White background on the outer canvas; colored backgrounds only inside each tile.
</consistency_rules>
<output>2:2 square grid, no border, no watermark, total dimensions 2048x2048.</output>
<negative>no text other than tile labels, no logo, no other people, no extra accessories beyond what is in the reference.</negative>
notes: Identity preservation pattern adapted from youmind avatar pack conventions: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/profile-avatar.
Forcing the same head angle and palette anchor across tiles is the difference between a coherent set and four random portraits.
antiPatterns: Do not let each tile drift into a different pose. Anchor the head angle in the rules.
Do not add props or costumes unless they appear in every tile.

prompts.title: Cartoon style avatar treatment
prompts.when: You have a real face and want a friendly cartoon avatar for Slack, Discord, or a team page.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a cartoon avatar designer who keeps the likeness readable while exaggerating features for charm.</role>
<task>Turn the face in {{reference_image}} into a clean, friendly cartoon avatar on a solid color background.</task>
<subject>Subject traits to preserve: {{face_shape, eye_color, hair_color, signature_features}}. Hair must remain recognizably the same.</subject>
<style>Modern flat vector cartoon, thick uniform black outlines (3pt at 1024px), 4-color limited palette, soft cel-shading on skin, no gradients except in cheeks, geometric simplification.</style>
<background>Solid pastel background in {{brand_color}}.</background>
<pose>Three-quarter angle, slight smile, looking at viewer, shoulders visible.</pose>
<output>Square 1024x1024 PNG.</output>
<negative>no photorealism, no anime, no 3D render, no busy background, no text, no watermark, no extra people.</negative>
notes: Pattern inspired by the Slack/Discord avatar style guide and youmind cartoon portrait references: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/profile-avatar.
The 4-color palette rule is what stops the result from looking like a stock vector.
antiPatterns: Do not request 'Disney' or 'Pixar' as a style cue. They force a different genre.
Do not add a gradient background; flat color is what reads as an avatar at small sizes.

prompts.title: Retro pixel art avatar
prompts.when: You want a 16-bit or 32-bit pixel art avatar in the style of a SNES or PS1 character select screen.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a pixel-art sprite artist working in 16-bit or 32-bit color depth.</role>
<task>Render {{subject_description}} as a tight 64x64 pixel art portrait, scaled up with nearest-neighbor to 1024x1024.</task>
<style>{{color_depth}}: SNES-era 16-bit or PS1-era 32-bit palette, hard edges, no anti-aliasing, no gradients beyond 4-step ramp shading.</style>
<palette>Limited to {{n}} colors total. Choose a warm or cool palette that matches the subject's personality.</palette>
<background>Solid dark color with a single accent shape behind the head (crescent, sun, or geometric symbol).</background>
<framing>Head and shoulders only, centered, the sprite fills 80 percent of the canvas.</framing>
<output>1024x1024 PNG, no smoothing, no blur, pixel grid must be visible at zoom.</output>
<negative>no anti-aliased edges, no dithering if 16-bit, no modern 3D, no anime, no photoreal, no text.</negative>
notes: Pattern from the classic 16-bit RPG character portrait convention; adapted from youmind pixel-art references: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/styles.
Limiting the palette to 16 or 32 colors is the constraint that makes the result read as authentic pixel art.
antiPatterns: Do not request high resolution detail. Coarse pixels are the point.
Do not allow gradients beyond a 4-step ramp. Smooth shading breaks the genre.

prompts.title: Watercolor portrait avatar
prompts.when: You want a soft, hand-painted watercolor avatar for a personal site, newsletter signup, or author bio.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a watercolor portraitist who works wet-on-wet with controlled bleeds.</role>
<task>Render a watercolor portrait of {{subject_description}} as a profile avatar.</task>
<subject>Preserve hair color, eye color, and one signature feature (freckles, glasses, beard, mole).</subject>
<style>Loose watercolor wash, visible paper texture, controlled edge bleeds, soft color transitions, pigment pooling at edges. Pigments named in the palette: {{palette: indigo, burnt sienna, ochre, sap green, payne's gray}}.</style>
<background>White or cream watercolor paper with a single pale wash hint of {{background_color}}.</background>
<pose>Three-quarter angle, soft gaze, slightly downcast eyes.</pose>
<output>Square 1024x1024, paper texture visible at edges.</output>
<negative>no anime, no 3D, no digital smoothness, no hard outlines, no text, no watermark.</negative>
notes: Watercolor presets benefit from naming real pigments rather than abstract colors; youmind watercolor prompts use this convention: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/styles.
Forcing paper texture and edge bleed prevents the common 'digital watercolor' look that reads as plastic.
antiPatterns: Do not ask for 'watercolor effect'. Ask for visible pigment behavior.
Do not request sharp outlines. Watercolor is defined by soft edges.

prompts.title: Oil painting master portrait
prompts.when: You want a Rembrandt/Vermeer/Velázquez-style classical oil portrait for a brand, podcast cover, or editorial bio.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a 17th-century Dutch or Spanish master oil painter working in a single studio light source.</role>
<task>Render a classical oil painting portrait of {{subject_description}}.</task>
<style>{{school}}: Rembrandt chiaroscuro with strong directional window light from the upper left and deep umber shadows, OR Vermeer soft north-window daylight with mid-tone palette and luminous skin, OR Velázquez loose brushwork with restrained earth-tone palette.</style>
<lighting>Single light source, chiaroscuro, warm color temperature around 3500K, deep shadow falloff.</lighting>
<palette>Restricted to {{n}} oil pigments: {{naples yellow, burnt umber, raw sienna, ivory black, lead white, vermilion}}.</palette>
<brushwork>Visible impasto on highlights, smooth blending on skin mid-tones, loose textural strokes on clothing and background.</brushwork>
<canvas>Subtle weave texture, faint craquelure on edges.</canvas>
<output>Vertical 3:4, 2048 tall.</output>
<negative>no photorealism, no digital smoothness, no anime, no text, no watermark.</negative>
notes: Pattern from classical art-history study; school names anchor lighting and palette much better than abstract adjectives.
Limiting the pigment count is what stops the model from inventing colors the master would not have had on the palette.
antiPatterns: Do not mix schools. Pick one.
Do not ask for 'realistic'. The point is painterly.

prompts.title: Monogram initials avatar
prompts.when: You want a personal brand mark built from your initials with a small decorative motif.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a brand-mark designer working in serif and display typography.</role>
<task>Design a monogram avatar using the initials {{initials}} on a flat color field.</task>
<style>{{style_choice: 1) Garamond engraved style with thin border. 2) Art deco geometric with chevron accent. 3) Vintage engraved bookplate with laurel wreath. 4) Minimalist modern with single hairline rule.}}</style>
<palette>Background {{bg_color}}, initials in {{fg_color}}, single accent color {{accent_color}} used once.</palette>
<motif>Optional small motif: {{motif: cat, leaf, sword, moon, mountain, star}}. If used, place it under or around the initials at 30 percent the size of the letterforms.</motif>
<output>Square 1024x1024 PNG, centered, plenty of negative space around the mark.</output>
<negative>no photorealism, no busy background, no gradient, no more than 2 typefaces.</negative>
notes: Brand-mark style references adapted from monogram archive conventions.
Picking a single named style is more reliable than adjectives like 'elegant' or 'sophisticated'.
antiPatterns: Do not combine multiple style options. Pick one.
Do not add gradients. Monograms live or die on contrast.

prompts.title: Friendly brand mascot character
prompts.when: You need a brand mascot character (animal, object, or food) that can be reused across icons, stickers, and merch.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a mascot character designer who designs at 3 size tiers: tiny favicon, medium sticker, large hero.</role>
<task>Design a friendly mascot character representing {{brand_or_product_concept}}.</task>
<subject_choices>Pick ONE: {{subject: animal | food | object | abstract}}. Describe the specific creature or item, e.g., 'a chubby brown fox with a cream belly', 'a smiling avocado with arms', 'a retro cassette tape with legs'.</subject_choices>
<face>Two large round black eyes, a small smile, simple nose. The face must remain readable at 32x32 pixels.</face>
<color>Limited to {{n}} flat colors with one accent. No gradients on the body. Soft cel-shading allowed.</color>
<pose>Standing, three-quarter angle, waving with one hand. Body proportions: head about 1/3 of total height.</pose>
<background>Solid {{brand_color}} on the main canvas; mascot isolated with no ground shadow.</background>
<output>Square 2048x2048, mascot centered, 20 percent padding on all sides.</output>
<negative>no photorealism, no scary expressions, no extra limbs, no text, no watermark, no busy background.</negative>
notes: The 'must read at 32x32' rule forces simple shapes and is the single most useful constraint in mascot design.
Pattern inspired by youmind mascot/character prompts: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/character.
antiPatterns: Do not give the mascot detailed human anatomy. Keep it simple.
Do not add a background scene. The mascot is the product.

prompts.title: Cyberpunk neon portrait avatar
prompts.when: You want a neon-lit cyberpunk avatar for a Discord, gaming handle, or tech-punk personal brand.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a cyberpunk concept artist working in Blade Runner 2049 / Ghost in the Shell visual vocabulary.</role>
<task>Render a tight cyberpunk avatar portrait of {{subject_description}}.</task>
<lighting>Dual-source: {{neon_color_1}} rim light from the left, {{neon_color_2}} rim light from the right. Face lit by a soft frontal fill at 30 percent intensity. Hard rim falloff.</lighting>
<wardrobe>Minimal sci-fi details: {{wardrobe_detail: visor | cybernetic tattoo | metallic collar | LED ear cuff}}. No full costume.</wardrobe>
<skin>Subsurface translucency with neon bounce light. Visible pores or texture at high zoom.</skin>
<background>Dark cyberpunk alley at night, out-of-focus neon signage in {{language}}, rain-slick reflections on ground, bokeh.</background>
<composition>Tight head-and-shoulders crop, subject slightly off-center to leave negative space for neon bleed.</composition>
<output>Square 1024x1024 PNG.</output>
<negative>no anime, no daylight, no cute mascot feel, no text in the foreground, no watermark.</negative>
notes: Cyberpunk is defined by dual-color rim lighting. Forcing both colors and directions is what makes it read as the genre.
Youmind cyberpunk prompts pair the rim-light rule with rain/bokeh for depth: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/styles.
antiPatterns: Do not use a single light source. Dual neon rims are the genre's signature.
Do not add full face paint or heavy makeup. Cyberpunk reads as subtle tech, not costume.

prompts.title: Medieval fantasy character avatar
prompts.when: You want a tabletop-RPG-style fantasy avatar (knight, mage, ranger, rogue) for a D&D character sheet.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a fantasy character illustrator in the style of classic D&D Player's Handbook portraits.</role>
<task>Render a fantasy character portrait for a {{class: warrior | mage | rogue | ranger | cleric | druid}} of level {{level}}.</task>
<race>{{race: human | elf | half-elf | dwarf | tiefling | dragonborn | halfling}}</race>
<armor>One signature armor or robe element visible in the crop: {{armor_piece}}. No full plate unless the class is paladin.</armor>
<lighting>Single torch or hearth fire light from the lower left, warm orange-amber, deep umber shadows.</lighting>
<background>Painted backdrop hint of {{setting: castle interior | ancient forest | dungeon corridor | mountain pass}}. Painterly, not photoreal.</background>
<style>Traditional fantasy oil painting with brushwork visible, color palette of earth tones plus one accent gem color.</style>
<output>Vertical 3:4 portrait, 1024 wide.</output>
<negative>no photorealism, no anime, no modern weapons, no text, no watermark.</negative>
notes: Class-specific armor cues help the model anchor the fantasy genre without over-specifying gear.
Youmind medieval and fantasy portrait references: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/styles.
antiPatterns: Do not stack every fantasy trope in one image. Pick the class and let the costume follow.
Do not add text runic engravings unless explicitly requested.

prompts.title: Furry / anthropomorphic avatar
prompts.when: You want an anthropomorphic character avatar (fox, cat, wolf, dragon) for a community handle or streaming profile.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a character designer specializing in anthropomorphic portraiture.</role>
<task>Render an anthropomorphic character portrait of {{species: fox | cat | wolf | dragon | rabbit | raccoon}}.</task>
<expression>Specific micro-expression: {{expression: smug smile | curious tilt | sleepy half-eyes | confident grin | bashful}}. Pick one.</expression>
<fur_palette>{{primary_fur_color}} as base, {{secondary_color}} on chest, ears, and tail tip. One signature marking: {{marking: stripe | spot | blaze | heterochromia}}.</fur_palette>
<eyes>Detailed iris with visible pupil, single highlight reflection, characterful shape.</eyes>
<clothing>Optional simple shirt or scarf in {{clothing_color}}. No full outfits.</clothing>
<background>Soft pastel gradient from {{bg_top}} to {{bg_bottom}}, no scenery.</background>
<output>Square 1024x1024 PNG, head-and-shoulders, centered.</output>
<negative>no photorealism, no horror, no extra characters, no text, no watermark.</negative>
notes: Anthropomorphic avatar convention requires a single micro-expression, not a 'happy' default. The micro-expression carries the personality.
References adapted from youmind character avatar prompts: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/character.
antiPatterns: Do not render the animal as fully realistic. Anthropomorphic means stylized human-animal blend.
Do not add busy backgrounds. The character is the avatar.

prompts.title: Corporate headshot with bokeh
prompts.when: You need a LinkedIn-style corporate headshot without paying a photographer.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a corporate headshot photographer using natural light and clean bokeh.</role>
<task>Render a corporate headshot of {{subject_description}} for a LinkedIn or company about-page profile.</task>
<lighting>Soft natural window light from the subject's left at 45 degrees, large silver bounce fill on the right. No flash.</lighting>
<background>Out-of-focus office environment with bokeh highlights, neutral tones (warm beige, soft gray), no identifiable logos or text.</background>
<wardrobe>Smart business casual: {{wardrobe: navy blazer over white oxford | charcoal suit with light tie | knit blazer over crew neck}}. No logos visible.</wardrobe>
<expression>Calm confident smile, eyes on the lens, head turned 5 degrees to the subject's left.</expression>
<crop>Head-and-shoulders, top of head to mid-chest, ample headroom.</crop>
<output>Vertical 4:5 or 1:1, 1024 minimum edge.</output>
<negative>no sunglasses, no hat, no busy background, no other people, no visible text, no watermark.</negative>
notes: Corporate headshot convention: 45-degree soft window light + bokeh office background reads as professional without looking stock.
Reference patterns from common LinkedIn photo guidelines and youmind corporate portrait templates.
antiPatterns: Do not use dramatic or cinematic lighting. Corporate headshots need even, calm light.
Do not include any visible logos or recognizable office details in the background.

prompts.title: Silhouette minimal avatar
prompts.when: You want a clean silhouette icon (head + shoulders or full body) as a personal mark or podcast guest avatar.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a graphic designer specializing in single-shape silhouette marks.</role>
<task>Design a silhouette avatar of {{subject_concept: a dancer | a coder | a surfer | a chef | a violinist}} in a single flat color on a contrasting background.</task>
<silhouette>Single closed shape, no internal lines, no facial features, recognizable pose. The pose tells the story: {{pose: arms raised mid-leap | hunched over a laptop | riding a wave | holding a pan | playing violin}}.</silhouette>
<palette>Two colors only. Subject in {{fg_color}}, background in {{bg_color}}.</palette>
<negative_space>Generous padding around the silhouette, at least 15 percent on each side.</negative_space>
<output>Square 1024x1024, clean vector-like edges, no texture.</output>
<negative>no gradient, no internal detail, no text, no watermark, no busy background.</negative>
notes: Silhouettes work because they force pose-as-storytelling. The pose carries the brand.
Pattern adapted from youmind minimal icon references: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/styles.
antiPatterns: Do not include facial features. A silhouette without details is what makes it iconic.
Do not use more than two colors.

prompts.title: 3D clay render avatar
prompts.when: You want a soft 3D clay-style avatar (think modern Pixar-meets-Aardman) for a personal site or Notion cover.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a 3D character artist working in clay-render style with subsurface scattering.</role>
<task>Render a clay-style 3D avatar of {{subject_description}}.</task>
<render>Soft clay material with subtle subsurface scattering on ears, nose, fingertips. Matte finish, no specular gloss, no metalness.</render>
<lighting>Single soft area light from upper left at 30 degrees, soft fill from lower right. No hard shadows. Even ambient occlusion in crevices.</lighting>
<palette>{{n}} clay colors, limited to {{palette}}. Skin tone in {{skin_color}}, hair in {{hair_color}}, clothing accent in {{accent_color}}.</palette>
<style>Subtle handcrafted imperfection: fingerprints on the surface, slightly uneven edges, soft tool marks.</style>
<background>Soft pastel solid color {{bg_color}}.</background>
<output>Square 1024x1024 PNG.</output>
<negative>no photorealism, no anime, no hard edges, no glossy plastic, no text, no watermark.</negative>
notes: Clay render convention: subsurface scattering + matte + soft area light is the technical foundation.
Youmind 3D render style references: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/3d-render.
antiPatterns: Do not request 'realistic'. Clay is a stylization, not realism.
Do not use hard rim lighting. Soft area light is what gives clay its tactile feel.

prompts.title: Vintage photograph avatar
prompts.when: You want a vintage-photo avatar (1950s-1980s look) for an editorial bio or nostalgic project.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a portrait photographer working in {{era: 1950s | 1960s | 1970s | 1980s}} film and lighting conventions.</role>
<task>Render a vintage portrait of {{subject_description}} as if shot on {{film_stock: Kodak Portra 400 | Fuji Pro 400H | Kodachrome 64}}.</task>
<color_palette>Era-correct: 1950s muted sepia-tinted; 1960s punchy primary colors; 1970s warm amber and avocado; 1980s high contrast with neon glow.</color_palette>
<lighting>Period-correct: 1950s soft Hollywood glamour; 1960s Pop-Art flat; 1970s golden-hour softness; 1980s mixed fluorescent and tungsten.</lighting>
<wardrobe>Era-appropriate: {{1950s: cat-eye glasses, victory rolls, swing dress | 1960s: bouffant, mod shift dress | 1970s: sideburns, wide collar, earth tones | 1980s: permed hair, shoulder pads, neon accents}}.</wardrobe>
<film_grain>Visible grain at ISO-equivalent of {{iso}}, soft halation around highlights, slight chromatic aberration on edges.</film_grain>
<output>Square 1024x1024 or vertical 4:5.</output>
<negative>no modern smartphone look, no HDR, no AI-smooth skin, no text, no watermark.</negative>
notes: Naming the film stock anchors the color palette better than adjectives. Kodak Portra and Fuji Pro 400H are recognized references.
Youmind vintage photography reference patterns: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/retro-vintage.
antiPatterns: Do not mix eras. Pick one.
Do not request 'old-timey'. Name the decade.

prompts.title: Minimal flat color-block avatar
prompts.when: You want a Notion/Linear/Slack-style minimal avatar built from flat geometric shapes.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a graphic designer working in flat geometric vector style.</role>
<task>Design a minimal avatar of {{subject_concept}} using only 3 to 5 flat geometric shapes.</task>
<shapes>Available: circle, half-circle, quarter-circle, square, rectangle, triangle, thin line. No curves other than circles.</shapes>
<palette>Three colors: {{color_1}}, {{color_2}}, {{accent}}. One is reserved for the largest shape (head/body), one for a smaller shape (facial hint), and one as a single accent.</palette>
<face>Optional minimal face: two tiny circles for eyes, one small arc for smile. All in the accent color.</face>
<composition>Centered, generous padding, 80 percent of canvas filled by the largest shape.</composition>
<output>Square 1024x1024 PNG.</output>
<negative>no gradients, no shadows, no textures, no text, no watermark.</negative>
notes: Geometric minimalism is what makes an avatar read as 'design tool' (Linear, Notion, Figma community avatars).
Pattern inspired by Flat Design movement and modern app icon conventions.
antiPatterns: Do not use gradients. Flat is the point.
Do not add more than 5 shapes. Complexity is the enemy of minimal.

prompts.title: Instagram carousel template pack
prompts.when: You want a 5 to 10 slide Instagram carousel on a single topic with consistent visual language.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an Instagram carousel designer who produces 1080x1350 portrait slides in a consistent system.</role>
<task>Design a {{n_slide}}-slide Instagram carousel on the topic: {{topic}}.</task>
<slide_1_role>Hook slide: large bold text headline, supporting visual element, no detail.</slide_1_role>
<slide_2_to_n-1_role>Content slides: one idea per slide, a small numbered indicator in the corner.</slide_2_to_n-1_role>
<slide_n_role>CTA slide: call to action, brand handle {{handle}}, save/share prompt.</slide_n_role>
<visual_system>
- One primary brand color: {{brand_color}}.
- One accent color for emphasis: {{accent_color}}.
- Typography: single sans-serif family, 3 sizes (headline, body, caption).
- Same background treatment across all slides (solid, gradient, or photographic).
- Same composition grid across all slides.
</visual_system>
<text_in_image>All literal text inside double quotes. Font weight and color specified per slide. No extra text beyond what is specified.</text_in_image>
<output>{{n_slide}} separate 1080x1350 PNGs, or one 1080x(1350*n_slide) vertical strip with thin dividers.</output>
<negative>no watermark, no off-brand color, no more than one typeface family.</negative>
notes: Carousel convention: one idea per slide beats dense multi-point slides. The grid system stops slides from looking like a random collection.
Youmind Instagram post references: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/social-media-post.
antiPatterns: Do not vary the visual system across slides. Consistency is the genre.
Do not put more than 30 words on a content slide.

prompts.title: Twitter / X header banner
prompts.when: You need a 1500x500 Twitter/X header that frames a circular profile picture on the lower-left.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a social media banner designer who designs around the avatar cutout zone.</role>
<task>Design a Twitter/X header at exactly 1500x500 pixels for the account {{handle_or_brand}}.</task>
<avatar_zone>Reserve the lower-left quadrant (roughly 400x400) for the circular profile picture. No important content there.</avatar_zone>
<composition>Subject or visual hero on the right two-thirds. Asymmetric, left-heavy negative space.</composition>
<safe_zone>All critical text and visual elements must stay within the central 1500x400 strip to avoid mobile crop.</safe_zone>
<style>{{style: minimal flat geometric | cinematic photo | illustrated scene | gradient abstract | brand pattern}}.</style>
<palette>Brand colors {{brand_color_1}}, {{brand_color_2}}. Background should not be pure white or pure black.</palette>
<optional_text>If including a tagline, place it on the right third only and limit to {{n_words}} words.</optional_text>
<output>1500x500 PNG, exact dimensions.</output>
<negative>no watermark, no auto-emoji, no important element in lower-left avatar zone.</negative>
notes: The avatar-zone rule is the most-skipped constraint. Designers fill the lower-left and the user's profile picture gets covered.
Twitter/X header spec: 1500x500 with center safe zone for mobile crop, documented at https://help.twitter.com/en/managing-your-account/profile-header-photo-guidelines.
antiPatterns: Do not place text or key visuals in the avatar zone.
Do not design only for desktop. Half the views are on mobile.

prompts.title: TikTok vertical thumbnail
prompts.when: You want a 9:16 vertical TikTok thumbnail with a strong subject and a 1 to 3 word headline.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a TikTok thumbnail designer who treats the canvas as a 3-second attention war.</role>
<task>Design a vertical 9:16 TikTok thumbnail for the video topic {{topic}}.</task>
<hero>One main subject takes 60 percent of frame height, placed in the upper-center or right-third per rule of thirds.</hero>
<headline>1 to 3 words max, set in bold condensed sans-serif, placed at top-center or bottom-left, outlined or drop-shadowed for readability.</headline>
<expression>The subject's facial expression must be exaggerated: {{emotion: shocked | laughing | mind-blown | suspicious | disgusted}}. Eyes wide, mouth open.</expression>
<color>High contrast between subject and background. Use one punchy accent color {{accent_color}} on the headline.</color>
<background>Blurred or simplified to keep eyes on the subject. Avoid clutter.</background>
<output>1080x1920 PNG, exact 9:16.</output>
<negative>no watermark, no more than 3 words of headline, no realistic-looking AI skin texture, no UI elements.</negative>
notes: TikTok thumbnail convention: exaggeration + 3-word max headline + subject-dominant framing is the genre.
Youmind TikTok / short-form thumbnail references: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/social-media-post.
antiPatterns: Do not write a full sentence as a headline.
Do not make the subject small. The whole point is up-close.

prompts.title: YouTube clickable thumbnail
prompts.when: You want a 1280x720 YouTube thumbnail that survives the recommended-video sidebar at 246x138.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a YouTube thumbnail designer who works at thumbnail-of-thumbnails scale.</role>
<task>Design a 1280x720 YouTube thumbnail for the video titled {{video_title}}.</task>
<subject>One person or one object filling 50 to 70 percent of the canvas, with exaggerated expression or motion blur.</subject>
<headline>2 to 4 word hook in bold sans-serif, white with thick black outline, top-right or bottom-third. High contrast.</headline>
<background>Clean, blurred, low detail. No clutter, no text smaller than the headline.</background>
<color>3 colors maximum. Pick one dominant background, one subject color, one accent for the text. High contrast between all three.</color>
<thumbnail_test>Confirm the subject is recognizable at 246x138 (the recommended-video sidebar size). No small details that disappear at scale.</thumbnail_test>
<output>1280x720 PNG, exact dimensions.</output>
<negative>no watermark, no 'clickbait' red arrows, no more than 4 words of headline.</negative>
notes: YouTube thumbnail convention: the 246x138 sidebar test forces you to keep subjects large and bold. This is the rule that separates high-CTR thumbnails from wallpaper.
YouTube creator resources and youmind YouTube thumbnail references: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/youtube-thumbnail.
antiPatterns: Do not add small details. They vanish at sidebar size.
Do not exceed 4 words on the headline.

prompts.title: Viral quote card design
prompts.when: You want a quote card for X, Instagram, or LinkedIn with a single sentence and a strong visual.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a quote card designer who treats typography as the hero.</role>
<task>Design a square quote card for the quote: {{quote}}.</task>
<attribution>Attribution below quote: {{author_or_handle}}.</attribution>
<typeface>One display serif or one bold sans-serif. Pick {{choice: serif with character | modern sans | handwritten script | condensed display}}. Do not mix.</typeface>
<hierarchy>Quote at largest size, attribution at 1/4 the size, brand handle in corner at 1/8 size.</hierarchy>
<background>{{bg_choice: solid pastel | gradient | photographic with low opacity overlay | paper texture | hand-painted wash}}.</background>
<accent>One decorative element: {{accent: underline | period dot | pull-quote mark | thin rule | small icon}}. Single instance only.</accent>
<output>1080x1080 PNG, square.</output>
<negative>no more than one typeface, no more than one accent, no watermark, no border ornaments.</negative>
notes: Quote cards fail when designers add too many typefaces and decorations. One face, one accent, one rule.
Youmind quote card pattern references: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/social-media-post.
antiPatterns: Do not mix serif and sans on the same card.
Do not add drop shadows to the quote. Use color or size contrast instead.

prompts.title: Podcast cover art
prompts.when: You want a 3000x3000 podcast cover that reads at 140x140 on Spotify and Apple Podcasts.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a podcast cover art designer who designs at thumbnail scale first.</role>
<task>Design a podcast cover at 3000x3000 for the show {{show_name}} on the topic {{topic}}.</task>
<hero>Show name as the dominant typographic element, set in a typeface that conveys {{tone: serious | playful | mysterious | warm | bold}}.</hero>
<visual>One supporting visual: {{choice: abstract geometric | iconic object | character portrait | photographic scene | brand mark}}. Placed in upper third or behind the type.</visual>
<palette>{{n}} colors max. Background must contrast with the type at 100 percent.</palette>
<small_scale_test>The show name must be readable when the cover is shrunk to 140x140. No fine detail, no small subtitle text.</small_scale_test>
<layout>Optional subtitle in 1/4 size under the show name. Optional season/episode label in corner at 1/6 size.</layout>
<output>3000x3000 PNG, square, exact.</output>
<negative>no more than 2 typefaces, no small text that disappears at thumbnail scale, no watermark, no Apple/Spotify logos.</negative>
notes: Podcast cover convention: design at 3000x3000 but verify at 140x140. The thumbnail test is what separates covers that pop in a queue from covers that vanish.
Apple Podcasts cover guidelines and youmind podcast art patterns: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/social-media-post.
antiPatterns: Do not put a long show name in a tiny typeface. If the name is long, shorten it.
Do not use a low-contrast color combo. The cover must pop in a dark-mode queue.

prompts.title: Event promo poster
prompts.when: You want a 1080x1350 event promo poster with date, time, location, and one hero visual.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an event poster designer who balances information density with one striking visual.</role>
<task>Design an event promo poster for: {{event_name}}. Date: {{date}}. Time: {{time}}. Location: {{location}}. Lineup or speakers: {{lineup}}.</task>
<hero_visual>One striking image filling 60 percent of the canvas: {{visual_choice: abstract gradient | photographic scene | illustrated character | typographic expression | product render}}.</hero_visual>
<typography_hierarchy>Event name largest, then date and time, then location, then lineup as small caps. Each on its own line with clear spacing.</typography_hierarchy>
<palette>{{brand_colors}} plus 1 accent. Background must contrast with text.</palette>
<cta>Optional RSVP line at bottom: {{cta_text}}.</cta>
<output>1080x1350 PNG (Instagram portrait).</output>
<negative>no watermark, no more than 4 typefaces, no more than 5 text blocks.</negative>
notes: Event posters succeed when hierarchy is strict. Each text block gets one line.
Youmind poster/flyer references: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/poster-flyer.
antiPatterns: Do not bury the date in tiny text.
Do not use a busy photo background. The text must dominate.

prompts.title: Before/after comparison frame
prompts.when: You want a 1:1 before/after comparison for a transformation, recipe, or process reveal.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a comparison-frame designer who lays out two equal panels with one consistent rule.</role>
<task>Design a before/after comparison frame for {{subject}}: '{{before_state}}' vs '{{after_state}}'.</task>
<layout>Vertical or horizontal split exactly 50/50, divided by a thin {{divider_color}} rule. Each panel labeled 'BEFORE' and 'AFTER' in small caps top-left of each panel.</layout>
<consistency>Same camera angle, same lighting direction, same framing across both panels so the change is the only difference the eye registers.</consistency>
<palette>Neutral background that does not bias the comparison. Same background in both panels.</palette>
<output>1080x1080 square or 1080x1350 portrait, depending on orientation of subject.</output>
<negative>no watermark, no extra text beyond BEFORE / AFTER labels.</negative>
notes: Before/after convention: identical framing across panels is the rule. If the camera moves, the comparison is no longer apples-to-apples.
Youmind comparison-style references: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/social-media-post.
antiPatterns: Do not let the panels use different lighting.
Do not add decorative elements that draw attention away from the change.

prompts.title: Meme template with caption
prompts.when: You want a meme image with classic top/bottom caption text on a simple visual.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a meme designer working in the classic Impact-font top-and-bottom caption tradition.</role>
<task>Create a meme image using the template {{template_choice: dramatic chipmunk | distracted boyfriend | expanding brain | this is fine | custom scene}} for the joke: {{punchline}}.</task>
<top_caption>{{top_caption_text}} in Impact font, white with black stroke, top-center.</top_caption>
<bottom_caption>{{bottom_caption_text}} in Impact font, white with black stroke, bottom-center.</bottom_caption>
<image>Simple, clear, recognizable visual with one focal point. Do not invent new meme formats; pick from the well-known list or describe a custom simple scene.</image>
<output>1080x1080 or 1080x1350 PNG, square or vertical.</output>
<negative>no watermark, no more than 2 lines of text, no AI art look on the visual itself.</negative>
notes: Meme convention: Impact font + white with black stroke + top/bottom caption is the format. Anything else is not a meme in this genre.
Youmind meme / social post references: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/social-media-post.
antiPatterns: Do not mix two meme formats. Pick one.
Do not use a fancy typeface. Impact is the standard.

prompts.title: Twitter single-image post
prompts.when: You want a single 1600x900 image to attach to a tweet, with a clean visual that survives in-feed at small scale.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a tweet-attach image designer optimizing for in-feed legibility.</role>
<task>Design a single 1600x900 image for the tweet text: {{tweet_text}}.</task>
<composition>Single focal subject or single statement visual. No grid, no collage.</composition>
<readability>No text smaller than 24pt at 1600x900. If including a short quote, place it in the upper or lower third.</readability>
<palette>{{brand_color}} with one accent. High contrast.</palette>
<crop>Visual should still read at 600x337 (the in-feed thumbnail size).</crop>
<output>1600x900 PNG, 16:9.</output>
<negative>no watermark, no busy grid, no more than 12 words of in-image text.</negative>
notes: Twitter single-image convention: single focal statement beats a collage. In-feed legibility is the constraint.
Youmind Twitter / X social references: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/social-media-post.
antiPatterns: Do not create a 3-panel collage. Use a single statement.
Do not put text below 24pt. It will vanish in-feed.

prompts.title: Pinterest vertical pin
prompts.when: You want a 1000x1500 Pinterest pin optimized for the vertical scroll feed.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a Pinterest pin designer who treats the vertical scroll as a one-second impression.</role>
<task>Design a 1000x1500 Pinterest pin for the topic {{topic}}.</task>
<hero>One bold focal visual in the top 60 percent of the canvas. Bottom 40 percent holds the headline and brand.</hero>
<headline>3 to 6 word headline in bold display sans-serif, white on a dark plate, or dark on a light plate, contrast must be high.</headline>
<brand>Optional small brand wordmark in the bottom-right corner at 1/8 the size of the headline.</brand>
<palette>3 colors max. Pick one warm dominant, one neutral, one accent.</palette>
<output>1000x1500 PNG, exact 2:3 vertical.</output>
<negative>no watermark, no more than 2 typefaces, no small text.</negative>
notes: Pinterest convention: 2:3 vertical with top hero and bottom headline is the dominant pattern.
Pinterest pin spec docs and youmind Pinterest references: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/social-media-post.
antiPatterns: Do not put the headline at the very top. The top must be visual.
Do not use a square crop. 2:3 is the standard.

prompts.title: Product launch announcement card
prompts.when: You want a single social card announcing a product launch with date, name, and one tagline.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a product launch card designer who balances hype with restraint.</role>
<task>Design a 1080x1080 product launch announcement card for {{product_name}}.</task>
<hierarchy>Product name largest, then launch date 'AVAILABLE {{date}}', then one-line tagline, then brand mark in corner.</hierarchy>
<visual>Hero shot of the product or an abstract representation of the category, in the upper half. Lower half is for typography.</visual>
<palette>Brand colors only. One accent for the date highlight.</palette>
<typeface>One typeface, two weights max.</typeface>
<output>1080x1080 PNG.</output>
<negative>no more than 4 text blocks, no more than 2 typefaces, no watermark.</negative>
notes: Launch card convention: hierarchy in 4 blocks is the structure. Anything more feels cluttered.
Youmind product launch / announcement patterns: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/product-marketing.
antiPatterns: Do not bury the launch date. It is the action.
Do not add icons. Type only.

prompts.title: Customer testimonial card
prompts.when: You want a testimonial card with a customer photo, quote, name, and brand watermark.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a testimonial card designer who balances a real human face with copy.</role>
<task>Design a 1080x1080 testimonial card for the quote: '{{quote}}' by {{customer_name}} of {{customer_company}}.</task>
<portrait>Photographic or illustrated headshot of the customer on the left 40 percent. Soft natural light, friendly expression.</portrait>
<right_block>The quote in 18 to 24pt serif, attribution '~ {{customer_name}}, {{customer_company}}' below in 12pt sans-serif.</right_block>
<brand>Small brand wordmark in the bottom-right corner.</brand>
<palette>Neutral background {{bg_color}}, text in {{fg_color}}, brand mark in {{brand_color}}.</palette>
<output>1080x1080 PNG.</output>
<negative>no watermark beyond the brand mark, no more than 2 typefaces, no decorative ornament.</negative>
notes: Testimonial convention: portrait + quote + attribution is the structure. Decorations dilute trust.
Youmind testimonial / social proof patterns: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/social-media-post.
antiPatterns: Do not put the quote across two typefaces. One face for the quote.
Do not use a fake-looking stock portrait. Use a friendly illustrated alternative or a clearly photographic style.

prompts.title: Instagram/TikTok story card
prompts.when: You want a 9:16 story card for a 24-hour Instagram or TikTok story with one announcement.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a stories designer who works with the 24-hour ephemerality constraint.</role>
<task>Design a 1080x1920 story card for the message: {{message}}.</task>
<hero_text>The message as the dominant visual element, centered, in bold sans-serif at 60 to 80pt equivalent.</hero_text>
<safe_zones>Top 250px reserved for the platform UI (username, close button). Bottom 350px reserved for reply input.</safe_zones>
<background>{{bg_choice: solid brand color | gradient | photographic with overlay | textured wash}}.</background>
<accent>Optional sticker or hand-drawn arrow pointing to a CTA at the bottom.</accent>
<output>1080x1920 PNG, 9:16 vertical.</output>
<negative>no critical text in top or bottom 250/350 px, no more than 12 words, no watermark.</negative>
notes: Stories convention: the UI safe zones are non-negotiable. The platform covers them.
Instagram Stories safe-zone docs: https://help.instagram.com and youmind story patterns: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/social-media-post.
antiPatterns: Do not put critical text in the top 250px or bottom 350px.
Do not use more than 12 words. Stories are short.

prompts.title: Open Graph link preview card
prompts.when: You want a 1200x630 Open Graph card that displays when someone shares a URL on Slack, Twitter, LinkedIn, or iMessage.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an Open Graph card designer who works at the in-feed preview size.</role>
<task>Design a 1200x630 link preview card for the article titled: {{title}}.</task>
<left_half>Article title in 36 to 48pt bold sans-serif, set on 2 to 3 lines max. Subtitle or category label above it in 14pt caps.</left_half>
<right_half>One supporting visual: hero photo, illustration, or product render. Tied visually to the title.</right_half>
<brand>Small wordmark in the bottom-left corner.</brand>
<palette>Brand colors. Background {{bg_color}}, type {{fg_color}}.</palette>
<output>1200x630 PNG, exact.</output>
<negative>no text smaller than 14pt, no more than 2 typefaces, no watermark beyond the wordmark.</negative>
notes: Open Graph convention: 1200x630 with title on the left and visual on the right is the dominant social-share pattern.
Open Graph spec: https://ogp.me/ and youmind link-preview patterns: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/social-media-post.
antiPatterns: Do not use a square crop. 1200x630 is the standard.
Do not include the full article text. The title is the hook.

prompts.title: Step-by-step instruction diagram
prompts.when: You want a clean horizontal numbered step-by-step diagram for a process, recipe, or how-to.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an instructional designer who turns a process into a 4 to 6 step visual.</role>
<task>Design a horizontal step-by-step infographic for the process: {{process_name}}.</task>
<steps>
Step 1: {{step_1_label}}. Step 2: {{step_2_label}}. Step 3: {{step_3_label}}. Step 4: {{step_4_label}}. Step 5: {{step_5_label}}. (Up to 6 steps.)
</steps>
<layout>Horizontal panel layout, one panel per step, equal width. Numbered circle in top-left of each panel.</layout>
<icon>One simple flat icon per step illustrating the action. Single accent color for icons.</icon>
<caption>3 to 8 word caption per step under the icon. Single sans-serif typeface.</caption>
<palette>Neutral background, one accent color for numbering and icons, one ink color for text.</palette>
<output>1600x900 PNG, 16:9.</output>
<negative>no watermark, no more than 2 typefaces, no decorative ornament.</negative>
notes: Step diagram convention: equal panel widths + numbered circles + single-icon-per-step is the structure that scales.
Youmind instructional / educational references: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/infographic-edu-visual.
antiPatterns: Do not vary panel widths.
Do not include more than 6 steps. Compress or split.

prompts.title: Anatomical cross-section diagram
prompts.when: You want a labeled cross-section diagram of an object, organism, or system for educational use.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a scientific illustrator producing a labeled cross-section diagram.</role>
<task>Design a labeled cross-section of {{subject: human heart | plant cell | combustion engine | flower | layered cake}}.</task>
<cutaway>Show the interior structure exposed by a clean horizontal or vertical cut. Clean cut edge in a distinct stroke.</cutaway>
<labels>Use exactly {{n_labels}} leader lines pointing to distinct parts. Each label in 12pt sans-serif, consistent position on the right side of the subject.</labels>
<style>Clean flat illustration with thin outlines, single light source, subtle shading to indicate depth. Limited to {{palette_n}} colors.</style>
<background>White or cream background, no scenery.</background>
<legend>Optional small legend box at bottom-right naming any symbols used.</legend>
<output>1600x1200 PNG or 1200x1600 PNG, 4:3 or 3:4.</output>
<negative>no watermark, no photorealism, no more than {{n_labels}} labels, no decorative background.</negative>
notes: Cross-section convention: clean cut edge + consistent leader lines + label-on-right is the structure that reads as 'scientific diagram'.
Pattern from scientific illustration tradition and youmind infographic references: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/infographic-edu-visual.
antiPatterns: Do not mix shading styles across labels.
Do not put labels on both sides. Pick one side.

prompts.title: Clean flowchart with branches
prompts.when: You want a flowchart with decision diamonds, action rectangles, and clean arrow flow.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a flowchart designer using standard flowchart symbols.</role>
<task>Design a flowchart for the process: {{process_name}}.</task>
<shapes>
- Rectangle: process step.
- Diamond: decision (yes / no labeled on outgoing arrows).
- Oval: start / end.
- Parallelogram: input / output.
</shapes>
<flow>Arrows are straight 90-degree bends with arrowheads. Single direction, top-to-bottom or left-to-right.</flow>
<labels>Each shape contains a short label, 3 to 6 words. Sans-serif, consistent size.</labels>
<palette>White background, black outlines, single accent color for arrows or decision paths.</palette>
<output>1600x1200 PNG.</output>
<negative>no watermark, no 3D effect, no shadow on shapes, no more than 12 shapes.</negative>
notes: Flowchart convention: standard shapes (rect, diamond, oval) + 90-degree bends is what makes it read as a flowchart and not an art piece.
Pattern from UML and flowchart tradition.
antiPatterns: Do not use diagonal arrows.
Do not use more than 2 typefaces.

prompts.title: Horizontal timeline visual
prompts.when: You want a horizontal timeline with date markers and milestone labels for a history or project roadmap.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a timeline designer who treats the line as the spine of the composition.</role>
<task>Design a horizontal timeline for the topic: {{topic}}.</task>
<spine>A single horizontal rule across the middle of the canvas. {{accent_color}}.</spine>
<milestones>{{n_milestones}} milestones along the line, each with a date above and a short label (3 to 8 words) below.</milestones>
<marker>Each milestone has a single small icon or dot on the line. Alternating above-and-below label placement to avoid crowding.</marker>
<palette>Neutral background, single accent color for the spine and markers, ink color for text.</palette>
<typography>One typeface, two sizes: dates 14pt, labels 18pt.</typography>
<output>2000x800 PNG, 5:2.</output>
<negative>no watermark, no 3D effect, no more than 12 milestones.</negative>
notes: Timeline convention: single spine + alternating above/below labels is the structure that survives density.
Youmind timeline / infographic patterns: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/infographic-edu-visual.
antiPatterns: Do not put every label on the same side.
Do not use curved spines. Straight line is the genre.

prompts.title: Pie or donut chart visual
prompts.when: You want a pie or donut chart with 4 to 6 labeled segments for a share or breakdown visualization.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a data visualization designer who treats the chart as a single statement.</role>
<task>Design a donut chart for the breakdown: {{breakdown_description}}.</task>
<data>
Segment 1: {{label_1}} = {{percent_1}}%.
Segment 2: {{label_2}} = {{percent_2}}%.
Segment 3: {{label_3}} = {{percent_3}}%.
Segment 4: {{label_4}} = {{percent_4}}%.
(Up to 6 segments.)
</data>
<donut>Donut style: 60 percent outer radius, 40 percent inner radius. Center holds one short headline number or label.</donut>
<palette>Distinct color per segment from a categorical palette. No gradients. White 2-pt stroke between segments.</palette>
<labels>Leader lines from each segment to an external label with name and percent. Labels on the right or alternating sides.</labels>
<output>1200x1200 PNG.</output>
<negative>no watermark, no 3D effect, no exploded slices unless specified, no more than 6 segments.</negative>
notes: Donut chart convention: 60/40 ring ratio + center headline is the pattern that turns a chart into a statement.
Pattern from data visualization best practices.
antiPatterns: Do not use rainbow palettes. Pick a categorical scheme.
Do not put labels inside the donut if there are more than 4 segments.

prompts.title: Comparison table visual
prompts.when: You want a side-by-side comparison table (A vs B) for a product, plan, or option decision.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a comparison-table designer who treats the columns as parallel stories.</role>
<task>Design a side-by-side comparison for {{option_a}} vs {{option_b}} on the dimension {{dimension}}.</task>
<structure>Two columns of equal width, each headed with the option name in a single bold typeface. Subhead: {{dimension}}.</structure>
<rows>{{n_rows}} rows of comparison attributes. Each row has a short label on the left and one value per option.</rows>
<highlight>Optional checkmarks / crosses / color coding per cell to indicate winner or status.</highlight>
<palette>Neutral background, single accent color for the highlight.</palette>
<output>1600x1200 PNG, 4:3.</output>
<negative>no watermark, no more than 8 rows, no more than 2 typefaces.</negative>
notes: Comparison table convention: equal column widths + parallel structure is the rule. Asymmetry reads as bias.
Youmind comparison / versus patterns: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/infographic-edu-visual.
antiPatterns: Do not give one column a wider treatment than the other.
Do not put text below 14pt.

prompts.title: Icon system grid
prompts.when: You want a 3x3 or 4x3 grid of consistent icons for a concept, app, or product feature set.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an icon designer who treats the grid as a system, not a collection.</role>
<task>Design a {{n_x_n}} grid of icons representing {{concept_set}}.</task>
<style>Flat line icons, consistent {{stroke_width}}pt stroke, rounded corners, single accent color, uniform grid sizing. Each icon fits inside an invisible square with 2px padding.</style>
<labels>Each icon has a 3 to 6 word label centered below it in 14pt sans-serif.</labels>
<palette>Neutral background {{bg_color}}, ink color {{ink_color}}, accent {{accent_color}}.</palette>
<output>1200x1200 PNG, square.</output>
<negative>no watermark, no more than 16 icons, no more than 2 typefaces, no decorative ornament.</negative>
notes: Icon grid convention: identical stroke weight + identical padding + uniform grid is the system that makes a collection feel designed.
Pattern from design system references like Material Icons and Phosphor.
antiPatterns: Do not mix stroke widths across icons.
Do not use multiple accent colors.

prompts.title: Map / route illustration
prompts.when: You want a stylized illustrated map or journey route with stops and labels for an explainer.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a stylized map illustrator in the vein of vintage travel poster cartography.</role>
<task>Design a stylized map showing the route from {{start}} to {{end}} via {{stops}}.</task>
<base>Flat illustrated terrain in a single muted palette: {{palette: warm beige + sage + slate + terracotta}}. No photorealism.</base>
<route>A single colored line connecting the stops, with arrows indicating direction. Accent color {{route_color}}.</route>
<stops>{{n}} stops marked by a single icon (circle, pin, or flag) and labeled in 14pt sans-serif.</stops>
<compass>Optional small compass rose in one corner. Optional distance scale.</compass>
<legend>Optional small legend at the bottom explaining icons.</legend>
<output>2000x1200 PNG, 5:3.</output>
<negative>no photoreal satellite imagery, no watermark, no text below 14pt.</negative>
notes: Map convention: flat illustration palette + single accent route line is the style that reads as 'stylized map' and not 'Google Maps screenshot'.
Pattern from vintage travel poster cartography and youmind map references.
antiPatterns: Do not use satellite imagery. Flat is the genre.
Do not use more than 2 typefaces.

prompts.title: Data visualization as poster
prompts.when: You want a single big-statistic poster where one number is the hero and supporting context surrounds it.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a data-poster designer who treats the big number as the hero.</role>
<task>Design a poster communicating the statistic: '{{big_number_statement}}'.</task>
<hero_number>The number or short phrase at 200 to 300pt equivalent, set in a single bold display typeface, occupying 40 to 60 percent of the canvas.</hero_number>
<supporting_facts>3 to 5 short supporting facts, each in 14 to 18pt, placed in margins around the hero.</supporting_facts>
<source>Small source line in 10pt at the bottom: 'Source: {{source}}'.</source>
<palette>Limited to 3 colors: background {{bg_color}}, type {{fg_color}}, accent {{accent_color}}.</palette>
<output>1080x1350 PNG.</output>
<negative>no watermark, no more than 5 supporting facts, no decorative ornament, no chart chrome.</negative>
notes: Big-stat convention: one number as the hero + small source line at the bottom is the structure used by Pew, NYT Upshot, and similar data publishers.
Youmind data viz poster references: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/infographic-edu-visual.
antiPatterns: Do not include more than one hero number.
Do not skip the source line. Even a vague source is required.

prompts.title: Classroom wall poster
prompts.when: You want a wall-poster sized educational illustration: subject hero, large title, brief explanation.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an educational poster designer in the style of a museum classroom exhibit.</role>
<task>Design a 3:4 educational poster about {{subject}}.</task>
<hero>A central illustration of {{subject}} filling 60 percent of the canvas, rendered in detailed flat illustration with subtle shading.</hero>
<title>The subject name as a large bold display title at the top, 72pt equivalent.</title>
<caption>One paragraph of 30 to 60 words explaining the subject, in 18pt body type, placed in the lower third.</caption>
<palette>Warm neutral background {{bg_color}}, type {{fg_color}}, hero illustration uses 5 to 7 colors.</palette>
<output>1500x2000 PNG, 3:4 vertical.</output>
<negative>no watermark, no more than 60 words of body copy, no decorative border.</negative>
notes: Classroom poster convention: hero illustration + title + 1 paragraph is the structure. More text breaks the genre.
Youmind educational poster references: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/infographic-edu-visual.
antiPatterns: Do not write more than 60 words of body copy. Posters are visual.
Do not use a busy photographic background.

prompts.title: Product hero shot on clean background
prompts.when: You want a clean studio hero shot of a single product on a white or color background for an e-commerce listing.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a commercial product photographer working in a softbox-lit studio.</role>
<task>Render a hero shot of the product: {{product_name}}.</task>
<studio>Seamless paper background in {{bg_color}}. Product on the paper with a soft natural shadow.</studio>
<lighting>Two softbox lights: key at 45 degrees camera-left, fill at 30 percent intensity camera-right. White reflector below. No hard shadows.</lighting>
<camera>Eye-level, 85mm equivalent, f/8 for full-product sharpness. Centered framing, product fills 70 percent of canvas.</camera>
<detail>Visible material detail: texture, stitching, labels, branding. No retouching artifacts.</detail>
<output>2048x2048 PNG, square.</output>
<negative>no watermark, no people, no props other than the product, no busy background.</negative>
notes: Product hero shot convention: seamless background + softbox pair + 85mm-equivalent eye-level framing is the industry standard.
Youmind product marketing references: https://youmind.com/prompts/image/product-marketing.
antiPatterns: Do not crop the product. The whole product must be visible.
Do not use dramatic lighting. Studio hero shots are even and clean.

prompts.title: Product worn by a model
prompts.when: You want a clothing or accessory product shown on a model in a lifestyle or studio setting.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an e-commerce model photographer combining a fit reference with lifestyle framing.</role>
<task>Render the {{product_type: shirt | dress | jacket | shoes | bag | hat | glasses | watch}} being worn by a model.</task>
<model>Single model, {{gender_expression}}, age range {{age_range}}, body type described neutrally. Friendly relaxed expression.</model>
<product_focus>The {{product_type}} is the focal point: full silhouette visible, label or logo readable, fabric texture and seams visible.</product_focus>
