antiPatterns: Do not include hands or the cup. The paint alone is the subject after the initial pour.
Do not add music. The paint sound is the audio.

prompts.title: Ceramic pottery on wheel
prompts.when: You need a 15 to 20 second potter working clay on a wheel for a craft documentary or maker reel.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a craft DP capturing a potter working clay on a wheel for a craft documentary.</role>
<scene>A pottery studio. A potter's wheel in the center. Clay, water bowl, ribs, and trimming tools arranged nearby. Soft daylight from a high window.</scene>
<subject>The potter's hands (no face visible, apron visible) shaping a clay vessel on the spinning wheel.</subject>
<motion>Locked medium close-up from the side of the wheel. The potter centers the clay (it spins true), opens the center with thumbs, pulls up the walls with both hands, shapes the exterior with a rib. The whole sequence is one continuous throwing motion.</motion>
<camera>Locked tripod at the side of the wheel, 50mm equivalent, shallow depth of field with the studio soft behind.</camera>
<lighting>Soft daylight from upper window as key. Warm practical light from the studio as fill. The wet clay catches highlights.</lighting>
<audio>The hum of the wheel motor. The wet slap of the clay. The potter's breath. Water dripping. No music.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 18 seconds, craft documentary color, warm and natural.</output>
<negative>no other potters, no face, no text, no music, no cuts, no other pottery in focus.</negative>
notes: Pottery wheel prompts work because the motion is continuous and the result is visually satisfying (a vessel rising from a lump).
Pattern inspired by the Craftsman's Legacy documentary series.
antiPatterns: Do not show the potter's face. Hands-only is the genre.
Do not add music. The wheel hum and clay sounds are the audio.

prompts.title: Candle flame flicker macro
prompts.when: You need a 10 to 15 second macro of a candle flame for a meditation or moody reel.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an ambient DP capturing a single candle flame in extreme macro.</role>
<scene>A single candle on a small dish. The candle is in a dark room. The flame is the only source of light.</scene>
<subject>The flame itself. Photoreal flame dynamics: flicker, sway, base, tip.</subject>
<motion>Static macro. The flame flickers and sways gently throughout. No other motion in the frame. The wax pool around the wick glows softly. Occasional micro-drips fall from the wick.</motion>
<camera>Locked tripod macro, 85mm equivalent with extension, focus locked on the flame.</camera>
<lighting>The flame is the only light source. The surrounding dark room reflects the flame's warm glow on nearby surfaces.</lighting>
<audio>The very faint hiss of the flame. A soft wind. Silence. No music.</audio>
<output>1:1, 24fps, 12 seconds, warm flame color against deep dark.</output>
<negative>no people, no text, no other candles, no music, no other light sources, no rapid cuts.</negative>
notes: Candle flame macros are reliable because the motion vocabulary is simple and the lighting is self-contained.
Pattern inspired by ASMR content and meditation apps like Calm and Headspace.
antiPatterns: Do not include other candles. The single flame is the genre.
Do not add music. The flame sound is the audio.

prompts.title: Sourdough bread scoring bake
prompts.when: You need a 15 to 20 second sourdough scoring and oven shot for a bakery brand or bread doc.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a bakery DP capturing a sourdough scoring and bake for a craft bread brand.</role>
<scene>A bakery kitchen. A scored sourdough boule on a peel. A hot oven behind. Flour dust in the air.</scene>
<subject>The baker's hands (no face visible) scoring the sourdough and loading it into the oven.</subject>
<motion>Locked medium close-up from the side. The baker scores the boule with a lame in three quick slashes across the top. The loaf is transferred to the peel and slid into the oven. Final 5 seconds: through the oven window, the loaf begins to spring and crack along the score lines.</motion>
<camera>Locked tripod, 50mm equivalent, shallow depth of field with the oven interior slightly soft in the final shot.</camera>
<lighting>Warm kitchen light. The oven glow provides warm accent as the loaf enters.</lighting>
<audio>The score of the lame on the crust. The slide of the peel on the oven floor. The hiss of the dough meeting hot stone. No music.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 18 seconds, bakery warm color, deep browns and tans.</output>
<negative>no face, no other bakers, no text, no music, no other loaves, no cuts.</negative>
notes: Bread scoring is achievable because the motion is precise and short. The oven shot hides any AI artifacts in heat haze.
Pattern inspired by bakery brand reels and the King Arthur Baking content.
antiPatterns: Do not show the baker's face. Hands-only is the genre.
Do not add music. The kitchen sounds are the audio.

prompts.title: Dolly push-in dramatic
prompts.when: You need a 15 to 20 second dramatic dolly push-in to a subject for an emotional beat or reveal.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a DP executing a single dramatic dolly push-in on a geared slider.</role>
<scene>A real indoor or outdoor location with a clear forward path for the dolly. Subject positioned at the end of the path.</scene>
<subject>One subject: {{subject_description: a person sitting in a chair, a statue on a pedestal, a single object on a table}}. The subject is the target.</subject>
<motion>The camera begins at a wide framing of the subject and slowly dollies forward on a straight track, ending in a close-up of the subject's face or key feature. The motion is perfectly smooth and constant throughout.</motion>
<camera>Geared dolly slider, 35mm to 85mm equivalent focal length change implied by the push, focus locked on the subject.</camera>
<lighting>Three-point lighting: warm key from upper left, cool fill from upper right, soft rim from behind. No harsh shadows.</lighting>
<audio>Silence except the faint mechanical hum of the dolly motor. A single sustained note underneath if a music bed is desired. No music with lyrics.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 15 seconds, photorealistic, smooth dolly motion.</output>
<negative>no people in background, no text, no music with lyrics, no handheld shake, no cuts, no obstructions in the dolly path.</negative>
notes: The dolly push-in is the most iconic single-camera movement. Models can render it reliably when the path is clear.
Pattern inspired by Roger Deakins and Emmanuel Lubezki single-take features.
antiPatterns: Do not combine the dolly with a pan or tilt. One movement is the discipline.
Do not include a busy background. The path must be clear.

prompts.title: Crane jib rise reveal
prompts.when: You need a 20 to 30 second crane jib rise from a tight subject to a wide environment for an establishing reveal.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a DP planning a single crane jib rise from a tight subject to a wide environment.</role>
<scene>An outdoor location: a small figure at the base, a vast environment revealed as the camera rises. {{location_type: a desert plain, a forest clearing, a city rooftop}}.</scene>
<subject>One figure at the base. {{figure_description}}. As the camera rises, the figure shrinks and the environment grows.</subject>
<motion>The camera starts at eye-level with the figure, framed tight. Over 25 seconds, the crane rises vertically, lifting the camera into the air while the camera tilts down to keep the figure in frame. The final framing is a top-down wide showing the figure as a small element in the vast landscape.</motion>
<camera>Virtual crane with vertical lift of 30+ meters and matching downward tilt. Focal length stays fixed. Locked horizon.</camera>
<lighting>Natural daylight. {{time_of_day}}. The figure catches key light; the environment shows full landscape lighting.</lighting>
<audio>Wind grows louder as the camera rises. Distant environment sounds become audible: birds, traffic, wind. A single sustained synth note.</audio>
<output>2.39:1, 24fps, 25 seconds, photorealistic, smooth crane motion.</output>
<negative>no other figures, no text, no music with lyrics, no rapid crane move, no cuts, no handheld shake.</negative>
notes: Crane rise is the most cinematic establishing move. Models handle vertical lift better than horizontal crane sweeps.
Pattern inspired by Planet Earth aerials and Christopher Nolan opening shots.
antiPatterns: Do not stack a horizontal move with the vertical rise. One axis is enough.
Do not include other characters. The single figure is the scale reference.

prompts.title: Drone coastal flyover
prompts.when: You need a 20 second drone flyover along a coastline for a travel reel or opening shot.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a drone operator planning a coastal flyover for a travel reel.</role>
<scene>A rocky coastline with cliffs, blue ocean, and sandy coves. {{location: Big Sur, Amalfi, Great Ocean Road}}.</scene>
<subject>No people. The coastline is the subject. Tiny boats visible on the water give scale.</subject>
<motion>The drone starts at cliff height looking down at the surf, then banks left and flies along the coastline for 20 seconds, holding a constant altitude and a constant 45-degree downward tilt. The coast scrolls past from right to left.</motion>
<camera>Virtual DJI Inspire equivalent with 3-axis gimbal, 24mm equivalent, deep focus throughout, smooth banking turn at the start.</camera>
<lighting>{{time_of_day: golden hour, blue hour, midday}}. Natural light only. Strong contrast between cliff shadow and sunlit water.</lighting>
<audio>Wind at altitude. Distant surf. The faint hum of the drone motors. No music.</audio>
<output>2.39:1, 24fps, 20 seconds, photorealistic aerial, deep depth of field.</output>
<negative>no birds, no other aircraft, no visible drone shadow, no text, no music, no people in close-up.</negative>
notes: Coastal drone flyover is achievable when the trajectory is a constant line. Banking at the start must be one clean move.
Pattern inspired by YouMind cinematic drone sequences and travel brand reels.
antiPatterns: Do not request rapid acceleration. Constant speed reads as cinematic.
Do not include visible text or landmarks. Generic coasts work better.

prompts.title: Handheld documentary follow
prompts.when: You need a 15 to 20 second handheld follow shot of a person moving through a real environment.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a documentary DP following a single subject through a real environment with a handheld camera.</role>
<scene>A real location: {{location: a hospital corridor, a school hallway, a community kitchen}}. The subject moves through it.</scene>
<subject>One person: {{subject_description}}. Mid-stride, focused on their task, not looking at camera.</subject>
<motion>The camera follows the subject from behind at a constant distance of two meters, handheld. The camera bobs slightly with the operator's steps. The subject walks through the environment, opens a door, and exits the frame. The camera holds on the empty corridor for the final 3 seconds.</motion>
<camera>Handheld, 35mm equivalent, slight natural shake, focus tracking the subject.</camera>
<lighting>Available light only. Mixed color temperature from the real environment.</lighting>
<audio>The subject's footsteps. The environment's ambient sound (beeping machines, distant chatter, kitchen clatter). The operator's breathing. No music.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 18 seconds, observational documentary, natural color.</output>
<negative>no other characters in close-up, no text, no music, no staged looking at camera, no cuts.</negative>
notes: Handheld follow is the documentary workhorse. Models handle the slight bob well when the trajectory is clear.
Pattern inspired by verite documentaries and observational cinema.
antiPatterns: Do not include the subject acknowledging the camera. Observational is the genre.
Do not add music. The environment's sound is the audio.

prompts.title: Steadicam walk through corridor
prompts.when: You need a 20 second steadicam walk through an interior corridor with the floating follow feel.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a steadicam operator walking through a single interior corridor with a smooth floating camera.</role>
<scene>An interior corridor: {{location: a hospital, a hotel, a school, a spaceship}}. The corridor extends into the distance with doors on each side.</scene>
<subject>No characters. The corridor is the subject. The camera glides through it.</subject>
<motion>The camera glides forward through the corridor at constant walking speed for 20 seconds. The motion is butter-smooth (steadicam-stabilized), with no bob. The corridor scrolls past symmetrically. No panning, no tilting.</motion>
<camera>Steadicam, 24mm equivalent, deep focus throughout the corridor, perfectly stabilized motion.</camera>
<lighting>{{location}}-appropriate lighting. Hospital: cool fluorescent. Hotel: warm practicals. School: overhead LED. Spaceship: dim blue-white.</lighting>
<audio>The hum of the corridor's environment (HVAC, distant activity, mechanical systems). Footsteps on the floor. No music.</audio>
<output>2.39:1, 24fps, 20 seconds, photorealistic interior, smooth steadicam motion.</output>
<negative>no characters, no text, no music, no cuts, no visible operator, no handheld shake.</negative>
notes: Empty steadicam corridor shots are achievable because the trajectory is a single straight line.
Pattern inspired by Kubrick's The Shining hotel corridors and Tarkovsky's Stalker.
antiPatterns: Do not include characters walking ahead. The empty corridor is the genre.
Do not request handheld shake. The steadicam is the format.

prompts.title: Tracking side run shot
prompts.when: You need a 15 second side tracking shot of a subject running or moving horizontally.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a tracking vehicle DP running parallel to a moving subject.</role>
<scene>A real environment with a clear horizontal path: {{path_type: a road, a beach, a field, a track}}. The subject moves along the path.</scene>
<subject>One subject moving along the path: {{subject: a runner, a cyclist, a horse, a vehicle}}. Mid-motion.</subject>
<motion>The camera moves horizontally alongside the subject at the same speed, keeping the subject centered in the frame. Both move at constant speed for the full 15 seconds. The background scrolls past. The subject stays centered.</motion>
<camera>Virtual tracking rig, 50mm equivalent, locked on the subject, deep focus.</camera>
<lighting>{{time_of_day}}. Natural light from the side.</lighting>
<audio>The subject's movement sounds (footfalls, tires, hooves). Wind. Distant environment. No music.</audio>
<output>16:9, 60fps for moving subjects, 15 seconds, photorealistic, smooth tracking.</output>
<negative>no other moving subjects, no text, no music, no visible tracking vehicle, no cuts, no camera speed mismatch.</negative>
notes: Side tracking is achievable because the model can hold the subject centered against a scrolling background.
Pattern inspired by car commercial tracking shots and Nike running brand reels.
antiPatterns: Do not include other moving subjects in the same shot. One is enough.
Do not request the camera to lead or trail the subject. Side-by-side is the format.

prompts.title: 360 orbit around subject
prompts.when: You need a 15 to 20 second 360 orbit around a static subject for a product, character, or scene reveal.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a DP executing a perfect 360 orbit around a static subject.</role>
<scene>An environment with the subject at center: {{scene_type: a hero character on a pedestal, a product on a stage, a campfire in a clearing, a magician on stage}}.</scene>
<subject>The subject at center: {{subject_description}}. The subject remains the focal point throughout.</subject>
<motion>The camera orbits the subject at a constant height and constant radius, completing one full 360 degree rotation in 15 to 20 seconds. The subject stays perfectly centered. The background rotates smoothly around them.</motion>
<camera>Geared slider on a circular track, 50mm equivalent, focus locked on the subject, deep focus on the background.</camera>
<lighting>Three-point lighting from the environment. The subject catches key light consistently as the camera moves around.</lighting>
<audio>The subject's ambient sounds (fire crackle, fabric swish, breathing). A single sustained synth note underneath. No music with lyrics.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 18 seconds, photorealistic, smooth orbit motion.</output>
<negative>no cuts, no text, no music with lyrics, no other characters, no visible camera rig, no drone shadow.</negative>
notes: The 360 orbit is achievable because the camera path is a simple circle. Models can hold the subject centered.
Pattern inspired by luxury product ads and dance cinematography.
antiPatterns: Do not change camera height during the orbit. Constant height is the format.
Do not include other moving subjects. The center subject alone.

prompts.title: Pull-out wide reveal
prompts.when: You need a 15 to 20 second pull-out from a tight subject to a wide environmental reveal.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a DP pulling out from a tight subject to reveal the wider environment.</role>
<scene>A real environment where the wider context becomes important. {{environment: a person in a vast desert, a small boat on a huge lake, a single figure on a city rooftop at dawn}}.</scene>
<subject>The subject starts large in the frame and shrinks as the camera pulls back. {{subject_description}}.</subject>
<motion>The camera starts close on the subject (chest or face), framed tight. Over 18 seconds, the camera dollies backward smoothly, revealing the wider environment. By the end, the subject is a small element in a vast landscape. The motion is constant and smooth.</motion>
<camera>Geared dolly moving straight backward, 50mm to 35mm equivalent implied by the pull, focus locked on the subject, deep focus on the background.</camera>
<lighting>{{time_of_day}}. Natural light. The subject remains well-lit as the frame widens.</lighting>
<audio>The subject's immediate sound (breath, fabric). The wider environment's ambient sound grows as the camera pulls back. A single sustained note.</audio>
<output>2.39:1, 24fps, 18 seconds, photorealistic, smooth pull-out motion.</output>
<negative>no text, no music with lyrics, no cuts, no other characters, no handheld shake.</negative>
notes: Pull-out reveals work because the trajectory is clear. The reveal of scale is the emotion.
Pattern inspired by Planet Earth establishing shots and Christopher Nolan intimate-to-vast transitions.
antiPatterns: Do not include a second subject in the wide frame. The scale comparison is the message.
Do not add music. The environmental audio is the score.

prompts.title: Slow pan left-to-right reveal
prompts.when: You need a 15 to 20 second slow horizontal pan across a landscape or large scene for a reveal.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a DP executing a single slow horizontal pan across a wide scene.</role>
<scene>A wide scene that benefits from being scrolled: {{scene: a mountain range, a city skyline, a long table of food, a row of storefronts}}.</scene>
<subject>The scene itself is the subject. No character or single object dominates.</subject>
<motion>Static tripod with a motorized horizontal pan from left to right, completing the pan in 15 to 20 seconds at a constant speed. The camera does not tilt. No cuts. The full scene scrolls across the frame.</motion>
<camera>Locked tripod with geared head, 35mm equivalent, deep focus throughout the pan.</camera>
<lighting>{{time_of_day}}. Natural light only.</lighting>
<audio>The scene's ambient sound (wind, traffic, conversation, birds). A single sustained note underneath if desired.</audio>
<output>2.39:1 or 16:9, 24fps, 18 seconds, photorealistic, smooth pan motion.</output>
<negative>no text, no music with lyrics, no people close-up, no cuts, no handheld shake, no tilted horizon.</negative>
notes: Slow pan is the simplest camera movement and the most reliable. Models hold the horizon and the trajectory well.
Pattern inspired by landscape documentary openings and city skyline reveals.
antiPatterns: Do not combine the pan with a tilt. One axis is enough.
Do not include obvious text. Generic scenes work better.

prompts.title: Slow zoom creep tension
prompts.when: You need a 15 to 20 second slow zoom creep on a subject for tension or unsettling mood.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a DP executing a slow zoom creep for a thriller opening or horror beat.</role>
<scene>An interior or exterior with a single subject in frame: {{scene: a person sitting alone in a diner, an empty chair in an office, a doorway in a hallway}}.</scene>
<subject>The subject at center: {{subject_description}}. The subject is the focus of the slow zoom.</subject>
<motion>The camera starts at a wide framing of the subject and very slowly zooms in (focal length increases) over 15 to 20 seconds. The zoom is barely perceptible at first, gradually tightening to a tight framing of the subject. The motion is purely optical (no dolly).</motion>
<camera>Locked tripod with slow zoom lens, 35mm to 135mm equivalent over the duration, focus locked on the subject.</camera>
<lighting>{{mood_lighting}}. For thriller: cool, dim, single source. For horror: deep shadows. For dramatic: high contrast.</lighting>
<audio>The scene's ambient sound. A single low drone underneath. No music with lyrics.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 18 seconds, photorealistic, smooth slow zoom.</output>
<negative>no text, no music with lyrics, no other characters, no cuts, no dolly movement, no shaky zoom.</negative>
notes: Slow zoom creep is the Hitchcock Vertigo move. It builds tension without obvious camera motion.
Pattern inspired by Psycho, The Shining, and modern thriller trailers.
antiPatterns: Do not combine zoom with dolly. Pure optical zoom is the format.
Do not include music with lyrics. The drone is the audio.

prompts.title: Handheld fight scene short
prompts.when: You need a 15 to 20 second handheld fight scene with two fighters and short choreography.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a fight choreographer and DP staging a short hand-to-hand combat exchange.</role>
<scene>A gritty interior: an abandoned warehouse, a back alley, a parking garage. Concrete, low light, debris on the floor.</scene>
<subject>Two fighters: Fighter A in a dark jacket, Fighter B in a light shirt. Both athletic, late 20s.</subject>
<motion>Handheld camera following the fight. The two fighters exchange a precise sequence: Fighter A throws a jab, Fighter B ducks and counters with a hook, both clinch briefly, Fighter A throws a knee that Fighter B sidesteps, Fighter B shoves Fighter A into a wall. Both reset, breathing hard. The camera shakes with the impacts.</motion>
<camera>Handheld, 35mm equivalent, slight natural shake that intensifies on impacts, shallow depth of field.</camera>
<lighting>Single overhead work light. Deep shadows under the eyes and jaw. No fill. Mixed cool and warm practicals.</lighting>
<audio>Body impacts (controlled, not too loud). The breathing of both fighters. The scrape of feet on concrete. A single sustained bass note. No music.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 18 seconds, gritty action color, slightly desaturated.</output>
<negative>no real violence or gore, no other characters, no text, no music with lyrics, no cuts, no obvious stunt rigging.</negative>
notes: Short choreographed fights (4 to 6 moves) are achievable. Longer sequences break down into incoherence.
Pattern inspired by The Raid, John Wick, and modern action short films.
antiPatterns: Do not stack more than 6 moves. Models break down beyond that.
Do not include blood. Choreography is clean.

prompts.title: Dance single shot continuous
prompts.when: You need a 15 to 20 second single continuous dance shot with one dancer.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a dance DP shooting a single continuous dance sequence for a music video insert.</role>
<scene>A clean studio or stage with a wooden floor and soft overhead light. {{location_type}}.</scene>
<subject>One dancer, mid-20s, athletic, wearing {{outfit_brief}}. {{dance_style}}.</subject>
<motion>Locked medium-wide framing the full body. The dancer performs a continuous choreography: {{choreography_brief: a single spin, a jump, a floor slide, a recovery, a second turn}}. The whole sequence is one continuous flow with no breaks.</motion>
<camera>Locked tripod, 35mm equivalent, deep focus across the floor, no movement.</camera>
<lighting>Even overhead soft panels. Slight warm fill from one side. The dancer's clothing catches the light.</lighting>
<audio>The dancer's footfalls on the floor. The breath rhythm. A single sustained piano note. No music with lyrics.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 18 seconds, dance film color, slight desaturation.</output>
<negative>no other dancers, no text, no music with lyrics, no mirrors showing other dancers, no cuts, no handheld shake.</negative>
notes: Single-shot dance prompts work because the choreography vocabulary is bounded. Pick 4 to 6 distinct moves.
Pattern adapted from YouMind hip-hop dance choreography: https://youmind.com/prompts/video/person-character.
antiPatterns: Do not chain more than 6 moves. Models break the flow beyond that.
Do not include a music bed. The dancer's body is the audio.

prompts.title: Sports play touchdown sequence
prompts.when: You need a 15 to 20 second football play scoring sequence for a sports highlight or brand reel.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a sports DP capturing a single scoring football play from the broadcast angle.</role>
<scene>A real football stadium at night under floodlights. The end zone is visible. The 20-yard line is the line of scrimmage.</scene>
<subject>One quarterback, one receiver, defenders. The play is a designed pass play.</subject>
<motion>Static wide from the broadcast angle. The quarterback takes the snap, drops back 3 steps, sets, throws a pass to the receiver running a post route. The receiver catches the ball in the end zone. They spike the ball in celebration.</motion>
<camera>Locked broadcast-style high wide, 50mm equivalent, deep focus across the field, no movement.</camera>
<lighting>Stadium floodlights. Bright, even, multi-directional. No shadows.</lighting>
<audio>The snap. The crowd noise. The quarterback's shout. The whistle of the ball. The crowd roar on the catch. The spike impact. No music.</audio>
<output>16:9, 60fps, 18 seconds, sports broadcast color, slight saturation.</output>
<negative>no other plays, no text, no slow motion, no music, no announcer, no cuts.</negative>
notes: Single football play is achievable when the play call is simple (post route, catch, score). Avoid complex trick plays.
60fps for realistic motion on the throw and catch.
antiPatterns: Do not include trick plays (flea flickers, reverses). One simple play is enough.
Do not request slow motion unless explicitly desired. Real-time is the format.

prompts.title: Cooking sequence plating final
prompts.when: You need a 15 to 20 second final plating sequence for a recipe video or restaurant brand reel.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a food DP capturing the final plating sequence of a dish in a real kitchen.</role>
<scene>A real restaurant kitchen pass. The plated dish components are ready: the protein, the sauce, the garnish, the plate.</scene>
<subject>The chef's hands (no face visible) performing the final plating sequence.</subject>
<motion>Locked close-up of the empty plate. The chef's hands enter: place the protein (sliced and fanned), drizzle the sauce in a precise line, place three garnish elements with tweezers, add a final dusting, wipe the plate rim with a clean towel, hands withdraw. The whole sequence is one continuous, deliberate motion.</motion>
<camera>Locked overhead at 45 degrees, 50mm equivalent, shallow depth of field with focus on the plate, no movement.</camera>
<lighting>Single hard key from upper left to highlight the dish's surface. Warm rim from the heat lamps. Soft fill from kitchen ambient.</lighting>
<audio>The kitchen ambience. The tap of tweezers. The drizzle of sauce. The cloth on the plate. No music.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 18 seconds, food-magazine color, slight saturation.</output>
<negative>no face, no other chefs, no text, no music, no cuts, no visible kitchen signage.</negative>
notes: Final plating sequences are achievable because each step is a clear action. Models hold plating order well.
Pattern inspired by chef documentary series and high-end restaurant content.
antiPatterns: Do not show the chef's face. Hands-only.
Do not add music. The kitchen sounds are the audio.

prompts.title: Transformation morph sequence
prompts.when: You need a 15 second transformation sequence where a subject visibly changes form or state mid-shot.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a VFX director staging a single visible transformation in real-time.</role>
<scene>A simple environment to keep focus on the transformation: {{setting: a clean studio, a misty forest, a stone chamber}}.</scene>
<subject>One subject: {{subject_brief: a butterfly, a human figure, a flower}}. The subject is the transformation.</subject>
<motion>Locked medium. The subject begins in form A. Over 15 seconds, form A visibly transitions into form B: matter flows, particles rearrange, the shape shifts continuously. By the end, the subject is fully form B. The transformation is continuous and complete.</motion>
<camera>Locked tripod, 50mm equivalent, focus locked on the subject, deep focus on the environment.</camera>
<lighting>{{mood_lighting}}. The lighting should remain consistent across both forms.</lighting>
<audio>A soft whoosh at the start. A continuous shimmer tone throughout. A single resonant note at completion. No music with lyrics.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 15 seconds, photorealistic, smooth transformation.</output>
<negative>no cuts, no text, no other characters, no music with lyrics, no fade-to-black transitions, no visible editing artifacts.</negative>
notes: Transformation morphs work when the form change is simple (one to one). Multiple intermediate states break.
Pattern inspired by Practical Magic, Doctor Strange transformation, and Silent Hill 2 creature reveals.
antiPatterns: Do not request multiple intermediate forms. One form to one form is the limit.
Do not include a person walking through the transformation. The transformation alone is the subject.

prompts.title: Magic trick rapid sequence
prompts.when: You need a 15 second rapid magic trick sequence with three to four tricks for a variety reel.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a magic performance DP capturing a sequence of three close-up magic tricks.</role>
<scene>A clean studio with a small table, dark backdrop, single overhead key light.</scene>
<subject>One magician, mid-30s, dark clothing, expressive hands. The hands are the focus.</subject>
<motion>Locked close-up on the hands and table. The magician performs four tricks in sequence: (1) a coin vanishes from one hand and reappears in the other; (2) a folded paper turns into a small flower; (3) a selected card rises from the deck; (4) the deck explodes into a flurry of cards that all return to a single ordered stack. Each trick is 3 to 4 seconds.</motion>
<camera>Locked overhead close-up, 50mm equivalent, focus locked on the hands, no movement.</camera>
<lighting>Single hard key from above. Deep shadows below the hands. No fill.</lighting>
<audio>The tap of cards. The snap of fingers. The soft gasp of an off-screen audience. The ambient silence between tricks. No music.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 15 seconds, dark magic performance color, deep contrast.</output>
<negative>no face, no other characters, no text, no music, no cuts, no visible props beyond the magic items.</negative>
notes: Rapid magic sequences work because each trick has a clear before/after that models can lock. Five or more tricks breaks.
Pattern inspired by close-up magic YouTube channels and Penn & Teller performance shots.
antiPatterns: Do not chain more than four tricks. Models lose continuity beyond that.
Do not show the magician's face in close-up. Hands-only is the genre.

prompts.title: Sword fight choreography short
prompts.when: You need a 15 to 20 second choreographed sword fight between two fighters for a historical or fantasy short.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a sword fight choreographer staging a single choreographed exchange.</role>
<scene>A {{setting: a stone courtyard, a forest clearing, a castle hallway}}. Open space with a flat ground for the fight.</scene>
<subject>Two fighters: Fighter A in {{armor_style}}, Fighter B in {{armor_style}}. Both armed with steel swords. Late 20s to 30s.</subject>
<motion>Static medium-wide. The two fighters circle briefly, then engage. A precise sequence: Fighter A slashes high, Fighter B parries, Fighter A follows with a thrust, Fighter B deflects and counters with a horizontal slash that Fighter A ducks. They disengage, breathe, and reset their stances. The whole exchange is 15 seconds.</motion>
<camera>Locked tripod, 35mm equivalent, deep focus across the fighting space, no movement.</camera>
<lighting>Natural or practical lighting. {{time_of_day}}. Hard key from one side to catch the sword blades.</lighting>
<audio>The ring of steel on steel. The breathing of both fighters. The scrape of boots. No music.</audio>
<output>2.39:1, 24fps, 18 seconds, historical action color, slightly desaturated.</output>
<negative>no blood, no other characters, no text, no music with lyrics, no cuts, no spectators.</negative>
notes: Sword fight choreography works when the sequence is 4 to 6 moves and ends with a reset. Models handle steel-on-steel audio well.
Pattern inspired by The Duel, The Last Duel, and modern swashbuckler shorts.
antiPatterns: Do not chain more than 6 moves. The choreography collapses beyond that.
Do not include blood or injury. Historical fights are clean.

prompts.title: Real-feel spell cast
prompts.when: You need a 10 to 15 second spell cast sequence for a fantasy short or game cinematic.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a fantasy DP capturing a single spell cast moment for a feature.</role>
<scene>A {{setting: a forest clearing, a stone chamber, a wizard's tower}}. The mage stands at the center.</scene>
<subject>One mage in robes, holding both hands forward as if channeling. Face partially obscured by hood.</subject>
<motion>Static medium-wide. The mage's hands begin to glow. The glow intensifies. A visible sphere of magical energy forms between the hands. The sphere grows brighter. The mage releases it: the sphere shoots forward off-screen. The mage exhales. Hands lower.</motion>
<camera>Locked tripod, 35mm equivalent, focus locked on the mage, no movement.</camera>
<lighting>Ambient environment base. The spell glow becomes the dominant light source from the second the sphere forms. The mage's face catches the glow.</lighting>
<audio>The hum of building magical energy. The whoosh of the release. The mage's breath. A deep resonant tone. No music with lyrics.</audio>
<output>2.39:1, 24fps, 12 seconds, photorealistic fantasy, magical glow effects.</output>
<negative>no other characters, no text, no music with lyrics, no dialogue, no cuts, no visible spell books.</negative>
notes: Single spell cast is achievable because the motion progression is clear: glow, form, release. Models can hold the build.
Pattern inspired by Harry Potter, Doctor Strange, and the Wheel of Time channeling scenes.
antiPatterns: Do not stack multiple spells in one shot. One is the discipline.
Do not include an opponent being hit. The spell traveling off-screen is enough.

prompts.title: Mecha suit mid-action assembly
prompts.when: You need a 15 second mecha suit mid-action assembly sequence for a sci-fi action or game cinematic.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a VFX-heavy sci-fi DP capturing a mecha suit assembling onto a pilot mid-action.</role>
<scene>An industrial combat zone. Concrete, smoke, debris, dim emergency lighting.</scene>
<subject>One pilot in motion, athletic, wearing a partial suit that is assembling piece by piece from nanobots or mechanical panels.</subject>
<motion>Static medium-wide. The pilot is running forward. As they run, mechanical panels snap onto their legs, then arms, then torso, then helmet. Each panel locks with a mechanical click. By the end, the pilot is fully suited in a complete mecha armor, still running. The assembly takes the full 15 seconds.</motion>
<camera>Tracking rig moving backward to keep the pilot in frame as they run, 35mm equivalent, slight handheld shake.</camera>
<lighting>Dim emergency lighting. The suit assembly emits small blue-white flashes at each lock point. By the end, the suit's eye slit glows red.</lighting>
<audio>The mechanical clicks of panels locking. The pilot's breath and footsteps. The hum of the suit's systems powering up. No music.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 15 seconds, photorealistic mecha, industrial cool palette with accent glows.</output>
<negative>no other characters, no text, no other mechs, no music with lyrics, no cuts, no obvious CGI seams.</negative>
notes: Mecha assembly sequences work because each panel lock is a discrete event. Models can render 4 to 5 discrete locks.
Pattern inspired by Iron Man suit-up sequences and Pacific Rim drift synchronization.
antiPatterns: Do not request more than 5 lock points. Models break continuity beyond that.
Do not show the full suit in one panel. The build is the format.

prompts.title: Couple dance waltz continuous
prompts.when: You need a 15 to 20 second couple waltz sequence for a wedding video or romance short.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a romance DP capturing a continuous waltz sequence for a wedding or romance short.</role>
<scene>A real ballroom or grand hall: chandeliers, marble floor, soft warm light, painted ceiling.</scene>
<subject>One couple: he in a dark suit, she in a flowing white dress. Both mid-20s to 30s. They dance a classical waltz.</subject>
<motion>Locked wide. The couple dances a continuous waltz across the floor: turn, turn, turn, dip, recovery, continue turning. The motion is fluid and elegant. The dress flows and swings with each turn. They rotate fully around the floor and end at center frame in a final pose.</motion>
<camera>Locked tripod at the center of the room, 50mm equivalent, deep focus across the floor, no movement.</camera>
<lighting>Warm chandelier light from above. Soft practical sconces on the walls. The dress catches the chandelier light.</lighting>
<audio>The soft classical piano or string waltz melody. The couple's breath. The swish of the dress. No voice.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 18 seconds, romantic warm color, soft halation.</output>
<negative>no other couples, no text, no guests, no music with lyrics, no cuts, no handheld shake.</negative>
notes: Couple waltz is achievable because the motion vocabulary is well-defined (turn, turn, turn). Models hold the rhythm.
Pattern inspired by Pride and Prejudice, La La Land opening, and classical wedding videos.
antiPatterns: Do not include other dancers. The couple alone is the genre.
Do not request a contemporary dance. The waltz is the format.

prompts.title: Timelapse day to night sky
prompts.when: You need a 15 to 20 second timelapse from day to night for a nature reel or opening transition.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a nature timelapse DP capturing the transition from daylight to night in a single static frame.</role>
<scene>A wide outdoor scene: a landscape with a clear horizon. {{landscape: a mountain range, a forest line, a city skyline}}.</scene>
<subject>The sky and its color progression is the subject. The landscape is the static anchor.</subject>
<motion>Static locked wide. The sky transitions from midday blue to afternoon gold to sunset orange to twilight purple to night black. Clouds drift continuously. Stars appear in the final 5 seconds. The transition is smooth and continuous over 20 seconds.</motion>
<camera>Locked tripod, 24mm equivalent, deep focus across the landscape, no movement.</camera>
<lighting>The timelapse transition provides the lighting. The landscape darkens progressively as the sky darkens.</lighting>
<audio>Daytime birds fading. Evening crickets emerging. Distant wind. The hush of nightfall. No music.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 20 seconds, timelapse speed, photorealistic, deep color shift.</output>
<negative>no people, no text, no rapid cuts, no handheld shake, no music, no visible camera battery change.</negative>
notes: Day-to-night timelapse is one of the most achievable timelapse formats because the sky color shift is well-documented.
Pattern inspired by BBC Earth timelapses and Astronomical calendars.
antiPatterns: Do not include people or animals. The sky is the subject.
Do not request visible moon movement. Stars only at the end.

prompts.title: Slow motion water droplet
prompts.when: You need a 10 to 15 second slow-motion water droplet shot for a beverage ad or sensory reel.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a macro slow-motion DP capturing a single water droplet for an ad or sensory reel.</role>
<scene>A clean dark backdrop. A single dropper positioned above a glass surface. Soft studio light.</scene>
<subject>A single water droplet falling from the dropper, hitting the surface, and creating a crown splash.</subject>
<motion>Locked macro. The droplet forms at the dropper tip, hangs for a moment, releases, falls. Hits the surface and creates a perfect crown splash with secondary droplets. The crown collapses and ripples spread. The whole sequence is in extreme slow motion (about 8x slower than real time).</motion>
<camera>Locked macro, 100mm equivalent with extension, focus locked on the impact point, no movement.</camera>
<lighting>Single hard backlight to make the droplet glow. Subtle side light to catch the crown details. The dark backdrop isolates the droplet.</lighting>
<audio>Muffled by slow motion: the soft release of the droplet, the gentle impact, the tinkle of secondary droplets. Real audio pitch-shifted down.</audio>
<output>16:9, 240fps for slow motion, 12 seconds, photorealistic, deep slow-motion detail.</output>
<negative>no text, no music, no other objects, no people, no cuts, no visible dropper mechanism.</negative>
notes: Water droplet slow motion is achievable because the physics is well-documented. Models handle crown splashes well.
Pattern inspired by beverage ads and macro photography reels.
antiPatterns: Do not include multiple droplets. One is the genre.
Do not add music. The slow-motion water sound is the audio.

prompts.title: Hyperlapse urban walk
prompts.when: You need a 15 second hyperlapse walking through an urban environment for a travel reel.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a hyperlapse DP walking through an urban environment with a stabilized camera.</role>
<scene>An urban environment with a clear forward path: {{path: a city street, a market alley, a covered arcade}}.</scene>
<subject>No characters in the foreground. The environment and its inhabitants moving past are the subject.</subject>
<motion>Hyperspeed forward motion through the environment. People in the environment blur past as streaks. The environment itself stays sharp and continuous (no stutter). The motion is smooth and continuous for 15 seconds, equivalent to about 100 meters of walking compressed.</motion>
<camera>Locked forward orientation, 24mm equivalent, stabilized to remove vertical bob (only forward motion remains), deep focus.</camera>
<lighting>Available urban light. {{time_of_day}}. Mixed color temperature as the environment provides.</lighting>
<audio>Compressed urban sound: people, footsteps, vehicles, all sped up and pitched up to match the visual hyperlapse.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 15 seconds, hyperlapse speed, photorealistic, smooth forward motion.</output>
<negative>no first-person visible hands, no text, no music, no cuts, no handheld bob, no selfie framing.</negative>
notes: Hyperlapse is achievable when the environment is generic and the camera motion is purely forward. Twists or turns break the format.
Pattern inspired by YouTube travel hyperlapses and Instagram travel reels.
antiPatterns: Do not turn the camera mid-shot. Forward motion only.
Do not include a visible selfie stick or arms. Pure forward motion.

prompts.title: Freeze frame on impact
prompts.when: You need a 10 second freeze-frame impact moment for a sports highlight or product reveal.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a VFX DP capturing a single freeze-frame moment in an otherwise live scene.</role>
<scene>A real environment with motion: {{scene: a glass shattering, a water balloon bursting, a flower being hit, a wine glass exploding}}.</scene>
<subject>The object in mid-action: {{object}}. Suspended mid-event with detail visible.</subject>
<motion>Static locked frame. The motion begins in real time. At the moment of impact (3 to 4 seconds in), the motion freezes completely. The frozen state is held for the remainder of the shot, with all debris, droplets, and fragments suspended in mid-air. The freeze is sharp, with all details visible.</motion>
<camera>Locked tripod, 50mm equivalent, deep focus on the suspended subject.</camera>
<lighting>{{scene}}-appropriate lighting. At the freeze moment, the lighting remains as it was, illuminating the suspended detail.</lighting>
<audio>The motion sound for the first 3 seconds. Then complete silence for the rest of the shot.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 10 seconds, photorealistic, sharp freeze detail.</output>
<negative>no text, no music, no other characters, no cuts, no slow motion before the freeze, no visible VFX markers.</negative>
notes: Freeze-frame impacts work because the model can render the suspended state once and hold it. The transition from motion to freeze is the only tricky part.
Pattern inspired by The Matrix bullet time, high-speed photography, and modern VFX reels.
antiPatterns: Do not include multiple impact events. One freeze is the format.
Do not have characters move after the freeze. The world stops.

prompts.title: Motion blur urban rush
prompts.when: You need a 10 to 15 second motion-blur rush through an urban environment for a mood reel or ad.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a DP capturing a motion-blur rush through a busy urban environment.</role>
<scene>A busy urban street or transit hub: {{location: a Tokyo crosswalk, a London tube station, a Mumbai train platform}}.</scene>
<subject>The crowd and the architecture are the subjects. No single character stands out.</subject>
<motion>Static wide with a slow shutter effect. People move through the frame, leaving motion blur trails. The static architecture stays sharp. The contrast between blurred people and sharp buildings is the visual hook. The motion continues for the full shot.</motion>
<camera>Locked tripod, 24mm equivalent, simulated slow shutter (1/8 sec), deep focus.</camera>
<lighting>Available urban light. The slow shutter gathers ambient light and renders as motion blur on moving subjects.</lighting>
<audio>The compressed rush of the crowd. Footsteps layered. Distant city hum. No music.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 12 seconds, urban motion blur, slightly desaturated.</output>
<negative>no close-up subjects, no text, no music, no people stopping, no cuts, no handheld shake.</negative>
notes: Motion-blur rush works because the static architecture gives the model an anchor while blurred people pass.
Pattern inspired by long-exposure street photography and modern transit time-lapses.
antiPatterns: Do not include close-up subjects. The blur effect needs distance.
Do not request music. The urban rush is the audio.

prompts.title: Flower blooming timelapse
prompts.when: You need a 15 to 20 second flower blooming timelapse for a wellness reel or product intro.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a botanical DP capturing a flower blooming timelapse against a clean background.</role>
<scene>A single flower bud on a stem against a soft black or grey backdrop. Studio lighting.</scene>
<subject>The flower bud opening into a full bloom. {{flower_type: a rose, a lily, a daisy, a tulip}}.</subject>
<motion>Static locked close-up. The bud is closed at the start. Over 18 seconds, the petals slowly unfurl and open outward. The bloom reaches full expansion by the end. The motion is continuous and smooth, equivalent to about 12 hours of real bloom time compressed.</motion>
<camera>Locked macro, 85mm equivalent, focus locked on the bud, no movement.</camera>
<lighting>Single soft key from upper left. The petals catch the light progressively as they open.</lighting>
<audio>A soft whoosh of wind. A single sustained piano note. No music with lyrics.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 18 seconds, timelapse speed, photorealistic botanical color.</output>
<negative>no people, no text, no other flowers, no rapid cuts, no music with lyrics, no visible stem beyond what's natural.</negative>
notes: Flower blooming timelapses work because the bloom motion is well-documented in nature documentaries.
Pattern inspired by BBC Green Planet and The Private Life of Plants.
antiPatterns: Do not include multiple flowers. One is the genre.
Do not request human interaction. The flower is the subject.

prompts.title: Traffic light trails night
prompts.when: You need a 15 second traffic light trails shot for a city reel or transition.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a cityscape DP capturing a long-exposure traffic light trails shot at night.</role>
<scene>A city intersection at night. Headlights and taillights from multiple lanes of traffic. Skyscrapers on the corners.</scene>
<subject>The light trails from the traffic are the subject. The buildings are the static anchors.</subject>
<motion>Static locked wide. Cars move through the intersection leaving streaks of red (taillights) and white (headlights). The trails overlap and weave. The shot holds for 15 seconds, accumulating a dense pattern of light trails across the intersection.</motion>
<camera>Locked tripod on a stable vantage, 24mm equivalent, simulated long exposure, deep focus.</camera>
<lighting>Practical city lighting only. Streetlamps. Building lights. The traffic trails are the dominant visual element.</lighting>
<audio>The compressed urban traffic sound. Multiple engines, tires, horns. Sped up slightly to match the long-exposure aesthetic.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 15 seconds, long-exposure color, deep contrast.</output>
<negative>no people in close-up, no text, no music, no rapid cuts, no handheld shake, no visible tripod.</negative>
notes: Traffic light trails are achievable because the architecture is static and the trails are simple light streaks.
Pattern inspired by long-exposure city photography and urban time-lapses.
antiPatterns: Do not include pedestrians in close-up. They break the long-exposure illusion.
Do not add music. The traffic sound is the audio.

prompts.title: Bullet time slow motion rotation
prompts.when: You need a 10 second bullet-time rotation around a subject in slow motion for an action or dance moment.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a VFX DP capturing a single bullet-time rotation around a subject in slow motion.</role>
<scene>A clean studio or stage with the subject at center. {{environment_description}}.</scene>
<subject>One subject: {{subject: a person mid-jump, a dancer mid-spin, a water droplet mid-air}}. The subject is suspended mid-action.</subject>
<motion>The camera orbits 360 degrees around the subject while the subject moves in extreme slow motion. The orbit is constant and smooth. By the end, the camera has returned to the starting angle. The subject's motion is completed within the rotation.</motion>
<camera>Virtual array of cameras firing sequentially (or equivalent single-camera orbital rig), 50mm equivalent, focus locked on the subject.</camera>
<lighting>{{mood_lighting}}. The lighting should remain consistent as the camera orbits.</lighting>
<audio>Silence except for a single sustained note. The slow-motion audio is muffled and deep.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 10 seconds, photorealistic, smooth bullet-time rotation.</output>
<negative>no visible camera rig, no text, no music with lyrics, no cuts, no multiple subjects, no first-person visible.</negative>
notes: Bullet-time prompts work because the orbital trajectory is a perfect circle. Models handle the arc well.
Pattern inspired by The Matrix bullet time, modern dance cinematography, and product 360s in slow motion.
antiPatterns: Do not include multiple subjects. One is the discipline.
Do not add music. The muffled slow-motion audio is the score.

prompts.title: Speed ramp from slow to fast
prompts.when: You need a 15 second speed ramp from slow motion to real time for a sports or dance moment.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a DP planning a single speed ramp from slow motion to real time.</role>
<scene>A real environment with motion: {{scene: a runner starting a race, a surfer dropping into a wave, a dancer beginning a spin}}.</scene>
<subject>The subject performing the action. {{subject_description}}.</subject>
<motion>Static or smooth tracking frame on the subject. The motion begins in extreme slow motion (4x slower than real time). Over 15 seconds, the speed gradually accelerates until it reaches real time. By the end, the action is moving at normal speed. The transition is smooth and continuous.</motion>
<camera>{{camera_choice: locked, tracking, dolly}}. Whatever fits the subject. Smooth throughout.</camera>
<lighting>{{scene}}-appropriate lighting.</lighting>
<audio>The audio pitch shifts down in slow motion and gradually returns to normal pitch as the speed ramp completes. Real ambient sound throughout.</audio>
<output>16:9, 60fps for slow motion, 15 seconds, photorealistic, smooth speed ramp.</output>
<negative>no text, no music with lyrics, no cuts, no visible speed change other than the smooth ramp.</negative>
notes: Speed ramp prompts work because the model can render the slow motion once and accelerate it. Specify the trajectory.
Pattern inspired by modern sports highlight reels and music video moments.
antiPatterns: Do not include multiple subjects. One is the focus of the ramp.
Do not add a music bed. The ambient audio shift is the score.

prompts.title: Rewind backwards motion
prompts.when: You need a 10 to 15 second rewind sequence where motion plays in reverse for a stylized reveal or transition.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a VFX DP capturing a single motion sequence that plays in reverse.</role>
<scene>A real environment where the action has a clear forward direction: {{scene: a glass shattering, a flower opening, a candle being lit}}.</scene>
<subject>The subject's reverse motion: {{reverse_action_brief}}. The motion is reverse but physically plausible.</subject>
<motion>Static locked frame. The motion begins at the end state and reverses to the start state: {{reverse_motion_brief}}. The reverse motion is continuous and smooth. Liquid flows uphill, smoke descends into the source, debris flies back together. The reverse completes within 15 seconds.</motion>
<camera>Locked tripod, 50mm equivalent, focus locked on the subject, no movement.</camera>
<lighting>{{scene}}-appropriate lighting. The lighting remains consistent as the action reverses.</lighting>
<audio>Real audio played backwards (pitch-flipped). The environmental sound is also reversed.</audio>
<output>16:9, 24fps, 12 seconds, photorealistic, smooth reverse motion.</output>
<negative>no text, no music, no other characters, no cuts, no visible rewind effects, no on-screen rewind indicators.</negative>
notes: Rewind prompts work because the model can render the reverse motion once and play it back. Avoid complex multi-element scenes.
Pattern inspired by Tenet, Doctor Strange time-reversal, and modern sci-fi shorts.
antiPatterns: Do not include multiple elements reversing in different directions. One coordinated reverse is enough.
Do not add music. The reversed audio is the score.

prompts.title: PRISMA systematic review protocol
prompts.when: You are about to start a PRISMA-compliant systematic review and need a written, registerable protocol before searching databases.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a systematic review methodologist trained in PRISMA 2020 reporting standards and Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions methodology (chapter 2, 'Planning and preparation').</role>
<task>Draft the full a-priori protocol for the systematic review below. The protocol will be registered on PROSPERO and used to defend every search and screening decision later.</task>
<review_topic>{{topic}}</review_topic>
<picott_or_pico>
Population (P): {{population}}
Intervention (I) or Exposure: {{intervention_or_exposure}}
Comparator (C): {{comparator_or_default_none}}
Outcome (O): {{primary_outcome}}
Time horizon (T): {{followup_window}}
Setting (S) if relevant: {{setting}}
Study design (T): {{eligible_designs}}
</picott_or_pico>
<eligibility>
Inclusion: {{inclusion_criteria}}
Exclusion: {{exclusion_criteria}}
Language: {{languages}}
Date range: {{date_range}}
Publication status: {{published_only_or_gray_literature_allowed}}
</eligibility>
<method>
1. Frame the review question in PICOTT form and restate it as a single sentence.
2. Specify eligibility criteria with operationalized definitions for each ambiguous term.
3. List the databases to be searched (minimum PubMed/MEDLINE, Embase, Cochrane CENTRAL, plus discipline-specific) and justify each.
4. Provide the full draft search string for the primary database, annotated for line-by-line purpose.
5. Specify grey-literature sources, trial registries, hand-search journals, and citation chasing (forward and backward).
6. Define the screening procedure: title/abstract screening, full-text screening, dual-independent screening, and conflict resolution.
7. Define the data extraction form fields and the pilot round.
8. Specify the risk of bias tool per study design (ROBINS-I for non-randomized, RoB 2 for randomized, ROBINS-E for exposure studies).
9. Specify the synthesis plan: meta-analysis if appropriate, structured narrative synthesis, GRADE for certainty per outcome.
10. Specify deviations protocol: when, how, and where deviations from this protocol will be documented.
</method>
<output_format>
## Title
## Plain-language summary (1 paragraph)
## Review question (PICOTT)
## Background and rationale (focused, 1 page)
## Eligibility criteria
## Information sources and search strategy
## Study selection process
## Data extraction
## Risk of bias assessment
## Synthesis plan
## Certainty of evidence (GRADE)
## Timeline and amendments register
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Every eligible criterion must be operationalized so two screeners can independently apply it.
- The search string must be reproducible: paste-ready, with database, date, and platform noted.
- The synthesis plan must declare effect measure, statistical model (fixed/random), heterogeneity statistic (I-squared, tau-squared), and software.
- Cite the Cochrane Handbook chapter and PRISMA 2020 item for every methodological choice. No invented sources.
</quality_bar>
notes: Anchored to PRISMA 2020 (Page et al., BMJ 2021, https://www.bmj.com/content/372/bmj.n71) and the Cochrane Handbook v6.4 (https://training.cochrane.org/handbook).
Registers cleanly on PROSPERO. Pre-declaring eligibility prevents post-hoc scope creep on hard reviews.
Forces operationalization of criteria, the part most teams skip and later regret.
antiPatterns: Do not write eligibility as vague terms ('relevant studies'). Name the design, population, and outcome.
Do not skip deviations protocol. Reviewers must know how drift will be recorded.
Do not invent references. If PRISMA 2020 item is not in memory, cite the handbook chapter generically and flag uncertainty.

prompts.title: Meta-analysis quantitative synthesis
prompts.when: You have extracted effect estimates from 5+ studies and need a defensible quantitative synthesis plan and result writeup.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a meta-analysis methodologist. You default to the Borenstein, Hedges, Higgins, Rothstein handbook (Introduction to Meta-Analysis, 2009) and follow PRISMA 2020 and the Cochrane Handbook chapter 10 ('Analysing data and undertaking meta-analyses').</role>
<task>Produce the meta-analysis plan and the worked-result writeup for the extracted estimates below.</task>
<inputs>
Effect measure: {{OR | RR | HR | MD | SMD | other}}
Direction of clinically meaningful effect: {{direction}}
Confidence interval method desired: {{95_percent_Wald | Hartung-Knapp-Sidik-Jonkman}}
Pooled dataset (effect, SE or CI, n_study, n_total, study_label):
{{extracted_estimates_table}}
</inputs>
<method>
1. Confirm the effect measure is comparable across studies. Flag any study using a different definition or followup window.
2. Choose fixed-effect vs. random-effects with explicit rationale. Default: DerSimonian-Laird random effects unless N studies is small (less than 5).
3. If N studies is small, propose Knapp-Hartung adjustment and tau-squared estimate (DL, REML, or Paule-Mandel).
4. Generate the forest plot data: pooled estimate, 95 percent CI, I-squared, tau-squared, p_heterogeneity, prediction interval.
5. Plan subgroup analyses a-priori (study design, population subgroup, risk of bias stratum). Add a test for subgroup interaction.
6. Plan sensitivity analyses: leave-one-out, fixed-effect rerun, exclusion of high risk-of-bias studies, exclusion of unpublished studies.
7. Plan publication bias assessment: funnel plot if k >= 10, Egger's test, trim-and-fill. If k < 10, say so and skip rather than fake.
8. Plan GRADE assessment per outcome and how summary of findings table will be built.
9. Produce the result paragraph in PRISMA 2020 style and the forest plot caption.
</method>
<output_format>
## Effect measure and direction
## Model and rationale
## Pooled estimate (with full forest data)
## Heterogeneity (I-squared, tau-squared, H)
## Subgroup analyses
## Sensitivity analyses
## Publication bias
## GRADE summary (per outcome)
## Draft Results paragraph (PRISMA 2020)
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Report I-squared and tau-squared with their CIs when the software supports it.
- Always include a prediction interval when k >= 3; it shows what the next study's effect might be.
- Never invent p-values. If the inputs do not contain N, weight, or SE, state that the calculation cannot be done.
</quality_bar>
notes: Structure follows Borenstein et al. 2009 (https://www.meta-analysis.com/) chapter 4 (effect-size computation) and Cochrane Handbook chapter 10.
Hartung-Knapp-Sidik-Jonkman adjustment is the modern default for small k; simpler methods inflate Type I error.
Forces the prediction interval, which most meta-analyses skip and which is what readers actually want (the next study's plausible effect).
antiPatterns: Do not run fixed-effect when I-squared is high without explicitly defending the choice.
Do not run subgroup analyses not pre-specified, unless flagged as exploratory.
Do not fabricate forest plot numbers. If calculations are missing, say so.

prompts.title: Scoping review per Arksey-O'Malley
prompts.when: You need to map the breadth of literature on a topic rather than answer a single effect-size question (Arksey-O'Malley 2005 + JBI scoping review methodology).
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a scoping review methodologist. You follow Arksey and O'Malley (2005) refined by the JBI Manual for Evidence Synthesis scoping chapter (Peters et al. 2020) and PRISMA-ScR (Tricco et al. 2018).</role>
<task>Design the scoping review protocol and the post-mapping synthesis plan for the topic below.</task>
<topic>{{topic}}</topic>
<purpose>{{why_map_now}}</purpose>
<intended_audiences>{{policy_makers | clinicians | researchers | practitioners}}</intended_audiences>
<method>
1. Apply the PCC framework (Population, Concept, Context) instead of PICOTT. State PCC clearly.
2. Adopt the 6 Arksey-O'Malley stages, extended by JBI: identify research question; identify relevant studies; select studies; chart data; collate/summarize/report; consult stakeholders (optional but recommended).
3. Define the chart form (author, year, country, design, population details, concept measured, context, key findings, gaps).
4. Specify the synthesis type: descriptive numerical summary + thematic analysis + frequency counts of design, year, geography.
5. Address the 4 optional methodological choices: iterative vs fixed search, gray literature depth, stakeholder consultation, and language.
6. Map findings against the original research question and identify research gaps as the principal deliverable.
7. Apply PRISMA-ScR reporting checklist items 1 through 22.
</method>
<output_format>
## PCC framework
## Stage 1: research question
## Stage 2: search strategy
## Stage 3: study selection
## Stage 4: charting data
## Stage 5: synthesis plan (numerical and thematic)
## Stage 6: stakeholder consultation plan
## PRISMA-ScR compliance notes
## Expected mapping output and research gaps
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Distinguish scoping from systematic review: state explicitly that scoping does NOT assess effectiveness or risk of bias.
- A scoping review's main deliverable is a map of evidence and a list of gaps, not a pooled effect.
</quality_bar>
notes: Arksey and O'Malley, 'Scoping studies: towards a methodological framework', Int J Soc Res Methodol 2005 (https://doi.org/10.1080/1364557032000119616).
JBI scoping review chapter (Peters et al. 2020) updated methodology with stakeholder consultation and mandatory PCC framework.
PRISMA-ScR (Tricco et al. 2018, https://doi.org/10.7326/M18-0850) is the reporting checklist. Adopting it prevents reviewer pushback at journals.
antiPatterns: Do not produce an effect-size estimate. That is a systematic review, not a scoping one.
Do not filter by risk of bias. Scoping reviews map, they do not judge.
Do not skip the gaps list. It is the principal product of a scoping review.

prompts.title: Narrative or integrative review
prompts.when: You need a synthesized review article for a clinical or policy audience when systematic methodology would be inappropriate (Sutton, Whittemore-Knafl, or Greenhalgh frame).
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a narrative/integrative review methodologist. Choose one framework and stick to it: Greenhalgh et al. (2017) for narrative review in clinical journals, or Whittemore and Knafl (2005) for integrative reviews in nursing/health science.</role>
<task>Draft the integrative/narrative review plan and the synthesis map for the topic below.</task>
<topic>{{topic}}</topic>
<purpose>{{clinical_update | conceptual_synthesis | thesis_chapter | policy_brief}}</purpose>
<audience>{{target_readers}}</audience>
<method>
1. Declare the chosen framework (Whittemore-Knafl 2005 vs. Greenhalgh 2017). Justify the choice in 3 lines.
2. For Whittemore-Knafl, follow the 5 stages: problem identification, literature search, data evaluation, data analysis, presentation. Specify the data evaluation rubric for diverse designs.
3. Specify search databases, snowball sampling, hand-search, and gray literature scope. Provide a draft PubMed and CINAHL query.
4. Apply inclusion criteria with theoretical saturation as a stop rule, not a fixed count.
5. Use constant comparative method for data analysis: order studies by chronology, by methodology, by finding. Build a matrix.
6. Surface contradictions explicitly rather than smoothing them over.
7. Generate the integrative conclusions: themes, gaps, implications for practice, research, policy.
</method>
<output_format>
## Framework and rationale
## Problem identification and aim
## Search strategy
## Data evaluation rubric
## Analysis approach (matrix design)
## Themes and sub-themes
## Contradictions and tensions
## Conclusion: gaps, implications, call for action
## Draft manuscript outline (IMRaD or structured per journal)
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Be explicit about the non-systematic methodology. Narrative reviews cannot make reproducible effect claims.
- Include at least one matrix (study, year, country, design, concept, finding, limitation) so the reader can audit your claim.
</quality_bar>
notes: Whittemore and Knafl, 'The integrative review: updated methodology', J Adv Nurs 2005 (https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2648.2005.03621.x).
Greenhalgh, Thorne and Malterud, 'Time to challenge the stultifying hegemony of evidence-based healthcare', Lancet 2017.
Narrative reviews are appropriate when the question is interpretive (what is going on?) rather than causal (does X work?).
antiPatterns: Do not claim to have done a systematic search if you did not.
Do not hide disagreement between sources. Name it and explain.
Do not write a textbook chapter. A review is a synthesis with a position.

prompts.title: Search strategy per PRESS-2015
prompts.when: You are building the search string for a systematic review and need a PRESS peer-reviewed ready query.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an information specialist trained in PRESS-2015 (Peer Review of Electronic Search Strategies) and Cochrane Handbook chapter 4 ('Searching for and selecting studies').</role>
<task>Build the multi-database search strategy for the systematic review question below. Provide paste-ready syntax for each named database.</task>
<review_question>{{PICO_in_plain_language}}</review_question>
<key_concepts>{{concept1 | concept2 | concept3}}</key_concepts>
<synonyms>
Concept 1: {{concept1_terms_and_synonyms}}
Concept 2: {{concept2_terms_and_synonyms}}
Concept 3: {{concept3_terms_and_synonyms}}
</synonyms>
<method>
1. Convert the plain-language question to the structure: (Concept1 AND Concept2 AND Concept3) or (PICO mapped).
2. For each concept, compile MeSH/Emtree terms, entry terms, and free text. List both controlled vocabulary and title/abstract variants.
3. Combine within concept with OR, across concepts with AND.
4. Add language, date, and study-design filters as last-line ANDs.
5. Provide search syntax for PubMed/MEDLINE, Embase (Emtree), Cochrane CENTRAL, CINAHL, and one discipline-specific database.
6. Translate each query line so the syntax matches the platform's quirks (Embase emtree focus, Cochrane wildcard, CINAHL exact-match).
7. Apply PRESS-2015 check: spelling, syntax, operators, MeSH, line numbers, exclusions. Score each line.
8. Plan dual-screener Peer Review: ask a second librarian to PRESS-check before locking the query.
</method>
<output_format>
## Search concept map
## PubMed search (line-numbered)
## Embase search (line-numbered)
## Cochrane CENTRAL search
## CINAHL search
## Discipline-specific search (pick database)
## PRESS-2015 checklist with line-by-line rating
## Yield estimate per database
## Record of search (PRISMA-S extension)
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Each line item must be operable: paste-ready, with line numbers and platform noted.
- Estimate yield from a quick sensitivity pilot (PubMed only, 50 most recent).
- PRISMA-S (Rethlefsen et al. 2021) is required to document databases and full search strategies.
</quality_bar>
notes: PRESS-2015 statement (McGowan et al., J Clin Epidemiol 2016, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclinepi.2016.01.021).
Cochrane Handbook v6.4 chapter 4 guides database selection and translation.
PRISMA-S extension (Rethlefsen et al. 2021) requires documenting the search and is now expected by high-tier journals.
antiPatterns: Do not use only one database unless justified. Minimum 3 for a clinical review.
Do not include 'randomized controlled trial' filters for non-RCT questions.
Do not skip the PRESS-2015 review; search errors are the most common reason for retraction.

prompts.title: Screen and select studies (dual-screener)
prompts.when: You have a merged library from a systematic search and need a defensible screening workflow with inter-rater reliability reporting.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a systematic reviewer responsible for the screening phase. You work in Covidence, Rayyan, or DistillerSR and you report inter-rater agreement with kappa or percent agreement per PRISMA 2020 item 13.</role>
<task>Design the dual-screener, dual-extractor workflow and produce the screening report skeleton for the review below.</task>
<review_topic>{{topic}}</review_topic>
<screening_tool>{{Covidence | Rayyan | DistillerSR | other}}</screening_tool>
<piloted_criteria>{{pilot_test_result_for_screening_criteria}}</piloted_criteria>
<method>
1. Define the title/abstract screening pilot: 50 records dual-screened with kappa reported. If kappa less than 0.8, iterate criteria before continuing.
2. Define the conflict resolution procedure: a third senior reviewer adjudicates unresolved disagreements.
3. Define the reason codes for exclusion so reasons can be tabled in the PRISMA flow diagram.
4. Specify per-stage quota. Stage 1 volume, Stage 2 expected volume, expected final inclusion.
5. Specify the screening form fields: include date screened, screener ID, decision, exclusion reason, free-text note.
6. Plan the PRISMA 2020 flow diagram data: identified, deduplicated, screened, excluded at title/abstract, sought for retrieval, not retrieved, assessed at full text, excluded (with reasons), included.
7. Plan the kappa calculation: who calculates it, at what stages, with what threshold (kappa >= 0.8 acceptable, 0.6 to 0.79 needs iteration).
8. Build the per-stage throughput estimate (records per hour per reviewer).
</method>
<output_format>
## Pilot and iteration plan
## Inclusion and exclusion criteria (operational)
## Reason codes (exhaustive list)
## Screening form schema
## Conflict resolution procedure
## Kappa tracking plan and thresholds
## PRISMA 2020 flow diagram data block
## Throughput estimate and timeline
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Report kappa at both title-abstract and full-text stages, not just one.
- Pre-register reason codes so exclusion reasons roll up cleanly.
</quality_bar>
notes: PRISMA 2020 item 13 (screening methods) and item 16a (study selection) require explicit dual-screening detail.
Reference: Covidence Knowledge Base and Cochrane Handbook chapter 4, section 4.6.4 on screening.
Most review retractions follow screening failures. Dual screening plus kappa is the only validated defense.
antiPatterns: Do not skip the pilot. Piloting is what catches ambiguous criteria before they bias thousands of decisions.
Do not exclude reasons that are not pre-defined. Post-hoc reasons inflate selection bias.

prompts.title: Data extraction form (Cochrane handbook ch.5)
prompts.when: You have included studies and need a structured data extraction form that survives 2 independent extractors per Cochrane Handbook chapter 5.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a systematic reviewer applying Cochrane Handbook chapter 5, section 5.1 ('Data collection processes') and section 5.3 ('Data extraction').</role>
<task>Build the data extraction form schema and pilot plan for the review below.</task>
<review_topic>{{topic}}</review_topic>
<extractor_tool>{{Excel | Covidence | DistillerSR | REDCap | other}}</extractor_tool>
<method>
1. List the data items grouped by domain: study attributes (citation, country, setting, design, funding), population (sample size, eligibility, demographics), intervention/exposure (dose, route, duration, comparator), outcomes (definition, scale, timepoint, result with measure of dispersion), risk of bias per domain, remarks.
2. Define field types and validation: numeric ranges for sample size, controlled vocabularies for design, ISO date format, free text limited to a count of characters.
3. Specify the pilot round: 3 studies dual-extracted, disagreements reviewed in a reconciliation meeting, form revised and locked.
4. Specify the data item source: page and table number from which each datum is drawn (so it is auditable).
5. Specify handling of multiple reports per study (data tracking by study ID, not by report).
6. Specify handling of missing data: record as 'not reported', contact author with 14-day reminder, then mark 'not available'.
7. Plan extraction time, dispute resolution, and form version control.
</method>
<output_format>
## Field schema (name, type, description, validation, source hint)
## Controlled vocabularies
## Pilot plan
## Reconciliation procedure
## Missing-data handling protocol
## Time and workload estimate
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Every numeric field must have a unit, a precision, and a source-citation hint (table or figure number).
- 'Not reported' must be a distinct value from 'not available'. Reviewers need to tell the difference for risk of bias.
</quality_bar>
notes: Cochrane Handbook v6.4, chapter 5, https://training.cochrane.org/handbook/current/chapter-05.
Pilot-and-revise is the single highest-leverage practice in extraction. Skipping it leads to data points that are not comparable.
antiPatterns: Do not use generic free-text fields for outcome results. Always require effect estimate, dispersion, and sample size.
Do not record 'consensus' on disagreements without naming the rationale. Future reviews need to know why.

prompts.title: Risk of bias 2 (RoB 2) for RCTs
prompts.when: You have included RCTs in a systematic review and need a defensible RoB 2 (Sterne et al. 2019) judgment per outcome per study.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a systematic reviewer trained in RoB 2 for randomized trials (Sterne et al., BMJ 2019) and Cochrane Handbook chapter 8 ('Assessing risk of bias in a randomized trial').</role>
<task>For each included randomized trial, produce the structured RoB 2 domain-by-domain judgment per outcome. Output is the row of the RoB 2 traffic-light plot.</task>
<trial_id>{{study_id}}</trial_id>
<trial_design>{{individually_randomized | cluster_randomized | crossover}}</trial_design>
<outcome>{{outcome_name_with_definition_and_timepoint}}</outcome>
<study_report_or_protocol>{{paste_or_describe}}</study_report_or_protocol>
<method>
1. Use the 5 RoB 2 domains: D1 bias arising from the randomization process, D2 bias due to deviations from intended interventions, D3 bias due to missing outcome data, D4 bias in measurement of the outcome, D5 bias in selection of the reported result.
2. For each domain, answer the signaling questions (1.1-1.2 in D1, 2.1-2.6 in D2, etc.). Provide free-text evidence for each answer.
3. For each domain, output Low risk / Some concerns / High risk based on the algorithm in the official RoB 2 guidance.
4. Issue the overall judgment: Low (all domains low), Some concerns (some concerns in at least one, none high), High (high in at least one, OR multiple some-concerns).
5. Cite the exact location of evidence: section, table, figure, page.
6. Flag indirectness if the trial does not directly answer the systematic review's PICO.
</method>
<output_format>
## Trial ID, design, outcome
## Domain-by-domain table
(domain | signaling questions summary | evidence | judgment | source)
## Overall RoB 2 judgment and rationale
## Indirectness flag
## Contribution to GRADE certainty (per outcome)
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Signaling questions must be answered with quoted or paraphrased evidence, not from general impression.
- Overall judgment must follow the official algorithm. Do not improvise.
</quality_bar>
notes: Sterne et al., 'RoB 2: a revised tool for assessing risk of bias in randomised trials', BMJ 2019 (https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.l4898).
Cochrane Handbook chapter 8 plus supplemental RoB 2 guidance document.
RoB 2 SUI extension covers cluster-randomized and cross-over trials. Pick the right variant.
antiPatterns: Do not judge a domain without answering the signaling questions.
Do not collapse multiple outcomes into one judgment per trial. Risk of bias is outcome-specific.
Do not score 'unclear' when evidence is present. Work harder.

prompts.title: Formal peer review per COPE
