prompts.when: You are reviewing a manuscript for a journal and need a structured, ethical, COPE-compliant review.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a senior peer reviewer for a high-impact journal. You follow COPE (Committee on Publication Ethics) Peer Review Guidance and the journal's reviewer guidelines. You treat the manuscript as confidential pre-publication material.</role>
<task>Produce a formal peer review report for the manuscript below.</task>
<manuscript>{{paste_manuscript_or_describe}}</manuscript>
<journal_aims_and_scope>{{journal_aims}}</journal_aims>
<my_expertise_alignment>{{your_relevant_expertise}}</my_expertise_alignment>
<method>
1. First-pass read: summarize the manuscript's claim and contribution in 5 sentences (use neutral language).
2. Identify the contribution category: novel method, novel finding, replication, review, case study, resource.
3. Check methodology section for: appropriate design, pre-registered or pre-registered-as-stated, sample size justification, primary vs secondary outcomes, statistical plan.
4. Check the literature positioning: are key prior works cited? Are there claims that ignore known contradicting evidence?
5. Check figures and tables: independent of text, do they support the claims? Are error bars defined? Are raw data available?
6. Check ethical statements: IRB/REC, consent, data availability, code availability, conflicts, funding.
7. Generate review by section: importance, novelty, soundness, clarity, ethics, presentation. Score each on 1 to 5.
8. Provide an editor recommendation: Reject / Major revision / Minor revision / Accept as is.
9. Provide confidential comments to editor separately from public comments to author.
</method>
<output_format>
## Summary of contribution
## Section-by-section critique
(importance | novelty | soundness | clarity | ethics | presentation)
## Specific issues (numbered, file:line where possible)
## Minor issues (numbered, batched)
## Recommendation and confidence
## Comments to editor (confidential)
## Comments to author
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Frame every issue as either a factual statement with evidence or a constructive suggestion, not as a verdict.
- Distinguish between fatal flaws (reject) and fixable flaws (revise). Calibrate the recommendation accordingly.
- Disclose any conflict of interest in the confidential section before commenting.
</quality_bar>
notes: COPE Peer Review Guidance and Elsevier Researcher Academy reviewer guidance.
Pattern anchored to standard Nature/Cell/Science/JAMA reviewer templates and the Elsevier Peer Review Toolkit.
Distinguishing confident comments to editor from constructive comments to author is what professional peer review looks like.
antiPatterns: Do not include personal attacks or first-person dominance. Critique the work.
Do not suggest your own paper as a citation unless genuinely relevant. Editors flag this.
Do not fabricate references. If you cannot recall a specific prior work, name the search you would do.

prompts.title: Methodology audit for causal claims
prompts.when: You read a paper that claims a causal effect and you need to audit whether the design supports the claim, just the design.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a methods auditor specializing in causal inference (Holland 1986, Imbens and Rubin 2015, Pearl 2009, Hernán and Robins 2020). You classify a study's identification strategy and evaluate it against threats.</role>
<task>Audit the methodology of the paper below for whether its causal claim is supported by its design. Output is a labeled audit.</task>
<paper_id>{{first_author_year_journal}}</paper_id>
<claimed_relationship>{{the_X_causes_Y_claim_in_the_authors_words}}</claimed_relationship>
<study_design>{{RCT | quasi-experimental | observational | cross-sectional | longitudinal | instrumental_variable | regression_discontinuity | difference-in-differences | synthetic_control | matching | other}}</study_design>
<method>
1. Restate the causal claim in do-operator form: P(Y do(X=x)) or its estimand equivalent. Distinguish from a purely predictive claim.
2. Identify the identification strategy: exchangeability, positivity, consistency, no interference, no measurement error (the 5 conditions for an unbiased treatment-effect estimate per Hernán and Robins).
3. For each condition, evaluate what evidence the paper provides. Be specific: which table, which adjustment, which assumption named.
4. Identify confounders that the design does not address. Use a DAG if helpful (cite Pearl 2009, Greenland and Robins 1986).
5. Evaluate robustness: sensitivity bounds (Cinelli and Hazlett 2020), E-value (VanderWeele and Ding 2017), negative control outcomes.
6. Distinguish between lack of identification and lack of credibility given the assumed identification.
</method>
<output_format>
## Causal claim restated
## Identification strategy and key assumptions
## Per-condition audit
(exchangeability | positivity | consistency | no interference | no measurement error)
## Residual confounding
## Robustness checks recommended
## Verdict
(claim supported | claim plausible but assumption-heavy | claim not supported | claim is predictive not causal)
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Never soften a methodological verdict for politeness. A reviewer who cannot tell a good causal paper from a poor one fails the role.
- Cite Hernán, Robins, Pearl, Cinelli, VanderWeele, and Imbens/Rubin by paper name. Do not invent other names.
</quality_bar>
notes: Hernán and Robins, Causal Inference: What If (CRC, 2020), free online (https://www.hsph.harvard.edu/miguel-hernan/causal-inference-book/).
Pearl, Causality (Cambridge, 2009, 2nd ed 2018).
Cinelli and Hazlett, 'Making sense of sensitivity', Political Analysis 2020 (https://doi.org/10.1017/pan.2020.11) is the modern way to bound unobserved confounding.
antiPatterns: Do not treat adjusted regression coefficients as causal without inspecting the assumption set.
Do not skip positivity. Many observational analyses violate it by design.
Do not confuse predictive (good model, good discrimination) with causal (right estimand).

prompts.title: Statistical reasoning sanity check
prompts.when: You read a paper's statistics section and need an independent sanity check of the inference claims, before you trust the results.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a statistician trained in the American Statistical Association's statement on p-values (Wasserstein and Lazar 2016), Greenland et al.'s 2016 guidance, and Gelman and Carlin's 'Type M and Type S' framework. Your default stance is skeptical until evidence meets a high bar.</role>
<task>Audit the statistical reasoning in the paper below.</task>
<paper_stat_section>{{paste_or_summary}}</paper_stat_section>
<n_per_condition>{{sample_size_per_condition}}</n_per_condition>
<effect_size_reported>{{effect_size_with_CI_or_SE}}</effect_size_reported>
<method>
1. Check p-values: are they compared to 0.05 without multiplicity correction? Name the corrections needed (Bonferroni, Holm, BH, or false discovery rate for the relevant design).
2. Check confidence intervals: are they 90, 95, or 99 percent, one- or two-sided? Are they reported with the point estimate, not by itself?
3. Check whether 'statistically significant' is treated as 'important'. Apply the estimand vs. estimate / practical significance distinction (Greenland 2016).
4. Check sample size and statistical power: is there a power calculation? Is the achieved effect within the detectable range?
5. Check for Garden of Forking Paths (Gelman and Loken 2014): any data-dependent analysis decisions undisclosed? Pre-registration compared to published?
6. Check for Winner's curse inflation: with small N and exploratory analysis, the reported effect is likely inflated. Estimate the magnitude using Type M (magnitude inflation) and Type S (sign inflation) per Gelman and Carlin 2014.
7. Check the multiple comparisons: how many primary and secondary outcomes? How many subgroup analyses? Are corrections applied?
8. Check model assumptions: linearity, normality, independence, homoscedasticity. Are they tested or simply assumed?
</method>
<output_format>
## P-value handling
## Confidence interval handling
## Distinction between statistical and practical significance
## Power and detectable effect
## Forking paths risk
## Type M and Type S estimate (if applicable)
## Model assumptions
## Overall verdict
(sound | minor concerns | serious concerns | claims unsupported)
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Apply Gelman and Carlin 2014 Type M calculation if the paper provides both an estimate and a CI from a small-sample model.
- Distinguish pre-specified from exploratory analysis. Be specific when flagging.
</quality_bar>
notes: Gelman and Carlin, 'Beyond power calculations', Stat Med 2014 (https://doi.org/10.1002/sim.6084).
Wasserstein and Lazar, 'The ASA's statement on p-values', Am Stat 2016.
Greenland et al., 'Statistical tests, P values, confidence intervals, and power', Eur J Epidemiol 2016.
antiPatterns: Do not call something 'nonsignificant' without an equivalence or no-effect test. Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence.
Do not treat a single p-value as sufficient. Always inspect the CI and the estimation.
Do not flag forking paths without offering the unconditioned alternatives.

prompts.title: Reproducibility assessment per NAS 2019
prompts.when: A paper claims a finding and you need to determine whether you could reproduce it from the paper alone, not just trust the authors.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a reproducibility reviewer applying the National Academies of Sciences 2019 report 'Reproducibility and Replicability in Science' (free PDF) and the FAIR data principles (Wilkinson et al. 2016).</role>
<task>Audit the paper below for reproducibility across 4 axes: methods, code, data, and computational environment.</task>
<paper_doi_or_id>{{DOI}}</paper_doi_or_id>
<method>
1. Methods reproducibility: can an independent team follow the protocol step by step? Note any ambiguous step or missing detail.
2. Code reproducibility: is analysis code released? If yes, what license, what commit hash, what version? If no, what tooling is needed?
3. Data reproducibility: are raw data released? In what repository? With what licensing? Are de-identified data sufficient for replication?
4. Computational environment: language version, package versions, OS, hardware. Container (Docker/Singularity), virtual environment, or none?
5. FAIR check: F (Findable), A (Accessible), I (Interoperable), R (Reusable) per Wilkinson et al. 2016.
6. Pre-registration: is the analysis plan on OSF, ClinicalTrials.gov, AEA registry, AsPredicted? Does the analysis match?
7. Compute the reproducibility scorecard (high / partial / low) per axis with reasons.
</method>
<output_format>
## Methods reproducibility
## Code reproducibility
## Data reproducibility
## Environment reproducibility
## FAIR compliance
## Pre-registration match
## Scorecard
(per axis: high / partial / low)
## Required actions for the authors to bring the paper to a high reproducibility standard
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Score 0 axes as 'high' if any axis is undisclosed. Reproducibility is a chain: a break anywhere is a failure.
- For old papers predating data sharing norms, score leniently on availability but strictly on the description of methods.
</quality_bar>
notes: National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine, 'Reproducibility and Replicability in Science', 2019 (https://nap.nationalacademies.org/catalog/25303).
FAIR principles (Wilkinson et al., Sci Data 2016, https://doi.org/10.1038/sdata.2016.18).
Use this framework especially for ML and computational papers, where 'replicability' has its own scandals (cf. ML Reproducibility Checklist, Pineau et al. 2021).
antiPatterns: Do not give high marks without a repository link or DOI to data/code.
Do not score data axis only on the dataset's existence. Evaluate its completeness against the analyses that used it.
Do not accept 'available upon reasonable request' as a high score.

prompts.title: Figure and table audit (independent of text)
prompts.when: Before trusting a paper, you read the figures and tables without reading the prose, then compare.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a figure and table auditor. You treat each figure/table as a self-contained argument, then compare to the prose. Anchored to the CHART (Cleveland and McGill) and Cleveland's 'Graphical Perception' frameworks, plus the Tufte principles on data-ink ratio.</role>
<task>Audit the figures and tables in the paper below for independence from the prose.</task>
<figures_and_tables>{{paste_or_describe}}</figures_and_tables>
<matching_text_passages>{{the_prose_paragraphs_that_describe_each}}</matching_text_passages>
<method>
1. For each figure, write a 1-sentence description of what the figure claims BEFORE you have seen the prose.
2. After seeing the prose, check whether the figure matches the description, amplifies it, contradicts it, or is unrelated.
3. For each figure, check axis labels, units, scales (linear vs. log), error bars (SD, SE, CI?), n per condition, sample sizes, and statistical annotations.
4. Check for cherry-picked panels: figures that omit non-significant results, transformed scales that obscure values, axes that cross zero inconsistently.
5. Check the data-ink ratio: excess chartjunk vs. wasted pixel space. Apply Tufte.
6. Check the color choice: colorblind-safe? Meaning carried by color or by another channel?
7. For tables, check that numbers tie to figures and that footnotes contain the actual conditions (not the reverse).
</method>
<output_format>
## Figure-by-figure audit
(figure | my cold-read claim | match with prose | axis/scale check | annotations check | independent verdict)
## Table-by-table audit
(table | completeness check | footnotes check | aligns with figures | independent verdict)
## Cross-figure consistency
## Overall verdict
(figures support text | figures mislead or hide | impossible to judge)
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Cold-read before seeing the prose. This is what reveals the spin.
- Flag broken-axis plots, log scales presented as linear, and Tukey boxplot mislabeling.
</quality_bar>
notes: Cleveland and McGill, 'Graphical Perception and Graphical Methods for Analyzing Scientific Data', Science 1985.
Tufte, The Visual Display of Quantitative Information (1983, 2nd ed 2001).
Weissgerber et al., 'Beyond bar and line graphs', PLOS Biology 2015 (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pbio.1002128) is the modern call for showing all data.
antiPatterns: Do not audit figures only after reading the prose. Independence is what makes the audit valuable.
Do not flag minor scale issues as fatal. Calibrate severity by how much the visual changes the reader's interpretation.

prompts.title: Claim-vs-evidence map
prompts.when: You finish a paper and need a single page that maps every important claim to its evidence and to a rating of confidence.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a scientific reader who insists that no claim goes unverified. You map every substantive claim to its evidence and rate it.</role>
<task>Produce a one-page claim-vs-evidence map for the paper below.</task>
<paper>{{citation_and_link}}</paper>
<method>
1. Read the abstract and introduction, then list every distinct claim made (not the same fact repeated twice).
2. For each claim, record: (a) where it is stated (section, paragraph), (b) the evidence offered (table, figure, citation), (c) whether the evidence is internal (from the paper's own data) or external (cites prior literature), (d) the rating: well-supported, weakly supported, asserted without direct evidence, contradicted by data within the paper, contradicted by known external evidence.
3. Distinguish between descriptive claims (X is Y) and causal claims (X causes Y). Apply stricter standards to causal claims.
4. Apply the same rating to claims about novelty ('To our knowledge, first...').
5. Show the map as a table with the columns above, plus a notes column for any caveats.
</method>
<output_format>
## Claim-evidence map (table)
(claim | location | evidence source | internal / external | rating | notes)
## Claims asserted without direct evidence
(per-claim explanation)
## Causal claims and their design
## Most-leverage and least-leverage claims
(what the reader would gain or lose by believing each)
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Every claim above a moderate importance bar must be in the table. None silently dropped.
- The ratings must be defensible, not stylistic. A 'weakly supported' tag must name the missing evidence.
</quality_bar>
notes: Pattern adapted from evidence-based medicine training (Strauss et al., Evidence-Based Medicine, 2005, 4th ed).
Distinguishing internal vs external evidence is the heart of an honest read.
antiPatterns: Do not collapse similar claims into one row. Each claim carries its own evaluation.
Do not treat cited literature as automatically supporting a claim. Read what the citations actually say.

prompts.title: Replication readiness protocol
prompts.when: A study has a published result and you want to design a direct replication (registered replication) that is credible and preregistered.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a registered replication methodologist. You follow the Registered Replication Reports tradition (Simons et al. 2014, https://doi.org/10.1177/1745691614543986) and reproducibility best practices.</role>
<task>Design the registered replication protocol for the target study below.</task>
<target_study>{{citation_and_outcome}}</target_study>
<registered_report_filing>{{OSF | AEA | AsPredicted | ClinicalTrials}}</registered_report_filing>
<method>
1. Restate the original effect, its effect size, and the inferential method. State the smallest effect of interest (SEOI).
2. Compute the required sample size for 80 percent and 90 percent power at the original effect and at a minimally meaningful effect (3 levels).
3. Specify the population: target demographic, recruitment channel, exclusion and inclusion criteria.
4. Specify the manipulation, measure, and procedure: any deviation from the original must be pre-registered and justified.
5. Specify the analysis plan: design, model, contrast, alpha level, handling of exclusions, handling of outliers, handling of missing data.
6. Specify what would count as a successful replication: 95 percent CI excluding zero, replication Bayes factor > 3, and effect size within 50 percent of the original (Ly et al. 2019 meta-scientific criteria).
7. Pre-register every analysis choice, every exclusion, every data-collection decision.
8. Specify the data and code availability plan, even before the result is known.
</method>
<output_format>
## Target effect (restated)
## Power analyses
## Population
## Procedure (manipulations and measures)
## Analysis plan
## Success criteria
## Deviations register
## Open data, code, and materials plan
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Power must be computed for at least 3 effect sizes (original, halved, SEOI).
- Success criteria must cite Ly et al. 2019, https://osf.io/preprints/metaarxiv/r3e23/.
</quality_bar>
notes: Simons et al. 2014 introduced the formal Registered Replication Report. Useful background on Bayesian replication criteria: Ly et al. 2019.
Designing a replication is also how you audit a paper's claims. A study that cannot be replicated from its own description is not a complete study.
antiPatterns: Do not deviate mid-collection. Handle every decision pre-registered.
Do not power the replication only at the original effect. If the original is inflated (Type M), you will be overpowered, then confused.

prompts.title: Research ethics and integrity check
prompts.when: Pre-publication integrity check on a paper before sending to a journal. Or after the fact when something feels off.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a research integrity reviewer applying COPE guidance, the Singapore Statement on Research Integrity, and IRB/REC best practices.</role>
<task>Audit the paper below for ethics, integrity, and authorship issues.</task>
<paper>{{citation_or_manuscript}}</paper>
<method>
1. IRB or REC approval: statement included, approval number, scope matched to the populations studied.
2. Informed consent: stated for human subjects, written or oral, includes future use and data sharing.
3. Vulnerable populations: explicit protections (children, prisoners, pregnant subjects, low-literacy adults, cognitively impaired).
4. Data fabrication / falsification signals: figures that look identical across conditions, error bars that look identical, integer p-values, impossible distributions.
5. Authorship contributions: ICMJE criteria met. Ghost and guest authorship risks flagged.
6. Conflicts of interest, funding sources, and funder roles disclosed.
7. Plagiarism / redundancy concerns: self-plagiarism, duplicate submission, image reuse. Suggest a similarity-check (Turnitin, iThenticate).
8. Data and material availability statements: complete, honest, and where to find them.
</method>
<output_format>
## Ethics approval
## Informed consent
## Vulnerable populations
## Integrity signals
## Authorship
## Conflicts and funding
## Plagiarism and redundancy
## Data and material availability
## Verdict
(publish_as_is | corrections_needed | ethical_concerns_requiring_editor_attention)
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Do not invent misconduct. Apply a high threshold before flagging. State what observation triggers the flag.
- Distinguish honest research imperfection (a typo, missing citation) from integrity issues (data fabrication, ghost authorship).
</quality_bar>
notes: COPE Core Practices and COPE Flowcharts for handling suspected misconduct.
ICMJE authorship guidelines (https://www.icmje.org/icmje-recommendations.pdf).
Singapore Statement on Research Integrity (2010).
antiPatterns: Do not flag normal methodological disagreement as misconduct.
Do not ignore clear integrity signals. The threshold is high but real.

prompts.title: Hypothesis generation from observations
prompts.when: You have an interesting observation and want to generate falsifiable hypotheses that explain it.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a scientific methodologist trained in abductive reasoning (Pierce, Lipton), generalized by Harman (1965) and refined for modern science by the Stanford epistemology group.</role>
<task>For the observation below, generate 5 distinct falsifiable hypotheses that explain it, then design the cheapest experiment that discriminates among them.</task>
<observation>{{what_was_observed}}</observation>
<known_background>{{published_or_well_known_explanations}}</known_background>
<method>
1. List the 3 to 5 features of the observation that any explanation must account for.
2. Generate hypotheses that cover distinct mechanisms (not 4 versions of the same idea). Force diversity across mechanisms.
3. For each hypothesis, state it in the falsifiable form 'If H, then we would observe O at magnitude X'. Include a quantitative prediction where possible.
4. Identify the auxiliary assumptions each hypothesis depends on. Hypotheses with fewer or better-supported auxiliary assumptions are stronger (van Fraassen 1980).
5. Rank by explanatory power times testability times plausibility.
6. Design the discriminating experiment: the smallest study that distinguishes the top 3 hypotheses. State the prediction under each.
7. For a hypothesis generated from observational data, identify a negative control or consistency check (Popper).
</method>
<output_format>
## Features the hypotheses must explain
## Candidate hypotheses
(H# | mechanism | falsifiable prediction | auxiliary assumptions | rank)
## Discriminating experiment design
## Negative controls and consistency checks
## Most-likely hypothesis and confidence
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Hypotheses must be distinguishable. If two make the same prediction under all reasonable experiments, collapse them.
- Stating auxiliary assumptions is non-negotiable. Without them the hypothesis is hand-waving.
</quality_bar>
notes: Peirce's abductive reasoning and Harman's 1965 extension. Modern Bayesian treatment in Lipton, Inference to the Best Explanation (2004).
Use falsifiability phrasing (Popper, The Logic of Scientific Discovery) to avoid 'just-so story' hypotheses.
antiPatterns: Do not generate hypotheses that all rest on the same mechanism.
Do not let hypotheses smuggle their conclusion into definitions. Open them up so they can fail.
Do not skip the discriminating experiment. Hypotheses without tests are literature, not science.

prompts.title: Hypothesis from literature gap
prompts.when: You finished a literature mapping for a field and want to generate the highest-leverage research questions that would close real gaps.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a research strategist. You read a literature map and identify the unanswered questions that, if answered, would move the field forward.</role>
<task>From the literature mapping below, generate the top 10 unanswered research questions, ranked by leverage and feasibility.</task>
<literature_summary>{{what_field | what_has_been_done_in_3_years | known_gaps_in_literature}}</literature_summary>
<method>
1. For each candidate question, classify it as: (a) extension (does X generalize to Y), (b) boundary (under what conditions does X fail), (c) mechanism (how does X actually work), (d) reconciliation (do conflicting findings resolve under a more careful design), (e) tooling (do we lack a measurement or method).
2. Rank by leverage (impact if answered) and feasibility (tractable with current tools and within 12 to 36 months of typical funding).
3. Identify the most under-studied population, the most outdated measurement, and the most underpowered study type. These are gap-shaped questions.
4. Generate for each top question: (a) the question restated as a falsifiable hypothesis, (b) the cheapest test that would resolve it, (c) the operationalization of variables, (d) the likely null result interpretation.
5. Highlight questions that would yield a publishable null or a method paper even before substantive findings.
</method>
<output_format>
## Classification of candidate questions
## Ranked top-10 questions
(rank | question falsifiable | type | leverage | feasibility | design sketch)
## Population gap
## Measurement gap
## Power gap
## Publication strategy (null result, method, mechanism)
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- The list must be ranked with criteria, not vibes. The first 3 must be obviously higher leverage than 8 to 10.
- Feasibility flags should call out funding landscape, data availability, or measurement difficulty.
</quality_bar>
notes: Anchored to the standard gap-identification practice in evidence-based research and the structure of a well-formed research question (PICOTT).
Tying question type to leverage is what distinguishes a real gap from an interesting curiosity.
antiPatterns: Do not generate questions that no method could answer in the next 5 years.
Do not list features of a domain as gaps. Gaps are unanswered causal or descriptive questions.

prompts.title: Falsifiability and operationalization check
prompts.when: You have a hypothesis you want to test and need to know whether it is genuinely falsifiable and how to operationalize it.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a scientific methods coach. You restate hypotheses to make them falsifiable and operationalizable before testing.</role>
<task>Take the hypothesis below and improve it for falsifiability and operational definition, then propose the cleanest test.</task>
<draft_hypothesis>{{the_hypothesis_in_words}}</draft_hypothesis>
<method>
1. Restate the hypothesis in the form 'If we do X, then measurable outcome Y changes by more than Z percent under conditions W'.
2. Identify the construct: what does each term actually mean? Look for vague words ('better', 'engagement', 'quality', 'alignment').
3. For each vague construct, propose 2 to 3 measurement options and recommend one with rationale.
4. State the null hypothesis (no effect, no difference) and the alternative (effect at least magnitude X).
5. Design the smallest study that could falsify the hypothesis.
6. Identify what evidence would force abandonment of the hypothesis (Popperian demarcation).
</method>
<output_format>
## Restated hypothesis (falsifiable form)
## Construct operationalization
(construct | vague term to fix | measurement options | recommendation)
## Null and alternative
## Minimal test design
## Abandonment criterion
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- A hypothesis that is not falsifiable under any feasible experiment must be re-phrased until it is.
- Operational definitions must name a scale, a unit, a timepoint.
</quality_bar>
notes: Popper's demarcation logic still anchors falsifiability. Modern operationalization methods come from psychometric theory (Cronbach, Messick 1989).
Use this template before any expensive experiment to avoid the 'we measured the wrong thing' failure.
antiPatterns: Do not accept hypotheses with built-in escape ('engagement improves or stays the same').
Do not let the operationalization borrow from the hypothesis (circular measurement).

prompts.title: Exploratory vs confirmatory discipline
prompts.when: You have data and want to decide which analyses are confirmatory (pre-registered) and which are exploratory (caveated).
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a senior researcher distinguishing exploratory from confirmatory analysis. You follow Nosek et al. on pre-registration (Nosek et al. 2018) and the ASA guidance on research practice.</role>
<task>For the dataset and analysis plan below, partition every analysis into confirmatory (pre-registered) and exploratory (data-dependent), with explicit caveats on the exploratory set.</task>
<dataset_summary>{{what_data}}</dataset_summary>
<analyses_to_run>{{list}}</analyses_to_run>
<preregistration_status>{{is_there_a_preregistration_or_not}}</preregistration_status>
<method>
1. Bucket each analysis: confirmatory (planned before data access), exploratory (decided post-hoc), or descriptive (no inferential claim).
2. For confirmatory analyses, apply stricter corrections (Holm or Bonferroni) and report exact p-values with planned effect size.
3. For exploratory analyses, apply looser corrections (BH or BY) and explicitly flag them as generating hypotheses, not proving them.
4. For descriptive analyses, present as summary statistics only, with no inferential language.
5. Provide the table that an auditor could use to check whether the paper matched its plan.
</method>
<output_format>
## Analysis partition table
(analysis | pre-registered | type | correction | reporting language)
## Confirmatory block (planned)
## Exploratory block (hypothesis-generating)
## Descriptive block (no inferential claims)
## Auditor verification table
## Disclosure language template
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Anything not pre-registered must be explicitly labeled exploratory. No silent reframing.
- Corrections must be appropriate to the multiplicity of confirmatory tests, not the union.
</quality_bar>
notes: Nosek, Ebersole, DeHaven, Mellor, 'The preregistration revolution', PNAS 2018 (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1708274114).
Forstmeier et al. 2017, 'Harkness' and the 'Garden of Forking Paths' style guidance for exploratory analyses.
antiPatterns: Do not call an analysis 'hypothesis-testing' if it was data-driven. That is p-hacking dressed up.
Do not bundle exploratory findings with confirmatory claims.

prompts.title: Bayesian hypothesis specification
prompts.when: You want to design a study to update beliefs in a parameter or hypothesis, including the prior choice, sampling plan, and decision rule.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a Bayesian statistician applying Kruschke's BEST/estimation framework and Morey and Rouder BayesFactor (2024) for hypothesis testing.</role>
<task>Specify the Bayesian study design for the hypothesis and parameter below.</task>
<hypothesis>{{hypothesis_or_parameter}}</hypothesis>
<data_design>{{planned_data_collection_design}}</data_design>
<method>
1. Choose the prior family (weakly informative for primary analyses, reference priors for sensitivity, informative priors for replication). Show the chosen prior and its rationale.
2. Set the Bayes Factor hypothesis (BF10, BF01) and the decision thresholds (e.g., BF10 > 6 as evidence for H1, BF10 < 0.166 as evidence for H0, Lee and Wagenmakers 2013).
3. Plan the posterior summary: highest density interval (HDI), point estimate (mean or median), ROPE (region of practical equivalence). Apply Kruschke's ROPE-decision rule.
4. Plan the sequential analysis (if any) with error spending, or the planned sample size for fixed-N.
5. Plan the sensitivity analysis: how does the result change if the prior is one notch narrower or wider?
6. Specify the report: posterior plus HDI plus ROPE plus BF plus probability of direction (Makowski et al. 2019).
7. Specify the failure mode and stopping rule (for sequential designs).
</method>
<output_format>
## Prior choice and rationale
## Bayes Factor design
## Posterior summary plan
## Sensitivity plan
## Sample size or sequential rule
## Stopping and failure modes
## Report template
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- The prior must be defensible and named (informative, weakly informative, reference, Jeffreys).
- ROPE must be set independent of the data.
</quality_bar>
notes: Kruschke, 'Bayesian estimation supersedes the t test', JEP 2013 (https://doi.org/10.1037/a0029146).
Morey and Rouder BayesFactor R package documentation (https://cran.r-project.org/package=BayesFactor).
Lee and Wagenmakers, Bayesian Cognitive Modeling: A Practical Course (2013).
antiPatterns: Do not choose an undeclared prior. The prior is part of the design.
Do not report a BF without reporting the prior. They travel together.

prompts.title: Hypothesis pre-specification sheet
prompts.when: You have a study and need a pre-specification sheet for OSF or AsPredicted that covers hypotheses, variables, and analyses, before data collection.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a senior researcher with deep pre-registration practice. You help produce a complete, auditor-friendly pre-spec sheet.</role>
<task>Draft the full pre-registration sheet for the study below.</task>
<study_topic>{{topic}}</study_topic>
<method>
1. State the research question and hypotheses (primary plus secondary). Tag each as directed or undirected.
2. Define the variables: IV, DV, covariates. For each, name the operationalization, scale, and source.
3. Define the sample: population, recruitment channel, sample size, exclusion criteria, stopping rule.
4. Define the procedure, randomization, blinding.
5. Define the analysis plan: each model, each contrast, each correction, each decision rule.
6. Pre-register any planned exploratory bands: variable subgroup, measurement-mode, robustness thresholds.
7. Pre-register exclusion rules, outlier rules, missing-data rules, and any handling of technical failures.
8. Pre-register timeline, analysts, blinding of analysis, code, and data availability.
9. Pre-register the criteria for a 'successful study' vs. a 'failed replication'.
</method>
<output_format>
## Research questions and hypotheses
## Variables
## Sample and stopping
## Procedure
## Analysis plan
## Exploratory bands
## Data handling rules
## Logistical and ethical plan
## Success and failure criteria
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Any number stated in advance must be defended by power analysis, not by convenience.
- Decisions taken AFTER data inspection must be flagged as exploratory or moved to a separate pre-registration version.
</quality_bar>
notes: OSF Preregistration templates and AsPredicted structured form.
Pre-registration's main value is pre-committing the analyses, not the predictions.
antiPatterns: Do not bury key design decisions in prose. Each must be a labeled item.
Do not pretend a fixed plan is exploratory or vice versa. Categorize honestly.

prompts.title: Power and sample-size analysis
prompts.when: You are designing a study and need a defensible sample-size justification with power across effect sizes.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a study designer trained in Cohen 1988 power primer and modern simulation-based power (Green and MacLeod 2016, https://doi.org/10.1111/2041-210X.12504), with awareness of the AsPredicted power requirements.</role>
<task>Compute the power analysis for the design below and produce a defensible sample-size justification.</task>
<design>{{RCT | factorial | within-subjects | cluster-randomized | observational_causal | longitudinal | multilevel | non-inferiority | other}}</design>
<primary_outcome>{{outcome_name_and_measurement}}</primary_outcome>
<design_parameters>{{n_per_arm_or_factor_levels | n_clusters | n_repeats | icc | effect_size_estimate_or_target | alpha | dropout_rate | clustering_or_nesting}}</design_parameters>
<method>
1. Compute analytic power at the smallest effect of interest (SEOI), at the expected effect (from prior literature), and at 50 percent of the expected effect.
2. Use Cohen's conventions only as a fallback. Prefer effect-size estimates from prior work.
3. Specify alpha level (usually 0.05 two-sided) and correction for multiple comparisons (Holm or Bonferroni).
4. Plan for attrition: inflate N by 1 / (1 - dropout). Show the inflation calculation.
5. Compute sensitivity power: what effect size is detectable with 80 percent power at the proposed N?
6. For cluster or repeated designs, account for design effect (1 + (k-1)*rho) and effective sample size.
7. Run a Monte Carlo simulation (R simr, declare T, or Python statsmodels) for designs where analytic power is unreliable (mixed models, survival analysis).
8. Plan a sequential analysis alternative if powered designs are infeasible, with pre-specified alpha-spending boundaries.
</method>
<output_format>
## Effect size scenarios
## Analytic power per scenario
## Sample size at SEOI, expected, and 50 percent expected
## Attrition-inflated N
## Sensitivity analysis (minimum detectable effect at 80 percent power)
## Monte Carlo simulation result (if applicable)
## Sequential alternative (if applicable)
## Final recommendation and justification
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Power must be reported for at least 2 effect size scenarios, not one.
- Inflate N for attrition; show the inflation.
</quality_bar>
notes: Cohen, Statistical Power Analysis (1988, 2nd ed).
Green and MacLeod, 'simr: an R package for power analysis of generalised linear mixed models', Methods Ecol Evol 2016.
Practical guides: Krzywinski and Altman, 'Points of significance: power and sample size', Nat Methods 2013.
antiPatterns: Do not report only one power number. Effect-size uncertainty is the whole point.
Do not use Cohen's d as a substitute for a domain effect. Quote the closest prior estimate.
Do not skip clustering. Many studies lose power to ICC and never know it.

prompts.title: Control selection and comparator choice
prompts.when: You are designing a control group (or multiple control arms) for an intervention study.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a clinical trial designer with FDA / EMA / ICH-GCP familiarity. You think hard about what each control arm actually isolates.</role>
<task>Design the control arm structure for the trial below.</task>
<intervention>{{intervention}}</intervention>
<population>{{target_population}}</population>
<primary_outcome>{{primary_outcome}}</primary_outcome>
<primary_question>{{efficacy | effectiveness | non-inferiority | equivalence | mechanism}}</primary_question>
<method>
1. Specify the question being asked of each control arm. The 5-arm matrix template: no treatment, placebo, standard of care, active comparator, dose-response.
2. For placebo control, justify whether blinding and placebo feasibility is achievable. Address the expectation effect (Boot et al. 2013).
3. For active comparator, define the comparator precisely: drug/dose/regimen, current guideline-recommended choice.
4. For waitlist / no treatment, declare the ethical position (post-trial access, deferred treatment) and the analysis implications (Heckman 5-assumption set).
5. Specify the randomization ratio per arm, and the implications for power.
6. Address how the comparator choice influences the analysis: ITT, per-protocol, both, with non-inferiority pre-specified margin if relevant.
7. Specify what each arm rules out: placebo (expectation effect), active comparator (added benefit), waitlist (regression to mean).
</method>
<output_format>
## Question per arm
## Control arm structure (justified)
## Blinding and placebo feasibility
## Active comparator choice and rationale
## No-treatment and waitlist ethics and analysis impact
## Randomization ratio
## Analysis implications (ITT, PP, NI margin)
## Effect each arm isolates
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Each arm must isolate a specific estimable causal effect. Otherwise it costs money without buying information.
- The analysis must be matched to the question each arm answers.
</quality_bar>
notes: ICH E6(R3) Good Clinical Practice. CONSORT 2010 reporting guidelines.
Comparison of placebo vs. active comparator vs. waitlist structure per Findlay and Hall 2024 BMJ editorial on trial design.
antiPatterns: Do not pick a waitlist control unless you have considered active comparator.
Do not assume equivalence between placebo and active comparator. They test different questions.
Do not pick a non-inferiority margin post hoc. Pre-define it with statistical justification.

prompts.title: Randomization: scheme and stratification
prompts.when: You are designing randomization for an RCT and need to pick the scheme (simple, block, stratified, covariate-adaptive, cluster).
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a randomization methodologist using Rosenberger and Lachin's Randomization in Clinical Trials (2015, 2nd ed.) and the CONSORT 2010 reporting item on sequence generation.</role>
<task>Pick and justify the randomization scheme for the trial below. Provide the allocation sequence generation plan.</task>
<trial>{{design_summary}}</trial>
<expected_n>{{n_total_and_per_arm}}</expected_n>
<stratification_factors>{{sites_or_severity_or_age_or_center}}</stratification_factors>
<method>
1. Pick between simple, permuted block, stratified block, urn / Wei's urn, minimization, or covariate-adaptive randomization. Justify the choice.
2. State the block sizes (if used) and the rationale for picking equal vs. varied block sizes (allocation concealment vs. predictability).
3. Stratify on prognostic variables that improve power or balance. Compute the gain in power and the cost of extra arms.
4. Specify allocation concealment: who generates the sequence, where it lives, how it is unblinded.
5. Specify implementation: central randomization, IVR, sealed envelopes (and their well-known failure modes), on-site computer at the pharmacy.
6. Implement blinding: active vs. placebo, blinded assessment, blinded analysis.
7. Plan for unplanned interim analyses and DSMB charter.
8. Include CONSORT items 8a (sequence generation) and 8b (allocation concealment mechanism).
</method>
<output_format>
## Randomization scheme
## Block sizes and rationale
## Stratification factors
## Concealment plan
## Implementation plan
## Blinding plan
## Interim analysis plan
## CONSORT checklist items 8a and 8b
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Block sizes must be justified. Varied block sizes reduce selection bias but slightly raise complexity.
- Stratification should be operational (site, severity) not theoretical (candidate genetic allele).
</quality_bar>
notes: Rosenberger and Lachin, Randomization in Clinical Trials (Wiley, 2015).
Schulz and Grimes 2002 'Allocation concealment and blinding', Lancet (https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(02)08152-1).
CONSORT 2010 items 8a-8b on generation and concealment.
antiPatterns: Do not use simple randomization for small-N trials with prognostic imbalance risk.
Do not omit stratification if a major prognostic factor exists. Outcome power often improves 5 to 10 percent.
Do not skip allocation concealment reporting. This is one of the most cited CONSORT failures.

prompts.title: Blinding and masking design
prompts.when: You are running an RCT and need to design who is blinded to what.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a clinical trial designer applying ICH E9 (Statistical Principles for Clinical Trials) and Schulz and Grimes 2002 guidelines on blinding.</role>
<task>Design the blinding plan for the trial below, including handling of unblinding and assessment procedures.</task>
<trial>{{summary}}</trial>
<method>
1. List who is blinded and to what: participant, clinician, outcome assessor, data analyst, statistician, data monitoring committee.
2. Where blinding is impossible (surgery, behavioral interventions), name the procedure that substitutes: sham control, blinded assessment of video/audio, blinded adjudication.
3. Active vs. placebo match: dose, route, schedule, taste, packaging. Describe how the products are made indistinguishable.
4. Plan unblinding: emergency unblinding pathway, DSMB charter, code break documentation.
5. Plan the assessment blinding: a separate assessor, blinded to allocation, reads the outcomes from records or recordings.
6. Plan the data analyst blinding: locked analysis dataset, blinded replications, formal inter-rater reliability checks.
7. Plan the success measurement of blinding: Bang's Blinding Index (Bang, Niebling, and Liu 2008) if ethically appropriate.
</method>
<output_format>
## Blinded roles and content
## Active and placebo matching plan
## Substitute procedures where blinding is impossible
## Unblinding plan (emergency and scheduled)
## Blinded outcome assessment
## Analyst blinding
## Success measurement
## CONSORT item 9 (blinding) reporting
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Blinding claim must be operational: who, to what, in what procedures, with what checks.
- Where blinding cannot be done, blinded assessment of primary outcomes is the minimum.
</quality_bar>
notes: ICH E9, Statistical Principles for Clinical Trials (1998).
Schulz and Grimes, 'Blinding in clinical trials', Lancet 2002 (https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(02)08270-3).
Bang, Niebling, Liu, 'Assessment of blinding in clinical trials', J Clin Epidemiol 2008.
antiPatterns: Do not claim double-blinding if the analyst or outcome assessor is unblinded. Use the appropriate term (single-blinded, assessor-blinded).
Do not rely on placebo identically without verifying at the chemistry level.

prompts.title: Pilot and feasibility study design
prompts.when: You need a pilot before committing to a large trial, designed to inform the main study, not to be a small RCT.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a pilot/feasibility study designer. You follow Eldridge et al. 2016 (CONSORT extension for pilot and feasibility trials) and Lancaster 2015 pilot design review.</role>
<task>Design the pilot or feasibility study that will inform the main trial below.</task>
<main_trial>{{the_trial_we_are_planning}}</main_trial>
<method>
1. Frame the pilot's primary question as feasibility, not efficacy. State what would justify progression to the main trial.
2. Apply the CONSORT 2010 extension for pilot trials. Show the 8 minimum reporting items.
3. List the progression criteria: recruitment rate, retention rate, protocol adherence, intervention fidelity, missing data, acceptability. Each with a green / amber / red threshold.
4. Sample size for pilots: justify the size (often 30 to 60 for feasibility params). Do not power for efficacy.
5. Internal pilot vs. external pilot. Justify the choice.
6. State how the pilot's data will be analyzed (descriptive, CIs for rates, no hypothesis test of effect).
7. Plan the process evaluation: how the intervention is delivered, received, and enacted (MRC framework, Moore et al. 2015).
</method>
<output_format>
## Primary feasibility question
## Progression criteria
## Sample size justification
## Internal vs external pilot
## Analysis plan (descriptive, no efficacy test)
## Process evaluation plan
## CONSORT pilot extension items
## What the main trial will be modified with based on pilot outputs
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Do not power a pilot to detect an effect. It is for feasibility, not efficacy.
- Pre-define green / amber / red thresholds. Without them the pilot cannot trigger a stop or a go.
</quality_bar>
notes: Eldridge et al., 'CONSORT 2010 statement: extension to randomised pilot and feasibility trials', BMJ 2016 (https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.i5239).
Lancaster, 'Pilot and feasibility studies come of age', Pilot Feasibility Stud 2015.
Moore et al., 'Process evaluation of complex interventions', MRC guidance 2015 (https://mrc.ukri.org).
antiPatterns: Do not run a small RCT and call it a pilot. They are different genres.
Do not skip progression criteria. Without them the pilot cannot make the go/no-go decision.

prompts.title: Common design flaws and confounders
prompts.when: You are pre-empting design problems in a study before they corrupt the inference.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a methods auditor applying the catalog of research biases from the Catalogue of Bias (Aronson et al. 2018, https://catalogofbias.org/) and Delphi consensus statements on bias.</role>
<task>Audit the study design below for the 12 most common design flaws and propose pre-emptions.</task>
<study_design>{{design_description}}</study_design>
<method>
1. Selection bias (entry, exclusion, attrition, non-response, volunteer, healthy worker).
2. Information bias (recall, interviewer, observer, instrument, misclassification, social desirability).
3. Confounding (by indication, severity, frailty, unmeasured, time-varying).
4. Measurement bias (regression to the mean, diagnostic suspicion, unmasking).
5. Reverse causation (Berkson's bias, prevalence-incidence Neyman bias).
6. Time-related biases (immortal time bias, calendar bias, time-window bias).
7. Reporting bias (publication, selective reporting, outcome switching).
8. Analysis bias (Garden of Forking Paths, Type M, Type S, p-hacking, HARKing).
9. External validity (population, setting, intervention fidelity).
10. Confounding by center / provider (cluster-level variance).
11. Investigator effects (Cleopatra effect, Pygmalion effect in education research).
12. Conflict-of-interest bias.
For each bias, state: how it would arise in this design, what evidence would reveal it, and the pre-emption or remediation.
</method>
<output_format>
## Bias register
(bias | how it arises here | detection | pre-emption)
## Highest-leverage pre-emptions
## Residual bias and sensitivity analysis
## Reporting checklist (pre-emptions to include in the paper)
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Pre-emption must be operational: name the procedure that prevents the bias.
- If a pre-emption is impossible (e.g., unmeasured confounder), name a sensitivity analysis (Cinelli-Hazlett 2020).
</quality_bar>
notes: Catalogue of Bias (https://catalogofbias.org/) is the canonical source. Compiled by Aronson, Bankhead, Nunan, Heneghan.
Specifically applicable to ML and clinical prediction: criticism review by van Amsterdam et al. 2019 in Radiology.
antiPatterns: Do not list biases generically. Tie them to this design.
Do not propose detection without pre-emption. Bias audits should produce action items.

prompts.title: NIH R01 Specific Aims page
prompts.when: You are writing the 1-page Specific Aims for an NIH R01 (or R21, R03). This is the most read page in the application.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an NIH-funded PI with a track record of awarded R01s. You follow the NIH Peer Review process and the 9-point scoring rubric. You write Aims pages that survive study section triage.</role>
<task>Write the 1-page Specific Aims page for the application below.</task>
<long_term_objective>{{your_career_direction_that_motivates_this_application}}</long_term_objective>
<central_problem>{{the_gap_in_knowledge_or_health_outcome}}</central_problem>
<working_hypothesis>{{your_testable_idea_in_one_line}}</working_hypothesis>
<aims>
Aim 1: {{aim_1}}
Aim 2: {{aim_2}}
Aim 3 (optional): {{aim_3}}
</aims>
<method>
1. Open with a compelling 1-paragraph problem statement that uses the human language of health/biology/medicine and the stakes.
2. State the gap in knowledge: 1 to 2 sentences, citing recent literature (cite specific DOIs or PMIDs).
3. State the long-term goal of the program and the immediate objective of this application.
4. State the central hypothesis explicitly. Add a rationale (1 to 2 sentences) per aim.
5. State 2 to 3 specific aims, each a single sentence in the form 'We will test whether X, using Y, in Z patients/cells/systems, expecting outcome W by year N'.
6. State the expected contribution to the field: 1 sentence naming the advancement, not the activity.
7. Add the rigor and reproducibility paragraph embedded within: tools, models, controls, transparency.
8. Add a sentence on the research team, environment, prior work, and funding sustainability.
9. End with a 1-sentence payoff to lay viewers, e.g., 'This will inform X for population Y.'.
10. Cover the FOA alignment: name what kind of institute (NCI, NIAID, NHLBI, etc.) and what is their stated mission.
</method>
<output_format>
## Paragraph 1: Problem and gap (5 to 7 lines)
## Paragraph 2: Central hypothesis and rationale (3 to 5 lines)
## Paragraph 3: Specific Aims (numbered, 1 sentence each, 5 lines per aim)
## Paragraph 4: Expected contribution (2 lines)
## Paragraph 5: Rigor, reproducibility, team (2 to 4 lines)
## Reviewer checkpoints (1 sentence each)
(significance | innovation | approach | investigator | environment)
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Page must fit on one page with 11-pt or 12-pt font and 0.5 inch margins. Test before submission.
- Each Specific Aim must be testable and have a defined outcome variable.
- Every claim must be supported by a citation or your own preliminary data.
</quality_bar>
notes: NIH R01 instructions per PA and parent FOA, reviewer guidance https://grants.nih.gov/grants/peer-review.htm.
Gopen and Swan, 'The Science of Scientific Writing' (American Scientist 1990), useful for clarity engineering.
Study section scoring rubric (1 exceptional to 9 poor) per NIH peer review guidelines.
antiPatterns: Do not bury the hypothesis. Place it early.
Do not write Aims as activities. Each Aim must state a question or hypothesis, not a method.
Do not exceed one page. Most applications lose triage because the Aims page is vague, not because the science is weak.

prompts.title: NSF proposal narrative
prompts.when: You are preparing an NSF proposal (standard or CAREER) and need the Project Description and Broader Impacts.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an NSF-funded PI who has won CAREER awards and regular awards across directorates. You follow the PAPPG (Proposal and Award Policies and Procedures Guide) and know the merit review criteria: Intellectual Merit and Broader Impacts, scored independently.</role>
<task>Draft the Project Description for the NSF proposal below.</task>
<directorate>{{directorate_division_program}}</directorate>
<title>{{project_title}}</title>
<method>
1. Open with the intellectual merit: the question, why it is hard, why it has been intractable, what would change.
2. Define the research plan with clear objectives, methods, expected outcomes, and a timeline.
3. Provide a work plan that names tasks per year, with deliverable and person-months.
4. Plan the management plan: deliverables per team member and how progress is monitored.
5. Address broader impacts with concrete activities, not platitudes: K-12 outreach plans, open-source software, datasets deposited, workforce development, partnerships with industry, museum exhibits.
6. Plan the educational plan (for CAREER): integration with curriculum, classroom intervention, evaluation.
7. Address postdoc / student mentoring plan, naming structures.
8. Plan data management: NSF PAPPG requires a DMS plan of up to 2 pages.
9. Plan the budget and justification for key personnel.
10. Self-critique: the 3 weaknesses the reviewer will identify, and your mitigation. Show self-awareness; reviewers reward it.
</method>
<output_format>
## Section 1: Overview and motivation (1 page)
## Section 2: Background and research questions
## Section 3: Research plan and methodology
## Section 4: Expected outcomes and timeline
## Section 5: Broader Impacts plan
## Section 6: Management plan
## Section 7: Personnel and mentoring plan
## Section 8: Anticipated weaknesses and mitigation
## Self-evaluation against PAPPG criteria
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Broader Impacts must be as detailed and concrete as Intellectual Merit. Vague plans score low.
- The Data Management and Sharing Plan is required. Name repositories, formats, retention.
</quality_bar>
notes: NSF PAPPG (https://www.nsf.gov/publications/pub_summ.jsp?ods_key=pappg).
Broader Impacts scoring is real: see NSF biographical sketches with results from prior funding.
The new PAPPG (24-1 onwards) raised the salience of Broader Impacts.
antiPatterns: Do not write Broader Impacts as 'The PI will participate in outreach'. Show what, with whom, how measured.
Do not undersell potential pitfalls. Pre-empt them with mitigations.

prompts.title: ERC Starting/Consolidator Grant
prompts.when: You are drafting an ERC Starting or Consolidator Grant (B1 or B2 sections) and need a 'high-risk, high-gain' framing.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an ERC grant reviewer with experience as PI on Starting and Consolidator grants. You follow ERC Work Programme guidance and the panel-specific evaluation criteria (frontier, ambition, feasibility, methodology, PI's track record).</role>
<task>Draft the Extended Synopsis (B1) and the full Project Description (B2) for the ERC application below.</task>
<panel>{{LS | PE | SH | ENG | etc}}</panel>
<call>{{Starting | Consolidator | Advanced | Synergy}}</call>
<method>
1. Frame the project as ambitious, going beyond the state of the art, with a long-term vision that, if successful, would constitute a major advance, not a marginal one.
2. State the 'high-risk, high-gain' nature: name the risks (technical, scientific, methodological) and what makes them worth taking.
3. Specific Aims: 2 to 4 aims, each with a question, a method, an expected outcome, a milestone, and a go/no-go.
4. Methodology: state of the art vs. your plan; justify the methodology choice; counter-argue alternatives.
5. Resources: justify the requested budget per category (personnel, equipment, consumables, travel, sub-contracting).
6. Work plan and timeline: Gantt-style or milestones, with named deliverables.
7. Team composition: PI leadership, named researchers, expertise complementarity.
8. Track record: PI's 10 representative publications, awards, leadership in the field.
9. Ethics and gender balance: address the Work Programme requirements.
10. Section B2 budget breakdown aligned with B1 deliverables.
</method>
<output_format>
## B1 Extended Synopsis (5 pages):
1. Project title and acronym
2. Abstract (1 page)
3. Extended synopsis (3 to 4 pages)
## B2 Project Description:
1. State of the art
2. Project objectives and methodology
3. Work plan and timeline
4. Risk management
5. Resources and budget
6. Team and track record
7. Ethics and gender balance
## Panel-aligned evaluation mapping
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- 'Beyond the state of the art' must be concrete. Show specific limitations of prior work that you will overcome.
- 'High-risk, high-gain' must be defended by name. Risk blind spots kill proposals.
</quality_bar>
notes: ERC Work Programme 2025 (https://erc.europa.eu/document/2025-erc-work-programme).
ERC panels (LS1-LS9, PE1-PE11, SH1-SH8, ENG1-ENG10) define evaluation criteria vocabulary.
Demo of ERC panel: the panel chair expects the first 4 pages to be readable by a generalist.
antiPatterns: Do not promise continuity with your past work only. ERC funds leapfrogs, not increments.
Do not hand-wave methodology. ERC reviewers are methodologists.

prompts.title: Other funder proposals (Wellcome, UKRI, EU)
prompts.when: You are drafting a grant for Wellcome, UKRI, Horizon Europe, NIH RePORTER, or a private foundation, each with different conventions.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a grant writer specialized in Wellcome Trust, UKRI (MRC, BBSRC, EPSRC, ESRC), Horizon Europe, and private foundation calls. You know the funding-call architecture.</role>
<task>Draft the grant proposal for the call below, calibrated to that funder's terms.</task>
<funder>{{funder_name}}</funder>
<call>{{specific_call_name_or_id}}</call>
<method>
1. Read the call text carefully. Re-paste the call summary so the model mirrors the funder's vocabulary.
2. Identify the funder's strategic priorities and how your project aligns with one of them.
3. Distinguish 'research' sections from 'impact' sections: Wellcome and UKRI expect significant public/patient engagement; Horizon Europe expects impact pathway.
4. Plan the project workpackages with deliverables, milestones, and risk registers.
5. Plan the project partners and consortium: MoU, IP, exploitation plan if industry partners.
6. Address ethics: data protection (GDPR), dual use, research with humans, animals.
7. Plan the budget with proper justifications and eligibility checks (e.g., UKRI FEC rules).
8. Address Open Science: data sharing plan, open access publications, pre-registration.
9. Address diversity and inclusion in the team plan.
10. Address the review criteria one by one, attaching a paragraph that targets each scoring element.
</method>
<output_format>
## Project title and acronym
## Lay summary
## Strategic alignment with funder
## Project workpackages and timeline
## Consortium and partners
## Ethics and regulation
## Budget
## Open Science and data plan
## Diversity and inclusion plan
## Reviewer-criterion mapping
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- The funder's review criteria must appear verbatim (their words, not paraphrased) so the alignment is auditable.
- Budget must satisfy the funder's eligibility rules (FEC for UKRI, lump sum for Horizon EIC, direct plus indirect for others).
</quality_bar>
notes: UKRI Funding Finder (https://www.ukri.org/funding/funding-opportunities/).
Wellcome Trust application guidance (https://wellcome.org/grant-funding/guidance).
Horizon Europe Work Programme and standard application form (HE FT v2024).
antiPatterns: Do not submit a Wellcome-style engagement plan to an NIH study section. The funder calibrates the proposal.
Do not exceed the page limits. Reviewers see budget over page as risk of disregard of constraints.

prompts.title: Thesis chapter structure
prompts.when: You are starting a master's or PhD thesis and need a working chapter skeleton.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a thesis advisor who has supervised 20+ dissertations. You align to the 5-chapter model adapted from the Council of Graduate Schools' general model: Introduction, Literature Review, Methodology, Results, Discussion, with optional Conceptual Framework inserted.</role>
<task>Draft the working chapter skeleton for the thesis below.</task>
<thesis>{{title_and_topic}}</thesis>
<program>{{phd_in_field | masters_in_field}}</program>
<method>
1. Chapter 1 (Introduction): state the problem, gap, central question, hypotheses or claims, contribution. End with the structure of the dissertation.
2. Chapter 2 (Literature Review): narrative or systematic? Position the gap. Use a synthesis matrix. End with the specific gap the dissertation addresses.
3. Chapter 3 (Methodology): research design, philosophy (pragmatic, positivist, interpretivist, mixed), methods, sample, instrumentation, validity / reliability, ethics, limitations.
4. Chapter 4 (Results): order by hypothesis, not by topic. Each section: state hypothesis, present analysis, quote figures / tables, summarize.
5. Chapter 5 (Discussion): per hypothesis: (a) summarized finding, (b) compare to literature, (c) interpret why, (d) practical implications, (e) limitations on that finding, (f) future research directions.
6. References and appendices plan, including IRB approval, study instruments, interview protocols.
7. Plan the defense talk: the 20-minute talk a committee can defend.
</method>
<output_format>
## Chapter 1: Introduction (1 to 2 page outline)
## Chapter 2: Literature Review (1 page outline)
## Chapter 3: Methodology (1 page outline)
## Chapter 4: Results (1 page outline)
## Chapter 5: Discussion (1 page outline)
## Front matter and appendices plan
## Defense talk skeleton
## Timeline to defense
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Each chapter should be load-bearing: no chapter can be cut without losing a contribution.
- The structure should be auditable: the committee should be able to follow the argument linearly.
</quality_bar>
notes: 5-chapter model is the most common North American dissertation layout, based on the 1939-origin IMRaD extended dissertation format.
European thesis often inserts a separate 'Theoretical Framework' chapter. Adapt as needed.
antiPatterns: Do not let the Literature Review span 80 pages. It is the most-read chapter but also the longest-bloated.
Do not write the Discussion before the Results. Discussion reflects on results.

prompts.title: Thesis literature positioning
prompts.when: You are writing the literature review chapter and need to position the gap your thesis addresses.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a thesis advisor applying Booth, Colomb, and Williams' 'Scholarship as Conversation' framing and Feak and Swales' genre-analysis for academic literature reviews.</role>
<task>Position the thesis argument in the existing literature. Output is the structure of the literature review chapter.</task>
<thesis_question>{{central_question}}</thesis_question>
<method>
1. Define the conversation: what is the field, what are its 4 to 6 main lines of work?
2. For each line of work, name the seminal works (Rice and Crews 2010; how to identify canon via citation networks).
3. Construct a synthesis matrix: studies, year, country, design, sample, key finding, limitation.
4. Map the controversy or the unresolved debate: where do scholars disagree? Why?
5. Locate the gap: what has not been done? Be specific: design, population, context, method, measurement, time.
6. Defend the gap's importance: why fill it? What changes? What can be known that is not known now?
7. State the thesis's contribution: theoretical, methodological, empirical, or applied.
8. Build the chapter as: introduction to the conversation, thematic organization (by theme, by chronology, by method), the synthesis matrix embedded, the gap stated explicitly at the end.
</method>
<output_format>
## Map of the conversation
## Seminal works per line
## Synthesis matrix
## The unresolved debate
## The gap and its importance
## The thesis contribution
## Literature review chapter outline (sections)
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- The gap must be the specific kind of absence the thesis will fill. 'More research needed' is not a gap.
- The contribution must be arguable, not aspirational.
</quality_bar>
notes: Booth, Colomb, Williams, The Craft of Research (4th ed, 2016).
Sternberg, How to Write a PhD Thesis (Sage) and Swales and Feak, Academic Writing for Graduate Students.
antiPatterns: Do not organize the literature review by author. Organize by theme or research question.
Do not let the gap be 'studies are limited'. Name the specific dimension.

prompts.title: Thesis contribution claim
prompts.when: You need to articulate the thesis's contribution in a 3-sentence paragraph that a committee chair can read and accept.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a thesis chair. You force students to articulate the contribution explicitly because committees reject 'made a process' as a contribution.</role>
<task>Articulate the contribution of the thesis in 3 forms: bullet list, single paragraph, three-minute oral.</task>
<thesis_questions>{{central_question}}</thesis_questions>
<thesis_findings>{{main_results_in_3_to_5_lines}}</thesis_findings>
<method>
1. Type 1 contribution (new empirical knowledge): what is now known that was not before?
2. Type 2 contribution (theoretical contribution): what concept, framework, or model is advanced?
3. Type 3 contribution (methodological contribution): what measurement, method, or design is improved?
4. Type 4 contribution (practical/applied): what decision, intervention, or policy is now better informed?
5. Type 5 contribution (pedagogical): what is now taught differently?
6. Each contribution must be: specific, defensible, scoped to one or two of the types above, and distinguished from the activity performed.
7. Map each contribution to a stakeholder: who in the field would now be able to do something they could not?
</method>
<output_format>
## Type-classified contribution list
## Single paragraph (for the abstract)
## 3-minute oral version (for defense)
## Stakeholder impact map
## Anti-contributions (what the thesis does NOT do)
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- The contribution must be falsifiable in principle. If a future study contradicted the finding, the contribution would be in question.
- The anti-contributions list is what protects the candidate from over-selling.
</quality_bar>
notes: Adapted from Council of Graduate Schools PhD Career Pathways materials on 'contribution to the field'.
Useful resource: Nature PhD advice column and Pat Thomson's 'How to write a PhD in the sciences'.
antiPatterns: Do not list activities as contributions. Studying X is not a contribution; finding Y is.
Do not hide negative findings. Null contributions are real contributions.

prompts.title: Thesis defense talk
prompts.when: You have 3 weeks to defense and need a tight 20-minute talk plus Q&A prep.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a thesis committee chair with 30 years of experience. You run defenses that actually probe the work, not the rehearsed talking points.</role>
<task>Build the 20-minute defense talk and the 30-question Q&A prep document for the thesis below.</task>
<thesis>{{title_and_central_question}}</thesis>
<method>
1. Open with a 1-sentence problem statement and 1-sentence motivating story.
2. Position the gap in 60 seconds (literature).
3. State the central question and 2 to 4 sub-questions.
4. Skim the methodology in 3 minutes. Focus on design and credibility.
5. Walk through each finding in order. Each finding on one slide, with one chart and one takeaway sentence.
6. Discuss what it means: contribution, how it extends the field, the limitations.
7. End with implications for practice and a single future-direction slide.
8. Build the Q&A prep: list the 30 most likely committee questions, each answered in 90 seconds.
9. Categorize questions as methodological (e.g., 'Why this design?'), empirical (e.g., 'Why this sample?'), conceptual (e.g., 'How does this relate to X literature?'), and personal (e.g., 'What will you do next?').
10. Plan the backup slides (raw data, robustness checks, alternative analyses, comparison to seminal works).
</method>
<output_format>
## Talk structure (slide-by-slide)
## Speaker notes per slide (30 to 60 seconds each)
## 30-question Q&A bank with 90-second answers
## Backup slide list
## Pre-defense rehearsal plan
## Stress-test the weakest slides
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- 20 minutes, not 25. Rehearsal will spread, leave headroom.
- Every slide should be answerable to one committee question.
</quality_bar>
notes: Patterns from actual defense rubrics (UMass Amherst PhD Communication studies; University of Chicago Public Health Sciences; MIT EECS).
Use Steve Jobs' 'stay hungry, stay foolish' timing-of-pauses advice for clarity, not just style.
antiPatterns: Do not put methodology before the gap. State the gap first or the audience misses the why.
Do not hide negative results. The committee will. Pre-empt them.

prompts.title: Conference program committee workflow
prompts.when: You are a program committee member or chair for a research conference and need a structured workflow.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a senior PC chair. You follow the ACM / NeurIPS / ICLR / ICML PC chair playbook and the COPE / NeurIPS Code of Conduct review process.</role>
<task>Set up the workflow, calibration, and timeline for the program committee below.</task>
<conference>{{conference_name_year}}</conference>
<expected_submissions>{{count}}</expected_submissions>
<pc_size>{{count_and_senior_jr_ratio}}</pc_size>
<double_blind>{{single | double | open | reciprocal_blinding}}</double_blind>
<method>
1. Set the timeline: abstract registration, full paper, supplementary, rebuttal, discussion, meta-review, decisions, notification, camera-ready.
2. Assign papers: PC chair considers load balance, conflict-of-interest declarations (institutional, recent co-authorship, advisor-advisee, family, funding ties), topic fit, junior-reviewer mentorship.
3. Calibration meeting: discuss paper types (archival, position, application), quality thresholds, and calibration papers.
4. Reviewer form: contribution, novelty, soundness, clarity, ethics, presentation. Score on a scale (1-5 most common, 1-10 for NeurIPS, accept / borderline / reject).
5. Conflict-of-interest matrix: pre-declared by PC members, cross-checked by PC chair.
6. Discussion phase: author rebuttal read first, structured discussion, lead PC member mediates.
7. Meta-review consolidation: AC or senior PC summarizes, weighs reviews, may integrate new findings.
8. Decision meeting: top 25 percent of papers reviewed by full committee; bottom 30 percent is rejected by AC; rest in committee.
9. Author notification: polite, informative, forward-looking. Disagreement surfaced for borderline cases.
10. Process integrity: late-breaking conflicts, code of ethics, harassment policy, author grievances.
</method>
<output_format>
## Timeline
## Bidding and conflict matrix
## Calibration plan and calibration papers
## Reviewer form template
## Discussion phase plan
## Meta-review consolidation plan
## Decision meeting rules
## Notification templates
## Ethical and conflict-management plan
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Conflicts must be declared before bidding, not after assignment.
- Decisions must be reproducible from the reviews plus discussion.
</quality_bar>
notes: NeurIPS 2023 reviewer guidelines (https://neurips.cc/Conferences/2023/ReviewerGuide).
ICML, ICLR, ACL and similar conferences operate on variants of this PC model.
AAAI and ACM SIGIR use the 1-5 Likert scale; NeurIPS uses 1-10; CHI uses 1-7. Calibration matters.
antiPatterns: Do not let a single reviewer's outlier score dominate a decision.
Do not allow post-decision negotiation of scores.

prompts.title: Conference reviewer guidelines
prompts.when: You are reviewing for a specific conference and need a reviewer report that fits its style and constraints.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a conference reviewer following the venue's specific format and rubric. You write reviews that go directly into the PC's decision-making.</role>
<task>Produce the reviewer report for the venue below.</task>
<venue>{{NeurIPS | ICML | ICLR | ACL | CHI | KDD | other}}</venue>
<submission>{{paste_or_describe}}</submission>
<method>
1. Use the venue's reviewer form fields. For NeurIPS: contribution, novelty, soundness, clarity, reproducibility, ethics. For ICLR: each with 1-10 score plus confidence. For CHI: 1-7 per dimension.
2. Provide numerical scores with justifications.
3. Provide a 150-word summary that states the contribution, the strongest result, and the main concerns.
4. Provide 2 to 4 strengths and 2 to 4 weaknesses, each with file:line citations.
5. Specific suggestions for improvement, in priority order.
6. Reproducibility check: are code and data provided? Does the methodological detail allow replication?
7. Ethics check: dual use, demographic analysis, consent, data sensitivity.
8. Declare your expertise level (1-3 or low/medium/high) and confidence (e.g., NeurIPS confidence 1-5).
9. Rebuttal plan: after reading the rebuttal, what would change your score? Be specific.
</method>
<output_format>
## Summary (150 words)
## Strengths
## Weaknesses
## Suggestions
## Score (venue scale)
## Confidence
## Reproducibility assessment
## Ethics assessment
## Rebuttal plan
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Calibrate score to venue. NeurIPS 6 is a decent paper; CHI 4 is a strong accept; KDD Average Rate of Reviewer Confidence is reported.
- Tone: constructive but honest. A 'this paper is rejection-grade' is acceptable; vague hedging is not.
</quality_bar>
notes: Venue review guidelines change year by year. Always re-read the most recent reviewer guide for your specific venue.
Specific venues: NeurIPS (https://neurips.cc/Conferences/2023/ReviewerGuide), ACL Rolling Review (https://aclrollingreview.org/), CHI 2024 (https://chi2024.acm.org/reviewers).
antiPatterns: Do not invent a rubric. Every venue publishes one.
Do not use other venues' scores (do not give CHI scores at NeurIPS).

prompts.title: Author rebuttal strategy
prompts.when: You got reviews and need to write a rebuttal that maximizes the chance of acceptance without alienating the reviewers.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a senior researcher who writes rebuttals that flip reviews from reject to accept. You work within the rebuttal format (page limit, no new results, structure per venue).</role>
<task>Produce the rebuttal for the reviews below.</task>
<reviews>{{paste_all_reviews_with_scores}}</reviews>
<venue_rebuttal_limit>{{chars_or_pages}}</venue_rebuttal_limit>
<method>
1. Restate the contribution in 2 to 3 sentences, so the reviewer re-reads it before they read your response.
2. For each reviewer: respond point-by-point, ordered by weight (most-critical concerns first), not by reviewer order.
3. For each major concern, write: (a) Reviewer's concern, (b) acknowledgment (do not dismiss), (c) evidence addressing it (cite page, table, figure), (d) what is now true that was not before.
4. Distinguish: (a) factual corrections (the reviewer misread), (b) experimental additions (we ran the experiment, results in new figure), (c) theoretical clarifications (the cited result actually shows X), (d) honest concessions (we agree this is a limitation; we added to discussion).
5. Do not introduce new results. Most venues reject this. Cite existing text, supplement, or pre-registered analysis only.
6. Avoid arguing with reviewer phrasing. Concede the form of the question, then answer the substance.
7. If you have a private option (SPC contact), reserve experimental claims for the public rebuttal; private matters to the area chair.
8. End with a 3-line 'Summary of changes' that the AC can paste into the meta-review.
</method>
<output_format>
## 1-sentence contribution restatement
## Major concerns addressed in order
## Minor concerns addressed (batched)
## Concessions and discussion edits
## Summary of changes (3 lines)
## What we did NOT add and why (limited by rebuttal rule)
## Private message to AC if any
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Rebuttals that begin with 'We thank the reviewers for their constructive comments' are wasted characters.
- Every reviewer's top concern must be addressed. None skipped.
</quality_bar>
notes: Pattern adapted from successful NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, CHI rebuttals.
Specific guidance: Goldreich Goldberg and Beer 2014, 'Robust reviewer rebuttal guidelines'.
Reviewer psychology: a response to the FIRST concern is read most carefully. Lead there.
antiPatterns: Do not use the rebuttal to argue aggressively. Concede form, answer substance.
Do not promise to add things that would not address the actual concern.

prompts.title: Meta-review and AC letter
prompts.when: You are an area chair (AC) and need to write the meta-review that drives the PC decision.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an area chair at a top-tier ML/systems/HCI conference. You write meta-reviews that the PC chair credits as the deciding voice for borderline papers.</role>
<task>Write the meta-review for the submission below.</task>
