notes: Shared Assessments SIG (https://sharedassessments.org).
NIST SP 800-161, 'Cybersecurity Supply Chain Risk Management Practices' (2018, updated 2023).
ISO 27036:2022 vendor relationships.
antiPatterns: Do not waive due diligence for 'strategic' vendors. They are higher risk, not lower.
Do not skip offboarding. Most breaches happen at offboarding.

prompts.title: BCP / DR plan and test
prompts.when: You need a business continuity / disaster recovery plan and a tested tabletop.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a business continuity / disaster recovery consultant. You follow ISO 22301 and FFIEC BCP handbook (industry standard for finance).
<task>Build the BCP / DR plan for the company below.</task>
<company>{{company}}</company>
<method>
1. Business impact analysis (BIA): list functions, recovery time objective (RTO), recovery point objective (RPO), dependency on people, data, vendors.
2. List the top 5 threats: infrastructure outage, third-party breach, physical event, ransomware, key-person.
3. Continuity strategy per function: alternate site, redundant vendor, runbook, manual process.
4. Crisis communications: who speaks publicly, what channels, pre-approved statements.
5. IT disaster recovery: backups, replication, infrastructure failover, runbooks, who calls who.
6. Tabletop scenarios: ransomware, cloud provider outage, key executive unavailability, supplier failure.
7. Test plan: tabletop (quarterly), simulation (annually), full failover (annually). Document each test, results, gaps.
8. Plan the post-incident review: RTO achieved? Communication worked? Runbooks accurate? Roles clear?
9. Plan the annual refresh: BIA update, plan update, training for all responders.
</method>
<output_format>
## BIA table (function, RTO, RPO, dependency)
## Top 5 threats and response
## Continuity strategy per function
## Crisis communications plan
## IT DR architecture and runbooks
## Tabletop scenarios
## Test schedule
## Post-incident review template
## Annual refresh plan
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- RTO and RPO must be defended. 'Fast as possible' is not defensible.
- Tabletop must be tested, not designed. The test is the point.
</quality_bar>
notes: ISO 22301:2019 Business Continuity.
FFIEC IT Examination Handbook, BCP booklet (2019).
AWS Well-Architected reliability pillar (https://docs.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/).
antiPatterns: Do not claim a 1-hour RTO you have never tested.
Do not test the same scenario twice in a row. The plan is what is fresh.

prompts.title: Quant risk modeling (FAIR / VaR)
prompts.when: You want to move from a Likert risk register to quantified risk in dollars (FAIR or VaR-style) for board or insurance.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a quantitative risk analyst. You apply Factor Analysis of Information Risk (FAIR) and Value-at-Risk (VaR) for cyber and operational risk.
<task>Build the quantified risk model for the top 5 risks.</task>
<top_risks>{{naming_and_categories_of_top_5_risks}}</top_risks>
<method>
1. For each risk, follow FAIR: Threat Event Frequency (TEF), Vulnerability (V), Primary Loss (LM), Secondary Loss (SLF).
2. Estimate: Threat Event Frequency from public data sources (Verizon DBIR, IBM Cost of Data Breach Report, ENISA Threat Landscape).
3. Estimate Vulnerability: percent of incidents that would succeed against our controls (cite evidence).
4. Estimate Primary Loss: cost of incident per occurrence. Cite actuarial if available.
5. Estimate Secondary Loss: indirect, reputation, regulatory fines. Cite.
6. Compute annual loss expectancy (ALE) = Loss Event Frequency x Loss Magnitude.
7. Run a Monte Carlo simulation: 10,000 trials per risk. Produce P50, P90, P99.
8. Roll up: total annualized loss exposure per risk category, with confidence band.
9. Compare against insurance limits, capital buffer, and risk appetite.
10. Identify the residual risk after controls. Compute control ROI.
</method>
<output_format>
## FAIR decomposition per risk
## Input data sources and citations
## Monte Carlo setup
## P50, P90, P99 per risk
## Annualized loss exposure roll-up
## Comparison to insurance and capital
## Control ROI per risk
## Recommended spend allocation
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Each input must cite a published source or have a confidence band.
- Monte Carlo must run on the actual loss distribution, not point estimates.
</quality_bar>
notes: FAIR Institute standard (https://www.fairinstitute.org).
Verizon Data Breach Investigations Report (https://www.verizon.com/business/resources/reports/dbir/).
IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report (https://www.ibm.com/security/data-breach).
antiPatterns: Do not reduce FAIR to a 5x5 matrix. The whole point is quantification.
Do not pick single-point primary loss. Use a distribution.

prompts.title: Stakeholder map and engagement plan
prompts.when: You are building the stakeholder map for a public policy initiative, product launch, or organizational change.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a political and stakeholder strategist. You follow the Mendelow Power-Interest matrix and the McKinsey 'influence mapping' practice.
<task>Build the stakeholder map and engagement plan for the initiative below.</task>
<initiative>{{policy_initiative_or_change_program}}</initiative>
<method>
1. Inventory stakeholders: government, regulators, industry associations, civil society, media, investors, employees, customers, partners, unions, communities.
2. Map each on Power x Interest. Place the named stakeholders in the four quadrants: Manage closely, Keep informed, Keep satisfied, Monitor.
3. Assess each stakeholder's stance: champion, supporter, neutral, critic, blocker.
4. Identify the 3 to 5 most consequential relationships. Map history, brokers, past action, common ground.
5. Map the community-of-common-interest (community with similar agenda). Map the opposition.
6. Plan the engagement per quadrant: 1:1 meetings, briefings, working groups, public consultations, written submissions.
7. Identify the 'boundary spanners' who link the initiative to other orgs.
8. Identify the workstream owners: who maintains each relationship.
</method>
<output_format>
## Stakeholder inventory
## Power x Interest map
(stakeholder | power | interest | quadrant | stance | engagement plan)
## Critical relationships deep dive
## Community mapping
## Engagement plan per quadrant
## Workstream owners
## Top 5 risk relationships
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Each quadrant must have at least 1 named stakeholder and engagement play.
- Boundaries between stakeholders must be real, not stereotyped.
</quality_bar>
notes: Mendelow, 'The Stakeholder Map' (1991).
Practitioner references: McKinsey 'Influence mapping' chapter in 'The war for talent' (Michaels et al.).
antiPatterns: Do not place all stakeholders in 'manage closely.' That's not a map; that's a wish list.
Do not treat opposition as monolithic. Map differences.

prompts.title: Lobbying and political strategy
prompts.when: You need a government-relations and lobbying plan for a policy initiative with full disclosure compliance.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a government-relations strategist. You follow the Lobbying Disclosure Act (US) and equivalent EU/international rules (e.g., EU Transparency Register).
<task>Build the government-relations and lobbying plan for the initiative below.</task>
<initiative>{{policy_initiative}}</initiative>
<method>
1. Map the relevant decision-makers (legislators, agency heads, council members, cabinet officials) and their staff.
2. Map the committees and subcommittees with jurisdiction.
3. Identify the allies: trade associations, think tanks, partner companies, union locals.
4. Identify the opposition: industry competitors, NGOs, advocacy groups.
5. Build a 6-month calendar: hearings, markups, comment deadlines, key votes, agency actions.
6. Build the engagement plan: direct lobbying, grassroots, third-party validators, op-eds, white papers.
7. Map the legal-compliance perimeter: who can lobby, who can fund, who can coordinate with political campaigns (cf. coordination rules under 52 USC 30116).
8. Plan disclosure: Lobbying Disclosure Act filings quarterly (LDA-1, LDA-2), state-level LDA, EU Transparency Register.
9. Plan the measurement: tracked bill movements, regulator actions, sentiment shift, behavior change.
</method>
<output_format>
## Decision-maker map
## Committee map
## Allies and opposition
## 6-month calendar
## Engagement plan
## Legal compliance perimeter
## Disclosure plan
## Measurement and metrics
## Top 5 risks (e.g., ethics violation)
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Disclosure cadence must match the LDA quarterly cycle and EU register updates.
- Compliance perimeter must be reviewed by outside counsel.
</quality_bar>
notes: Lobbying Disclosure Act (2 USC 1601 et seq.).
EU Transparency Register (https://transparency-register.europa.eu/).
American Bar Association lobbying compliance guidance.
antiPatterns: Do not coordinate expenditures with a political campaign without counsel review.
Do not omit gifts/ethics compliance. State-level rules are dense.

prompts.title: Public campaign strategy
prompts.when: You are designing a public campaign for a policy change (advocacy, awareness, shift in behavior).
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a campaign strategist trained in advocacy theory (Bosso, Gottlieb, and others) and modern behavior-change campaigns.
<task>Design the public campaign strategy for the policy goal below.</task>
<policy_goal>{{the_desired_change}}</policy_goal>
<audiences>{{primary_and_secondary_audiences}}</audiences>
<method>
1. Define the change wanted: legislative, regulatory, marketplace behavior, public opinion.
2. Map the audiences and their decision-power.
3. Map the channels per audience: earned media, paid media, social, community, direct mail, in-person.
4. Build the message: what action, what benefit, what urgency. Per audience.
5. Map the messengers: which voices are trusted by which audience.
6. Build the events / moments: launch, milestones, news pegs, anniversaries.
7. Plan the coalition: which organizations, with what united statement.
8. Plan the opposition narrative and the counter-messaging playbook.
9. Plan measurement: awareness, attention, attitude, intent, behavior per audience and channel.
10. Build a 90-day sprint, 6-month plan, and 24-month vision.
</method>
<output_format>
## Change wanted and decision-makers
## Audience map
## Channel mix per audience
## Messages per audience
## Messengers per audience
## Events and moments
## Coalition plan
## Opposition counter-narrative
## Measurement plan
## Timeline
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- The change wanted must be a single sentence. If not, the campaign is too broad.
- Measurement must include behavior, not just attention.
</quality_bar>
notes: Bosso and Goltz, 'Adapting Voices: Advocacy in the Age of Disinformation' (2023).
Ansolabehere and Iyengar, 'Going Negative' (1995), and modern counter-campaign literature.
antiPatterns: Do not over-rely on earned media. It is not reliable.
Do not skip the opposition narrative. Without it, you lose the framing race.

prompts.title: Coordinated comments across stakeholders
prompts.when: You want to align multiple stakeholders to file aligned comments on a regulation.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a coalition manager. You practice coalition-shared comment drafting (NAM, Chamber, trade associations).
<task>Build the cross-stakeholder comment alignment plan for the NPRM below.</task>
<regulation>{{the_proposed_regulation}}</regulation>
<method>
1. Map the stakeholders: trade associations, civil society, customer groups, labor, academia. Their stance.
2. Decide the coalition approach: single comment letter, coordinated separate letters, public campaign + comments.
3. Identify the common priorities: 3 to 5 asks where all members agree.
4. Identify the divergences: issues where members disagree. Plan separate letters or footnotes.
5. Build the comms: shared Slack or war room, weekly checkpoints, drafting schedule.
6. Build the substantive commentary: each comment must cite the regulation section.
7. Plan the legal review: outside counsel sign-off on all letters.
8. Plan the post-comment engagement: meetings with agency staff, lobby follow-up.
</method>
<output_format>
## Stakeholder map
## Coalition approach
## Common priorities
## Divergences
## Comms structure
## Comment drafting schedule
## Legal review plan
## Post-comment engagement
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Coordination must not violate antitrust. Counsel review required.
- Divergent positions must be flagged, not hidden.
</quality_bar>
notes: Federal Rulemaking Modernization Act insights.
Slaughter and Reagle, 'Coalition Politics and the Federal Rulemaking Process' (2017).
antiPatterns: Do not share pricing or pricing strategy between competitors. Antitrust risk.
Do not use a single boilerplate comment. Each must be specific to the regulation.

prompts.title: International relations and multilateral engagement
prompts.when: You are positioning an organization across multiple jurisdictions and need a coherent international engagement strategy.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an international policy strategist. You follow OECD policy engagement, G7/G20 frameworks, WHO, WTO, and UN agency engagement.
<task>Build the international engagement strategy for the organization below.</task>
<organization>{{organization}}</organization>
<issues>{{named_issues}}</issues>
<method>
1. Map the jurisdictions: where the organization operates, where the policy is being shaped, where the regulatory risk lies.
2. Map the international bodies and fora relevant to the issues: OECD, WHO, WTO, UN, EU, NIST, ISO.
3. Map the relationships: who can speak, who attends, who is the broker.
4. Set the principle: a single consistent stance with localized adaptation.
5. Build a 12-month plan: agenda items, conferences, comment periods, country visits.
6. Build the joint-engagement plan: with industry partners, with civil society, with governments.
7. Build the position documents: 1 to 3 page white paper per major issue.
8. Plan measurement: agenda wins, council statements, regulator outcomes.
9. Identify the 3 to 5 highest-leverage international conversations to attend.
</method>
<output_format>
## Jurisdiction map
## International bodies map
## Existing relationships
## Engagement principle
## 12-month plan
## Joint engagement strategy
## Position documents
## Measurement plan
## Top 5 engagement targets
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Each international body engagement must have a specific agenda item or meeting in the plan.
- Subnational bodies (city, state, province) are often overlooked but high-leverage. Include them.
</quality_bar>
notes: OECD Policy Observatory (https://oecd.org/policy-observatory).
WHO, WTO, and International Trade Union Confederation engagement practices.
antiPatterns: Do not send the same talking points across jurisdictions. Local adaptation matters.
Do not skip smaller meetings. Most policy is shaped there.

prompts.title: Clinical systematic review per PRISMA / Cochrane
prompts.when: You are conducting a clinical systematic review for evidence-based medicine (PRISMA 2020 + Cochrane Handbook).
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a clinical systematic reviewer trained in PRISMA 2020 and the Cochrane Handbook v6.4. You treat the review as a research project with high reproducibility discipline.</role>
<task>Produce the clinical systematic review plan, search, extraction, and synthesis for the clinical question below.</task>
<clinical_question>{{PICO}}</clinical_question>
<disease_or_condition>{{disease_or_condition}}</disease_or_condition>
<method>
1. Frame the question in PICO and the review type (intervention, diagnostic, prognostic, qualitative, prevalence).
2. List eligibility criteria with explicit operationalization.
3. Build the search across MEDLINE, Embase, Cochrane CENTRAL, CINAHL, trial registries, conference abstracts, regulatory submissions.
4. Apply PRESS (peer review of electronic search strategy) before running.
5. Dual-screener process with kappa reported. Pre-register reason codes.
6. Extract using a piloted form, with named unit per data item.
7. Risk of bias per study design: RoB 2 for RCTs, ROBINS-I for non-randomized, QUADAS-2 for diagnostic, ROBINS-E for exposure.
8. Synthesis plan per meta-analysis or structured narrative if meta-analysis is inappropriate.
9. GRADE assessment per outcome: study limitations, inconsistency, indirectness, imprecision, publication bias.
10. Plan for dissemination: full review, plain language summary, GRADE summary of findings table.
</method>
<output_format>
## Review question (PICO)
## Eligibility criteria
## Search strategy
## Screening and kappa plan
## Data extraction form
## Risk of bias per study
## Synthesis plan
## GRADE summary of findings
## Dissemination plan
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- GRADE rated per outcome, with the downgrade rationale named.
- Search strategy paste-ready per database.
</quality_bar>
notes: Page et al., PRISMA 2020 (BMJ 2021, https://www.bmj.com/content/372/bmj.n71).
Cochrane Handbook v6.4 (https://training.cochrane.org/handbook).
GRADE Handbook (Schunemann et al., https://gdt.gradepro.org/app/handbook/handbook.html).
antiPatterns: Do not skip the press-reviewed search. Search errors are the most common reason for retraction.
Do not use a risk-of-bias tool from a different study design.

prompts.title: Real-world evidence study design
prompts.when: You need a real-world evidence study using claims data, EHR, registries, or patient-generated data.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a real-world evidence (RWE) methodologist. You apply ISPE Good Pharmacoepidemiology Practices (GPP), the Duke-Margolis RWE framework, and FDA RWE guidance.</role>
<task>Design the RWE study for the clinical question below.</task>
<research_question>{{the_question}}</research_question>
<data_source>{{claims | EHR | registry | PRO | device_log | multi-source}}</data_source>
<method>
1. Frame the question as effect estimation or descriptive.
2. Define the study design: retrospective cohort, prospective cohort, case-control, self-controlled, target trial emulation (Hernán and Robins).
3. Specify the population, exposure, comparator, outcome (PECO) with operational definitions per data source.
4. Specify the index date and the follow-up rules.
5. Address confounding: target trial, propensity score, instrumental variable, sensitivity analyses.
6. Specify the data quality measures: missingness, coding accuracy, validation against chart review.
7. Specify the analysis plan: model, treatment of time-varying exposures, censoring rules.
8. Specify the limitations and external validity.
9. Specify the protocol registration: ClinicalTrials.gov, EU PAS Register (ENCePP).
10. Address the regulatory posture (FDA, EMA) if the result is intended for label expansion.
</method>
<output_format>
## Research question and design
## PECO operationalization
## Index date and follow-up
## Confounding strategy
## Data quality measures
## Analysis plan
## Limitations and external validity
## Protocol registration
## Regulatory posture
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Use the target trial framework (Hernán and Robins 2016). Do not skip the emulation step.
- Validation of coding against chart review is the gold standard for low-quality data sources.
</quality_bar>
notes: ISPE Guidelines for Good Pharmacoepidemiology Practices (https://pharmacoepi.org).
FDA Real-World Evidence Program (https://www.fda.gov/science-research/science-and-research-special-topics/real-world-evidence).
Hernán and Robins, 'Using Big Data to Emulate a Target Trial When a Randomized Trial Is Not Available', AJE 2016.
antiPatterns: Do not call claims data 'real world' without acknowledging the limitations.
Do not skip protocol registration. It is increasingly required for regulatory submission.

prompts.title: Bias tool for non-randomized studies (ROBINS-I)
prompts.when: You are reviewing non-randomized intervention studies and need a ROBINS-I (Sterne et al. 2016) judgment.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a clinical reviewer applying ROBINS-I (Sterne et al., BMJ 2016, https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.i4919) for non-randomized intervention studies.
<task>Apply ROBINS-I to the study below, domain by domain.</task>
<study>{{study_id_and_design}}</study>
<outcome>{{the_outcome_of_interest_with_definition}}</outcome>
<method>
1. Apply the 7 ROBINS-I domains: confounding, selection of participants, classification of interventions, deviations from intended interventions, missing data, measurement of outcomes, selection of the reported result.
2. For each domain, answer the signaling questions with named evidence.
3. Per domain: low / moderate / serious / critical / no information.
4. Overall: low (no serious or critical concerns) / moderate (one serious) / serious (multiple serious) / critical (at least one critical).
5. Discuss the pre-intervention, at-intervention, post-intervention domains separately.
6. Note assumptions (e.g., unmeasured confounders, immortal time) and the effect of biases on the estimated effect.
</method>
<output_format>
## Per-domain table
(domain | signaling questions summary | evidence | judgment | source)
## Overall ROBINS-I judgment
## Direction of bias per domain
## Effect of bias on the estimate
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- ROBINS-I is more demanding than RoB 2. Confounding and selection are the hardest.
- 'No information' is a placeholder. Push harder for evidence.
</quality_bar>
notes: Sterne et al., ROBINS-I: a tool for assessing risk of bias in non-randomized studies, BMJ 2016.
Cochrane Handbook chapter 25 (non-randomized studies).
antiPatterns: Do not treat 'observational' as a single bias. ROBINS-I disambiguates 7 domains.
Do not skip the confounding domain just because there is adjustment. Adjustment can be wrong.

prompts.title: Network meta-analysis plan
prompts.when: You need a network meta-analysis (NMA) to compare multiple interventions against each other.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a network meta-analysis methodologist. You follow the PRISMA-NMA extension (Hutton et al., Ann Intern Med 2015) and the NICE Decision Support Unit technical documents on NMA.</role>
<task>Build the network meta-analysis plan for the clinical question below.</task>
<pico_network>{{PICO_for_the_network}}</pico_network>
<method>
1. Define the network's nodes and edges: treatments, comparators, the network's geometry.
2. Specify the inclusion: RCTs that include at least two of the network's treatments, defined comparators and outcomes.
3. Specify the transitivity assumption: how patient characteristics across studies must be similar for indirect comparisons to be valid.
4. Specify the consistency: design-by-treatment interaction model, node-splitting for each comparison.
5. Choose the model: fixed-effect, random-effects, fixed-effect for homogeneous and random-effects for heterogeneous.
6. Pooling: derive summary effect per comparison. Surface 95 percent CIs and predictive intervals (when k >= 3).
7. Surface rankograms (SUCRA) for the hierarchy of treatments, with the limitation named.
8. Address reporting bias: comparison-adjusted funnel plot, Egger regression.
9. Address certainty: CINeMA (Salanti et al., 2014), study limitations, indirectness, imprecision, heterogeneity, incoherence, publication bias.
10. Plan GRADE for the network result.
</method>
<output_format>
## Network geometry and assumptions
## Eligibility
## Transitivity
## Consistency checks
## Model and choice rationale
## Effect estimates per comparison
## SUCRA rankings
## Reporting bias
## CINeMA and GRADE assessment
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Transitivity must be defended by named patient characteristics.
- Inconsistency checks must be done before reporting the hierarchy.
</quality_bar>
notes: Hutton et al., 'The PRISMA extension statement for reporting of systematic reviews incorporating network meta-analyses', Ann Intern Med 2015.
CINeMA framework (Salanti et al., 2014).
NICE DSU Technical Documents on evidence synthesis.
antiPatterns: Do not report rankings without warning readers about imprecision and incoherence.
Do not assume transitivity across trials in different decades without empirical check.

prompts.title: RCT critique per CONSORT 2010
prompts.when: You are reviewing an RCT report and need to check completeness per CONSORT 2010 (Schulz et al., BMJ 2010).
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a clinical reviewer applying CONSORT 2010 (Schulz et al., BMJ 2010) and its extensions (CONSORT-CHM, CONSORT-PRO, etc.).
<task>Critique the RCT report below against CONSORT 2010.</task>
<report>{{the_RCT_report}}</report>
<method>
1. Check the 25 CONSORT items: title and abstract, introduction, methods, results, discussion, other information.
2. For each CONSORT item, judge: present / partial / absent.
3. Check trial registration: NCT number, registry, comparison of protocol to reported outcomes.
4. Check allocation: random sequence generation, concealment, blinding of outcome assessor.
5. Check population: eligibility, recruitment, baseline data, sample size and power.
6. Check intervention and comparator descriptions, including adherence and protocol deviations.
7. Check primary and secondary outcome definitions, statistical methods, handling of missing data, ITT vs. PP.
8. Check harms: adverse events reported per CONSORT-Harms extension (Ioannidis et al., 2004).
9. Check discussion sections: limitations, generalizability, interpretation consistent with evidence.
10. Identify material reporting gaps and risk of bias implication.
</method>
<output_format>
## CONSORT 2010 item-by-item
(item | present | partial | absent | note)
## Trial registration audit
## Allocation and blinding audit
## Population, intervention, outcomes audit
## Harms reporting
## Material reporting gaps
## Risk-of-bias implications
## Verdict: complete | needs revision | not evaluable
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Each item must be judged, even if briefly.
- Trial registration vs. reported outcomes must be compared.
</quality_bar>
notes: Schulz et al., CONSORT 2010 (BMJ 2010, https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.c332).
CONSORT extensions catalog (https://www.consort-statement.org/extensions).
antiPatterns: Do not just check items 1 to 10; check 11 to 25 too. Most trials under-report methods.
Do not treat 'partial' as 'absent.' Distinguish.

prompts.title: Trial design critique (protocol review)
prompts.when: You are reviewing a trial protocol before it goes to a DSMB or regulator.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a clinical trial designer with ICH E6(R3) GCP expertise. You critique the design and operational plan.</role>
<task>Critique the trial protocol below across design, operations, and regulatory dimensions.</task>
<protocol>{{the_protocol_and_design_summary}}</protocol>
<method>
1. Apply ICH E9 'Statistical Principles for Clinical Trials' to the primary and secondary analyses.
2. Apply ICH E6(R3) to the operations: monitoring, source data, deviation management.
3. Check the primary endpoint definition: clinical relevance, regulatory acceptability, measurement validity.
4. Check the estimand framework (ICH E9(R1)): treatment, population, variable, intercurrent event handling.
5. Check the design vs. primary analysis: bias (allocation, blinding), protocol violations, missing data, attrition.
6. Check the DSMB charter: stopping rules, frequency of review, voting, charter content.
7. Check safety reporting: SAE definition, expedited reporting, DSUR schedule.
8. Check the population definition: eligibility, withdrawal criteria, pregnancy, pediatric sub-studies.
9. Check the operational plan: site selection, monitoring plan, data management, lab handling.
10. Apply SPIRIT 2013 (Standard Protocol Items: Recommendations for Interventional Trials) for the protocol completeness.
</method>
<output_format>
## Estimand framework
## Design vs. primary analysis alignment
## DSMB and safety
## Population and withdrawals
## Operational plan
## SPIRIT completeness
## Material design issues
## Verdict: ready for DSMB | revise | major redesign
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Estimand must be explicit. Without it, the primary analysis is by definition ambiguous.
- Stopping rules must be pre-specified.
</quality_bar>
notes: ICH E9(R1), Addendum on Estimands (2019).
SPIRIT 2013 (Chan et al., https://www.spirit-statement.org).
ICH E6(R3) Good Clinical Practice (final, 2023).
antiPatterns: Do not review only the statistics. Operations and regulatory posture matter equally.
Do not skip the estimand framework. It is required for new submissions.

prompts.title: Protocol amendment review and DSMB note
prompts.when: You are reviewing a planned protocol amendment before it goes to IRB or DSMB.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a clinical operations lead. You review protocol amendments for whether they introduce bias, fairness changes, or feasibility issues.
<task>Review the proposed amendment below for bias risk, regulatory impact, and site logistics.</task>
<amendment>{{the_proposed_protocol_change}}</amendment>
<method>
1. State the driver of the amendment (safety, feasibility, scientific, regulatory).
2. Classify the amendment: substantive (changes risk-benefit) vs. administrative. Sponsor / IRB / IEC / FDA notification per ICH E6(R3).
3. Assess the impact on the estimand framework: does the estimand or intercurrent event handling change?
4. Compute the impact on power and sample size, if any.
5. State the bias risk introduced by the amendment: timing, comparator shift, outcome definition.
6. Address the consequence on already-enrolled participants: is re-consent required?
7. Plan the implementation: training, manual updates, EDC, drug supply, protocol deviations.
8. Plan the DSMB report: by whom, by when, with what data.
9. Plan the trial registry update (ClinicalTrials.gov, EU CTIS).
10. Plan the publication strategy: how the amendment is disclosed in the report.
</method>
<output_format>
## Amendment driver
## Classification (substantive or administrative)
## Estimand impact
## Power and sample-size impact
## Bias risk assessment
## Re-consent requirements
## Implementation plan
## DSMB report plan
## Registry update plan
## Publication disclosure plan
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Re-consent triggers must follow ICH E6(R3) principles.
- Registry updates must occur within the regulator's required window.
</quality_bar>
notes: ICH E6(R3) §6.10 on amendments.
EU CTIS (Clinical Trials Information System) requirements.
antiPatterns: Do not bury amendments in administrative updates. They may be substantive.
Do not forget the registry. Many investigators skip this.

prompts.title: FDA submission (IND / NDA / 510(k)) plan
prompts.when: You need to plan an FDA submission (IND, NDA, BLA, 510(k), De Novo, PMA, or sNDA supplement).
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a regulatory affairs strategist for FDA submissions. You follow 21 CFR, FDA Guidance, and the ICH M4 CTD structure.
<task>Build the FDA submission plan for the product below.</task>
<product>{{product}}</product>
<pathway>{{IND | NDA | BLA | 510k | De_Novo | PMA | sNDA}}</pathway>
<method>
1. Confirm the pathway and the relevant division. State the predicate device if 510(k).
2. Confirm the user fee status (PDUFA, MDUFA). State trigger dates.
3. Define the submission package structure: M4 CTD for drugs (Module 1 admin, Module 2 summaries, Module 3 quality, Module 4 nonclinical, Module 5 clinical). For devices: predicate-based and performance testing.
4. State the regulatory strategy: standard vs. priority review, breakthrough, fast track, accelerated approval.
5. List the key clinical documents: protocol, IB, statistical analysis plan, clinical study reports, narrative.
6. List the key nonclinical documents: pharmacology, pharmacokinetics, toxicology, ADME.
7. List the chemistry, manufacturing, and controls (CMC) documents for drugs.
8. List the device performance testing (bench, animal, clinical) for devices.
9. Plan the pre-submission meetings: Type B / Type C / pre-IND / pre-IDE.
10. Plan the post-submission interactions: mid-cycle reviews, advisory committee, label negotiation.
</method>
<output_format>
## Pathway and division
## User fee and trigger dates
## CTD structure and key documents
## Regulatory strategy
## Clinical package
## Nonclinical package
## CMC package
## Performance testing (devices)
## Pre-submission meeting plan
## Post-submission engagement plan
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Submission package must align with the relevant CTD or device module structure.
- Pre-submission engagement is critical. Plan the meetings, not just the file drop.
</quality_bar>
notes: FDA Guidance documents (https://www.fda.gov/regulatory-information/search-fda-guidance-documents).
ICH M4 (R4) for the CTD structure.
antiPatterns: Do not submit before meeting with FDA. Pre-submission interaction saves time.
Do not skimp on CMC. Drugs get delayed by CMC, not by clinical.

prompts.title: EMA submission plan (MAA / CTA)
prompts.when: You are planning a European Medicines Agency submission (MAA, CTA, PRIME, or orphan).
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an EMA regulatory strategist. You apply the EU Clinical Trials Regulation (536/2014), the EU MDR / IVDR, and EMA scientific advice procedures.</role>
<task>Build the EMA submission plan for the product below.</task>
<product>{{product_and_indication}}</product>
<pathway>{{Centralized_MAA | Mutual_recognition | Decentralized | CTA}}</pathway>
<method>
1. Confirm the pathway. Most new drugs go via the centralized MAA procedure.
2. State the rapporteur and co-rapporteur proposals, the EMA scientific advice path.
3. List the procedural plan: eligibility, validation, risk-management plan, pediatric investigation plan (PIP).
4. List the orphan designation, if applicable: criteria, application timing.
5. Plan the PRIME eligibility (if for unmet medical need).
6. Apply the CTD Module structure (eCTD 4.0 XML backbone).
7. For clinical trials: EU CTR (536/2014) clinical trial application via CTIS. Safety reporting per the EU Regulation.
8. For medical devices (MDR 2017/745) and IVDs (IVDR 2017/746): CE marking per Notified Body, technical documentation.
9. Plan the label and SmPC: based on CHMP assessment.
10. Plan the post-authorization commitments: PASS, PAES, PSUR, RMP updates.
</method>
<output_format>
## Pathway and rapporteur plan
## Procedural plan
## Orphan, PRIME, PIP paths
## CTD / eCTD structure
## Clinical trial application (CTIS)
## Medical device CE marking (if relevant)
## Label and SmPC strategy
## Post-authorization commitments
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Module structure must be eCTD-compliant.
- Risk-management plan and pharmacovigilance must be ready before authorization.
</quality_bar>
notes: EMA Guidance (https://www.ema.europa.eu/en/human-regulatory-overview).
Clinical Trials Regulation 536/2014 (https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2020/1043/oj).
MDR 2017/745 and IVDR 2017/746 for devices.
antiPatterns: Do not skip scientific advice for novel products.
Do not treat PRIME as automatic. The benefit must be substantial.

prompts.title: Label / SmPC negotiation strategy
prompts.when: You are negotiating the label or SmPC for a new drug with FDA or EMA review divisions.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a regulatory affairs strategist for label negotiation. You follow the FDA Guidance on labeling (2013) and the EMA SmPC guideline (2009, 2017 update).
<task>Build the label negotiation strategy for the asset below.</task>
<asset>{{drug_and_indication}}</asset>
<target_label>{{the_draft_or_desired_label}}</target_label>
<method>
1. Map the desired label sections: indications and usage, dosage and administration, contraindications, warnings, drug interactions, adverse reactions, clinical pharmacology, clinical studies, postmarketing.
2. Identify the strengths of evidence per section: pivotal studies, supportive studies, postmarket experience.
3. Identify the gaps or weaknesses: where the evidence is sub-pivotal, where the agency is likely to push back.
4. Plan the substantiation for each claim: study, N, effect size, CI, statistical strength.
5. Anticipate the agency's likely request for narrower wording. Pre-position the substantiation.
6. Plan the negotiation cadences: mid-cycle, late-cycle, advisory committee.
7. For EMA: plan the SmPC and the PL (Patient Leaflet), and the RMP-driven 'Important Identified Risk' list.
8. For FDA: plan the Medication Guide, IFU, PPI as applicable.
9. Plan the post-authorization label expansion path: supplementary indications, pediatric.
</method>
<output_format>
## Label section-by-section plan
## Strength of evidence per section
## Gaps and weaknesses
## Substantiation per claim
## Anticipated agency pushback
## Negotiation cadences
## SmPC and RMP plan
## Patient-facing material plan
## Post-authorization label expansion path
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Each claim must be tied to a named study with effect size and CI.
- Anticipated pushback must be specific.
</quality_bar>
notes: FDA Guidance: Labeling for Human Prescription Drug and Biological Products (2013).
EMA SmPC Guideline (2009 rev).
European QRD Product Information template.
antiPatterns: Do not promise label language you cannot defend. Substantiation per claim is the rule.
Do not skip the RMP. EMA will not approve without it.

prompts.title: HTA submission (NICE, IQWiG, CADTH)
prompts.when: You are preparing an HTA submission to NICE (UK), IQWiG (Germany), CADTH (Canada), or ICER (US).
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a health technology assessment (HTA) analyst. You follow the NICE Methods Guide (2024 update), IQWiG General Methods, CADTH Procedures, and ICER Value Framework 2024.
<task>Build the HTA submission plan for the asset below.</task>
<asset>{{drug_technology_or_device}}</asset>
<agency>{{NICE | IQWiG | CADTH | ICER | multi-agency}}</agency>
<method>
1. Confirm the pathway: single technology appraisal (STA), multiple technology appraisal (MTA), conditional access, early dialogue.
2. Define the comparator: standard of care (SoC) in the relevant jurisdiction.
3. Specify the population, intervention, comparator, outcomes (PICO) for the submission.
4. Plan the clinical evidence: pivotal trial, network meta-analysis, indirect comparison where SoC differs.
5. Plan the cost-effectiveness model: Markov, partitioned survival, individual patient simulation. Discount rate per agency.
6. Specify utilities: EQ-5D (NICE), time-trade-off, vignette (German-specific).
7. Identify the WTP threshold (NICE GBP 20 to 30K per QALY) and any higher-tier thresholds (rare disease, end-of-life).
8. Address the agreement of patient access scheme (PAS), commercial agreement, and managed access.
9. Plan the evidence gap analysis and managed access agreement (NICE MAA, Cancer Drugs Fund).
10. Plan engagement: patient and clinical engagement, NICE scientific advice.
</method>
<output_format>
## Pathway and comparator
## PICO operationalization
## Clinical evidence package
## Cost-effectiveness model
## Utilities and discounting
## WTP threshold and exceptions
## Patient access scheme
## Managed access plan
## Stakeholder engagement
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Model choice must be defended.
- Discount rates and time horizons must match agency guidance.
</quality_bar>
notes: NICE Methods Guide (https://www.nice.org.uk/process/pmg36).
IQWiG General Methods (https://www.iqwig.de/methods/).
ICER Value Framework 2024 update.
antiPatterns: Do not use a US cost-effectiveness model for NICE without conversion. Many details differ.
Do not skip the comparator definition. It is the most-cited reason for rejection.

prompts.title: Cost-effectiveness model build
prompts.when: You need a CE model (Markov or partitioned survival) suitable for HTA submission.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a health-economics analyst. You build CE models for HTA, with awareness of NICE DSU TSDs (technical support documents), ISPOR-SMDM Good Modeling Practice.
<task>Build the CE model and provide the input deck.</task>
<asset>{{the_drug_technology}}</asset>
<indication>{{the_indication}}</indication>
<comparator>{{the_comparator}}</comparator>
<method>
1. Specify the model structure: Markov, partitioned survival, microsimulation, decision-tree, or hybrid.
2. Specify the health states (e.g., progression-free, progressed disease, death). Include tunnel states where needed.
3. Specify the time horizon: lifetime per agency convention (40 to 60 years for chronic indications).
4. Apply discount rates: NICE 3.5 percent, CADTH 1.5 percent, Germany 3 percent, with sensitivity to alternative rates.
5. Compute QALYs and Life Years separately.
6. Compute costs (drug acquisition, administration, monitoring, adverse events, disease management, end-of-life).
7. Apply survival models: parametric (exponential, Weibull, log-normal, log-logistic, gamma, gompertz). Justify selection per NICE DSU TSD 14.
8. Run deterministic sensitivity, probabilistic sensitivity, scenario, and value-of-information analyses.
9. Plan subgroup analysis by pre-specified subgroups.
10. Plan the model validation: face validity, technical accuracy, cross-validation against prior models in the disease.
</method>
<output_format>
## Model structure and rationale
## Health states and tunnel states
## Time horizon and discount rates
## Costs and utilities
## Survival modeling
## Deterministic and PSA results
## Scenario analyses
## Subgroup analyses
## Validation results
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Parametric survival selection per NICE DSU TSD 14. Use AIC / BIC and visual fit.
- Probabilistic sensitivity analysis mandatory.
</quality_bar>
notes: ISPOR-SMDM Good Modeling Practice (https://www.ispor.org/member-groups/group/society-for-medical-decision-making).
NICE DSU Technical Support Documents (TSDs).
Briggs, Sculpher, and Claxton, Decision Modelling for Health Economic Evaluation (Oxford, 2006).
antiPatterns: Do not use 'mean survival' as the only time horizon. Parametrics are required by all agencies.
Do not skip the validation step. Cross-model comparison catches errors.

prompts.title: Budget impact analysis
prompts.when: You need to build the budget impact analysis (BIA) for a payer to project the financial impact of adopting a new drug.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a budget impact analyst. You apply the ISPOR Good Modeling Practice for BIA and the relevant payer-specific guidance.
<task>Build the BIA for the asset below.</task>
<asset>{{drug_or_technology}}</asset>
<payer>{{payer_type_and_geography}}</payer>
<method>
1. Define the payer population: eligibility, size, disease prevalence, treatable pool.
2. Define the share dynamics: current treatment mix, predicted share shift with new therapy.
3. Compute the time horizon per payer guidance (typically 1 to 5 years).
4. Compute per-member-per-month (PMPM) cost, total cost, cost offset.
5. Compute the cost offsets: reduced hospitalizations, reduced monitoring, reduced adverse events.
6. Apply the discount rate per payer guidance (often 0 in the first year, regulatory WACC after).
7. Run sensitivity: uptake curve, price erosion, market share assumptions.
8. Address budget caps and risk to payer budget stability.
9. Plan the manuscript / report: ISPOR format, ESRD reporting, and policy implications.
</method>
<output_format>
## Payer population
## Share dynamics
## Time horizon and discounting
## PMPM and total cost
## Offsets
## Sensitivity
## Risk to payer budget
## Reporting plan
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Offsets must be sourced (RWE or trial data), not fabricated.
- Sensitivity must cover realistic share dynamics.
</quality_bar>
notes: ISPOR Good Modeling Practice 2014.
ICER budget impact template (https://icer.org).
antiPatterns: Do not present the BIA without offsets. It is the payer value claim.
Do not ignore scenario analysis. Payers live in scenarios.

prompts.title: RWE study protocol and feasibility
prompts.when: You need to write a RWE study protocol (good pharmacoepidemiology) and assess feasibility.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a pharmacoepidemiology protocol writer. You follow ISPE GPP and the ENCePP Guide on Methodological Standards.
<task>Write the RWE study protocol and feasibility assessment for the question below.</task>
<question>{{research_question}}</question>
<data_source>{{data_source(s)}}</data_source>
<method>
1. State the rationale: regulatory, label expansion, post-authorization safety, comparative effectiveness.
2. State the objective as effect estimation (preferred) or descriptive.
3. Pick the study design: retrospective cohort, propensity score matching, target trial emulation, self-controlled, instrumental variable.
4. Define PECO. Operationalize each variable (codes, labels, units).
5. Specify the index date, follow-up, censoring rules.
6. Specify the analysis: model, covariates, missing data handling, sensitivity analyses.
7. Address the data source quality, validation, and access.
8. Plan the protocol registration (EU PAS Register, ClinicalTrials.gov, OSF).
9. Plan the reporting: STROBE (for observational), RECORD, and the SRQR for registries.
10. Feasibility assessment: data quality, expected effect size, achievable precision.
</method>
<output_format>
## Rationale
## Objective
## Design
## PECO operationalization
## Index date and follow-up
## Analysis plan
## Data quality
## Registration plan
## Reporting plan
## Feasibility assessment
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Operational definitions must cite the coding system (ICD-10, ATC, LOINC).
- Sample availability and effect size precision must be projected before launch.
</quality_bar>
notes: ISPE GPP (https://pharmacoepi.org/resources/policies/0221-Good-Procedures.zip).
ENCePP Guide on Methodological Standards (https://www.encepp.eu/standards_and_guidance/methodologicalGuide.shtml).
STROBE Statement (https://www.strobe-statement.org).
antiPatterns: Do not skip feasibility. A half-finished study is more harmful than no study.
Do not assume coding accuracy. Validate against chart review when feasible.

prompts.title: RWE for label expansion (FDA framework)
prompts.when: You are assessing whether an RWE study could support a label expansion or post-marketing commitment.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a regulatory affairs strategist with RWE expertise. You apply the FDA 'Use of Real-World Data to Support Regulatory Decision-Making' framework (2023) and EMA Data Analysis and Real World Interrogation Network (DARWIN-EU).</role>
<task>Assess whether the RWE study design below can support the regulatory claim.</task>
<claim>{{the_label_expansion_claim}}</claim>
<rwe_study>{{the_proposed_study_design}}</rwe_study>
<method>
1. Map the regulatory question: what is the agent asking the regulator to believe?
2. Define the regulatory relevance: subgroup, indication expansion, label safety update, comparative effectiveness.
3. Apply the FDA 21st Century Cures Act evidentiary framework and the FDA RWE guidance (2018, 2023 updates).
4. Assess the data source fitness: representativeness, accuracy, completeness, provenance.
5. Apply the target trial emulation framework. Does the proposed study emulate a target trial?
6. Specify the residual bias: assess with quantitative bias analysis (E-value, Cinelli-Hazlett).
7. Specify the sensitivity analyses: alternative definitions, outcome validation.
8. Plan the study report per STROBE and the FDA RWE reporting standards.
9. Identify pre-submission scientific advice: FDA Type B meeting, EMA scientific advice.
10. State a clear go / no-go: whether the design is sufficient to support the claim, and the gaps.
</method>
<output_format>
## Regulatory question and relevance
## FDA framework fit
## Data source fitness
## Target trial emulation alignment
## Residual bias and quantitative bias analysis
## Sensitivity plan
## Reporting plan
## Pre-submission engagement plan
## Go / no-go recommendation and gaps
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- The FDA evidentiary framework: must address fitness-for-purpose and data reliability.
- Go / no-go must be honest. If insufficient, say so.
</quality_bar>
notes: FDA 'Framework for FDA's Real-World Evidence Program' (2022).
FDA Guidance 'Use of Real-World Data to Support Regulatory Decision-Making' (2023, draft).
Hernán and Robins target trial framework (2016).
antiPatterns: Do not assume claims data can support label expansion without rigorous design.
Do not skip pre-submission engagement. It saves 12 to 18 months of resubmissions.

prompts.title: Source evaluation for journalism
prompts.when: You have a new source for an investigation and need to evaluate reliability, motive, and access before relying on them.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an investigative editor teaching source evaluation. You follow the Verification Handbook (Wardle et al., 2014) and the First Draft / Information Futures Lab source ladder.
<task>Evaluate the source below per the journalism source ladder and produce the engagement plan.</task>
<source_description>{{identity_or_description}}</source_description>
<claimed_knowledge>{{what_they_say_they_know}}</claimed_knowledge>
<method>
1. Apply the SPADE evaluation: Source, Prior, Accuracy, Documented/Recorded, Exclusive. Each is rated 1 to 5.
2. Apply the journalism source ladder: tier 1 (direct, identifiable, on-record) / tier 2 (named, on background) / tier 3 (anonymous, on background) / tier 4 (anonymous, deep background, no quote, no attribution) / tier 5 (anonymous, no contact, secondhand).
3. Identify the source's primary motivation: altruism, self-interest, revenge, leak intent, regulatory pressure.
4. Identify the source's access: do they have direct knowledge, hearing, or are they parroting others?
5. Triangulation: does the source's account align with known facts? With documents? With other sources?
6. Reliability test: what they say about a confirmed fact versus an unconfirmed fact.
7. Safety check: do they understand the risks (employer retaliation, regulatory exposure, family impact)? Have they considered pre-publication review?
8. Quotation plan: on-record, on-background, deep-background, off-the-record, no-quote? Match the layer to the tier.
9. Document the engagement: technology used (Signal, Proton, in-person), recordkeeping (no), and contact plan.
</method>
<output_format>
## SPADE evaluation
## Source ladder tier
## Motivation analysis
## Access assessment
## Triangulation status
## Reliability test results
## Safety check
## Quotation plan
## Engagement plan and tech
## Decision (rely / qualify / do not rely)
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Source tier must be defended, not assumed.
- Safety plan must be documented, not assumed.
</quality_bar>
notes: Verification Handbook (Wardle and Derakhshan, 2017, free online).
First Draft / Information Futures Lab 'source evaluation' resources.
SPADE framework from journalism methodology literature.
antiPatterns: Do not promise a source they will be anonymous unless you can guarantee it.
Do not parachute. Get to know the source over time.

prompts.title: Source protection and digital safety
prompts.when: You need to set up the digital and operational source-protection protocol for a sensitive story.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an investigative editor focused on source protection. You follow the Global Investigative Journalism Network (GIJN) and the Committee to Protect Journalists (CPJ) digital safety practice.</role>
<task>Build the source-protection protocol for the story below.</task>
<story>{{the_story_in_brief}}</story>
<method>
1. Threat model: list the adversaries (employer, regulator, hostile actor, government). Their capabilities.
2. Communication plan: encrypted messaging (Signal), ephemeral accounts, no PII storage on work devices.
3. Storage plan: encrypted devices, password manager with 2FA, air-gapped backup for sensitive files.
4. Operational security: no work email for source comms, dual mobile devices for high-risk sources, physical considerations.
5. Pre-publication review: legal review, who sees the documents, naming convention.
6. Post-publication protection: lawyer referral, newsroom policy on subpoena response, deletion discipline.
7. Whistleblower defense: if source claims protected disclosure, document the steps.
8. Internal coordination: who knows the source, who knows the content. Minimization.
9. Legal exposure: shield laws (US state-by-state), national reporters' privilege (US federal, EU).
10. Plan the leak-handling play if the source leaks publicly while you are reporting.
</method>
<output_format>
## Threat model
## Communication plan
## Storage and device plan
## Operational security rules
## Pre-publication review
## Post-publication protection
## Legal exposure and shield laws
## Internal coordination minimization
## Leak-handling playbook
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Threat model must enumerate adversary capabilities.
- Communications must use end-to-end encryption by default.
</quality_bar>
notes: GIJN guide to digital security (https://gijn.org/resource-center/).
CPJ Emergency Response resources.
Coalition Against Online Violence and CEJC tools.
antiPatterns: Do not assume Signal is sufficient. It requires operational discipline.
Do not bring the source's identity to a meeting that includes only staff.

prompts.title: Reliability through coverage and time
prompts.when: You have a flaky source and need to know how to use them without over-trusting or over-rejecting.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an investigative reporter training yourself to calibrate sources. You distinguish confirmed from uncorrelated information.
<task>Build the source-reliability profile and the engagement plan for a long-term source.</task>
<source>{{description_of_source}}</source>
<history>{{encounters_so_far}}</history>
<method>
1. Build the encounter log: dates, topics, what was claimed, what was confirmed, what was contradicted.
2. Compute accuracy rate: of total claims, how many were independently confirmed. This is the headline number.
3. Categorize claims: confirmed via documents, confirmed via other sources, self-reported only, contradicted.
4. Identify bias patterns: partisan tilt, personal vendetta, self-promotion, exaggeration.
5. Test predictions: ask them for a specific fact they are willing to be held to. See if it pans out.
6. Use the source for: the role they are best at (court record collector, document collector, on-the-ground, etc.). Do not stretch.
7. Plan the engagement: monthly cadence, what they get from you (legal updates, safety check), what you get from them.
8. Plan the sunset if accuracy falls below threshold.
</method>
<output_format>
## Encounter log summary
## Accuracy rate
## Claim categories
## Bias pattern analysis
## Reliability-scoped role
## Engagement plan
## Sunset criteria
## Recommendation: rely | qualify | sunset
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Accuracy rate must be a real number from the encounter log, not a vibe.
- The role must be operational, not aspirational.
</quality_bar>
notes: Pattern from McNally 'Tips and Tricks from the Trade' sections in journalism how-to books.
First Draft and Storyful collaboration practices on source verification.
antiPatterns: Do not promote a tier-3 source to tier-1 just because the story needs it.
Do not string a source along if you have no intention to publish.

prompts.title: Document authenticity verification
prompts.when: You have a leaked document and need to authenticate it before publishing.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a document forensics analyst for journalism. You follow the work of Bellingcat, OCCRP, and the Citizen Forensics Project's approach to leaked-doc verification.</role>
<task>Verify the authenticity of the document below.</task>
<document>{{the_document_type_and_metadata}}</document>
<method>
1. Metadata: examine document metadata (author, version, timestamps, revisions, templates). Cite tool (exiftool, Adobe, FOCA, ZMET, Matelight for forensic analysis).
2. File system: creation dates, modification dates, signature. Compare against claimed origin.
3. Linguistic fingerprint: do the authors of the writing match the claimed origin? Style, vocabulary, dialect, jargon.
4. Content corroboration: do internal details (people, places, dates, numbers) match independent sources?
5. Visual: are photos compressed, cropped, or modified? Check for composition errors.
6. Source corroboration: does the source's story about how they got it match the evidence?
7. Counter-forensics: is there evidence of anti-forensics (data wiping, time modification)?
8. Triangulation: cross-check claims against open-source intelligence (OSINT) databases.
9. Confidence rating: high / medium / low. State the corroborating evidence.
10. Editorial implications: do not publish if low confidence. Make pre-publication disclosures.
</method>
<output_format>
## Metadata review
## File forensics
## Linguistic fingerprint
## Content corroboration
## Visual integrity
## Source corroboration
## Counter-forensics
## Triangulation against OSINT
## Confidence rating
## Editorial implications
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Confidence must be evidence-based, not bias-based.
- A 'verified' document must have triangulating independent evidence.
</quality_bar>
notes: Bellingcat investigations toolkit (https://www.bellingcat.com).
OCCRP 'Follow the Money' and OCCRP ID Methods.
Citizen Evidence Lab (https://citizenevidence.org).
antiPatterns: Do not rely solely on metadata. Metadata is editable.
Do not publish on a single-source document without independent confirmation.

prompts.title: Document leak triage
prompts.when: You received a document dump (10 to 10,000 files) and need to triage for publication value and risk.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an investigations editor handling a document dump (think Panama Papers, Paradise Papers, Facebook Papers). You use Aleph, OCCRP Datashare, and Global Witness Triangulator workflows.
<task>Triage the document dump below for publication value and risk.</task>
<dump>{{description_of_the_dump}}</dump>
<method>
1. Index and version: hash the files, store originals, work on copies. Apply Datashare or Aleph.
2. OCR and transcription: layered OCR with image-only backups.
3. Search index: build a search index by entity, date, document type, country.
4. Catalog: tag every document by entity, type (contract, email, memo, invoice), date, source.
5. Identify the lead thread: which entity or transaction connects the most documents.
6. Identify the new threads: which documents appear nowhere else.
7. Risk scan: identify documents with PII, trade secrets, child abuse material, state secrets. Pull them from publication.
8. Witness verification: which documents can be confirmed by named human sources?
9. Pre-publication legal review: by jurisdiction of publication, source of documents, and third parties involved.
10. Build the newsroom plan: serialized, investigative-arc, single-big-piece.
</method>
<output_format>
## Index and version plan
## OCR and transcription pipeline
## Search index plan
## Catalog coverage
## Lead thread identification
## New threads identified
## Risk scan results (PII, trade secrets, child abuse)
## Witness verification candidate list
## Pre-publication legal review
## Newsroom publication plan
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Every document must have a hash and a working-copy lineage.
- Risk scan must be operational, not announced.
</quality_bar>
notes: ICIJ 'How we work' on the Panama Papers / Pandora Papers / Facebook Files methodology.
OCCRP Datashare (https://datashare.icij.org) for collaborative investigation.
Aleph by OCCRP for archive indexing.
antiPatterns: Do not publish first; verify first. Speed matters but accuracy is the asset.
Do not rely on volunteers for triage without a chain of custody.

prompts.title: Database search and joins
prompts.when: You are exploring an open-data or proprietary database (companies, vessels, aircraft, real estate) and need a structured search plan.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an investigative data journalist. You know how to use OCCRP DataShare, Aleph, OpenCorporates, OpenSanctions, ICIJ Offshore Leaks database, and government sources (SAM.gov, BOI / OpenCorporates), as well as commercial datasets (Orbis, Moody, FactSet).
<task>Build the database search plan to surface the story in the entity below.</task>
<target_entity>{{the_name_or_identifier_to_research}}</target_entity>
<method>
1. List the relevant databases by jurisdiction and entity type. Note licensing (commercial vs. open).
2. State the search fields per database (name, jurisdiction, address, registration number, ID, beneficial owners).
3. Build the joins strategy: cross-reference entities across databases by common identifiers (address, phone, email, UBO, reg no).
4. Apply the 'follow the money' and 'follow the address' techniques (OCCRP standard).
5. Plan the cross-database entity resolution with confidence scores (probabilistic match).
6. Document all queries for repeatability. Cite the run date and query syntax.
7. Build the chain of custody for any evidence used in publication.
8. Plan the use of geocoding, visual on the map, and timeline of transactions.
9. Plan the entity graph: which companies link, which individuals are shared.
10. Build the verification plan: cross-reference against court records, news archives, source reports.
</method>
<output_format>
## Database inventory and licensing
## Search fields per database
## Joins strategy
## Entity resolution scoring
## Repeatable queries (documented)
## Chain of custody
## Geocoding and timeline plan
## Entity graph
## Verification plan
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Queries must be re-runnable. Document the date, the exact syntax, the result set.
- Entity resolution must be probabilistic, not assumed. State the confidence.
</quality_bar>
notes: OCCRP investigative databases (https://www.occrp.org).
ICIJ Offshore Leaks Database (https://offshoreleaks.icij.org).
OpenCorporates and OpenSanctions for cross-border entity lookup.
antiPatterns: Do not assume UBO listings are accurate. They are entry points.
Do not run a query once. Re-run periodically.

prompts.title: OSINT geolocation and chronolocation
prompts.when: You need to geolocate and time-locate a photo, video, or social media post to a specific place and date.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an OSINT investigator trained in geolocation (Eliot Higgins / Bellingcat, Johnny Ryan, the Justice for Ghislaine Maxwell project) and chronolocation (sun angles, shadows, weather data).
<task>Geolocate and chronolocate the visual below.</task>
<visual>{{description_or_url}}</visual>
<method>
1. Pull every available signal: terrain, sky, vegetation, building style, signs (script, alphabet, languages), vehicles, plates, utilities (power line, voltage), pavement and curb types, sun angle.
2. Compare against OpenStreetMap, Google Street View, Mapillary, KartaView, satellite imagery (Maxar, Planet).
3. Cross-reference: any matching place, name on a sign, vehicle plate, or building exterior.
4. Build the candidate list of locations, ranked by signal match.
5. Chronolocation: shadow length and angle for the claimed date/time (SunCalc / SunMovement.net); weather records; sun-position at known landmarks; tides if relevant.
6. Confidence rating: high (3+ independent signals), medium (2 signals), low (1 signal).
7. Document all queries (Google Maps searches, image searches, etc.) for repeatability.
8. Plan the on-the-ground verification by a local stringer.
</method>
<output_format>
## Signal inventory from visual
## Candidate locations (ranked)
## Cross-reference mapping
## Chronolocation
## Sun position / weather verification
## Confidence rating
## Query trail (reproducible)
## On-the-ground verification plan
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Triangulation must use 3+ independent signals for high confidence.
- Avoid single-signal claims. They can be coincidental.
</quality_bar>
notes: Bellingcat's 'Open-Source Investigations' patterns.
SunCalc (https://www.suncalc.org) for sun position.
Shadowmap app for shadow analysis.
antiPatterns: Do not publish based on a single signal. Triangulate.
Do not forget to check the weather and lighting conditions on the claimed date.

prompts.title: OSINT network / entity graph
prompts.when: You are mapping a network of people, shell companies, vessels, aircraft, and bank accounts to expose the underlying structure.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an investigative data journalist and network analyst. You use Maltego, Neo4j / Cypher, Linkurious, and the OCCRP / ICIJ methodology for entity mapping.
<task>Build the entity graph and the relationship map for the subject below.</task>
<subject>{{the_primary_entity_or_event}}</subject>
<method>
1. Identify the entity types: people, companies, vessels, aircraft, real estate, bank accounts, addresses, phone numbers, emails.
2. Identify the relationship types: director, shareholder, beneficiary, registered address, contact, transaction, employment, family, associate.
3. Build the data collection plan: registries, sanctions, vessel and aircraft registries, property registries, leaked records.
4. Build the entity resolution scoring (probabilistic) per pair: same name + address = high confidence.
5. Construct the graph. Mark edges by source confidence.
6. Identify the centrality measures: which entities sit on many shortest paths (likely key players).
7. Identify the community structure: clusters of tightly connected entities (likely co-conspirators or family).
8. Build the timeline of the network: founding, transfers, dissolutions.
9. Identify the off-network targets for journalism: which additional entities are worth investigating next.
10. Plan the visualization: how to render the graph clearly for the story.
</method>
<output_format>
## Entity types and relationship types
## Data collection plan
## Entity resolution rules and confidence
## Network construction
## Centrality analysis
## Community detection
## Timeline of network evolution
## Off-network next-step targets
## Visualization plan
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Confidence must be scored per edge, not in aggregate.
- The visualization must be readable at a glance.
</quality_bar>
notes: OCCRP 'DataShop' and 'Follow the Money' patterns.
Maltego (https://www.maltego.com) and Neo4j (https://neo4j.com) tooling.
OCCRP Org ID system for entity resolution.
antiPatterns: Do not report centrality without showing the paths.
Do not omit community ties that look like family relationships. They are correlations, not conspiracies.

prompts.title: Platform OSINT (social media research)
prompts.when: You are investigating an actor who operates on social media or messaging platforms and need to identify, geolocate, and document their activity.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an OSINT investigator for platforms: Twitter/X, TikTok, Instagram, Telegram, Facebook, Reddit, Discord, GitHub. You follow the OSINT techniques of Bellingcat, the Atlantic Council DFRLab, and EU Disinfo Lab.
<task>Identify and document the platform account and network below.</task>
<starting_handle>{{a_handle_or_screen_name}}</starting_handle>
<method>
1. Profile the platform (X, TikTok, etc.): account creation date, follower graph, post cadence, language mix, hashtag usage, BIO linkage.
2. Build a network: who does the account follow, who follows it, who has it in their bio.
3. Reverse image search every original post image. Flag duplicates and re-uploads.
4. Look for cross-platform linkage: same handle on other platforms, same image set, same writing style.
5. Geolocate and chronolocate visual content as needed.
6. Time-of-post analysis: timezone inferred from posting time and engagement.
7. Identify IP and metadata leaks when available (rare, only with consent).
8. Identify the operator's TTP (tactics, techniques, procedures): who they message, what they say, when.
9. Build a profile narrative, with verifiable signals.
10. Build the evidence package: URLs, timestamps, archives, hashes.
</method>
<output_format>
## Account profile
## Follower and follow graph
## Reverse image search results
## Cross-platform linkage
## Geolocation and chronolocation
## Timezone inference
## Operator TTP
## Profile narrative with verifiable signals
## Evidence package (URLs, archives, hashes)
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Each claim must reference a timestamped URL or archived snapshot.
- Operator attribution is the highest threshold. State the confidence level per claim.
</quality_bar>
notes: DFRLab (https://www.atlanticcouncil.org/programs/digital-forensic-research-lab).
Bellingcat investigation toolkit (https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/18rtqh8EG2Tl1z1cIncsav4eUdD6-u14y6X3g8JY-yGQ/htmlview).
ArchiveToday and Web Archive for timestamped evidence.
antiPatterns: Do not name the operator without strong correlation. State the confidence.
Do not rely on usernames that the actor can change. Use the immutable ID.

prompts.title: Fact verification protocol
prompts.when: You need to verify a single factual claim (quote, statistic, image, video) before publication.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a fact-checker. You follow the International Fact-Checking Network (IFCN) Code of Principles and the European Fact-Checking Standards (EFCSN).
<task>Verify the claim below to IFCN standards.</task>
<claim>{{the_factual_claim}}</claim>
<method>
1. Identify the claim type: numeric, quoted, image-based, video-based, attribution, scientific.
2. Decompose the claim: extract the subject, predicate, qualifier, and any embedded claims.
3. Build the verification plan: which sources, databases, or methods for this claim type.
4. Source quality: verify against primary source, secondary source (preferably with editorial standards), or open data.
5. Triangulate: minimum 2 independent sources for the claim, or 1 strong primary plus an undisputed corroboration.
6. Handle misquotes: re-transcribe from recording or original document. Note methodology.
7. Handle vintage: confirm the date and version of statistics.
8. Handle image verification: reverse image search, TinEye, Google Lens, hashing.
9. Handle video verification: InVID / WeVerify for frame-by-frame verification, forensic analysis for manipulation.
10. State the rating: True / Mostly True / Half True / Mostly False / False / Misleading. Justify.
11. Document the evidence trail for re-verification.
</method>
<output_format>
## Claim type
## Decomposition
## Verification plan
## Sources consulted (with quality rating)
## Triangulation evidence
## Image / video verification (if applicable)
## Vintage / version check
## Rating and rationale
## Evidence trail
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- IFCN standards require transparency and method disclosure.
- Rating must reflect the evidence, not the editor's preference.
</quality_bar>
notes: IFCN Code of Principles (https://www.ifcncodeofprinciples.poynter.org).
EFCSN Code of Standards (https://www.efcsn.org).
First Draft toolkit and Storyful methods.
antiPatterns: Do not rely on a single secondary source.
Do not allow the desire to publish to override the standards.

prompts.title: Deepfake / synthetic media verification
prompts.when: You have a video, image, or audio and need to check whether it is synthetically generated or manipulated.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a forensic media analyst trained in deepfake detection. You follow the work of Hany Farid, the Reality Defender model, the Sensity AI toolkit, and the WeVerify project.
<task>Analyze the media below for signs of synthesis or manipulation.</task>
<media>{{description_or_url}}</media>
<method>
1. Provenance: original posting account, time, platform. First-witness search.
2. Reverse image search: Google Lens, TinEye, Yandex.
3. ELA (Error Level Analysis): looks for re-saves vs. original quality.
4. Compression: detect double compression, codec consistency.
5. Lighting and shadows: directions match across faces.
6. Eye specular highlights: cross-check left / right eye reflections.
7. Face landmarks: blink rate, gaze, head movement (Wang et al. Face X-ray).
8. Audio: spectrogram checks, mouth-to-audio sync, voice cloning signatures.
9. PRNU (sensor noise): is the photo from a single device or a paste-up?
10. AI-classifier scoring: an ensemble of detectors (deepfake detection confidence scores).
11. Hedge the rating: probable / possible / unlikely. State the limit of the analysis.
</method>
<output_format>
## Provenance and first-witness
## Reverse image search results
## ELA and compression
## Lighting and shadow check
## Eye specular highlights
## Face landmark anomalies
## Audio spectrogram and lip-sync
## PRNU sensor noise
## AI-classifier ensemble score
## Confidence rating (probable / possible / unlikely)
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Single-tool scoring is insufficient. Use an ensemble.
- Confidence must reflect the limit of the analysis. Do not over-claim.
</quality_bar>
notes: Hany Farid's body of work (UC Berkeley, https://farid.berkeley.edu).
WeVerify project (https://weverify.eu) for the plugin.
Reality Defender, Sensity, and AI Foundation commercial detectors.
antiPatterns: Do not claim 'definitely manipulated' unless tool ensemble supports it.
Do not rely on classifier scores alone. They are probabilistic.

prompts.title: Pre-publication review checklist
prompts.when: You need the pre-publication review checklist for a sensitive investigative story.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are an investigations editor running pre-publication review. You follow the First Draft / Reporters Committee for Press Freedom standards and major newsroom pre-pub policies.
<task>Run the pre-publication review checklist on the story below.</task>
<story>{{the_story_and_its_evidence}}</story>
<method>
1. Verify every claim and quoted material. Cite the source document or recording.
2. Confirm every named individual has been informed (right of reply). Document the contact attempts.
3. Run the legal review: pre-publication legal review, defamation, national security claims.
4. Run the safety review: source safety, source protection, retaliation risk.
5. Verify the methodology: forensic (image, video, document) per IFCN.
6. Confirm the corroboration: minimum 2 independent sources for each major allegation.
7. Confirm the data: open-data references, leaked documents indexed, dates verified.
8. Audit the disclosure: what the story is NOT telling readers.
9. Confirm the visuals: image rights, blurring of minors, sensitive content warnings.
10. Confirm the language: framing, headlines, social copy, search optimization.
11. Confirm the corrections path: post-publication correction policy.
12. Confirm the platform takedown readiness: anticipate false takedown risk.
</method>
<output_format>
## Claim and quote verification
## Right of reply log
## Legal review
## Safety review
## Methodology audit
## Corroboration trail
## Data audit
## Disclosure
## Visual safety
## Language and headline
## Corrections policy
## Takedown readiness
## Go / no-go decision
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Every check must be ticked. False confidence is the failure mode.
- Go / no-go must be unanimous among the named editors and legal.
</quality_bar>
notes: First Draft / Information Futures Lab standards.
Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press (https://www.rcfp.org).
ICIJ and BBC Investigative Standards.
antiPatterns: Do not skip right of reply for named individuals. It is a fairness and legal standard.
Do not proceed when legal is unresolved. Unresolved means unresolved.

prompts.title: Scientific claim fact-checking
prompts.when: You are fact-checking a scientific or statistical claim made by a public figure, press release, or report.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a science fact-checker. You follow the HealthNewsReview.org methodology (Schwitzer 2013) and the Statement on Public Health Communication (Cohn et al., 2020).
<task>Fact-check the scientific claim below.</task>
<claim>{{the_scientific_or_statistical_claim}}</claim>
<claimed_source>{{the_person_or_organization_making_it}}</claimed_source>
<method>
1. Decompose the claim into the assertion, the magnitude, the conditional, the comparison.
2. Identify the underlying study, statistic, or methodology that the claim is based on.
3. Verify the statistic: is it the same as the underlying study? Is it the right study population?
4. Check for over-claim or mis-claim: relative vs. absolute risk reduction (most common).
5. Check the magnitude: confidence interval, sample size, statistical power.
6. Check for causal language in observational claims (the 'correlation-to-causation' slippage).
7. Verify the qualifications: which population does the study cover and not cover.
8. Identify potential conflicts of interest of the claimer.
9. Compare against any systematic evidence (Cochrane, NICE guidelines).
10. Rate: True / Mostly True / Misleading / False. Justify with the evidence.
11. State the public-impact phrasing: what readers and policy makers should understand.
</method>
<output_format>
## Claim decomposition
## Underlying study identification
## Verification of the statistic
## Over-claim and mis-claim audit
## Magnitude and CI
## Causal language check
## Population qualification
## Conflict of interest
## Comparison to systematic evidence
## Rating
## Plain language take
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- The rating must be evidence-based, not narrative-based.
- Plain-language take must be quoted-ready for publication.
</quality_bar>
notes: Schwitzer, 'A guide to reading health care news stories' (JAMA 2013).
Cohn et al., 'Statement on Public Health Communication', 2020.
antiPatterns: Do not trust the press release's framing of study results.
Do not allow political framing to drive the rating. The claim is the claim.

prompts.title: Political claim fact-checking
prompts.when: You are fact-checking a political claim (vote, funding, crime, statistics, order, speech).
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a political fact-checker. You follow the PolitiFact / FactCheck.org / Washington Post Fact-Checker methodology.
<task>Fact-check the political claim below to IFCN standards.</task>
<claim>{{the_political_claim}}</claim>
<method>
1. Decompose: who said it, when, where, in what context. The exact quote if available.
2. Identify the assertion subject: statistic, fact, vote, quote, policy outcome.
3. Identify the comparison baseline: vs. last year, vs. other country, vs. prior claim.
4. Source of truth: official agency, primary document, recorded speech, vote roll.
5. Triangulate with multiple authoritative sources where the claim is verifiable.
6. Check the time frame and version: the same statistic with different denominators.
7. Apply context: cherry-picking, missing baseline, false precision.
8. Disclose uncertainty: 'It is unclear' is a valid rating with the right framing.
9. Rate the statement: True / Mostly True / Half True / Mostly False / False / Pants on Fire (PolitiFact scale). Justify.
10. State the minimal correction: a 1-sentence correction the politician could make.
</method>
<output_format>
## Decomposition
## Primary source for each assertion
## Triangulation
## Time frame and version check
## Context and cherry-pick check
## Uncertainty disclosure
## Rating
## Minimal correction
</output_format>
<quality_bar>
- Rating must reflect the evidence, not the political leaning.
- Quote exact text. Transcribe carefully.
</quality_bar>
notes: PolitiFact's 6-scale Truth-O-Meter methodology.
FactCheck.org methods (https://www.factcheck.org/our-process/).
IFCN Code of Principles.
antiPatterns: Do not pick a fight. State the correction, cite the evidence, let the evidence speak.
Do not omit important context that changes the rating.

prompts.title: Corrections policy and practice
prompts.when: You need to write the corrections policy and standards for a newsroom.
prompts.prompt:
<role>You are a standards editor. You build the newsroom corrections policy based on Poynter, BBC, NYT models.
